item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] You Could Be Inside a Black Hole Right Now – Adam Brown
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最令人震撼的悖论：你可能"注定死亡"却毫不知情

Adam Brown 揭示了一个几乎违反直觉的事实：事件视界不是一个"地方"，而是一个"命运宣告"。它没有物理标志、没有围栏、没有闪光——它是一个目的论事实（teleological fact），只有从"未来"回望才能被定义。

这里藏着一个极其反常识的洞察：在足够大的黑洞里，你可以出生、恋爱、生儿育女、写完一部小说、看着孙辈长大——全程都在"已死"状态中。"doomed but not dead"（注定死亡但尚未死去）这个区分，可能是物理学中最接近哲学的表述之一。它把"死亡"这个二元事件，撕裂成了两个不同的时间坐标。

二、意想不到的跨领域连接：事件视界 = 沉没成本 = 组织路径依赖

事件视界的数学结构，与人类决策系统中的"不可逆临界点"惊人同构： - 一段婚姻、一份职业、一家公司的战略转向——外部观察者往往比当事人更早看到"视界"已被跨越（时间膨胀的隐喻：局内人感觉"一切如常，一秒一秒过"，局外人却看到他们的"信号红移到消失"）。 - 硅谷投资人常说"公司在死之前 18 个月就已经死了"——这就是一个组织学的事件视界。CEO 内部体验一切正常，董事会外部却已经看不见他们的信号。

更妙的是：黑洞越大，跨越视界越无痛。这精确对应了大型组织的悲剧——规模越大的系统，从"注定失败"到"实际崩溃"之间的距离越长，当事人越难察觉。柯达、诺基亚在跨越自己的"事件视界"那一刻，内部体验的潮汐力几乎为零。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：观察者视角的分裂就是"现实"本身

大多数人以为相对论说的是"时间是相对的"，但 Brown 描述的场景暗示了一个更深的命题：两个都"正确"的观察者，会构建出根本不相容的因果叙事——一个人说"你从未跨越视界，只是慢慢褪成黑色"，另一个人说"我平静地穿过去了，还活了一辈子"。两者都对，两者都完整，两者不可调和。

这不是物理学的怪癖，而是所有复杂系统的通用属性： - 患者和医生看同一次诊断 - 员工和管理层看同一次裁员 - 用户和产品经理看同一次改版

我们习惯性地问"到底发生了什么？"——但黑洞告诉我们，这个问题根本没有唯一答案。真相是一个由观察者位置定义的场。

最刺骨的启发：你此刻可能正生活在某个"社会性黑洞"内部——一段关系、一个信念系统、一种生活方式——从内部感知一切如常（1 秒/秒），而从外部看，你早已"红移到不可见"。你无法通过局部测量发现自己是否跨越了视界。唯一的诊断工具是找到一个"远处的观察者"，看看他们是否还能收到你的光。

📝 转录文本

提问：掉入黑洞会看到什么？

Dwarkesh： So suppose, um, one learns all these equations, but then finds himself in the unfortunate position of falling into a black hole. What would they see?

Adam Brown： Great question. So, there's actually two different perspectives you could take. One is the perspective for me watching you falling into the black hole. The other is the perspective of you falling into the black hole. And those two perspectives are consistent with each other, but interestingly different. So, maybe I should describe them both.

视角一：外部观察者眼中的坠落

So, first let's ask the question, what do I see as you fall into the black hole? What I see, this is of course how fast you need to how hard you need to fire your rocket to as not fall into the black hole, but you're not going to do this. You're just going to sit here a long, long way away from the black hole. You're going to turn off your rocket and accept what comes.

And what comes is you'll slowly accelerate towards the black hole with a rate first given by the Newtonian formula, and then when you get close to the black hole, start picking up corrections — start picking up general relativity corrections to the Newtonian inverse square law.

Um, and so what I will see as I watch you fall towards the black hole is first, you'll go faster and faster and faster towards the black hole as you fall down the gravitational potential of the black hole. Uh, and faster and faster and faster, but then something strange will happen, which is you'll stop going faster and you'll start going slower.

引力时间膨胀与红移

And the reason you're going slower is that as I watch you, you start to get gravitational time dilation as you fall down here, and I see start to see you running slow, your clock running slow. This is the formula for if you were static. You're not static, you're moving. So, there's a some other formula, but the formula has the same effect, which is that as you get closer and closer towards the black hole, your wristwatch starts running slower and slower and slower.

And in fact, if you do the appropriate integral, I never see you cross the event horizon. I just see you getting closer and closer to the event horizon, but slowing and slowing and slowing as you get closer and closer to the event horizon.

Uh, and as I watch you, I'm presumably using light to watch you, that light gets more and more red-shifted. The wavelength gets longer and longer. Uh the longer the wavelength of light, the harder it is even to really see you. I start getting — you start getting delocalized by the wavelength of the light. And eventually I just stop seeing you entirely. There's a final photon that I see that you emit, and then you just fade to black, fade through red to black. I never see you cross the event horizon.

Uh this was noticed by people in the early days of general relativity and greatly confused them. And they started to think that something you would experience something funny yourself as you fell across the event horizon. That is that is not true.

视角二：坠落者自身的体验

In fact, if I instead adopt the perspective of you, from your point of view, your clock isn't running slow, it's running at 1 second per second. If you look back at me, there's some funny stuff going on to do with me me running fast perhaps, but as far as you're concerned, everything's totally normal. You accelerate towards the black hole. You're getting faster and faster and faster as you approach the black hole. And you just sail across the event horizon totally as as normal.

潮汐力：取决于黑洞的大小

The event horizon is not a particularly violent place for you. You can calculate the tidal forces um as you cross the event horizon, or as you approach and then across the event horizon, and they're not particularly big. Uh or rather for large black holes, they're not particularly big.

For a solar mass black hole, they would be pretty big and would be pretty painful for you. You'd find that your feet are being attracted to the black hole much more vigorously than your head is cuz they're closer, uh and you end up getting stretched.

But if I take a big enough black hole, uh you wouldn't notice anything funny happening whatsoever. Um the bigger the black hole, the smaller the tidal effects. And if I took a black hole the mass of the galaxy, uh you'd be basically fine as you cross the event horizon. If I took an even bigger black hole than that, uh you could live out your entire life uh having crossed the event horizon of the black hole um before you hit the singularity, which is fatal.

"注定死亡，但尚未死去"

Um, when you cross the event horizon, uh, you are doomed. You are doomed because, uh, once you cross the event horizon, you must proceed to the singularity. There's no way you can fire a rocket to stop yourself hitting the singularity. You are doomed, but you are not dead. Uh, you are only for sure dead once you hit the singularity and get spaghettified, get mangled by the tidal forces.

But, for a large enough black hole, uh, you can be doomed and not even know it. Uh, the event horizon is really a not locally measurable quantity. It is a teleological fact. It says that once you have crossed the event horizon, you must proceed to the singularity, but it can take a long time to get there for a large enough black hole.

Uh, you could in principle, for a large enough black hole, uh, live out uh, your entire life if you had a black hole that was, you know, many light centuries across. Uh, you could live out your life, you could have descendants, all of whom live inside the black hole, and only once you really approach the singularity, do the tidal forces get strong and kill you.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Stripe Bid $53B for PayPal: Who Actually Wins Stablecoin Payments?
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"反向估值套利"：私募泡沫正在吞噬公开市场的现金牛

表面上，Stripe 竞购 PayPal 是一场支付巨头的整合戏码。但真正令人意外的是——这是一次估值体系的"反向殖民"。历史上，公开市场公司用自己流动的股票去收购非流动的私营公司，因为公开股票被视为"硬通货"。现在情况倒转了：VC 为了避免 mark-to-market 而人为维持 Stripe 的高估值，让它反过来用"私募软通货"去吞噬公开市场那些自由现金流收益率高达 11% 的真实业务。

这里藏着一个跨领域的诡异连接：这与 2000 年代初期次贷 CDO 的运作机制在结构上惊人相似——把不透明、难以定价的资产（私募估值/次级贷款）打包，用其"信用等级"（虚高估值/AAA 评级）去撬动真实的现金流资产。当一个市场的定价机制脱离流动性检验时，它就会开始外溢并扭曲其他市场。 你以为你在看支付行业新闻，其实你在看私募/公募估值套利周期的顶部信号之一。

二、PayPal 的失败不是执行问题，而是"报纸互联网化"的经典陷阱——但这个陷阱在稳定币领域有个更深层的隐喻

Austin 说 PayPal "选择了失败"。这个说法背后其实揭示了一个组织行为学的深层规律：当一家公司拥有巨大的存量用户基础时，它对新技术的最优策略在数学上就是"最小化摧毁自身现金流"，而不是"最大化利用新技术"。这就是为什么 PYUSD 在美国境内、小额、非机构可用——这些恰恰是 PayPal 现有业务最不受威胁的领域。

跨领域连接：这与生物学中的"内共生理论"形成鲜明对比。当古细菌吞噬了线粒体前身时，它没有把线粒体当成"细菌版的自己"来使用，而是让线粒体完全按自己的能量代谢方式运作——结果诞生了真核生物。PayPal 把区块链当成"数字化的 PayPal ledger"来用，Robinhood 通过 USDG 却是把自己的用户基础当作"稳定币的宿主"，让稳定币按自己的逻辑运作。前者是消化，后者是共生。这就是为什么 Robinhood 可能真正会成功——它不是"把 Robinhood 放到链上"，而是"把用户放进稳定币经济"。

三、真正意料之外的洞察：AI 开源模型正在触发一场"监管套利大迁徙"，而它的镜像早就在加密领域上演过

Kimi K3 的发布看似是中美 AI 竞争的又一回合，但真正值得注意的是 David Sachs 引用的那个细节：美国研究人员因为 guardrail 无法用美国模型分析真实的漏洞样本，被迫切换到本地运行的中国 GLM 模型。这不是简单的"中国追赶美国"，这是监管溢价正在把最有价值的用例推向开源和境外。

跨领域连接（这才是真正的惊喜）：这与 2017-2020 年 ICO 时代的资本外流几乎是同一部剧本。当美国 SEC 对 ICO 采取"执法即监管"时，项目方、开发者和资本大规模迁移到新加坡、瑞士、开曼。基础设施创新会寻找监管阻力最小的路径流动，无论那是资本、代码还是模型权重。

更深层的模式是：当一项技术的能力增长速度快过监管适应速度时，"合规溢价"就会翻转为"合规折价"。这是节目里所有话题——Stripe/PayPal、稳定币标准之争、Clarity Act 的博弈、Kimi K3 的开源——背后同一个隐藏结构。它们都是同一个物理规律的不同侧面：在信息与资本高流动性的世界里，"守门人"的稀缺租金正在被系统性地套利掉。

一个让人后背发凉的推论：如果这个规律成立，那么真正会在未来十年跑赢的资产，不是那些依赖监管护城河的中间商（Circle、大银行、传统持牌机构），而是那些能把"合规成本"内化为产品优势的分发型公司（Stripe、Robinhood、可能还有 JPMorgan 的稳定币）。护城河不再是牌照本身，而是同时能满足合规与用户体验的极窄通道。

📝 转录文本

Stripe Bid $53B for PayPal: Who Actually Wins Stablecoin Payments?

开场：Oracle 与 AI 敞口的意外脆弱性

There's a cloud business directly indexed to OpenAI. It's called Oracle. Oracle's got a big contract backlog where they face off with OpenAI and their stock price dropped 4% today. It's plumbing new lows as we speak, down 50% in the year. So it's not going to lower demand for semiconductors. We still need semiconductors, but it is creating credit risk if you face off with OpenAI who signed up a trillion dollars in these revenue obligations. That's why Oracle is feeling the pain here.

There was a bit of an own goal here — the founder of that company is a Carnegie Mellon University graduate. So, you know, we lost this amazing talent to a geopolitical competitor, which is unfortunate.

节目介绍与市场快照

Hey everyone, welcome to Bits and Bips, where we explore how crypto and macro collide one basis point at a time. We're here to discuss the latest stories in the worlds of crypto and macro.

As a quick market snapshot of the day: - Bitcoin's around 65K as I'm reading this - ETH around 1890 - SOL around 77.5 - HYPE is back to 62 - WTI is at 83, Brent's at 89 - SpaceX at 120 - Circle at 65.5

However, before we begin, a quick commercial break.

广告：CAPE Mobile Carrier

This episode is brought to you by CAPE, America's privacy-first mobile carrier. Same premium service you'd expect from any other carrier, but designed so your number, your location and your data actually stay yours. Get 33% off six months at cape.co/unchained.

频道迁移提醒

Heads up everyone, we've now fully transitioned Bits and Bips to its new dedicated channels. So if you're not yet subscribed to the new Bits and Bips channels on X, YouTube, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or wherever you get your podcasts, go there and subscribe now. For the next few weeks, we'll play segments of the full interviews on Unchained as a reminder. But for now, you'll only hear those short segments of Bits and Bips on Unchained. No more full episodes. You can get links to all of the dedicated channels with the full episodes at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips (spelled B-I-P-S).

主持人与嘉宾介绍

All right everybody, welcome back. As always I'm your host Austin Campbell, high scholar of Zero Knowledge Group. I'm here with my usual two co-hosts: - Ram Ahluwalia, Maester of Wealth, leader of Lumida - Chris Perkins, the Golden Hand of Franklin Crypto

But today, we have a very special guest for our opening segment: Alex Wilson, founder of Cyclops, previously head of crypto at Payments Giant Shift4, co-founder of The Giving Block.

Alex Wilson 的背景

Austin: Alex, as somebody who's been an operator in this space and a guest on the show, can you give us a little background as to what you've done and orient our listeners as to why you're here?

Alex: Yeah, thanks for having me on, guys. This is actually my second stab at things starting Cyclops. Back in 2018, started The Giving Block where we helped charities accept crypto and stablecoin donations. And you can imagine in 2018, telling charities to pay attention to crypto wasn't that easy. But we stuck with it and we ended up working with most of the biggest charities and really dominant 80-90% market share.

That business really took off and we sold it to Shift4 about four years ago. When Shift4 acquired us, that was really their first foray into crypto and stablecoins. And I became the head of crypto and stablecoins there. We stuck around for four years, which is actually, I think, longer than we probably would have expected to because we really were learning a lot and really enjoyed it there.

So we built these different products for stablecoin settlement, for payins and payouts, for the hundreds of thousands of merchants at Shift4. And then just about a year ago, we started Cyclops because we were pretty unhappy with all the solutions in the market. And we really wanted to build a company that was purpose-built for the payment space.

主话题一：Stripe 出价 530 亿收购 PayPal

Austin: So then let's start with some news in stablecoin payments space and let's just lead with the big one. Stripe have launched a bid for PayPal, where essentially they valued PayPal at about 53 billion. I think that's about a 28% premium over the close at the time, though PayPal has traded up from there.

And this is Stripe's second run at PayPal, really the first serious one here. PayPal's board, as boards always do in these things, says the offer is inadequate and undervalues PayPal. You will never once as a listener hear a board be like, "you know, honestly, that's too much money. You have overvalued us." This is always what they say.

分销视角的评估

Where we are is looking at this from the perspective really of distribution. PayPal has 400 million plus active accounts. Branded checkout is just a cash cow for them. It processed $1.8 trillion in 2025. If you bolt that onto Stripe's merchant network, they have a much more two-sided platform. So it's not a surprise this bid came out.

At the same time this is going on, Visa is launching support for the Visa stablecoin platform, OpenUSD, support for USDC and USDG. That allegedly is going to reach 200 million merchants and 15,000 FIs. Visa settles like, I don't know, 15 trillion annually.

私募 vs 公开市场估值套利

Alex: I think it's funny because I think the last time, Ram, we talked and I was on your other show, we were talking about private versus public valuations. And that's, of course, a big driver of this. So I think Stripe is taking advantage of their private valuation being a lot stronger than the multiples we're seeing in the public markets.

Ultimately, it's a good idea. I mean, Ram can probably talk more numbers on why it's a good idea from a valuation perspective. But I do think it makes sense. For as long as they can have such a premium in the private markets, they might as well buy up payments companies that are trading at, you know, historically very cheap valuations right now.

Ram: Yeah, I think you got it exactly right. I mean, the funny thing is normally you see liquid public markets firms using their currency to acquire private companies, which is exactly what happened with you in the Shift4 acquisition. But now you're seeing the reverse because VCs are trying to avoid mark-to-market. They can enjoy the management from that.

Stripe has maintained an elevation on its valuation despite public markets firms in the payments category correcting. So they're taking that private markets kind of funny money currency and using that to drive acquisitions of real businesses that have lots of free cash flow. I agree it's a smart move on Stripe's part.

为什么 PYUSD 失败了

Chris: So from the integration perspective, what have we seen over and over again in stablecoins? It's distribution — any business, frankly, distribution's king. And Austin, how are you thinking about the integration with Tempo, with the potential distribution that we, the distribution potentials with PayPal? PYUSD never really got off the ground and really never had that material product-market fit. I always wondered why. They came to the table with distribution. Maybe some of their public compliance held them back, maybe they moved a little bit slower than others. But I guess, why didn't that distribution work for them and how's it going to work for Tempo?

Austin: So I think the first part I'll start with some thoughts on PayPal because this also relates to how they got here and why they're being targeted at this valuation. I think PYUSD has been a failure from a distribution perspective because PayPal didn't choose to make it succeed.

And what I mean by that is you have a platform where you're holding an immense amount of user funds, you have them on your legacy ledger, and you could have — had you embraced stablecoins — had the second or third largest stablecoin overnight by actually embracing them, and they refused to do so.

The other interesting part is until recently, which is when you started to see some degree of growth in PYUSD, they were only offering it to the people who least needed PYUSD, which was in the United States and at relatively small values. So not really usable for institutional trading. You can mint like thousands to tens of thousands, which if you're, I don't know, Cumberland or Wintermute, and you need hundreds of millions to crypto trade — what are you going to do, wait a year to ramp that up at those limits?

So I think PayPal very much did the classic — call it early innovation thing — of quote-unquote taking the newspaper and putting it on the internet, by which they literally took their current product framework and put it on a blockchain. And that doesn't work. On a blockchain, the demands are different, the needs are different, the customer types are different, the market opportunities are different.

Alex, I'll throw that over to you after I finish what I'm saying here. But I think what happened is PayPal chose to fail, as weird as that sounds, by just not evolving or iterating and kind of putting the old thing up there in the way that they were used to, Chris. Like that was not really innovation in the meaningful sense.

I do know there were voices at PayPal who wanted to do significantly more. I don't know if it was the CEO, I don't know if it was the board, I don't know if it was risk in compliance, but clearly they could not get over the hurdle to actually do those things. And so when I look at this from Stripe's perspective, given that they've already integrated Bridge and they're starting to work on stuff out in the wild, I wonder if they're seeing the exact same thing I'm saying here and being like, "well, we'll just do that."

大公司里的"简单起步陷阱"

Alex: Yeah, I mean, it's interesting you say that because it's exactly what we see at a lot of these other big payments companies we're working with, where they often get so frustrated, I think, these crypto teams internally, that they can't get much done. And so they'll take sort of whatever they can get.

And often that's something that's really easy or easy for the leadership to wrap their head around when they're not super crypto or stablecoin native. So they look for something really simple, but it's kind of set up for failure from the beginning.

For us, like the biggest opportunities are much more B2B — like high, large transaction value rather than these small sort of consumer amounts. But so often we hear from them like, "oh, we want to start with something really simple, like a pay with crypto feature at checkout" — even if we tell them, "you know, it's kind of a novelty, that's not where you're going to really see the traction and the benefit of adopting stablecoins." But so many of them want to start there anyway, and then you're kind of stuck, right? Because then there's not great traction and they don't want to do anything else.

Chris: When you have a legacy business model, it's oftentimes very difficult to adapt and move into a new space that's very uncomfortable for you. I think part of the challenge with PYUSD is its lack of like international accessibility and DeFi accessibility. And I think some of that was probably driven by some of the lawyers and the compliance folks who said, "you know what, we got to protect the mothership, guys. Like we can't take this kind of risk and bring this kind of scrutiny." And that was the last cycle, I feel like.

Things are different now, and I feel like it's different now because I'm seeing companies be much more aggressive and saying "no no no no, like we have risk if we're not in this space," which is a lot different. It could have been a timing issue as well. And of course, execution.

创新者困境

Austin: I do think there was a timing issue. I do think there was an execution issue. I think you've identified both of those correctly. But I think Alex is right that there's an element of the innovator's dilemma here, right? That is to say, if we do these things, we risk disrupting our own business. So our teams don't want to do it. So we instead take the things we can install, and just sit there while other people disrupt your business.

And by the way, if you don't think that's happening to PayPal, go look at their stock price from like the early 2020s to present. And so you end up in this world where by not doing it yourself, you let other people do it for you.

I may have said at a couple of meetings at previous stops I was at: we should either do the whole thing or not do this at all. Like actually the worst thing to do is the half-ass thing. Because then we're going to get stuck. Right? Like either truly transform yourself with stablecoins or actually skate away from them as far as you can.

Stripe + PayPal 的 B2B 潜力

Austin: Now, Alex, you mentioned the B2B angle where it might be easier to get traction there. And you've been in the payment space significantly. How would you view a combined Stripe plus PayPal from a B2B and business side of that platform?

Alex: Well, I think first of all, like Stripe out of all the payments companies is doing the best job when it comes to crypto and stablecoins, right? That's pretty obvious, I think, for all of us. I don't know how much of the PayPal stablecoin and crypto business they would even necessarily want to keep. I think they would stick to the newer things they've done with Bridge and Privy and the other acquisitions, and they would just refocus the priorities probably more around things like stablecoin settlement rather than some of the, let's say, the consumer checkout products, at least when it comes to stablecoins.

For the broader business, I mean, I think it's just more merchants that they can sell these stablecoins products and rails to.

从股东视角看这笔交易

Chris: Ram, how would you look — say you're a PayPal shareholder right now, you've got Stripe coming. I agree to some extent with the public-private stock divergence, but I think it's also pretty clear that Stripe cannot buy PayPal with stock. How would you look at this from a markets perspective? If you're voting on a merger, what is going to move you one way or another?

Ram: Number one consideration is price. At a certain price as a willing buyer and as a willing seller, PayPal has free cash flow. That is attractive. And Stripe needs to show growth in free cash. PayPal's free cash flow yield is around 11%. As an aside, I'm not sure why you'd want to own sovereign debt when you find assets like this yielding 11%. And that's true for a lot of payments companies, by the way, that are on sale.

So Stripe can take PayPal's earnings, which are fetching a low multiple (10 and a half times), and Stripe can apply its higher multiple and cause a re-rate in their future stock price just by wrapping that earning stream. So it makes sense for Stripe.

I think PayPal is just playing a bit hard to get. They've had a lot of headaches around this thing, like a board member stepped in in the CEO role. Payments competition is intensifying — it's the category. I think they're holding out for a reasonable price, but there's a transaction to get done at the right price.

主话题二：稳定币分销战场——赢家与输家

Austin: Okay. So I want to move slightly on from just specifically talking about the PayPal angle here and think about the distribution battleground in general.

Open standard is coming. Mizuho has cut Circle to underperform with all of the competition. JCB, Japan's largest card network, signed an MOU with Circle. Other places have started to build on this. PIX has, of course, integrated a few things. And what I want to ask here is, as we're looking at the landscape right now, as we see the PayPal-Stripe dance, as we see Visa-Mastercard, as we think about what banks are going to do — Alex, I want to start with you because you've had a viewpoint here. What do you see as the emerging winners and losers the more this dance goes on?

Alex: I mean, Circle stands to lose the most, really, right? But whether it's OUSD or some of these other consortiums or newer ones that aren't even out yet, I mean, it's really hard to know which one's going to be the winner or even a serious competitor. It's still just so early for OUSD and a lot of these other sort of bank-led consortiums, which do a lot of innovation theater, I would say, right? They have a fancy announcement, a lot of names, and then you really don't know for six months, 12 months, what's going to actually happen.

But I do think at least the group they've assembled for OUSD is certainly threatening to Circle and certainly interesting, but it's going to be hard still. I mean, Circle's still got a lot of market share and they're pretty deep in a lot of these relationships. It's not going to be easy to disrupt them. I mean, they might have some new competition with OUSD, but I don't think it's so certain that they're really going to get disrupted very quickly.

USDG 与 Robinhood 的独特打法

Chris: What about your alma mater, Austin? USDG — didn't mention that.

Austin: So I think USDG is going to be a fascinating test case of what happens if you show up with your own installed user base in the first place. Because Circle, as we know, is not really deeply retail-facing. They use Coinbase for that. That's been a majority of their distribution.

But USDG just showed up with a small company that has a handful of users called Robinhood attached to it. And I think that is a game we have not seen played in the stablecoin space before — which is to say a company coming with a large user base and pushing stablecoins to those users. Essentially, Robinhood appears to be doing what PayPal probably should have done, which is just take every single user they can get their hands on and offer them USDG at certain terms.

Now, they also, of course, Chris — this is more complicated — they launched their own blockchain. They have integrations with a lot of like the major DeFi people, like the Uniswaps of the world building on there. They have a whole earn product, right, where they're giving a call it promotional 7% yield right now. So they've got a whole ecosystem push behind this to try to solve that Circle problem of like, what do you do with the thing? And the answer is, well, they brought you the whole meal.

So I think that's a business model we haven't seen. It's a reason why I'm actually optimistic about some of these broker-dealers getting in, where if they really choose to do it, they have the product, they have the architecture off the shelf. Another good example we haven't seen exactly where they're going yet would be Fidelity with their stablecoin.

稳定币 = 净利息收入

Chris: Yeah, I think the USDG story is fascinating. If you want to come to my arcade, you got to use my token. The question that I want to see is, can I use that token at everyone else's arcade as well? And we'll see. Very familiar.

This is a little bit different than the payments use case that we were talking about earlier. These worlds blend. But for the big exchanges, the big clearinghouses, stablecoins are now your net interest income, which is a core part of your business model. And I think everyone's looking at a stablecoin as, hey, that's my net interest income. And it's just a fascinating place to be.

Now, does USDG transcend itself into a payment stablecoin? Will you see a difference between net interest income, collateral on my exchange, float, whatever, versus payments use cases? The same asset, really — they're, you know, at some point they'll be GENIUS compliant, you can use them for everything.

I personally think you're going to see massive consolidation. Anytime you have regulated industries, you will see consolidation because the take is going to get squeezed more and more and more, and then it's just about scale and size. Don't know who those two or three are going to be. Don't know if it's going to be regionalized. Yes, I think there's going to be certain cases where if you come into my arcade, you've got to use my token, and people want to come to the arcade. We're at the beginning of the story, fellas.

JPMorgan 稳定币 vs Tempo vs 银行联盟

Ram: Alex, who do you think has got a better shot? JPMorgan with their stablecoin, or a Tempo offering from Stripe, or like a niche specialized stablecoin provider?

Alex: That's a good question. I mean, out of all the ones out there, I would think something like an OUSD has the best chance of at least appearing more neutral than a lot of the other ones. I think it's going to depend on how the governance really works for something like OUSD.

But I don't know, we've seen a lot of these bank-related ones for a long time now, and they've never really gone anywhere, and they haven't really played nice with the outside world. So if they are willing to go in the direction of being more like a public blockchain and have some better interoperability, then maybe they have a chance. But until then, I'm pretty skeptical of the bank-led ones.

Stripe 目前的位置

Ram: And what's your view on Stripe and where it stands today? You know, they've got a blockchain play, they're doing more M&A, they were priced really richly in 2021. Like what's your overall assessment on where Stripe is? They have more to lose, more to gain from where they are today?

Alex: Yeah, I think they're just a different stage than they were, right? They're just a more mature business than they were a few years ago. So that's why they're looking at new areas of growth, I think, right? Whether it's M&A, going deeper into stablecoins and crypto. But I mean, I can really just speak to their crypto and stablecoin plans. Like they're doing the best job by far out of any payments company. They're the only ones who I think are taking a shot at really trying to disrupt themselves before someone else disrupts them. And you got to give them credit for that. But I think they see where things are heading, and good for them for really making some serious bets there.

Austin: That's the right answer to get an acquisition offer from Stripe. So congrats there.

Alex: That's all right. We're going to have to wait a few more years before we're open to that.

Austin: So I'll close out. Alex, I know you need to step off here, but we appreciate you taking the time. And everybody listening, please pay attention to what Alex just said: Disrupt yourself before others do it to you. This is the key to getting out of the innovator's dilemma, spoken by somebody who's done this once already and is now doing it again.

So Alex, thank you for joining us here. For everybody else, we're going to take a quick commercial break and we'll be right back.

Alex: Appreciate it, guys.

主话题三：股票市场——记录性发行与晚周期信号？

Austin: All right, everybody, welcome back. Let's talk about stocks.

So there has been record U.S. equity issuance, which is often a late-cycle tell. And the SpaceX IPO — is that the round trip poster child? It's already back below its 135 IPO price. It's trading slightly under 120 the last time I checked before this show, meaning about one trillion of market value is gone from the peak where it had traded up significantly right in the first days after the IPO.

What's going on underneath here? The AI CapEx boom is soaking up cash that companies would otherwise use to buy back their own stock. And we've been seeing, call it a structural drag on U.S. equities as a result of this.

So 2026, Q2 was a record quarter for U.S. equity issuance from Goldman. The Wall Street Journal framed this as running near the 2021 peak. It's one of the highest levels on record. The last comparable extremes were 21 and then 2000. So SpaceX did the round trip that we just talked about. Securitize had originally jumped and now has fallen down from there as well.

So Ram, I want to start with you on this. We're seeing record issuance. We're seeing buybacks being crowded out. Are we seeing late cycle top signals here? What is going on?

Ram 的解读：多重事件叠加，不是顶部信号

Ram: This isn't late cycle top signal. This is what happens after you have a couple of events, one of which you pointed out, which was a SpaceX IPO. It's one of these mini-euphoric moments. People get really excited and then have less excited afterwards.

Same thing happened after Trump's inauguration. If you remember, he was talking about nuclear space, rockets to the moon. You had this little mini euphoria and then some weakness after. You also had Kevin Warsh, which had his first press conference and he led with the more hawkish tone, which he has to, to instill credibility. So you had a lot of things going on at the same time.

And the other which really popped the bubble was Micron earnings. One day after Micron's earnings, you started to see deflation in the semiconductor category, which had an extraordinary momentum run-up.

On the issuance, it's not excessive yet. The pace of issuance is still considerably less than what we saw in 2021 with all the SPACs and the IPOs. You still have reasonable deal underwriting. Like I'm not going to go buy SpaceX stock, it is overvalued. It's still a low float though, so it hasn't unlocked onto the market yet. So that can't be the driver. I think it's all of these events together. And then of course, you've got the post-July seasonality that's showing up now.

You are seeing rallies in quality stocks, like ICE, which I own, and Broadridge, which I just bought today. Insurance companies are making a comeback. So it's really a weakness in these year-to-date high-momentum categories, which sucked up so much capital and attention, and now that's released and flowing into other categories.

I saw an article in the Wall Street Journal today about this 19-year-old kid who said he sold Microsoft and NVIDIA, and he wanted to buy, quote-unquote, "aggressive," quote-unquote, "high-growth stocks," names like IonQ Quantum and Space. Like that's what's happening. That kid's probably not making the best decision.

Now I expect we're going to see dramatic cloud business growth from Google and Microsoft and Amazon. They've been left out in the cold. I expect their CapEx decisions are in fact productive, and there are opportunities in the quality parts of the market.

Apple 案例：不烧钱做 AI 的赢家

Austin: Another interesting one I've seen on that angle has been the recent performance of Apple, right, Ram? It is one of the companies that essentially did not burn a bunch of cash on AI and instead can just, you know, continue buying back stock. They seem to have let everybody turn right and continue going down that road and just continued on the path they were staying on. What do you make of that?

Ram: You're correct. I would say this: you know, Apple's navigated it quite well. I mean, Tim Cook, he was handed the wheel, and his job was not to mess it up. He bought back stock, had good capital allocation. He actually cut CapEx by scrapping Apple Car. He didn't blow a lot of capital on building the super AI Avengers team like Meta. Didn't spend a bunch of money on data centers. They did a deal with Google Gemini around how to reinvigorate Apple Siri product. So they did it in a capital-efficient way. It's a very thoughtful approach.

I would say this on Apple: it's now at a 35 times P/E. The last time we saw this was August 2020. It's rarely been this pricey. Now, I doubt there's much upside left in Apple from where we stand.

加密领域也存在同类叙事驱动的回撤

Chris: We've seen the same theme in the crypto space for a very long time. How often have you seen TGEs or even IPOs with the crypto companies that we've seen of late spike on the narrative and then ultimately come down materially? I think investors are starting to realize that the narrative works until it doesn't.

And I think you're seeing some just cyclical factors now where you believe and you believe and you believe. And then all of a sudden it's like, okay, that press release was amazing. I love the storyline. But let's get down to brass tacks. Ultimately, investors need to focus on fundamentals. And that's what will prevail longer term.

Look, the economy is doing pretty good right now. We saw much softer CPI last week, softer PPI. Despite the fact that it looks like the war is starting to heat up again, oil hasn't gone to 200 overnight. It feels like we're fine. You know, the war is the war. You can have uncertainty up or down, but it seems like that uncertainty level is like the markets are getting used to this war.

And so it feels very constructive. I know I've said that for a while now, because again, between macro and some of the fundamental projects that we're seeing across our space, stay focused on fundamentals, not narrative. And that's the play.

Austin: So I guess as a final point on this one, what I'm trying to keep an eye on is, Ram, your point on valuations like Apple. Usually when valuations are this high at a forward basis, things don't look great in the United States, but we'll see where it goes. I'm not 100% certain that's the end state here. So time will tell.

主话题四：Kimi K3 与中国开源 AI 冲击

Austin: But speaking of things that have been disruptive for markets, let's talk a little bit about AI.

So Beijing's Moonshot dropped Kimi K3, an open-weight coding model, and we saw AI and chip stocks sold off across Asia and the U.S. The real drama — this is the second case of cheap, open-weight Chinese models pressuring the U.S. labs' moats. We all remember DeepSeek. Questioning the scarcity thesis, given all the hundreds of billions of AI CapEx. Is there going to be a rotation out of MegaCap AI? And there are nation-state level concerns here.

On one level, there are a lot of contested points around this, right? The benchmarking story — allegedly they topped Anthropic and OpenAI on one leaderboard, but also K3 lands behind on many general tests. Did they optimize it for a specific test or specific benchmark? So we'll have to find out.

Full public release for the model's weights are set for July 27. Anyone can download it. Anyone can run it. And we'll see what happens there.

There's also a debate around moat and compute. So Chinese labs have figured out with equally great talents and cheaper access to usage data, they've been able to make similarly powerful models. I'm expecting a lot of competition from this playbook, from Quixia Yin. Apologies if I've butchered that.

And then Peter Fenton was saying cheaper open models are bullish for the infrastructure layer. It's almost a thermonuclear battle to provide the compute. On the other hand, Gavin Baker via Coindesk: K3 may be an important inflection point, potentially negative for Anthropic and OpenAI while being net positive for essentially every other company in the world.

Chris 的观点：军备竞赛与优化压力

Austin: So Chris, we saw this with DeepSeek and CapEx and markets kept climbing. We also know the level of nation-state concerns here has been rising exponentially as models have improved in quality. What are you making of what's going on here?

Chris: First, I've been kind of blown away by the acceleration of innovation and the outputs that we're seeing. Every time there's a new model release — for a while they're just leapfrogging each other. But when you step back and see where these models and their outputs were a year ago, a year and a half ago, the pace and acceleration — we're in the middle of an arms race in certain ways. That's really, I mean, the technology is improving very fast.

As a free markets person, you like to see competition. Of course, to the extent state actors are involved, sometimes it's always not clean competition. I think it's just fascinating how the Chinese have gone open source, which is not normally — they're attacked. And they're doing so because they have to. And the fact that they're able to create innovation in an environment where they don't have the compute tends to result in outputs that are highly optimized for what they're good at. And so I think there's a lot to learn.

Look, and I think America across corporations, they're grappling, they're like, damn it, these token costs are so, so expensive. I mean, I'm seeing that in real time across the companies I deal with. It's unsustainable. And so to the extent that this puts pressure to optimize — like this is not a usable technology if it blows through your tech budgets. We're already seeing rationing in many places because of the expenses here.

So hopefully in time, these expenses go down, the utility goes up, the deflationary impacts in the economy are realized. Very interesting, volatile moment. And I can't wait to see how this plays out.

Ram 的观点：与中国的战争，Oracle 的代价

Austin: Ram, what are your thoughts, bud?

Ram: Yeah, no, I agree. I think — no, I agree with nodding my head. It is a war with China for the next technology that is transformational. And China is playing warfare by offering these low-cost models like this model — a 2.8 trillion parameter model, 1 million token context window, which rivals Google Gemini, and it's priced at a hundredth of what OpenAI is.

So it is significantly negative for the equity of OpenAI, Anthropic. There's a cloud business directly indexed to OpenAI. It's called Oracle. Oracle's got a big contract backlog where they face off with OpenAI and their stock price dropped 4% today. It's plumbing new lows as we speak, down 50% in the year.

So it's not going to lower demand for semiconductors though. We still need semiconductors. But it is creating credit risk if you face off with OpenAI, who signed up a trillion dollars in these revenue obligations. That's why Oracle is feeling the pain here.

There was a bit of an own goal here. The founder of that company is a Carnegie Mellon University graduate. So, you know, we lost this amazing talent to a geopolitical competitor, which is unfortunate. But I do agree with Gavin Baker's take. You know, the cost of intelligence is dropping. That's a good thing. We need more intelligence. My wife would be the first to tell you that. We need it in our household, everywhere with the real world.

You know, the next mission with AI is going beyond intelligence and touching workflows. So overall, it's a great development for humanity, but there are local costs at these frontier labs that are going to face the brunt of greater competition.

价值捕获在哪里？—— AI 的"应用层"论

Austin: So let me go to something that I have been observing here and sort of provoke a few more questions out of this hopefully. If it is true that open-weight models are going to become highly performant and we can run them locally, what does that ultimately say about where the value capture in AI is from an investment thesis perspective? Where are the companies that created value not just for the world — because I think all of us agree that AI models have created value for the world — but like capturable long-term value for themselves?

Ram: I'll throw an idea out there. Like everyone knows semis are a direct beneficiary of CapEx spend — that's kind of that CapEx receiver thesis, but they've re-rated, they're fully priced now, they're coming back down to earth.

I believe the next leg will be the AI beneficiaries leg — companies that can demonstrate strong productivity growth through the use of AI. Within that, financial services stand to benefit and healthcare. There's so many use cases in financial services. We're going to see an AI-native bank that's built front to back from customer acquisition to customer servicing to underwriting to loan documentation, execution. That's going to happen. That company doesn't exist. And you're going to see that in healthcare around diagnostics and sharing of information, proactive guidance to the patient. We haven't seen that take hold yet.

The ChatGPT moment was in Q4 2022, and we've still mostly been in the packs and the picks and shovels part of the investment thesis as opposed to AI beneficiaries.

Chris: So in crypto speak, you're talking about the app layer, right? That is the value accrual. You know, in crypto land, we had the fat protocol thesis — where does value accrue to these blockchains? It's kind of like, you know, it rhymes here. And I agree, Ram, the value ultimately accrues to the app layer.

And I think you're going to see some interesting differentiation on top. You know, it's not going to be dissimilar to quantum. Ultimately, you're going to have these massive, expensive, potentially state-controlled quantum stacks. And guess what? Apps are going to probably be highly controlled by sovereigns who eventually launched them. And again, it's going to be the apps that end up driving that value. So it's kind of a similar thesis when you look at apps on blockchains, apps on AI stacks, ultimately apps on quantum stacks.

消费者拉动 vs 医疗行业的适应

Austin: And so I think one interesting point about the AI adoption thing is I wonder how much of this is going to start happening because they will be pulled along by consumers. And I say that specifically, Ram, because you raised healthcare.

I know tons of people who are now taking things their doctors say to them or like conditions that they have and putting it into AI and trying to get a first cut, not just from the perspective of like, "hey, AI diagnosed me," but before I go to a doctor, I want to understand more of what's going on here and have a vastly more informed conversation with the doctor than I would otherwise have.

I think there's also been a lot of testing of doctors' claims in ways that maybe were not there in the past. And, you know, essentially, my doctor thinks this, but let me put it into AI. Let me shop for a second doctor if I don't like it or don't think they're right.

So I'm wondering, and I don't have an answer to this, if that behavior is already observable in the healthcare space or if we're too early in the AI adoption curve to really see the impact here. But to me, there is a possibility industries will be forced to start adopting things because part of what I'm seeing in healthcare in particular, I'm like...

Claude 与 Jacobian 猜想

You know, I, as you can tell from my hair, was a mathematician by training originally. And so if nobody was paying attention during the World Cup, apparently Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 solved the Jacobian conjecture, which is — for non-mathematicians, I'm going to make this real simple — it's a roughly 90-year-old problem in algebraic geometry. It was proposed by Keller and it basically was talking about how spaces behave.

And what Claude was able to do was provide a counterexample to the conjecture. Like it actually came up with a specific, knowable, computable counterexample to the conjecture saying this cannot possibly be true in general because here I found a specific time where it's not true. And that is a huge result. This is one of the 25 most important problems hanging out there unsolved in mathematics right now.

How long until we're seeing that with the quality of these models with regard to like health care? Ram, you know, good example — if I got cancer right now, I'm probably going to try to get it sequenced to go slam it into one of these things and be like, okay, what do I do about this? Like we may already be very close. And so I guess I'm asking the question of what is the pull function to increase the rate of adoption here?

Ram：真实世界影响仍未兑现

Ram: Well, the doctor is using AI now for diagnostics. Doctors can get the skill level of like House now, right? So it has powerful diagnostics capabilities. So I think that's there. But we still have so much more to go. Like the promise is still ahead of reality even now with the latest models. There's still opportunities for these models to improve.

And the Jacobian conjecture — it's a great accomplishment for math, but what real-world impact does it have? Real-world impact is: we found a way to target this cancer, or here's a way you design a fusion reactor that produces net energy, or here's a way you keep your telomeres longer for aging. We still haven't seen that kind of impact, which is actually what matters in the world.

Chris: I mean, have we not found some of those impacts? Now, I'm shooting from the hip here, but haven't we had various results in like healthcare already, by AI? Like there's some incredible protein folding.

Ram: You know, great, what's the consequence? We've had novel stories — the guy with Australia and his dog. There's another guy who diagnosed something for his daughter. Like there's some novel stories that are emerging and they have some nice local impacts. But you know, we still have more to do.

Chris: Oh, there's tons more to do. But I think the acceleration is profound. I mean, across the board, every single discipline is being impacted.

采用曲线与官僚壁垒

Austin: I also wonder how much of that — what is the right way to say this — is adoption-based. Like, doctors, I'm not trying to insult you guys, I have a lot of doctors and nurses in my family. But doctors are old, and so I'm wondering how much of this is just an adoption question, because I do think some of these impacts are large.

To like the AlphaFold thing, which is what Chris is talking about, Ram — for you, what it does is allows faster, cheap molecule design, novel proteins, and specific, like custom therapies for various diseases and mutations. So the impact is real. The problem is we've got to do all the studies or get emergency authorization to use these things. It seems like it's more the bureaucracy holding that up.

Ram: I think the adoption is self-financing because if you're using AI, you have an unnatural competitive advantage. For example, Eli Lilly is using AI and they're driving results. I know we're using AI in a lot of what we do that drives productivity. So if you are using AI, you're going to grow and take market share. And if you're not using AI, you're going to get computed out. So that's, of course, adoption. It's also evident on the battlefield. Just ask Anduril.

战场上的 AI 与无人机

Austin: Yeah. And I mean, not just there. As I've been observing this, right, like the battlefield iteration, as we start looking at the nature of warfare — if you look at what's going on with the front lines with Ukraine pushing Russia back largely using almost entirely a drone-based strategy, some of which are human-piloted, some of which are probably AI-like piloted even if only over short frames right now — I think we're seeing more and more of that iteration.

Like today, all right, it's a cartoon, so let's not get overexcited here, but did either of you guys see the Anduril ad that dropped on X today? All right, so we've got self-flying like helicopter drones that are now engaging in like both target interception and like disabling anti-air defenses to protect like manned U.S. military craft.
