There's also work from a public in this lab to have the cells secrete little vesicles and they harvest that in the cell culture media to measure what the cells are producing longitudinally. But there hasn't been technology that can let you measure the entire cell's transcriptome while still keeping the cell. You can't have the cake and eat it.

结语与招聘

[Brandon] Cool. Yeah, thank you for taking the time to chat with us. It's been great. We've learned a lot. It was a lot of really interesting discussions. I especially really appreciate your commitment to open science and all of the cool models and data, if you're at least. Is there any last thoughts you have or anything you'd like the audience to know? Follow up on that.

[Bo Wang] Overall, I think virtual cell is such a new and fast moving field. We hope to have more and more people join us and our Excel paper is out and we look forward to receiving your comments and feedbacks. And also we're hiring.

[Xi Chu] Yeah, we are always looking for talented engineers, technologists, biologists, drug hunters, AI scientists, computational scientists. So look on xaira.com. Look for the open roles. We'll be happy to chat with you.

[RJ] Thank you. Thank you very much. Great.

[Xi Chu] Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、意外观察：他们在“修改”细胞，而不是“书写”细胞
大多数生物学基础模型（scGPT、Geneformer）沿用了 ChatGPT 的**自回归范式**——像打字一样一个 token 接一个 token 地生成。但基因表达数据根本没有天然顺序！Bo Wang 团队做出的最激进选择，是把生成范式从"**打字**"切换到"**编辑**"——用扩散语言模型迭代式地精炼整个基因表达向量。
这里有个被访谈者本人没有明说的深层反讽：**整个 NLP 领域花了七年时间想把 Transformer 从"集合运算符"逼成"序列生成器"，而生物学领域正在反向走回去**——因为细胞根本不是句子，是同时炸开的 20000 维快照。Brandon 在采访里模糊地感觉到这点（"transformer 本来就是 set operation"），但没人挑明：**X-Cell 的成功其实在暗示——语言模型的"因果顺序"归纳偏置对语言可能都是次优的，只是被工程惯性锁死了**。生物学在这里像一个自然实验，暴露了 LLM 架构选择中一个被忽略的假设。
## 二、跨领域连接：从"图像分类中的坦克"到"批次效应中的

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Mark Cuban on the AI Bubble: Who Actually Gets Wiped Out?
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、这不是互联网泡沫，这是风投行业的自我引爆

Cuban 抛出了一个反直觉的诊断：这场 AI 泡沫破裂时，出租车司机不会谈论它，普通美国人也不会受伤——因为泡沫的能量被封闭在一个专业阶层内部。这与 2000 年那种"人民的泡沫"截然相反。

有意思的是这里有个隐藏的对称美感：VC/PE 曾用"风险民主化"的叙事把散户从公开市场吸走（因为好公司都留在私募里），结果他们成功地把风险也留给了自己。这是一个自我实现的因果闭环——他们把公众隔离在外，最终也把损失隔离在了自己手里。这或许是资本主义历史上第一次，"泡沫破裂"变成一场行业内部的清算，而不是社会性的伤口。

跨领域的连接：这很像加拉帕戈斯群岛的进化陷阱——一个封闭生态里的物种因为缺乏外部选择压力，会朝极端方向进化（估值从 500 万一路飙到 6000 万而产品还没上线），最终一个外部冲击就能引发局部灭绝，而大陆生态毫发无损。

二、"前置部署工程师"是 AGI 神话的证伪证据

这是整场对话里最锋利的一刀，也几乎无人注意到：如果 AI 真的接近 AGI，Anthropic 就不需要雇 6000 个"前置部署工程师"去帮企业实施 AI。 Cuban 用一个逻辑短路揭穿了整个叙事——一个能"取代 50% 白领"的技术，怎么会需要人类工程师才能被部署到白领的工作场景里？

更让人意外的是：这个悖论和 Palantir 早年靠"前置工程师"建立护城河的商业模式是一模一样的。Alex Karp 的焦虑不是"AI 会取代我们"，而是"这些 AI 公司在盗用我们十年前发明的商业形态"。也就是说，前沿 AI 公司正在悄悄地从软件公司退化成咨询公司——一个远古的、劳动密集型的行业形态。这是"技术进步"外壳下的商业模式退化。

跨领域连接：这就像 19 世纪的电力革命。电力本身很快就发明了，但让每家工厂真正用上电、并重构生产线布局，花了整整 40 年。真正的瓶颈从来不是模型智力，是社会技术界面。

三、大模型将成为算法喂养时代的解毒剂——这是本次对话最反常识的政治预言

Cuban 顺口说出了一个几乎没人在讨论的悖论：社交媒体的货币是"参与度"，大模型的货币是"正确性"。这两种货币系统正在同一个用户的注意力里打架。

反直觉的是：大多数人担心 AI 会加剧信息茧房，但 Cuban 说的正好相反——LLM 有一个结构性动机去"求真"，因为一旦用户发现它撒谎，产品就死了。而算法则相反：用户越愤怒、越沉迷，产品越活。所以我们可能正在见证一场媒介之间的自我制衡：一个媒介让你困在信息里，另一个媒介让你从中逃出来。

这里有一个更深的系统性洞见：AI 的商业模式恰好和民主健康所需要的"共同事实基础"是对齐的。这在人类媒介史上极其罕见。报纸靠订阅（真相有点重要），广播靠广告（娱乐重要），社交媒体靠参与（愤怒最重要），而 LLM 是第一个把"给出准确回答"作为核心商业指标的媒介。这不是道德胜利，是商业结构意外与公民健康对齐——这种对齐是稀缺资源。

跨领域连接：这类似 20 世纪初 AT&T 的"通用服务"承诺——一个垄断者为了保住信任而不得不承担公共品性质的责任。垄断者的商业模式如果依赖"被信任"，就会自愿地行使"准公共品"的克制。LLM 可能正走上这条路。

一个额外的、埋在对话里的信号

Cuban 反复告诉他的 portfolio 公司"go public, motherfuckers"——这不是投资建议，是他在为即将到来的 AI 并购潮做准备。逻辑链是这样的：AI 会让"小而快"的公司具备颠覆性能力 → 大公司必须收购它们才能自保 → 而收购需要股票作为货币，因为现金太贵 → 所以现在不上市的公司，等于放弃了参与未来重组的资格。IPO 从"退出机制"变成了"进入机制"——这是一个悄悄发生的、几乎无人察觉的资本市场语法翻转。

📝 转录文本

关于 AI 泡沫：这次和互联网泡沫不同

Jason： You and I live through a couple of bubbles. We've seen this movie before. Um, and this wave seems very different than dot waves. So, let's talk about that. Are you concerned about a bubble? We're seeing bubbly-like behavior. People —

Mark Cuban： It's not, it's not the traditional dot bubble, right? Because back then there was companies going public getting crazy valuations and people are buying them and the stock would go up, you know, 50% 100% with companies that had no revenue, no traffic, no nothing. And you'd go get a cab back then and people would be talking about them. Yeah. And you don't, you don't see that at all today.

So, it's not a bubble that's going to impact most people in the room, right? Or most people um across the US, but it could just destroy a lot of VCs and a lot of funds and a lot of PE, right? Because they're going all in.

I'm going all in.

[广告：AppLovin — App 111 started with an $8 domain and no VC funding and became one of the largest ad platforms in the world. Now that same engine powers AppLovin ads for e-commerce. Your ads run inside mobile games reaching over a billion people with full screen distraction-free attention. The platform finds buyers and optimizes for profit. You set the target, it does the rest. One cookware brand went from 4 million to $16 million, turned profitable, and is on pace for 80 million this year. Visit applovin.com/allin to launch your first campaign today.]

基金经理的"跑赢邻居"困境

Jason： It used to be that product managers for brokerages had to outperform the numbers, right, for, you know, the S&P or whatever. But now you got to outperform to keep the money coming in. You got to outperform the the fund next door. And they're all in Anthropic and getting their outcomes and SpaceX and celebrating, but if shit hits the fan —

Yeah. It really is like I've only done venture for just over 10 years and it is wild to watch so many people who deployed at the wrong time just out of business. They just invested at the peak and entry price matters. And you and I have been in a bunch of deals together and we used to get to investing companies at 5 million, 10 million as angel investors, and then all of a sudden the request was 40, 50, 60 and the product's not launched and you're like, how does this work?

Mark Cuban： Yeah. And you know, and what's happening now is the market leaders, Google, etc., Meta, they're borrowing hundreds of million, billions of dollars.

Jason： Yeah, that's interesting.

私募信贷、数据中心与"完美定价"的危险

Mark Cuban： And there's already a private credit problem right now, right? So you just layer on private credit like um Al Capital getting all the refunds and then you know, you have these huge companies that have cash flow, but there's, you know, they're they're spending all their cash flow on capex and then they're borrowing on top of that. Bonds, right? That's planning for perfection.

Jason： And that's going to be hard. And we're building these data centers and you know, if there's a price performance curve on AI that minimizes the power requirements, there's going to be a lot of data centers that are going to be turned into pickleball courts.

Mark Cuban： Interesting. Because they just can't get the power turned on.

No, just the power — just everybody thinks that okay, there's going to be so much more utilization and there will be, right? It'll just scale like everybody expects, but there's going to be breakthroughs — technological breakthroughs as well. Just like we saw fiber back in the day, it was all about putting in fiber. Then it went from 1 GB fiber to 10 to 100 gigabyte and then there wasn't a fiber problem anymore. There wasn't a bandwidth problem anymore.

Jason： Quite the opposite. We had dark fiber that people used. Yeah. Pennies on the dollar.

Mark Cuban： And just sitting there, right? And how is it not going to be the case that we don't get the same price performance improvements on the AI side and on the data center side?

Jason： Yeah. And then with all the hate going against the data centers, I think that could be, you know, protecting them. And I mean, spending — committing tens of billions of dollars for 10, 20 years going out.

Yeah. I mean, nobody can predict that. Well, I mean, you used the word "pricing to perfection," I think, earlier in the conversation, and that's really what's happening. I mean, you have to have a thesis that we're going to put a hundred, you know, OpenAI, uh, hundred billion dollars to work and that's going to come back not just in revenue, but it has to come back in earnings —

Mark Cuban： Margin dollars.

Jason： Yeah. It has to be profitable.

Mark Cuban： Yes. And you just don't know, you know.

IPO 作为"并购货币"：Cuban 的逆向建议

And I'll tell you the other in terms — the corollary for bubbles, right? In this particular bubble, because it's so driven by private capital, there should be a lot more companies going public, not at the SpaceX level, for not OpenAI, not Anthropic, but the hundred million dollar IPO.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Because if AI does what AI knows — we all know what it'll do in terms of disruption.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Then you want to have some sort of currency that allows you to buy all those companies like you guys were talking about buying different legal companies. Yeah, right. Well, if you don't have that currency — the stock is currency — you're going to have to go out and raise money to do it. And if anything happens, and that money is not cheap, that money is really, really expensive.

Versus if you go out and you do a hundred million dollar IPO or a $50 million IPO and you can do a secondary or whatever as many times as you need if you're performing. But the minute that company that you're competing with that is a legacy business can't keep up, or there's some other company that has domain expertise that you need or data that you need like you guys were talking about — you want to be in a position to buy them.

Jason： Yes, that is forward thinking. Yeah. No one is forward thinking like that at all. And I'm like trying to tell my portfolio companies, go public, motherfuckers. Go public. They just don't think that's the right thing to do.

反垄断、并购与 Lina Khan 的余波

Well, and M&A is, you know — not to make this political, but you know, when you're an entrepreneur, you have to play the game on the field. M&A was off the table. Lina Khan had a certain perspective, which was we have to be like precogs in Minority Report. We have to predict who's going to be a monopoly in the future and stop them. Now, it's like, okay, consideration with to compete with China and everything else that's going on globally.

Mark Cuban： Four years of no acquisitions. I mean the people I talked to who are in corporate development were like, we've been told to stand down.

Because it's not where we are, but even so, right, it's — you know, with mergers and acquisitions at that scale, fine, but if AI does what we know it will do, the companies that are disruptive don't have to be enormous companies.

Jason： No.

Mark Cuban： So you don't want to have to always raise cash to go out there.

Jason： Insight — like the —

Mark Cuban： Right. Because like back in the day, back with Broadcast.com, we bought like five companies just for stock.

Jason： And then there was always a bigger fish to come along —

Mark Cuban： Which we were happy to, you know. Because yeah —

"领口对冲"策略：如果你是 AI 公司员工

Jason： Yeah. The famous collar. Uh, if you were a Claude employee, if you're at Anthropic, you're at OpenAI, is it time to start thinking about the collar, thinking about pairing the position?

Mark Cuban： You know, I collared my stock because — how rich do I need to be?

Jason： Right.

Mark Cuban： You know, and if you're able to change your life. If I work for any SpaceX, any of them, whatever, I'd be like, you know, somebody put together a collar for me. Yeah. You know, because I just need to be protected — save part for my upside, but just cover my downside.

Jason： Yeah. Um, when you did it, you had to like actually orchestrate that, right? It didn't exist as a product.

Mark Cuban： No, it didn't. So, I had to actually create an index of internet stocks. I had Goldman Sachs create an index of internet stocks that I thought sucked. And then I shorted that index — because we had to follow all the laws and I just had to have that short in place until I was able to do a real collar in the market on my Yahoo stock. Yeah.

And I lost tens of millions of dollars on that short. Yeah.

Jason： But I made up for it. Yeah. I mean, the one of the great trades of all time.

AI 的落地现实：远比想象的难

Uh, what do you think of this wave of capabilities in AI? I'm sure you play with it because you've always been a tinkerer. So maybe what are you tinkering with and what do you think um is going to be the most world changing, you know, in the next 5, 10 years for entrepreneurs, for society? Where do you look at it and go, hey, let's work backwards? Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Okay, so AI is a lot harder to implement than anybody expected.

Jason： For sure.

Mark Cuban： Right? You know, you can do an agent pretty straightforward, right? You can prompt away — you know, we cheated on tests, we, you know, cheated at work, you know, did projects, improved productivity 100x, right? All easy peasy. And we just assumed, you know, at the enterprise it'd be just as easy.

It's hard.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： And it's terrifying. And not terrifying for employees because Dario and everybody saying 50% of white collar people are going to lose their jobs — here we are two years later, they said within two years, and employment's still growing, people are hiring, we need more AI-literate people, right?

So CEOs have no clue what is going on. None whatsoever. And that's not going to change, right? And so, you know, trying to explain to them that you need to add a harness to, you know, all the — there's no chance, right?

"前置部署工程师"作为 AGI 的证伪

And if you think about it from an AI perspective — if AI, we're talking about, you know, AGI and we're talking about taking over the world. If you need to have forward deployed engineers, that tells you all you need to know about AI. Because by definition you should just be able to ask AI to do what I need you to do and ask and tell me how to implement it, right?

Yet here you have Microsoft hiring 6,000 people, right? You have Anthropic, OpenAI, all saying they're going to deploy to these companies — which tells you AI is hard. And then you have um Alex Karp from Palantir freaking out saying, how can you give your alpha to these companies, when in my opinion he's just saying they're doing what we're doing, right? We're all about forward deploy engineers at Palantir and they're doing the same thing.

Jason： It it — again, it's a great insight. Because you can — if you're using it as a search engine or you're making your little agent to, you know, go do your cron job, okay, fair enough. It's going to work. You need to be — have a little bit of systems thinking. But when you start putting into the enterprise and, you know, this is mission — let's talk about the little bit of systems thinking, because that's the second part.

为什么 AI 现在还替代不了普通白领

And I'm not trying to shit on AI. I love it. I invest in it. It's going to be amazing. It's the most impactful technology that we've ever seen and may ever see. Right?

Mark Cuban： But try going into Claude or or ChatGPT and saying, okay, I just want you to do this search for Jason Calacanis investments and I want you to do report and I want you to email it to me every week.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： It can't do it.

Jason： Can't do it.

Mark Cuban： And then it says, "Okay, would you like for me to do an agent that gives you alerts?" and then you say, "Sure." And then you have to know how to program because it either gives you a JSON file or it gives you, you know, code and it comes out slop.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： And then you have to correct, right? You have to reiterate. You know, if you want to say, you know, hey, let me load a picture in and you tell me if I'm hot or not, or is this girl hot, what should I say to her? You know, or business versions. Great. Yeah.

Right. But AI is not going to, you know, take away 50% of the jobs. There's just so much it can't do that regular people need it to do.

创业者的黄金时代：Lovable 案例

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Right. Now, does that create opportunity? Enormous opportunity. Yes. Right. So, like I'm an investor in Lovable, and we were here this morning talking, and Anton was saying that people are using Lovable to create 770,000 applications a week.

A week.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Right. And that only 30% of their business is in the US and only 20% is engineers. And what's happening is entrepreneurs — to answer your question about where it's going, right — if you're an entrepreneur, like he was saying before, there's no better time to be an entrepreneur.

He nailed it, right? Because that's where AI is the most impactful. No matter where you are in the world — they have big utilization in Brazil, India, right? If you're in France, US, wherever, you know.

Like I gave it a prompt — I said, because I wanted to see what it would take. I was like, "Okay, we're going to create an imaginary company that has a button that can record 24-hour video." And then I wanted to be able to send it to me every 24 hours, and I want you to create a patent and a business plan and tell me what licensing or whatever I need. 12 minutes.

Jason： Yeah. Wow. Like — no, we've started tons of businesses.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. Six months to get a prototype, 12 months to launch it.

Jason： Yes.

Mark Cuban： And then I ask for a bill of materials and the source companies — like the basic stuff, the blocking and tackling stuff that every company has to do when you're setting it up and early in its life cycle. Done.

Jason： Done.

Mark Cuban： And even if it's wrong, so what? Because the one thing you and I both know, and everybody in the room should know — every single motherfucking business plan ever written in the history of business plans is wrong.

Jason： Of course. Yeah. It's a thought exercise that at least gives you some sort of direction you're going.

Mark Cuban： Guideline and a template for you, right? But it's wrong.

So what if the AI is wrong? If the model was wrong, you know — because you're going to learn and you're going to iterate, but it's still — again, all this talk about taking white collar jobs is ridiculous when it can't even do the basic.

AI 还不会自我学习错误

Jason： Right. And you have to have, like you said, a programming mindset in order to be able to iterate and figure it out. And like even you would think if AI is as advanced as we want to believe AI is right now, all the errors that we get when we ask it to do a project — it would learn from all those errors. And even have, "Hey, I'm Claude. I see you have a problem with this, right? And it failed on these three attempts. Let me just tell you what 97.6% of the other people who ran into this did." Right? And then it would fix it for you, would tell you.

Mark Cuban： It doesn't even do that. It just says failed, right? Or it says whatever.

You know, again, AI is amazing. And particularly for programmers, it's game changing. And when you have a narrow data set like code or legal, tax, it's magic. And it's just math, right? Basic data, right? But when normal people anywhere in the world want to use it for normal stuff — great. You start a business, great. But if you want to start getting advanced, it's like figuring out PowerPoint used to be, or Excel, right? You always have to have your Excel expert, you know, or your PowerPoint in your company.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Or whoever that you knew, right? Or there would be companies built — I even invested in a company like SlideShare that all they did was have PowerPoint templates that you could download and redo. AI is not even that advanced for, you know, once you get to the second level.

AI 咨询：巨大的创业机会

And so that creates so much opportunity for anybody to walk into a small, medium size, even large business and say, "Hey, I understand AI. I have a basic computer background or better. And so all these fails that you're running into across your company, big or small, I can help you fix them." Because what AI can do once those issues are fixed, is phenomenal.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： But even better, if you're thinking as an entrepreneur to start a business, those things will eventually break. Agents get bored, right? And they drift. Because as the underlying large language model starts to change, the way it was originally programmed doesn't match what the large language model turned into, right? You see what I'm saying? And so — you even wrote something about this, right? — where it's taking more people to manage all this stuff.

[广告：Northwest Registered Agent — Starting a business usually means juggling a bunch of different services just to look legit. Not anymore. With Northwest Registered Agent, you get a complete business identity all in one place. A business address, domain, website, phone number, and more with privacy protection built in from day one. Don't spend hundreds or thousands on services you can get from Northwest for free. Visit northwestregisteredagent.com/allinfree and start using free resources to build your business today. I'm going all in.]

AI 优先员工 vs 非 AI 员工：巨大差距

Jason： I'm trying to get the whole firm to be AI first. And even with young people, it's like — one group embraces it and, you know, they solve, let's call it five or six really pressing issues. The other group is not embracing it. And the difference between those AI first employees and the non-AI first employees — the gap is like, whoa. It's almost like when we got into the industry and somebody knew how to use the office suite in a PC and then somebody else was like on legal pads. It's like that much of a difference. It's so —

Mark Cuban： People who are AI literate.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： When did they run into a ceiling?

Jason： Well, you know, what I'm seeing is a lot of tool hopping — which is a good question because they started with OpenClaw. They started building it. It got brittle.

Mark Cuban： Wait — OpenClaw. Who's OpenClaw?

Jason： Anybody using Hermes agent? Okay, there you go. Who's on Claude Co-work? And then, oh, whoa. And Perplexity Computer, anyone? Okay, interesting. Those are the four that people have been bouncing around on.

And what I decided to do is I was just like, "Hey, token max it. You can each spend a couple thousand dollars a month. I don't care about that. I just care about the gains." And they all wound up going from OpenClaw — all the agents started breaking. It's hallucinating. It's like too frustrating. They started using Claude Co-work. Okay, great. Um, but kind of limited. And then they started using Lovable and they built intranets for the venture business that no venture business would spend a half million dollars and 12 months building an intranet. Like you'd just be like, I'm going to put it in Notion, Google Sheets, whatever.

And then they just started building more and more software. I have two or three people who are building software that I would say five years ago we would have spent maybe two or three million dollars a year building this with an outsource company. You just would not have done it — which means we wouldn't have done it. Right. You would just bought the SaaS application that did it. And then we would have tried to like shoehorn it into a SaaS app and customize it. It wouldn't have worked. We would have given up.

Mark Cuban： But how are you going to manage it now once they get there?

世界模型 vs LLM：AI 的未来在视频

Jason： Well, you know, again, to your company — Lovable, which, man, congrats on that investment because that company was going to go out of business four times. And I told Anton, every time they say you're going out of business, you add 100 million in revenue. 600 million in revenue. And four times, 200 million, 300 million. Oh, yeah. It's going to be absorbed into the frontier model.

Mark Cuban： The rest of the world, right? I mean, I've got Lovable, I've got Synthesia.io — that was the first investor in — 10 years ago, maybe, that is just killing it, right? I've got AMI, which is Yakun's world model. Yeah, we haven't even talked about world models versus LLM and Transformers, right?

Because everything we do is built on text and pictures.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Nothing's going to be text and pictures in 10 years, right? And but what's going to drive it? Like people say, well, AI is so smart, it's going to change everything. I'm like, "Okay, if you tell AI — if you show AI a video of a two-year-old on a high chair with a sippy cup, the two-year-old knows if you push the sippy cup over the edge, mom's going to come running and the kid's going to start laughing." Yeah. AI's got no clue what's going to happen. Yeah. None. Right?

Would you — if you're at a corner blindfolded and you have to cross the street, would you rather have a video? Would you rather have your phone with AI, or would you rather have a CNI dog?

Jason： Yeah, dog.

Mark Cuban： I'm taking the dog every time.

Jason： Of course. Yeah, it's not ready.

Mark Cuban： Right? Yeah, it's not ready. So, just think about how far we have to go. Yeah.

Jason： You know, and — huge opportunity though. I mean, the world models are crazy because you have videos available on YouTube and just — we have the world there. So, it's not enough.

Mark Cuban： It's not enough. They have to wear gloves and — you're — now they've got people in Manila doing recipes.

So I invested in the company matter.com and they're launching satellites just to take — and they do spectrography where they take, use the satellites to take videos of everything underneath them and use a spectrum. I don't even understand all — in order to be able to convert it into a world model that will provide algorithms that other world models can use.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Right? There's just no way that the future of AI doesn't include video.

Jason： Well, I mean, it's a huge part of it. And as the inference and the buildout of these data centers — as we wonder what's going to happen, people wanting to use more tokens and then these world building and video, man, that takes a magnitude more tokens.

Mark Cuban： Video — if I'm going to be wrong on the data centers, it's going to be because of video. Yeah, right. And world models and robotics, which is all related.

Jason： Yeah. Yeah. I mean, and robotics, you know, it's related but we still got a long way to go.

健康、Open Evidence 与自我导向医疗

Yeah. Uh, health, you look great, by the way. I don't know what's going on. You got the red — what do you got? Red tie. What's on the menu here? What's off the menu?

Mark Cuban： Follow me on Instagram. M Cuban. I just I'm eating better. Not —

Jason： I mean, you look better now than 20 years ago.

Mark Cuban： Oh, I appreciate that. Thanks. Tan helps too, right?

Jason： The tan. But I mean, you look thin and fit. And listen, we're getting up there now. We're not kids anymore. This is definitely going to help — AI, all the work being done.

Mark Cuban： I mean, and I use it too. Like I invested in a company called Open Evidence.

Jason： Yes.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. Which is for medical, right? And I used it — like I have one supplement I have to take because my iron levels are low and I have to take this one medication. And I was taking them at the same time and couldn't understand why I was having problems. I run it through Open Evidence and I said, "Here's everything I'm taking. Here's everything I'm eating." And they're like, "No, you got to do this. You know, when you get up to pee at 3 in the morning, take this medication."

Jason： Getting old sucks.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. Right. [laughter]

Jason： I was right there with you last night. What's going on?

Mark Cuban： What's going on? Right. And so, you know, but it's for health. It's going to make a huge difference, but it's not going to replace doctors.

Jason： No. But I mean, it is pretty amazing. Are you on Whoop or your Apple Watch?

Mark Cuban： I watch. Yeah.

Jason： Yeah. I mean, the Apple Watch, the Whoop, plus getting your blood test — just self-directed health care with AI, man, you're going to get some early wins. And then when you do go talk to your doctor —

Mark Cuban： No, you have it right there, right? My blood tested, you know, now is three to six months.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Right. And I've been doing it for 10 years, so I know all my trends.

Jason： Wow. So, you were doing it before Function and Superpower and all that.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. Like, you could go look up Mark Cuban blood test on Twitter, right? X. And I would get into it with doctors like 8, 10 years ago and saying you're going to need this for your benchmarks. But it's only going to get better.

Jason： Yeah. And it's going to be really interesting. We had this big data moment and it was like, okay yeah, we're storing all this data but we didn't have any intelligence against it. Now, that's incredible when Apple is now putting that data — they're doing studies. I don't know if you've been invited to studies on your Apple Watch. I'm like, well, this is going to get very interesting.

Whoop just added blood panels. So now your sleep data, your steps, your heart rate, your respiratory, EKG, plus your blood work. Then if you can get your diet into it as well.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. That's what I — Watch has an eye health, right?

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： So yeah, you're just going to get smarter. Yeah. You know, and that'll make your doctors able to be smarter because 95% of medicine is guessing. You know, because there's no way a doctor can memorize all the new that happens every single day. You just can't. But a doctor using these tools, having, you know, the empathy and the ability to communicate and the ability to see —

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Right. Because again, there's — you can't see.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： You know, you could have doctor — you know, why am I bleeding? "You got a — blood, looks like you got a gunshot wound," right? AI is not going to tell you that, Dwayne.

关于德州、社会主义、算法与政治

Jason： Um, when we look at the opportunity here, it's going to be a lot of change. A lot of talk about wealth disparity in the US. Uh, Invest America, our friend Brad Gerstner, and our friend Michael Dell, another great Texan. Uh, thanks for welcoming me. I'm enjoying my time in Texas.

Mark Cuban： I mean, it's great. I love Texas, man.

Jason： Texas is fun. It's kind of like — the heat's kind of like this, though.

Mark Cuban： Yeah, the heat.

Jason： We have air conditioning.

Mark Cuban： We do have air conditioning.

Jason： There's a record number of air conditioners coming from China to Europe this summer.

Mark Cuban： You know, I looked at what companies installed them and everything.

Jason： Oh, you always got to trade. You always got to trade.

Mark Cuban： I'm curious, but there's no real —

Jason： Sold out. They're all being shipped here from China at the moment.

But really interestingly, you know, feels like entrepreneurship, which has been our shared passion for many decades — investing, another shared one — giving people access to public markets, Invest America. We have this dueling narrative, socialism in America, capitalism, and it feels like this is — in our lifetimes — you and I are close in age. This is the most fevered we've ever seen it. I mean, we have states — maybe not since the 60s or 70s. I missed that. I was born 1970. You were born, I guess, 1943.

Mark Cuban： '23.

Jason： '23. I always joke he's my brother. You look good for 1923, but you know, it's — this is pretty polarized. And what's your take on the socialist —

Mark Cuban： There's two things here. One, all the DSA stuff is local, right? It's really easy to be Mamdani in New York when you have a budget, you get to control it, and it's local, right? You got elected, you just do, and you have to balance the budget. And you'll see it in Michigan and wherever else. I don't think it's going to be game-changing.

算法、社会媒体与年轻政客

But the more important thing is the people who are doing this are the people who are best at social media, right? You know, because the algorithms drive how people vote in the United States. I can't speak for the rest of the world more than anything else. Whoever controls the algorithm controls, you know, the election in many cases.

Trump knows how to use it. And what — what do Mamdani, you know — what is unique about Mamdani relative to Trump? He's what, 31, 32 years old?

Jason： Yep.

Mark Cuban： His whole adult life has been Trump. And social media.

Jason： He studied it.

Mark Cuban： And the two together.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： And the kid, the guy in Michigan, same thing. So if you're good at algorithm — you know, driving algorithms, whether it's flooding the zone or, you know, like Trump saying, "They're eating cats and dogs" —

Jason： Yeah. It's just attention.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. And it's just, you know, defining the algorithms — because whatever you search for, you get more of. And if they can get you to search for what they're saying and get show your interest, then you're going to just get Mamdani 24/7.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： And you'll get the positive sides of Mamdani. Or if you happen to be on the other side of the aisle and you are, you know, looking at stuff that's counter to that, you'll get more of that.

大模型作为算法喂养时代的解毒剂

We don't like — but one other piece I think is starting to change that, and that's the large language models.

The thing that I think will save us as a world more than anything else in terms of information availability and reducing the information asymmetry as it applies to politics are large language models — because large language models have to be as literal and honest as they possibly can.

Jason： Truth seeking.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. Truth is a better way to put it. Yeah. So they have to seek truth, otherwise you'll lose trust in them, and the last thing you know Claude, OpenAI, needs is, "Well, you know, they're lying their ass off about this."

Jason： Yeah. Their currency is getting you the correct answer and the correct knowledge. Social media's currency is keeping you engaged, right? It's two different missions.

Mark Cuban： Totally different missions, right? And so you're not going to get rid of social media, but I think people as they become more uncertain with their politics are going to go more and more and more to large language models and say, "Who should I vote for?"

And the large language model is going to come back and say, "Well, what do you think about this? What are your interests in this?" and da da da. And they'll give you honest answers.

Jason： Yeah. And if you ask it, "Hey, what's a reasonable immigration policy?" it'll give you a reasonable immigration policy, which is like —

Mark Cuban： Yeah, maybe shut the border and do a point-based system like Canada, New Zealand, whatever it may be, right?

Jason： I've done the same thing and it's impressive.

Mark Cuban： Very impressive.

Jason： You know, my favorite troll now is like JD or, you know, AOC will, you know, they'll come out with whatever their craziness is on either side of the aisle. And then I'm like, "@Grok, please vet these claims." [laughter] Be as objective and factual as possible. Check your work. Show your sources.

Mark Cuban： And then it just replies to them.

Jason： So, I've been just totally trolling Steven Miller.

Mark Cuban： Oh my god.

Jason： Who, you know, he's like anti-immigration. I'm very passionate about like legal immigration and recruiting great people because —

Mark Cuban： You need them. We need them.

Jason： We need them. Because you're running a basketball team. Every time you get Dirk Nowitzki, somebody else doesn't have him on the team. You get Jalen Brunson, he's not on the Mavericks anymore. Now he's on the Knicks. You know, like —

Mark Cuban： Well, that's why we come to these things in Europe, right?

Jason： Yes.

Mark Cuban： Because great talent, right? Smart people. Obviously, you guys are geniuses for being here, you know. But at the same time, there's a different vibe in every European country and India and the whole rest of the world, right? And I think you're starting to see some entrepreneurs leave us —

Jason： Yep.

Mark Cuban： To come here. And that's scary. And hopefully, you know, large language models seeking the truth will help educate people away from what the algorithms tell them.

对美国、乐观主义与领导力

Jason： Yeah, I think it's important. You optimistic now? It's been pretty chaotic, but we're seeing such great progress. You optimistic?

Mark Cuban： About — let's start with America and then we'll go to humanity.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. No, I mean, you know, like every country, we have our issues. Ours just happen to be kind of insane.

Jason： It is pretty wild.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. [laughter] But the good news is, you know, we have term limits for our president.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： And —

Jason： Yeah. And it seems like 12 — three terms is going to be the one for president. I mean, you're going to run. I mean, I hope you do, man. I would love to be your press secretary. That would be a — we'd have so much fun.

Mark Cuban： We'd have fun.

Jason： We would have fun.

Mark Cuban： Well, if he decides to run for a third term, then I'll run. But he's —

Jason： Yeah. Okay, that's good. That's breaking news. I mean, I, uh, honestly, if you think about what we need, I think, in leadership — executives who can, I think, you know, who are post-money, post needing to make a living. Um, that no — I'm not going to even go there. [laughter]

Well, and then maybe who are doing it for love of country and maybe some amount of civic pride, or —

Mark Cuban： No, I get what you're saying, right? And it's just like — you know, in the US, what — the Democrats don't know how to actually do anything, right? You know, they know how to take whatever happens and extrapolate that to the end of the world and the end of democracy, right?

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： But and the Republicans on the other hand, you know, do crazy shit, right? Like they don't care about the people in the country. There's no connection to people. There's no empathy. And so, you know, we're — in the short term. But I think as we get past this next — the midterms and the next presidential election — I think we'll get back to normalcy. I think we'll get — I think people wanted —

州治理对比：德州 vs 纽约

And you know, the thing that has been really eye-opening for me having lived in New York, California, now Texas — you know, in Texas, we spend half as much money per — sometimes less. I think it's two and — 2.2 times as much is spent on each citizen in New York. And we spend half as much, right? $67,000 per person. They spend like 14 or something, 12 to 14. And quality of life's better.

But on the flip side, New York contributes more to the federal treasury, right, than Texas does. And you would think it should be the opposite, right? Just because of the business and how things are run. So there are trade-offs.

Jason： Yeah. But it's changing. Like what do you think about all of us moving to Austin and like Texas? You got —

Mark Cuban： Smart, obviously, for obvious reason — particularly, you know, like the wealth tax shit is just dumb-dumb crazy.

Jason： They tried it here. Everybody works, and they did it for about two years in France.

Mark Cuban： Did they? Yeah. I don't know. But you know, when Elizabeth Warren first proposed the wealth tax and she said, "Well, we have these models and everything," so I found the economist at UC Berkeley and I said, "Would you share your model with me or at least tell me about it?" I'm like, "Was it more than one year?" And he goes, "No, it was just one year." I'm like, "Did you do any behavioral analysis to see how people would respond to the changes?"

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： No. And so, you know, whether it's Mamdani, Elizabeth Warren, whether it's California, it's showmanship more than reality.

Jason： Yeah. And I think people are forgetting that people are mobile, you know.

Mark Cuban： US.

Jason： Yeah, in the US it's pretty extraordinary. You got Travis, Sachs, myself, Elon — just a bunch of folks have all of a sudden just shown up.

Mark Cuban： I was there first.

Jason： You were. I remember talking to you about it. And the thing that's amazing to me is, you know, in California, they won't let you build anything. And then I come to Texas, I buy a ranch, and I asked the guy, "Hey, can I put a solar farm in here?" He's like, "On your ranch?" I'm like, "Yeah, here." He's like, "Oh, son, it's your ranch. You can do that." It's like, can I build like —

Mark Cuban： Trust me, in the cities themselves — try doing that in the city of Austin.

Jason： I mean, but just outside of it, you build. And housing prices have gone — three years in a row housing prices and rents have gone down. When you build a company in Austin now, and I'm sure it's like that in Dallas and Houston and San Antonio, when I have founders move there, the entire pressure is released. People can own a home. And I was like, wow, is that —

Mark Cuban： Why do people still go to Silicon Valley? That drives me pissed.

Jason： I think if you're a first-time founder, nothing better in terms of soaking in it. But when you're a second-time founder —

Mark Cuban： Soaking in it, but what they don't tell you, the shit is, right?

Jason： Yeah, it's tough. I think second-time founders have now realized: if I build it in Texas or Nevada or Florida, I'm going to — just all the talent's going to come.

Mark Cuban： In Silicon Valley just sets people's heads so wrong. Yeah. Right. So whether it's Travis, Mike — by the way, I've known Michael Dell since we were 22. Yeah. And I used to do business with him way back when. But in Texas, it's just like build your company and go.

Jason： Yes.

Mark Cuban： In the valley it's like, so are you series A? Are you series B? Are you series C?

Jason： A lot of distractions.

篮球时间：纽约尼克斯、独行侠、以及联盟规则

Yeah. 25 years I've been waiting for my Knicks to win a championship. And you played a significant role.

Mark Cuban： No, the Spurs — significant role. [laughter] They should have kicked your ass.

Jason： And why didn't they? Why didn't they?

Mark Cuban： I think it was coaching, lack of experience. Yeah. You know, cuz you're — put aside the 29, right? You're up in the fourth quarter of every game. WBY throws it into the back of some guy, misses two free throws. Dear Fox goes the wrong way.

Jason： Lot of mistakes.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. And you would be talking about firing Mike Brown. [laughter]

Jason： I mean, it was — we were concerned when he got hired and getting rid of Thibs. Oh my god.

Mark Cuban： Thibs. But JB was the real deal, right? Was my Indiana boy OG, right? He, you know, he saved you guys. So —

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Uh, and you had Jalen Brunson.

Jason： Yep. He left and that was kind of destiny. His family wanted to come.

Mark Cuban： That's what he wanted. I mean, I could show you a text where his agent, you know, texted me. He says he wants his own team.

Jason： He wants his own team, right? Yeah. He was ready to go before.

Mark Cuban： And I texted or emailed you and I said, "What's the story? Is this what we're going to base the franchise on?" You said he's really good. He's the real deal.

Jason： You know what I love? He's a great guy. That's the best part about JB. He's just really a good human. I was there for the fifth game, six rows behind the bench.

Mark Cuban： That's cool.

Jason： And, man, I just cried. I just cried. It's been so many years. And you got your chip, too, with Dirk.

Mark Cuban： Yep.

Jason： It's so amazing how sports plays such a role in so many people's lives.

Mark Cuban： Yeah. Especially now — look at the World Cup.

Jason： I mean, it's just like sports has just changed everything. And you know, like I've been to four World Cup games in Dallas and I hate soccer, football.

Mark Cuban： Right.

Jason： I hate it. But just the whole scene, the vibe, the energy — you can't beat it. It's like going to a Knicks game, going to a Mavs game. Same thing.

Mark Cuban： Yeah.

NBA 全球化与规则变化

Jason： And the NBA — is it peaked? It sounds like —

Mark Cuban： It's hard to say. I mean, you know, for fans, no. Because it just grows on social media, you know. I went — I was walking in Paris and there was a basketball store, you know, and it had Wembanyama jerseys and Gobert jerseys and everything. And so, I mean, it's just growing globally.

Jason： Yeah. Is Wemby the real deal?

Mark Cuban： Yeah. He got humbled though.

Jason： Yeah.

Mark Cuban： Which is good for him. It's like Dirk in 2006 — got humbled and came back better. He'll come back bigger, stronger, better.

Jason： Yeah. It's amazing how he went from this like incredibly — I don't know, there was a magic about him — to, no, he's a villain, and he turned into a villain.

Mark Cuban： Because it was New York and it was 50-some years, right? And just the circumstances. And you know, San Antonio thought they had it in the bag. Everybody — it's like I went to Indiana University, right? And if anybody here follows college football in the states — like we were the ultimate underdog. We had not won 10 games. We beat more top-10 teams last year than we did in 100 plus years of the program. And so that just changes people's attitude.

Jason： You were very much involved in rule changes. And we're going to get on AI next, but it's just great to talk to you about this since we're both such super fans of the NBA. Seems like the rule changes, the apron and how punitive it is, has created a lot more parity in the league. And nobody knows who's going to win, like —

Mark Cuban： Well, because you have to break up your team, right? Because at some point, OKC will have to break up their team. That's why they collect all their draft assets, because you can't have three max players. No. And if one of them turns out to be hurt or not what you expected, then you're in deep, right?

And so the second apron is just a complete game changer. And you have to be so much better at putting together a team than you were before. And you have to get lucky. Like the Spurs have — went beyond a rookie contract, you know. OKC has got, you know, Hartenstein on a rookie contract. He's not on a rookie contract any longer. So they have to be even more careful. And so it really changes the strategy behind building a roster.

Jason： So we're going to see back-to-backs anymore?

Mark Cuban： No chance.

Jason： No chance.

Mark Cuban： No. I — maybe back-to-backs, but not a — what do they call when you have three — the three-peat.

Jason： Three-peat. Yeah, no more three-peats.

Mark Cuban： Can be back-to-backs. Like, you know, if Jalen Williams — JDub — hadn't gotten hurt for OKC, I think they would have beat San Antonio and I think they would have beat the Nets —

Jason： I was worried about them versus the Knicks. I knew we could beat the Spurs but I was concerned about —

Mark Cuban： I did not know you could beat — I knew — we beat them two out of three of the games of the year. We spanked them. I knew it would be like a — I said Knicks in six. It was Knicks in five.

Jason： You guys barely beat the Hawks and then —

Mark Cuban： Oh, come on now. We were tweaking the offense and we just had to get Cat to buy in. Us — points.

Jason： Let's talk AI.

Mark Cuban： Let's talk AI.

尾声：冠军与球队估值的新逻辑

Jason： But it is so great to be a champion. God, it was — where's your ring? They didn't give you —

Mark Cuban： They didn't yet. No, I'm going to get it at the ring ceremony. [laughter]

Jason： I, you know, I always was like, man, I got to follow Cubes and get, you know, a piece of —

Mark Cuban： You always told me you wanted to buy a team.

Jason： I always wanted to get like a piece of the Knicks. And now it's like my net worth doing great, the value of the team disconnected from reality. I mean, my only chance is to call like Elon and be like —

Mark Cuban： Part about the valuations is it's not driven by attendance. It's not driven by wins or losses. It's driven by subscriptions to streaming services. And it's going to be interesting to see. Because it used to be we looked at ratings — TV ratings — and people said the NBA was underperforming versus everybody else. Now, the Knicks play, obviously there's some pent-up demand there, but what really matters is the number of subscribers Peacock gets, ESPN gets. It's the ratings sales, but it's more subscribers.

Jason： I mean, I literally had to subscribe to like, I think, one or two more services.

Mark Cuban： Well, that's the whole point, right? And did you keep them is the question.

Jason： Yeah, I'll keep them. Sure. I mean —

Mark Cuban： But there will be churn. If there's churn, who knows what happens with valuations. If there's not, valuations keep on going.

Jason： Wow. So crazy. All right, listen. There's your 45 minutes with Mark Cuban, the one and only.

[music]

Mark Cuban： I'm going all in.

[music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、这不是互联网泡沫，这是**风投行业的自我引爆**
Cuban 抛出了一个反直觉的诊断：这场 AI 泡沫破裂时，出租车司机不会谈论它，普通美国人也不会受伤——**因为泡沫的能量被封闭在一个专业阶层内部**。这与 2000 年那种"人民的泡沫"截然相反。
有意思的是这里有个**隐藏的对称美感**：VC/PE 曾用"风险民主化"的叙事把散户从公开市场吸走（因为好公司都留在私募里），结果他们成功地把风险也留给了自己。这是一个自我实现的因果闭环——**他们把公众隔离在外，最终也把损失隔离在了自己手里**。这或许是资本主义历史上第一次，"泡沫破裂"变成一场行业内部的清算，而不是社会性的伤口。
跨领域的连接：这很像**加拉帕戈斯群岛的进化陷阱**——一个封闭生态里的物种因为缺乏外部选择压力，会朝极端方向进化（估值从 500 万一路飙到 6000 万而产品还没上线），最终一个外部冲击就能引发局部灭绝，而大陆生态毫发无损。
## 二、"前置部署工程师"是 AGI 神话的**证伪证据**
这是整场对话里最锋利的一刀，也几乎无人注意到：**如果 AI 真的接近 AGI，Anthropic

item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Grant Williams: Gold Is Warning Us About What Comes Next
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：金本位不是"选择"出来的，而是被"逼"出来的

Grant 在这里颠覆了整个货币改革叙事。所有的评论员都在争论"金本位好不好、要不要回去"——这本身就是一个错误问题。真正的机制是反向的：社会动荡 → 政治动荡 → 金融市场动荡 → 巨型去杠杆事件 → 被迫用黄金重新稳定系统。

这里藏着一个更惊人的推论：2020 年那几万亿凭空印出来的美元，可能是"最后一次掷骰子"。因为它成功阻止了那次"巨型去杠杆事件"——也就是说，它推迟了通往金本位的最后一道触发器，但代价是把系统里的通胀压力封存起来，让它现在正在"试图挣脱"。换句话说，我们没有避免那一天，只是让那一天变得更贵。

二、跨领域连接：AI 时代的"懒惰陷阱"，与 Grant 从进攻转向防守，其实是同一件事

Grant 说"人类的所有发明本质上都是让自己更懒的方式"——这句话表面上是在批评有人用 ChatGPT 替代尽调，但把它和他自己的资产配置从 70/30 进攻变成 90/10 防守放在一起看，一个更深的模式浮现了：

过去 40 年，"懒惰"是被奖励的。你可以闭着眼睛买指数、买房、拿国债，甚至倒着摔进任何东西，只要拿住就赚钱。这是一个允许"外包判断"的时代——外包给美联储的看跌期权、外包给全球化、外包给下降的利率。AI 恰好在这个"懒惰红利"耗尽的临界点出现，让人们在最不能偷懒的时刻，获得了史上最强的偷懒工具。

这就是为什么 Grant 60 岁的"防守转向"和年轻人用 AI 找十倍股，看起来无关，其实是同一枚硬币的两面：一个人在识别范式转移，一个群体在用新工具加倍押注旧范式还会持续。

三、被忽略的信号：Bessent 的"语言学"，比 Fort Knox 里有没有金子更重要

大多数人只关心"Fort Knox 到底有没有那 8133 吨黄金"。但 Grant 抓到了一个更微妙的东西：财长 Bessent 措辞上刻意的距离感——"我没去，但我团队的人去了"。作为财长，他本可以说"黄金都在那儿，我亲眼见过"，但他没有。

这是一个语言学层面的信号交易。它和欧盟从"廉价货币的共同决策"（人人都爱）变成"紧缩的共同决策"（土耳其不会投票支持圣诞节）是同一种现象——当一个系统的一致性依赖于正和博弈，任何轻微的语言退让都是结构性裂缝的先兆。

四、China 长游戏的真正逻辑：不是为了锚定人民币，而是为了做"最后站着的人"

主流叙事说"中国囤金是为了给人民币背书"。Grant 的洞见更冷峻：中国根本不需要用黄金背书人民币。他们在囤金，是为了等到美国、欧洲、日本被债务危机逼着重新引入黄金的那一天——那时候，谁的黄金多，谁就是新的金融世界之王。

这个逻辑和围棋的"厚势"概念惊人相似：你不是为了当下的这块棋，你是为了三十步之后的全局。西方的四年选举周期天生无法执行这种策略——民主政治的时间贴现率太高，只有能"不需要每四年被再次选举"的体制，才能玩这种五十年的博弈。

五、真正意想不到的连接：DFW 的鱼缸寓言 → Grant 的日元套息 → 你的思维盲点

Grant 引用 David Foster Wallace 那个著名的"水"的寓言——鱼不知道自己泡在水里。他把它用在日元套息、NATO、联合国、"黄金很无聊"这些"二战后 70 年的默认假设"上。

但这里有一个更深的含义值得停顿：你无法通过思考跳出鱼缸，你只能通过被伤害跳出鱼缸。因为鱼缸的定义就是它是你所有思考的媒介。Grant 说得含蓄，但这是残酷的：那些正在读这个转录的人，大概率已经在鱼缸里想过"要不要跳出鱼缸"这个问题——但仍然没跳出去。真正让人跳出去的，从来不是洞察，而是成本。所以他给自己的解决方案不是"想得更清楚"，而是结构性地降低试错空间（90% 防守）。这才是他真正的答案。

📝 转录文本

开场：金融系统的重置

"This idea that the entire financial system is about ready to reset. Will it be reset or will it naturally reset? I don't know. Nobody chooses to go back to a gold standard. It's the other way round. You have social upheaval, you have political upheaval, you have financial market upheaval. That leads to a giant deleveraging event and the answer is to bring back gold to stabilize the financial system.

And you know, we've had social upheaval, that's clear. We've had political upheaval, that's abundantly clear. We almost had financial market upheaval in 2020, but we conjured another several trillion dollars out of thin air to paper over those cracks. So, we avoided the giant deleveraging event. But in doing so, that was perhaps the last roll of the dice."

主持人开场与足球寒暄

过去 8-9 个月最重要的转折点

Alexi: Apart from that, obviously like we haven't talked to each other I think since eight months. That was last time it was in Switzerland — and as we all know there's never a dull day in the macrosphere. Things happen almost on a weekly basis. It really feels like just confirming what you've been also telling us last time that we're in this very transitionary phase of different systems, not just one system but many in different levels.

So I would like to first maybe begin the conversation with asking you: what do you think were the most important turning points in the last 8 to nine months since we actually spoke last time? What surprised you the most?

Grant: Well, it's a great question. I've tried very hard to find a way to not be surprised by anything. And I think that's been important for me because I think we're at one of those turning points where really big change is happening, which we spoke about last time.

And I think anytime you get into this place where there are big changes happening, you can find pockets — and we had this in the '90s, where markets, because the world was on a trajectory of, you know, post the Berlin wall of more peace and more cohesion — and yes, we had 9/11 and we've had these various little kind of skirmishes, but the world in general was on a trajectory towards more peace and more cooperation and tighter knit and globalization and all these things. So you had that period where being ready for anything wasn't as important. It's always important to be prepared but it wasn't as important.

Now I think trying to find a way to let go of all your preconceptions about how the world works — that's not to say you completely change how you do everything. But you have to be more nimble and you have to realize that the kind of change that's happening is going to lead, by definition, to more unexpected outcomes.

从"可能"到"概率"

So I think that's how I've tried to approach the last couple of years. And so when you get the war with Iran — which obviously, I mean people could say they've seen it coming for many many years as the rhetoric's ratcheted up — but when it actually starts, it still catches you by surprise. And those kind of conflicts, conflicts involving China, trade wars, all these things for me have become probabilities rather than possibilities now. And so that shapes the way I think about them.

系统重置的临界点

And then there's the big one — you know, this idea that the entire financial system is about ready to reset. Will it be reset or will it naturally reset? I don't know. But I feel like that's now the big thing that people need to be conscious of. And again, it's not a definite, but 25 years ago, it was a definite that it was not going to change. The financial system was not going to change. It worked for everybody. Yeah, there were some parts of it that you could see, particularly around the debt, were starting to get a little bit out of hand. But now I think we are in the sweet spot where it needs to change, and anytime something needs to happen, it tends to whether we like it or not.

So that in a nutshell, that's for me the important thing. It's a mindset for us to say: right, we can't assume the world will work tomorrow the way it works today. And what does that mean for how I think about investing? How I think about whether I allocate my capital for growth or I allocate it for maintenance of my purchasing power?

投资组合的重塑：Rick Rule 的自然资源大会

Alexi: So maybe a blunt follow-up question would be then, what would that then be exactly, and how would you paint this, let's say, the portfolio construction that you just mentioned now? What would be, let's say, your first step right now in assessing which asset you would own in this kind of environment, and how would you then also question what you own at which step of the way?

Grant: Yeah, it's funny. I've just been at Rick Rule's conference in Boca Raton, Florida, which is always great. This year was particularly good. And this was a question that I found myself either being asked directly or in discussions about.

It was very interesting because you know the conference — it's a natural resource conference and you've got a lot of great companies there exhibiting and showing gold mines and tin mines and silver mines and uranium and every kind of natural resource on the planet, which I do believe is a space that's going to be in a bull market in the years to come. So you've got all these companies that are looking to raise capital, they're looking to find investors, and you've got an audience that are hungry to make money. So it should be, for both sides, like shooting fish in a barrel.

一位名叫 John 的先生

I sat down with a gentleman — really nice gentleman called John — who saw me and grabbed me and said, "I would really like to talk to you. Can you spare some time?" So I said, "You know, of course, let's sit." So we sat and chatted for a while and he asked me this exact same question. And the answer I gave him was this one:

"Look, I'm pushing 60. I'll be 60 in a few months time. And so my challenge now, having worked for 40 plus years and got myself in a position where I'm okay — you know, I don't live an extravagant life, but I'm okay now. I have the money I need, if I'm careful with it, to be fine now and to leave some money for my kids and do all the things that I set out to do when I started work 40 plus years ago.

"So for me the question is very very simple. How do I maintain that position? How do I make sure that I don't go backwards? How do I make sure that I don't make any stupid mistakes that mean I can't leave my kids some money and I can't live the life I want to live and I can't live in a way that I've worked so very hard for so long to do."

黄金的角色

And because of that, everyone listening to this, I'm sure, and anyone that's seen me talking before will know that I've been accumulating gold for many many years. I've continued to do that. As that mindset has taken hold, the older I've got, the more that mindset has become important to me. My gold positioning has gone higher — partly through more accumulation and partly through the rise in price.

And gold has done for me exactly what I wanted it to do when I first started buying it at $300-and-something dollars. It has protected my purchasing power and it has made sure that I am able, with the money I've accumulated, to buy with it today what I could buy with it when I started accumulating it. And that's great. And it's actually got me ahead of the game.

So I don't think about — oh, now gold's at $5,500 or $4,000 — about selling my gold. It's never been about that for me. And any interview you listen to for me over the last 20 years, you'll find a very consistent throughline with that.

My thought with gold has always been: there may be a point in time where I want to exchange it for something I want to own more. Right now, the gold is protecting my purchasing power. If a given amount of gold in 3 years time, in the depths of a housing crisis for example, can buy me the house of my dreams, I'll exchange the gold for the house. It doesn't matter what the price is of the gold. If the amount of gold I've got there I can exchange for something that for the next 20 years I want to own more, I'll do that without even worrying about the price.

5% 无风险利率作为锚点

And away from that, I want to earn — if I can earn 5% essentially risk-free, or as close to risk-free as I can, for me that is a great line. A little while ago, I could get 2-year treasuries at 5%. I put a whole bunch of money into those. They're going to start rolling off soon, so I need to re-examine where I can find 5%.

And yes, I understand the arguments. People will say, "Well, that's not real inflation. Real inflation is 10%." I get that. But over time, 5% for me is a line where if I can get that risk-free — particularly as I've seen 20 years where you couldn't get close to 2% risk-free — that is a place for me where I'm happy to park capital, because I think over time 5% is going to do a lot of the work for me that I need it to do in terms of maintaining that purchasing power without taking too much risk.

私人贷款与睡眠

I've got some private loans out to people, and I'm not saying I've invested in private credit, because that's become a commoditized thing. But I have made loans to people and businesses that I know well, I understand, and I'm as sure as any human can be on the planet that I'm going to get not only paid the coupon — which is above market rate — but I'm going to get paid back the principal. I don't lose any sleep over those.

I've really gotten myself in a position where I sleep really well. I don't have to sit and watch markets on an hourly basis. And that for me, as a former trader of many many years — once I've got money in the markets in active positions, I need to sit and watch them. It's not in my DNA to have capital invested in markets in stuff that moves around that I'm not going to sit and watch, cuz that's what I've been trained to do for three decades plus. So I'm set up in a very very specific way that works for me.

给年轻人的建议：十倍股与自然资源

Now, I also got, as I said, involved in conversations with a group of people, and everyone was sharing opinions. And it was very interesting to me — and made perfect sense — that some of the younger guys who were at the conference, they were looking for the 10-baggers, right? They were looking for — which gold mine is going to be the one, which uranium company is going to be the one that I get 10 times my money.

And I have nothing to say to those guys to discourage them. You know, they were in their 20s. They've got time. The only thing we spoke about was: be careful how much of your capital you commit to those ideas. Make sure you do your research on the companies, on the management. These are appropriate risks for you at that age if they're sized correctly and if you understand what to do when they go against you in the short term.

Rick Rule 的"living legends"面板

Listen to people like Rick talk at these conferences. He had a fantastic panel. Anybody who's interested in natural resources — I'm not getting paid by Rick to do this, he's a dear friend of mine, but the conference is good. That conference every year in July is a great place to go to learn how to invest in natural resources.

There was a terrific panel of what Rick calls the "living legends," where they spoke — and these are all guys who've been serially successful in building resource companies and selling them and making their shareholders tens of billions of dollars over the years. And every single one of them had, not just one but multiple stories of 50 to 60 to 70% drawdowns in their stock prices on their way to turning $50 million into a billion.

商品作为"必需品"

So resources are a great way to make money in a bull cycle because commodities are "need goods," right? The world needs commodities. People don't pull stuff out the ground for fun. Gold is slightly different — the world doesn't need gold to build anything with, but it needs gold because the monetary system has become unanchored. But everything else are need goods. And when you have need goods, no matter what happens, you're going to try and pull them out the ground. And the price of the finished commodity will reflect that. And the price of the companies that do it will also reflect that — at the ebbs and flows of regulation and price and supply and demand.

So putting money into commodity stocks I think is a really good idea if you are at the right age, you have the right amount of interest and the right amount of time to spend monitoring the companies, and you have the right risk tolerance and you don't get your position sizing wrong.

AI 与尽调：一个被外包的危险

A subject that also came up quite a lot was AI, understandably. And the point I was making on one of the panels is — for me, with regards to that second type of investing, AI is potentially incredibly dangerous because it feeds that insatiable human desire to be lazy.

Humanity is lazy. Every invention mankind has ever come up with, essentially, is a way to do less work or to lighten our workload and get the same output. That's the story of progress, the story of humanity. And what AI does is it gives people the misguided belief that they can outsource their due diligence around companies, around positions they own, to AI.

And people are coming saying, "Well, you know, I've got Claude or I've got ChatGPT, and I've set up different GPTs to act as mining analysts for me to find the companies that I need to buy."

There is no substitute. None. I don't care what AI is capable of doing, of going to an event and meeting the management of the company, meeting the CEO, meeting the geologist and sitting and talking to them and asking them pointed questions about the company. And then once you've decided that you like the asset, you like the management, you like the jurisdiction, all the things you have to do, and you invest your capital — leaving the maintenance due diligence up to AI is, I think, a massive mistake.

You have to read. You can set it up to give you alerts, but you have to be reading the press releases. You have to be understanding what a certain headline at a mine can mean, because the guys who've made money in that space understand that and they know which 50% drawdown is a massive buying opportunity and which 50% drawdown is halfway to zero. They're distinct and they're very important to understand.

从进攻到防守：过去 40 年的完美投资环境已终结

So it's a very long-winded and circular answer to your question. I'm sure you've got others, but that was my answer to that question.

I'm playing defense. And my balance of offense and defense has gone from probably 70/30 offense over the last 10 years — it's gradually gone the other way — and I am now at least 90% defense.

By that I just mean I don't want to take inappropriate risk that would set me back, because I think we've had the most perfect investment environment for 40 years now. A presentation I gave last year, I walked people through the environment we've had, and I asked for a show of hands, and the question I asked them at the end was: given what I've just showed you, should it have been easy or hard to make money these last 40 years? And every hand in the room went up for easy — because it should have been.

You had falling interest rates, you had central bank puts, you had low debt originally, you had globalization — you had everything you could want for making money. You don't have that environment anymore. And so making money is going to be difficult again. It's not going to be impossible, and there will be plenty of money to make, but you could have turned around and fallen ass-backwards into pretty much anything in the last 40 years, and if you just hung on to it, you'd have made money. Those days are gone, and my positioning has adjusted accordingly.

黄金过去 6 个月：不是"故事"

Alexi: In many ways, a lot of the factors that you just mentioned — the interest rates, but also volatility in general, inflation levels, and also the fact that there's sometimes some discrepancies or inconsistencies in what we've learned how assets behave in the last 40 years — that all of a sudden in the last maybe two years start dislocating more and more, right? Having different adverse responses to different conditions.

For example, maybe it would be interesting to also talk about how gold behaved in the last 6 months — the fact that it went from above the $5k mark, then corrected at least 25% since its peak. And also the fact that at the beginning gold was very sensitive to geopolitical instabilities. The Ukraine war in 2022 — it started rising from then onwards. But then with the Iran war, it did rise at the very beginning but then it started actually correcting and going sideways for the most part of the conflict.

Even though we have from one side inflation expectations that are just rising because of the energy shock, central banks that are still not hiking rates as we expected because inflation still remains high — pressured with, we talked a lot about the sovereign crisis. So there's a lot of moving elements and I'm curious to hear what you have to say about all that.

"The story is not that gold fell 20%"

Grant: Yeah, I think it's a great topic for discussion. The gold conversation came up a lot last week.

It's interesting — you know, what you said there: "gold has fallen 20% or 25%." 20%. And I find that interesting because that's not the story, but everybody thinks it's the story. Everybody is now talking about gold having fallen from — the intraday high was $5,589, I think — and it's back down at $4,000, and everyone's saying that's the story.

That's not the story. The story is gold — this boring rock — tripling in the space of a year from — well, more than tripling — it went from close to $2,000 to $5,500 in a year. That's the story, because gold shouldn't do that. It really shouldn't do that, and it did. And everybody, once it started moving, thought it was a meme stock. And it's not a meme stock. Gold is money, period.

And the reason for that was a lot to do with central banks buying gold and changing their behavior. And I've written at length about this over the last couple of years, ever since the freezing of Russian central bank assets, which I called "the end of the financial world as we know it."

抛物线尖峰的正常回归

So the story is not "gold has fallen 20%." It just isn't the story. And it drives me crazy when people think it is, because if you look at the chart of gold, anyone could see that that parabolic spike to $5,500 — it got way over its skis and it was massively extended. And of all things, that's not a chart that looks well with gold, and it doesn't end well with gold. It's corrected to a much more appropriate level.

But we're talking about $4,000 gold. And it wasn't that long ago that $2,000 gold was the insurmountable obstacle. And it went through 2,000, it went through 3,000, it went through 4,000, through 5,000 in quick succession. And there is a message in that — a message that I suspect is going to be a preview of what happens at a later date.

So to think the story is that gold's in a bear market — this arbitrary 20% correction thing that just makes me laugh every time I hear it — I just don't think that's the story. But it is what people want to talk about, because obviously the headlines are coming out — we had a Wall Street Journal cover article this week talking about how gold has now fallen into a bear market and it's disappointing everybody.

Yes, if you piled into gold above $5,000, you are definitely disappointed. And a lot of people did, because we've built a momentum culture. And gold is the ultimate value investment. So it doesn't surprise me that this is the way things have gone.

I'm constantly trying to preach the other side of this argument, and it falls on deaf ears when things are moving rapidly in either direction. But if you can find a way to step back far enough and look at gold, you will see that the story is not "5,000 to 4,000." It's "2,000 to 5-and-a-half thousand." That's the story.

And I continue to believe that gold, if you zoom out far enough, will just continue to march higher because it's responding to the macro situation, the debt situation, the monetary situation, the loss of trust between countries and central banks. All of these things are perennially playing into the price of gold. It's not just about "central banks are easing and there's too much debt." It's all of these things that shape the macro geopolitical landscape that are shifting, that is being reflected in the gold price. It's not a meme stock.

白银：同样的故事，更暴力的版本

Alexi: That's a very good point. And what do you — because we haven't talked in depth about silver, which is also partly a monetary metal, not to the same extent obviously as gold. It's main industrial demand that drives it. But there's also a case to be made here — and probably it's kind of like this the same in a different manner. This time silver also corrected about more than 50%. What would then be your — how would you approach the silver story?

Grant: Well look, I mean to me it's the same story, right? We spent years talking about, would silver get past $30? Would silver ever get back to $50 again? Years talking about that. It goes to $120 and it's back to $60. And so $60, which was a ceiling that people didn't talk about — it was always $50, "Will we ever get back to 50?" — it falls back to 60 and everyone's talking about how the silver story is over.

It's all perspective. It's all perspective. And I think the perspective that you read in a lot of the mainstream media stuff and a lot of discussions around silver are the perspective of people who have come into silver late because it was moving. And so of course you can understand that perspective — to them, it has been cut in half.

The perspective of people who have been buying silver for different reasons, not because of the price, for an extended period of time — they have a very different perspective on the story. And some of them might be thinking, "Oh, you know what? I knew I should have trimmed some when it got to 100, and I could have been buying it back now" — cuz they want to own silver, not buy it.

So there are always different stories around any asset at any price. And I'm sure my perspective is clouded from the fact that I've been buying gold since it was $300 and change, so my perspective is very different.

为什么白银总是更暴力

But silver — it's interesting because it always moves with gold. It sometimes moves later, but it always moves more violently in both directions. And that's what we've seen. We saw it very slow to catch up with gold. Then we saw it go absolutely bonkers, and we've seen it get slashed the way it always does.

I think a lot of the move is for the same reasons. There's a lot more speculative activity in silver just because it is inherently more volatile because of the lower price. Because of the lower price, more people will speculate on it. It's tougher to speculate on a $4,000 item than it is on a $50 item. People can get more leverage, more bang for their buck with it.

So nothing silver is doing surprises me. I expect it to remain volatile. I expect it to continue to go higher over time, get dragged along by gold, and as the belief in gold ebbs and flows, the volatility in silver will do likewise.

Fort Knox 与 Bessent 的语言学

Alexi: Did you also see recently the statement from Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent about the fact that he went to Fort Knox — he wanted to reassure everyone the gold is there, don't worry guys?

Grant: Well, no, actually he said he didn't go to Fort Knox. This was quite important actually. Some of his people had been to Fort Knox.

Alexi: Oh.

Grant: Yeah. He said, "I haven't been to Fort Knox, but some of the people that work for me have." So it was very interesting that he distanced himself from saying "I'm the Treasury Secretary and I have seen the gold." He did not say that, which is interesting. And again, feeds the conspiracy theories that surround gold.

They're fascinating, and there are some conspiracy theories that you can say with reasonable certainty, "Oh, it's just a conspiracy theory." I think this idea of all the gold owned by the United States, this 8,133 tons, not being where it's supposed to be — it would be far less of a surprise to me to find out that were true than a lot of other conspiracy theories.

But no — Bessent the other day said, "The gold is all there." And the guy said to him, "Oh, you've seen it?" He said, "No, I haven't seen it, but people from my staff have been there."

为什么真相永远不会浮出水面

And so it doesn't matter at the end of the day. It only matters if we get confirmation that it's not there. And if it's not there, we will never get confirmation of it, right? Because no one's going to be allowed in there to prove it's not there. So it will always be a rumor.

I don't waste a lot of time. I find it interesting, and when I see something like Bessent last week, I'm very attuned to the language used. And so it struck me quite hard that he was at great pains to make sure there was a bit of distance between him and the gold.

But like I said, if it's not there, they are never going to admit it. Anybody who comes into office somewhere and has ideas about maybe having a live audit of the gold in Fort Knox is going to be dissuaded in the strongest terms possible. So I think it's a canard. I think it's a waste of time to fixate on that. I think it's definitely a waste of time to invest solely on whether you believe the gold is there or not.

历史坐标：黄金覆盖美国债务需要多少价格

More importantly to me is if you look back at a chart of — let's assume the gold is there. Let's assume Scott Bessent's people were right when they went to see it and they told him what they'd seen — if you look back through history at the amount of the US debt that gold covers and the price you need for gold to reach those levels once again, there are three dates. There's 1917, there's 1940 after World War II, and then there's 1980 when we had these spikes in the gold price.

  • Currently we sit at, let's call it $4,000
  • To match 1980 coverage: gold would need to be $30,000 an ounce
  • To match 1917 levels: I think it would have to be $60,000 an ounce
  • To match 1940 levels: it would have to be $72,000 an ounce

That would be — and by that I mean to have the same amount of gold backing for the debt. You've got the same amount of gold, obviously, and at different prices it backs a different amount of debt. So those are levels: $30,000, $60,000, $70,000. We're currently at four.

And at some point, in every instance where a country starts to have the spotlight turned on its debt and the affordability of the debt and whether that debt will ever be paid back — those are the points in time where you say, "Well, what is that debt backed by?" And through history, it's been gold.

Now, people will argue that the United States debt is backed by its military, or it's backed by national parks and all these great assets that the United States has on its balance sheet. But it doesn't count. No one's going to be able to swap a billion dollars of treasury bonds for a nice little part of Yellowstone National Park. So it's a spurious argument. And yes, the military backs it — and if you kind of try and take it, they've got a lot of guns and aircraft carriers — but gold is the barometer. And so given the debt and the amount of gold that we have supposedly in Fort Knox at $4,000, it's way too low. Simple as that.

Judy Shelton 的黄金债券构想

Alexi: And also, I mean, not talking about all the other speculations — getting out of the way — the Judy Shelton gold-backed bond project that was kind of rumored to be announced for the 250th anniversary of the United States of America on the 4th of July was also kind of like this — yeah, one of those things that fans in the gold industry were kind of speculating on.

Grant: Look, Judy's terrific. Judy is absolutely terrific. She's been on my podcast. I've met her once. She's a terrific lady and doing great work, and incredibly well-intentioned in a field of people who are far less so.

But unfortunately, the chance of gold being voluntarily reintroduced in any way into the monetary system in the West, certainly, I think is a complete non-starter.

核心论点：没有人"选择"回到金本位

I talked about this in my presentation "Cry Wolf" in 2018, which I just kind of went back to at Rick's conference and did an update on it. The commonly accepted wisdom and the way people talk about a gold standard is this: they say, "Well, nobody would reintroduce a gold standard because if you do that, you have a massive deleveraging event that leads to social upheaval, political upheaval, financial market upheaval. So the gold standard is not coming back."
