And the point I made in 2018, which I make again this year, is that that's not how this works. Nobody chooses to go back to a gold standard. It's the other way round.

You have social upheaval, you have political upheaval, you have financial market upheaval that leads to a giant deleveraging event, and the answer is to bring back gold to stabilize the financial system.

  • We've had social upheaval. That's clear.
  • We've had political upheaval. That's abundantly clear.
  • We almost had financial market upheaval in 2020, but we conjured another several trillion dollars out of thin air to paper over those cracks. So we avoided the giant deleveraging event.

But in doing so, that was perhaps the last roll of the dice, because now we can see the inflation that's now embedded in the system is trying to break loose.

So to me, we're a giant deleveraging event away from being forced back onto a gold standard temporarily. This isn't the answer to our problems, because it does create problems having a gold standard. But the problems it creates are actually solutions. The problems it creates is that it stops politicians promising and spending. It stops you spending money you don't have. So that's the solution to the problem that got us here. But it creates the problems that you go through when you try and set a system like that — it does create deflation, it does create all the things that the gold standard creates, but we need that now to purge the system.

So ideas like Judy's of this gold-backed bond, I think, are great. And I think they will come. But they won't be a choice made to celebrate anything, let alone the 250th anniversary of the United States. They will be a choice forced in times of trouble, when you need people to buy bonds and they won't buy them unless they're backed by gold. And that's where I expect us to go back to — even if it's only for the short term to stabilize the system.

中国：为什么它在囤金

Alexi: So it would be kind of like maybe an emergency thing that is in the drawer, or like one of the optionalities that the government could actually explore in one way or another.

And when we look at then — because you just mentioned the West. In the West this is a non-starter, but the East — and I've had many conversations also with you last time about this, and now recently with Alasdair Macleod as well, that he's really bullish on the fact that China is preparing in the shadows, behind the spotlights, this huge accumulation of gold. He estimates for himself that there's more than 30,000 tons of gold owned by China in one way or another, indirectly or directly.

There's the Hong Kong clearing system that's also been inaugurated — I think this opened up on the 7th of July, there was a day I interviewed him — and this whole system of SGE vaults that around the world are envisioned to be also one way or another to peg the yuan to gold. Do you have any insights about how do you think that's going to play out?

长游戏的战略耐心

Grant: Yeah. Well, I don't know if you could call them insights. I'll leave that to people to decide. I've got some thoughts on it.

Look, I think there is more chance than not that Alasdair's right about China having a lot more gold than they say. I think that's a pretty safe bet. The amount, we won't know until we know, but I think he's most likely correct that they've got upwards of 20,000 tons.

But I don't think they are accumulating that to back the yuan with gold. I think they're accumulating it to be ready so that when the gold standard is the solution for other countries' problems — like the United States, like Europe, like wherever else has got these debt problems — when that becomes the solution, they are in the strongest position.

That makes perfect sense to me. And if you do play the long game the way the Chinese do, and you don't have to be reelected every four years, you can do things that you don't have to do in the West. You don't have to promise and spend the way you do every 2 to 4 years.

So I think China are absolutely doing that. I think they're accumulating as much gold as they can because they understand that sooner or later, it's going to be put back at the center of the monetary system through necessity, not choice. And when that happens, they will be in the strongest position.

So you're — I don't think you're going to see them say, "We're going to back the yuan with gold." I think when the world finally falls apart and these decisions are forced upon the United States and Europe and Australia and, God forbid, Canada — who've got no gold — or the UK, who've got no gold, when these decisions are forced and gold is forcibly reintroduced back into the financial system to stabilize it, the Chinese are going to be sitting there going, "Well, we are now the strongest nation on earth from a financial standpoint because we have three times the gold the United States has."

And that will put them in a very very strong position. So I think it's long-term planning. It's not a means to an end. It's not a means to back the currency, cuz they don't need to do that. But when we reach this point of crisis where gold is necessary to reintroduce, it will put them in the strongest position.

日本：架构的支点正在移动

Alexi: So there was one topic which I also want to discuss with you, and it's a bit of a recurring one since the past years — it's Japan. As we all know it's kind of like the source of virtually free capital for the rest of the world since the past year. It kind of changed recently where, for example, since I think we talked, I think within a year's time, the Japan's 10-year government bonds yield reached about 2.7 today. So it's 1 percentage point higher than it was a year earlier.

There's also rising oil prices, fiscal concerns that add pressure to Japanese yields, and all that. And then the yen carry trade unwind is still back on the table, but also just the way Japan fits within this whole country architecture — we've seen investors taking advantage of its low rates for so many years, recycling it and then just getting US assets and so on and so forth. Seems like there's something that is heating up. What are your thoughts about this?

从通缩到通胀，从依赖到不可依赖

Grant: Yeah, look, Japan has been pivotal to the entire architecture of capital flows for decades now. It's been pivotal in supplying low-cost capital. It's been pivotal as the center of the carry trade because you could rely on one thing: that Japan would have deflation, and Japan would continually keep rates low, and that JGBs would never again get off the floor.

Well, Japan now has inflation. They have started to raise rates, and JGBs are at 30-40 year highs in terms of yields. That's changed dramatically, and the pressure is such that it will continue to change.

Now, the yen's been the interesting thing because the yen continues to be weak despite the fact that they're raising their rates. I've seen great arguments on either side for why the yen will strengthen and will continue out to kind of 200.

But the important part of this, I think for me, is the sheer amount of Japanese capital that's overseas. They are the biggest creditor nation on earth by some distance. And there's been some whispers out of Japan the last couple of weeks about the GPIF — you know, the government pension fund — readjusting, making readjustments to certain investment parameters. And if they do that as a means to try and repatriate more capital, or to find homes for more JGBs, it's going to shift the financial flows once again.

鱼缸的水

And this brings us back to what we started this conversation with, Alexi — this idea that big changes are afoot, that won't happen in a two-day period, but are, if you step far enough back, shifts rather than changes.

And what's happening in Japan is a shift. The idea that you've been able to rely on for 40 years — that the yen will provide cheap carry and you're never going to get called on it, it won't be a problem — that idea is gone. It's not to say you can't do it. The part of the idea that's gone is the idea that you can rely on that to continue. I don't think you can anymore.

And you're seeing at the margin people less inclined to rely on that as a cheap source of funding. To a lot of people, the dollar has become a cheap source of stable funding for a while, and I doubt that's going to last too long either. So again, this is just another perfect example of the way that this architecture that we all have seen built around us and we've all come to believe "this is just how the world works" — you know:

  • Gold is boring
  • The yen is always weak
  • Japanese rates are always zero — it's a great cost to carry trade
  • NATO is solid
  • The United Nations is fit for purpose

All of these ideas that we've had, that over a 70-year period since the end of World War II, has been the water that we swim in — those no longer hold.

David Foster Wallace 的鱼缸

And it takes an ability to step back. If you continue the David Foster Wallace analogy about the water we swim in, you've got to try and find a way to step outside the fishbowl if you want to see the water for what it is. It's just this contained environment with the fish swimming in it. If you're in it, you can't see it. You've got to find a way to step back outside the fishbowl in order to see it.

And if you can step outside the fishbowl, you can see that the water is different. And whether you want to stretch the analogy and say you can see that the water's got a different color to it, or a different viscosity, whatever it may be, if you step outside the fishbowl you can see that it's changed. And because it's changed, you need to change.

You need to change the way you think. And only by changing the way you think can you get to the point where you can understand whether you need to change the way you act. And you may be set up correctly for this new environment, in which case you don't need to make massive adjustments to your portfolio. But it's not until you change the way you think about the water that you will have enough information to work out whether you need to change the way you act.

欧洲：本应是巨人的第三方

Alexi: And now there was also something else I was asking myself — that as we see those great powers, and this is mainly, we don't know, as you said, change is constant and you need to readjust more than you used to before because the foundation of this new game is being rewritten, the rules are being rewritten. But the players are kind of known, right?

And in this case, where I see the biggest weakness in, let's say, the reconstitution of the world order, is the role that Europe will play. And in this case, I can speak for myself, I can also speak for you as Europeans — we just look from both sides and we just seeing in one way or another, we're getting squeezed. We are between two giants trying to fight for their survival, or at least impose their view on the world in one way or another. How do you see that, especially — because you have kids as well, grandkids — what are the thoughts that go into that process for you?

应该有三个巨人

Grant: Well, look, it's a great way to put it, but it's also, if you actually stop to think about it, part of the problem — because there should be three giants. Europe should be a giant, right? It should be.

The European continent is bigger than America, more people than America. It's a massive market for China. It's a massive market for America. But it isn't a giant. And that is part of the problem.

You know, the EU was a project that was designed in good faith to avoid more conflict on the European continent, but also to create a giant. And so I think what you said there is again important, because people in Europe again have to step outside the fishbowl to understand that Europe is not what they believe it to be.

I think if you talk to any Europeans who are pro-Europe, pro-European, pro-the EU, their perspective on what Europe is and its place in the world will be very different to how people outside Europe look at it. And again, in Europe you've got multiple distinct countries, distinct cultural identities, distinct ways of looking at their place within the European Union. So it's very difficult to get an accurate perspective on Europe unless you are removed from it.

Even the Brits, who are now technically removed from it, they struggle to have a really good perspective on Europe because it's always "what-aboutism," right? It's always "we left and we're doing so much better than Europe," or in the same country, "we're screwed because we left Europe, we should have stayed in Europe." It's impossible to get that perspective.

从外面看进去

If you step outside to America or you step outside to Asia and you look at Europe dispassionately, you can see what a mess it is, and you can see that without the right sort of coordinated policy — primarily energy policy, which is a complete shambles across Europe — the Americans and the Chinese will be looking at Europe thinking it's almost a third world power. Which is crazy, because it absolutely shouldn't be.

The policies that it's embarked upon have made it weak. And so at some point Europe is going to have to find the strength to push back against the policies that have weakened it. Largely, you know, net zero policies; largely the kind of micromanaging of the EU and a lot of the subsidy culture. There are so many things wrong with the EU as a project. It's an admirable ideal, but there are so many things wrong with it that are actually hurting it and creating this kind of weak mass at the center of the world.

依赖美国防务的代价

The Russia war and Trump's comments about NATO have exposed that for all to see. Europe has become weak because it believed that it could rely on the United States to fund its defense forever. And so all that money that we could have spent on tanks and guns and missiles and defense systems, we can spend on social programs. Lovely, right? But two problems: you've created a weak bloc, and you've also created a culture of dependency. And until you can tear those up, which means reshaping the way the EU allocates its budget in a way that is going to lose votes right across the bloc, you've put yourself in a checkmate position.

So Europe is in a mess, I'm afraid. And it's not to say it can't get out of that mess. Europe has a tremendous history of culture and advancement and civilization and invention and all these things. But they're all unfortunately being hamstrung by an ideology that is about the ends rather than how do we fix the problems. It's about how do we get to a certain end — this "socialist utopia." And I just don't think that's possible.

And the dying of that ideal and a return to pragmatism is vital for Europe. Germany is the poster child of this. Germany is being forced into pragmatic decisions now because the idealistic ones that put it in this bind — where it's now virtually the sick man of Europe, has gone from the strongest country in Europe to the weakest almost because of these policies — they're having to rethink them. And this is why the AFD are doing so well, because they're the party that's come along and said, "You know, screw net zero. Our standard of living is going down, and we're not going to stand for that and we need to do something about it."

So Europe is a mess. It's a complete mess. And that's not — I'm not saying that as a Brit poking sticks at Europe. I'm saying that as someone stepping outside the fishbowl looking at Europe and going, "This is a complete mess. And the sooner you do something about it, the better." Because you will be forced to do something about it sooner rather than later.

欧盟的死结：从"廉价货币"到"紧缩"的不可能过渡

Alexi: On one side it seems like it can go either direction, but in extreme violent ways. On one side there's this trend of confederation of Europe, making Europe even stronger, removing unanimity — like the veto rights of certain voting countries. Now in that was the case with Hungary, for example, in packages for Ukraine that actually revealed the system within the decision-making of European countries — now okay, now we need to actually have a hard decision-making process, China-like, "okay, now we do this and we all do it, there's no blocking in the way." Or on one side, what you just described as well with other parties that are more on the right spectrum, extreme rights some would say, that say, "Well, we actually need to disengage totally from this project, this is not working at all." And there's no in-between in a way.

唯一能达成的共识：给所有人低利率

Grant: I think you're right, Alexi. But importantly, that first idea is unworkable. It's just unworkable.

If you think about it, the original — "let's have one decision, let's have one policy" — that the EU was essentially built on when it came into being in the late '90s. Obviously, we can go back to the Treaty of Rome and the way that the EU and the Steel Commission, all those things built. But if we go back to 1999, the introduction of the euro, which is probably the most important part, the one common idea that it was founded on essentially was: low interest rates for all. Right?

That's what we can agree on. We're going to take Greece and Italy and Spain and we're going to give them Germany's interest rates.

When the one common decision is free money for everybody, it works really, really well. "Surprise, we're all Europeans. We love Europe. Let's all be European."

When the decisions that need to be made are, "You know, less welfare, we need to cut immigration drastically," we need to — all these things that, as we rode that tide of cheap free money for 30 years, 25 years, we have to reverse all the things that we did there. Suddenly it becomes unworkable when the one idea that we now need to coalesce around is effectively austerity. "We can't afford migration. We can't afford social benefits. We can't afford all these things now. So we're going to have to reverse them." It's unworkable. It's absolutely unworkable. Because turkeys don't vote for Christmas, right? No one's going to vote to have things taken away from them.

一个无解的僵局

So I see that as an intractable problem for Europe. And they will arrive — ultimately, it's not going to be good. I really don't see how this doesn't end. Not in conflict between Europe, but countries in Europe finally standing up to the EU and saying, "No, no, either we're not going to pay for that, or we're not going to let you take this away from us." And then we're going to go back to having a whole series of national conversations about, "Okay, what is our place in Europe? Do we want to be part of this project now that it's not a tailwind for everybody? Now that it's more of a problem, do we want to be a part of it?"

And I suspect when push comes to shove, the answer is going to be no. Because people are very happy to take, but they're not so happy to sacrifice or give. And it's just human nature. That's just the way it is.

Alexi: Yeah. It's like organizing a huge open bar night with all your friends and comes and then no one's like —

Grant: "I didn't have the chicken tender. I didn't have the chicken tender." Yeah, exactly right, exactly right.

欧洲增长为何停滞

Alexi: So it's but it's also a bit of — I mean coincidentally, I wonder as well, why the European Union — I mean for the first maybe 10 years its GDP growth was actually quite good overall, right? As a bloc it was quite strong. But then the 2008 crisis happened, and it added just barely 5 trillion to its GDP in the last then 10 years, while the US added at least double of that in that same time period. China, let's not even talk about it. So what then revealed or got hampered its growth since the last 15 years? Is it just like all the regulations and net zero that you just said, all the policies, or is something else?

Grant: Well, I think that's a big part of it. But I also think that the amount of debt that's been saddled on these countries — you look at Greece and Spain. What Italy — what do you think they're going to do when interest rates go from 11% to 3% overnight? There's going to be a massive borrowing binge. There's going to be a massive misallocation of capital. Of course there is. That's not a surprise to anybody.

Margaret Thatcher 早就预言过

So Europe, as I said, Europe was a fantastic ideal. But if you go back and listen to Margaret Thatcher's last speech to Parliament, she laid out exactly what would happen if you have European Union but you don't have a common treasury. She laid out exactly what was going to happen. This shouldn't be a surprise to anybody. Because the one beautiful thing about human nature — as erratic as humans could be as a group, human nature and the way we respond to incentives is undefeated over time.

And so we're exactly where Thatcher said that we would be. And she didn't get to the point of saying what happens next, because her thrust was, "We don't want to be a part of that, cuz I can see where it's going."

So it shouldn't surprise anybody. And the fact that, as you say, GDP growth has been poor is because you're trying to carry different countries at different points in time. You're trying to pay for massive social programs. You're trying to do an awful lot of things that any one of these countries individually, if you'd given them low interest rates, they'd have got themselves into the same trouble. But getting out of them on an individual basis and finding ways to grow would have been easier. But now, obviously, Italy can't devalue its currency. Greece can't devalue its currency. These countries that are struggling can't do the things that as individual sovereign nations they could do to try and change things because they're part of the EU.

It worked great when everybody was lowering interest rates together — it's brilliant. It doesn't work so well once all the goodies have been taken off from under the Christmas tree, right? All you got left are the needles all over the floor. And that's kind of where we are.

Alexi: Yeah, it's a bit of a "one-size fits none" type of thing.

Grant: Yeah, it is now. Yeah, exactly right. Great way to put it. Great way to put it.

数字欧元、CBDC 与净零：意识形态政策的反噬

Alexi: But then what is then — because I mean on one side, and I see also the way the European Union tries to still consolidate that power, which I said would be one direction by, for example, putting in this digital identity, forcing Europeans then to having to log in online to track activities under the false pretense of, "oh yeah, we need to catch those pedophiles" and so on so forth. And then introducing the digital euro, because "you know, cash is bad, we need to have a system that is against those Visas and Mastercards from the US, we need to be independent" — while actually only strengthening any grip that the European Central Bank has over its own money. But then also making it kind of like a Chinese surveillance type of — yeah, well, a CBDC type of scenario. And now the question is how far will Europeans actually comply and go with this or not, right?

投票箱的反抗

Grant: Yeah. And those are the questions that we are going to find out at the ballot boxes, because these are the kind of issues that will be front and center in elections to come.

You know, it's interesting — people have been talking about CBDCs for a long time and digital currencies, and this idea that "this is where we're going, cash is going to be abolished." And the assumption has been made that "that's just the way things are going to happen." They've also been talking about net zero — "and this is just the way things are, we are going to go to net zero."

And what's been interesting in the last couple of years, as those insane net zero policies have actually caused real-world problems, people are refusing to accept it. Electric car sales are down. The politicians that are pro-net-zero are now finding themselves on the receiving end of a lot of abuse and a lot less votes.

You know, we saw this week in the UK, Ed Miliband, who's the net zero czar, saying, "Well, I'd be willing to compromise my net zero goals if I could be made chancellor."

生活水平是最后的红线

So digital currencies, net zero, all these idealist policies that are designed to create a much better world — once they are proven to actually lower the standard of living of people, people get very animated about them and refuse to vote for them. And it horrifies people that there could be people out there suddenly voting for more coal power, or whatever it may be. But the history of humanity has been a constant improvement in the standard of living. That's what we've done. We've improved the standard of living for thousands of years. And the policies that are in place that are demonstrably going to reduce the standard of living of people who have a vote — you're going to find they won't vote for a lower standard of living. No one's going to do that.

And when — you know, the — we've talked about this 40-year period of tailwinds where cheap money and stock markets are going up, your house price is going up, everything's going your way. "Wouldn't it be great to have net zero?" Brilliant! Yes, absolutely.

Now you're at the point where it's, "Wait, it's going to triple my energy bill? Well, no no no no no, I'm not voting for that. No. I'm not so keen on net zero anymore."

I think every human being on the planet, if you said, "We could have net zero and it would not impact your way of life" — go. We're all for that. There's not anyone that's going to vote against that. But "We're going to force net zero on you and it's going to lower your standard of living" — no, people are not going to go for that. A few will, a few will be willing to sacrifice, but in the aggregate, a population is not going to be pleased to see their standard of living go down. And that's just the reality of it.

收尾：足球，时间机器，与两年后的欧洲杯

Alexi: Fair points. Grant, I think we've covered a lot today. Is there anything else that you think is worth sharing on a concluding —

Grant: Yes, I'd like a recount of the England-Argentina game last night. I want to find out if maybe if England could find two more goals somewhere and win 3-2. I would be happy if you can arrange that for me before you and I meet each other on Saturday night in the third place playoff final, which nobody wants to watch. Then that would be great. But otherwise, no.

Alexi: Well, that's something that only a time machine could fix, but yeah.

Grant: I think everything we've talked about today can only be fixed by a time machine. But maybe that time machine needs to go forwards. We'll see.

Alexi: Yeah. Well, there's still the Euro to go, so it's not all all over for the —

Grant: Two more years. Two more years.

Alexi: And the French. So — well, talking about the Euro and the Euro Cup and the European Union and all that.

Grant: We'll see you in the final in two years.

Alexi: At least there's still football to kind of get our minds off all those other big shifts and complex topics and all that, and we can be monkeys hitting balls again, you know.

Grant: There you go. Amen.

Alexi: Grant, as always, a pleasure talking to you. Also if it's online, obviously much better face to face, but this does the trick as well.

Grant: Likewise, really enjoyed it as always. Good to see you.

Alexi: Thank you. All right.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：金本位不是"选择"出来的，而是被"逼"出来的
Grant 在这里颠覆了整个货币改革叙事。所有的评论员都在争论"金本位好不好、要不要回去"——这本身就是一个错误问题。真正的机制是**反向的**：社会动荡 → 政治动荡 → 金融市场动荡 → 巨型去杠杆事件 → 被迫用黄金重新稳定系统。
这里藏着一个更惊人的推论：**2020 年那几万亿凭空印出来的美元，可能是"最后一次掷骰子"**。因为它成功阻止了那次"巨型去杠杆事件"——也就是说，它推迟了通往金本位的最后一道触发器，但代价是把系统里的通胀压力封存起来，让它现在正在"试图挣脱"。换句话说，我们没有避免那一天，只是**让那一天变得更贵**。
## 二、跨领域连接：AI 时代的"懒惰陷阱"，与 Grant 从进攻转向防守，其实是同一件事
Grant 说"人类的所有发明本质上都是让自己更懒的方式"——这句话表面上是在批评有人用 ChatGPT 替代尽调，但把它和他自己的资产配置从 70/30 进攻变成 90/10 防守放在一起看，一个更深的模式浮现了：
**过去 40 年，"懒惰"是被奖励的**。你可以闭着眼

item.title: [a16z] Why Physical AI Is the Next Frontier | The a16z Show
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的反直觉真相："物理AI 的最大敌人不是技术，而是律师的调色板"

Kasar 讲了一个让我拍案的细节：GM 内部的安全评级系统不用红黄绿，而用"紫色"或"品红"——因为一旦上法庭，"你放行了标红的系统"会成为致命证据，而"品红"就能在庭审中制造歧义空间。

这是整段访谈里最刺眼的观察：决定自动驾驶何时能落地的，不是神经网络的参数量，而是律师写给工程师的颜色词典。 Cruise 之死不是技术败于物理世界，而是败于"未能与政府按正确节奏共舞"——他把这一步类比为《好家伙》最后一幕黑帮开会。

这里藏着一个跨领域连接：汽车行业的"品红"，与金融行业的"标准差单位"、医疗行业的"不良事件报告"、AI 行业的"model card"是同一物种——都是在诉讼/监管高压下演化出的语义防御层。 一个组织越成熟，它的语言就越多"品红"。这也解释了为什么 Waymo 慢——它继承了 Alphabet 的法务基因，而 Tesla 快——它把"品红"直接绕过成了"beta"。

二、出人意料的模式联系：物理AI 与"McDonald's 招不到人"是同一条曲线

访谈里 Kasar 用麦当劳招不到人的现象类比卡车司机短缺——"人不是不想工作，是 DoorDash 让他们能自己定时间"。但更深的模式他没说透：

过去三十年，人类劳动力从"固定物理位置 + 固定时间"逐步撤退到"移动数字位置 + 弹性时间"。物理AI 正在填补这场撤退留下的所有真空。

农业（58岁的美国农民）、矿业（全球1%劳动力，8%死亡）、长途卡车（预期寿命少10年）——这些不是"AI 抢工作"，而是"AI 接管人类正在集体逃离的岗位"。这也解释了一个反常识现象：在数字AI 领域，工程师害怕被替代；在物理AI 领域，客户跪求被替代。 Kasar 原话："在我们的宇宙里，运营商说的是'如果你能做到，我们把一切都给你'。"

跨领域连接：这与《人类简史》里农业革命的隐喻惊人相似——不是人类驯化了小麦，是小麦驯化了人类去它想去的地方。现在轮到机器把人类"驯化"回它们本应待的地方：家里、屏幕前、有空调的房间。 矿井、驾驶室、田野，这些地方从来就不是为人类肉身设计的，只是我们暂时没有别的选择。

三、被低估的战略深意："应用直觉"不是自动驾驶公司，而是"物理世界的台积电"

访谈中反复出现一个类比——Kasar 说他们"看起来、走起来、说起来都像一家芯片公司"。这不是修辞，是战略拓扑学。

注意这个隐藏结构：Tesla / Waymo 是垂直整合的苹果模式；应用直觉是横向赋能的 ARM/台积电模式；而 Dana（新发布的产品）是他们想成为的第三层——物理AI 领域的 iOS SDK。

一个让人后背发凉的推论：如果 Dana 真的让"高中生做出送外卖机器人"成为可能，那么杀死人类工作的最后一批技术门槛不是被 OpenAI 拆除的，而是被一群 15 岁的孩子拆除的。 就像 2007 年的 iPhone SDK 释放了 Instagram、Uber、Snapchat——但也永久摧毁了出租车行业、纸质地图、独立摄影师。

跨领域的更奇诡连接：这次的"iOS 时刻"发生的顺序是反的。 移动互联网是"先有硬件平台，再有开发者，再有应用"；物理AI 是"先有应用（Waymo/Tesla 做榜样），再有开发者工具（Dana），再等硬件平台成本坍缩到 $500"。这种"反向渗透"意味着物理AI 的爆发不会像 iPhone 那样有明确的分水岭年份——它会像"温水煮青蛙"式发生，等你意识到时，你楼下的清洁工、你孩子的补习老师、你父母的护理员，都已经悄悄被换过了。

最后一个啊哈时刻：Kasar 说他们最想做的不是折衣服的机器人，而是"娱乐"——彼得赌马戏团、西部世界、机器人杂技。这个偏好暴露了一个被主流叙事完全忽略的真相：物理AI 的第一个万亿市场很可能不是生产力，而是"活着的会动的内容"。 我们已经把注意力经济推到了平面的极限（TikTok），下一个疆界是三维的、会呼吸的、能被触摸的表演。Chuck E. Cheese 的机器人熊，可能才是通往通用机器人的真正祖先。

📝 转录文本

开场：物理AI 的使命

Our mission is to put intelligence on a billion machines and we think that can have a profound impact on society. Applied intuition is a physical AI company. We put intelligence on machines — cars, trucks, tanks, drones. It's a physical moving thing. We make it intelligent.

Digital AI, of course, is building software and optimizing ads and creating videos. That's all interesting and good, but really where you talk about the global economy, that's physical AI. In this intelligence revolution, the companies that impact the physical world might actually be bigger than the companies that impact the digital world.

How many things are there where the idea of physical AI, physical intelligence are going to matter?

There's no reason autonomy should be this obscure, difficult technology. Our vision for that is a high school kid that can make iPhone apps should be able to make autonomous systems. That platform for designing and developing is what we're launching. It's called Dana. Everything that we've built and developed over the past nearly a decade — that's available in Dana.

Which will we get first? A perfectly simulated real world environment for training autonomous devices, or Grand Theft Auto 6?

Kazer, Peter, welcome to the A16Z podcast.

Well, thanks for having us. Your name is just one of many.

I feel like we've — I think we've all each known each other for too long. More than I'd like to admit. Yeah. Long time. We're lucky to both be the first investors or among the first investor in the first round. Of course, different check sizes, but —

And I was an investor for you even before then.

Exactly.

一、什么是"应用直觉"（Applied Intuition）

Um, so let's do that as a segue. We have a lot to talk about today. We've, you know, the biggest launch in company history to talk about today, but first, why don't we just give an update — what does applied intuition do for those who —

Yeah, for the people who don't know, applied intuition is a physical AI company. We put intelligence on machines. That's the simple way of describing it. Um, and all types of machines. So cars, trucks, tanks, drones, you name it. It's a physical moving thing. We make it intelligent.

And the history of the company is: we originally started by making the tools that would make the intelligence, then we got into the actual intelligence itself. In some ways, like a very boring AI company in the sense of — you know, 83% of the company is engineering. We win by making really great products. It's not like a good sales or something like that. I don't think we're good enough for for a sales-enabled company. But yeah — over a thousand engineers, based in Silicon Valley but we have offices globally, 18 offices.

And our mission is to put intelligence on a billion machines. We think that can have a profound impact on society — both in the kind of pissy things everyone talks about: safety. If you really talk to somebody who's been in a car accident or in a mining accident or in a farming accident, those are real gnarly situations. Beyond just fixing that — if you can unlock productivity, we've seen the unlock in the digital world and everyone's super excited about it and you have trillion-dollar companies emerging.

I'm a pretty strong believer that when we look back 25 years — if you look back at the internet now, if you look at the original internet companies that are doing serving or they're doing some analytics, those are interesting. But really when you look back 25 years from now, the big monolithic companies are Amazon that delivers you stuff, Apple — these are the true kind of companies that come of age. And I think when we look back 25 years in this intelligence revolution, the companies that impact the physical world might actually be bigger than the companies that impact the digital world.

二、超越汽车：物理AI 的广阔市场

I would love for you to talk about the following: when you first started the company, the knock on the company was, "Oh well, it's making cars autonomous, right? Self-driving cars." But it's kind of like — okay, there's Tesla and Waymo building their own self-driving cars, and then there's like six or eight other car companies that matter, and then the company just could never get that big because there just aren't that many customers.

So how should people think about how many things are there that move, where the idea of physical AI is going to matter?

Yeah, I mean — even today, even if you put that view on us: automotive is like 30% of our business. So 70% already is non-automotive. And I think if you fast forward another 10-20 years, even the manufacturers themselves as a customer base will be a small amount. Our mission — just keep thinking, a billion machines becoming intelligent — you think about all the types of machines that exist. Automotive is just an easy one. I think it sticks in people's heads because we all drive cars and it's a big market. But I think it'll be a minority of the business. It is a minority of the business. I think it'll be increasingly a minority — but that doesn't necessarily mean it'll be small.

Automotive is still huge just as a part of the globe's GDP. Automotive is something like 3% of all GDP. The way we always think about it — as you try to get to your mission — initially the manufacturers were the distribution to that intelligence to consumers. But then you start working in defense, and you start working in construction and mining and agriculture, and suddenly the manufacturers are important, but maybe the mining operator is actually really important, or the Department of War is really important, and suddenly they become customers. And all of those are customers of ours as well.

Yeah. I think if you split AI into digital AI and physical AI: digital AI of course is building software and optimizing ads and creating videos, that sort of thing. That's all interesting and good, but really where you talk about the global economy, that's physical AI. And then we're talking about manufacturing and mining and logistics and transportation, all of these things —

Supply chains.

Yeah, supply chain. Exactly.

三、无人驾驶舱：机器形态的重塑

Well, let's build on that though for a second. Things that move today, or historically things that move, are things that have human beings at the wheel or at the controls in some form. Airplanes have had to get designed around a human in the cockpit. Boats have had to get designed around a human steering things. In a world of autonomy — do we already know what the things are that move, or are we going to discover that there are a lot of new things that are going to get built when you don't need a human in the driver's seat?

I think both. The thing that you have to remember is: you take a haulage system that's on in a port, like a Komatsu dirt mover in a mine — those are made for 20-25 years. So the buyers of those products might not have gotten their full ROI on them. So they're not immediately going to buy something new no matter how much better it is. So one part of our strategy is: you got to make those things intelligent because they're not going anywhere.

The second is what you're talking about — well, that depends on a human in a cab. If you don't have a human in cab, you can run the machine — it can be smaller, it can be shaped in very different ways. You talk about mining underground: the constraint actually is the human, because the human needs to breathe and it's very dangerous. And so you can build a very, very different machine. We're doing both of those things.

And then the thing that we're not talking about is: we're all talking about intelligence almost like within a system, but the system-level intelligence is where the unlock is. We're already doing work like that where you say, "Hey, let's take an entire port, let's take an entire mine, let's take an entire quarry" — and this heterogeneous mix of machines, they can all talk to each other and they can optimize and be efficient. When one machine goes down or one machine has an issue, the rest of the mine doesn't have to stop.

When it's human-driven, we don't even know the machine's going to go down because there's no analysis — the human is not plugged into the core systems of the machine. So a simple thing like knowing when a brake system is going to break is actually huge because you can start preparing for it in advance. "Oh, this wear and tear is higher than in other mines" — just using an example.

The other macro point: if you look at agriculture as an example — the average American farmer is 58 years old. Under 35, it's like less than 10% of farmers are that young. So what's going to happen? The need for food growth is continuing to grow. The need for rare earth materials is continuing. So these demands are only growing, but the humans who are the bottleneck are decreasing. Trucking is the same way.

And so you can really just unlock a lot more efficiency. Imagine if the cost for food decreases because it's way, way more efficient — what's the downstream impact? Imagine for goods being transported: let's say instead of a few dollars a mile, it's 20 cents a mile. Suddenly the unlock is very, very, very big. And that doesn't necessarily need all the machines to be redesigned from the ground up.

四、公司规模与执行

Give us a sense of the scope and scale of a company today.

Yeah — north of a thousand engineers. And those engineers are, obviously, the classic software and AI engineering teams, but we also have engineers who really know safety systems. We also have engineers who really know hardware — because the important thing that we're stepping around is: all this stuff is hard because it ultimately has to meet the real world, and the real world has way more complexity and a lot more issues.

We have engineering teams that can — I mean, we've deployed our models onto like 50-some platforms. Even that sounds trivial because when you think about models, you think about deploying them through a browser or on a phone, and everything's abstracted away because of iOS and Android and Windows and Linux. In the real world you don't have that, and so we have engineering teams that can do that as well.

Our claim to fame: we've raised about a billion dollars in the company's history, all that is sitting in the bank. I always say that with an asterisk — doesn't mean we're not going to spend it next month.

Good news, bad news.

Yeah, good news bad news. We're at that phase — you talk about scale — where these giant markets are around us and we can make the decision how aggressive do we want to pursue those, because [we have] a decade of execution and deployment into production. I think the hallmark of our engineering team is putting products into production. That really is a big — I don't know, how do you think about scale?

Yeah, I think that's roughly — I mean, the mission of bringing intelligence to a billion machines, that is how we think about it. And then thinking about, well, what are the types of machines that we'll have the most impact on, and focusing on those areas first. But we'll get there.

五、数字AI vs 物理AI：进展与瓶颈

Let's go deeper into the differences between digital and physical AI, and where we are today. What progress has been made, what are some of the major bottlenecks in physical AI?

Yeah. A lot of times people think about the progress in physical AI is limited to basically two use cases — just because they're obvious and interesting — which is robotaxis and humanoids. They're very visceral. They excite you and they're kind of sci-fi. Those are very interesting, and there is real work being done by us and other people in those domains. But I think all the other domains are going to be just as important. You just think about what happens on a port — that's a huge unlock. That's the area we're really focused on: all the other nooks and crannies.

If you look at the rise of Cisco and how networking went from individual machines to entire countries getting networked — there's a similar thing happening with AI. AI's getting to that level of "sovereign AI" being a discussion now. Sovereign AI really is about physical AI, because that's where you're talking about AI in defense, AI in the physical machines that are moving around.

If you look at the example of Waymo from America and Pony from China trying to deploy in other countries — not America, not Europe, not China — every one of those spaces is way more hesitant to say, "Yeah, thumbs up, your robotaxis can run unfettered in our country." If you look back at the arc of the internet — when the first internet companies come, nobody's really thinking about sovereignty at all. The browser goes everywhere, the internet goes everywhere. That's almost the power of it. Then when social media emerges, there's a bit more of "hey, actually not every social media" — and then you have China not allowing Facebook to come in. Then you get into the online-offline stuff: there's more resistance, the Ubers, the DoorDashes — suddenly there's local players being favored very aggressively.

When we get to physical AI, I think there's going to be huge [sovereignty concerns]. Also there's a larger geopolitical theme of more fracturing than globalization. You're going to have this demand for AI to be localized, and I think that has to play into our strategy as well. We're a technology provider, so we can provide that technology across the globe. That's something that's understated in this conversation.

六、数据、安全与专有数据集

A few other things on digital versus physical AI. In digital AI, the state of the art is: you can train models effectively on the entirety of the internet, and then maybe augment that with additional data collected and refined with some hired experts. This is sort of a hot field right now. But generally you're talking about a foundation model that's built on internet data.

In physical AI, the internet data is useful too. However, to actually build a foundation model in physical AI, there's also a lot of private data collection. When we're talking about mines or logistics or any of these other fields, the data that's useful for training models there is not necessarily available. So we have to do a lot of work ourselves actually going out and collecting that data.

And then the other key factor is safety. If you're building a smartphone app, you don't necessarily care about a safety-critical application. But when you're talking about moving a machine that weighs many tons, or think of a humanoid which could fall over on your children — you care a lot about safety and the evaluation of that safety. That is really getting to the state of the art of physical AI — really proving out the safety case around some of these state-of-the-art models.

Yeah. Humanoid data collection has been its own little area of interest, but when you talk about collecting data in places like Korea — with North Korea nearby, they don't allow mapping companies, let alone allowing an American company to come in and collect data. We've figured out over the years — whether it's the Middle East, whether it's LATAM — how to get into these countries, work with the governments, and get the thumbs up to collect proprietary data.

So in the way that it is similar to other digital AI systems — proprietary data sets, scaling laws, all that stuff is the same — it's just applied in a very different way. The way to think about it is: the diffusion of these models is very different because it's not everyone can just access them through a phone. And so, ironically, that actually plays in our favor. Because once we have a massive proprietary data set — we already have hundreds of petabytes of data — and then we have our own tools (synthetic data tools, neural sim), we can use our own tools with our own proprietary data and that allows us to build some of the best systems in the business.

Is there kind of a chicken-and-egg thing — in order to build an autonomous physical thing, you need a lot of data; to gather that data, you need a lot of physical autonomous things running around collecting the data. So once you have a giant network of physical things running around, you have the data that makes them all work. Is there a flywheel aspect of that? What's the level of difficulty involved in booting up that flywheel?

It's difficult, but it's also not difficult. We have one of the largest data collection fleets on the planet, frankly speaking. That's how you bootstrap your way into it. That's just money and resources and technical knowledge. But it's not — there's probably more than five companies that have that technical knowledge. So it's not extremely obscure.

What is more difficult is: how do you actually have that model which is going to work on lots of different hardware, and is tested appropriately? Because you saw it in Cruise, right? Cruise was this company that did amazing self-driving work, and then one accident — General Motors owns them and they get super scared and they pull back. So just getting these things into production is actually more difficult than it seems.

We believed synthetic data was going to be important — so we started our synthetic data team like 5+ years ago now. We're a strong believer that synthetic data can accelerate autonomy development. We've just seen that. And then there are lots of other secondary and tertiary technical innovations that happen. Obviously the transformer revolution hitting self-driving — basically everything done in self-driving pre-'21/'22 [is less] relevant. That's kind of the starting point, but those four-five years there has been a lot of work done. You can see it most clearly with Tesla, but there's other folks.

Historically — and I'm just simplifying here — imitation learning was the way: collect a bunch of data and then the models would basically imitate what human drivers do. The real state of the art right now is end-to-end reinforcement learning in a closed loop in your tools. It's a little simplified to say the system learns itself — it identifies where the issues in the self-driving system are, and essentially you then find data like that, or you synthetically create data like that, and then you close that loop and you see: are you performing in those same scenarios better and better?

If you fast forward some years, that will be a completely closed loop with no humans intervening. Right now you still have — like the fog error we saw yesterday — we still see errors in the real world that impact self-driving.

So what are the bottlenecks? There's plenty of them, but whenever you're dealing with physical systems, you inevitably hit a lot of gnarly hardware problems. It could be anything from overheating, to a sensor being slightly miscalibrated, or a funny issue we saw yesterday was basically fog impacting a sensor. But these are the things that you actually have to solve for this stuff to work very reliably in the real world.

七、Cruise 的教训：与体制共舞

Yeah, so I want to ask you a question — you can decide whether you want to engage on it or not. Were you surprised? Cruise was a super high-flying Silicon Valley autonomy startup that was kind of running neck and neck with Tesla early on. Very top-end team. And then they famously got bought by General Motors —

One of my first distributions personally.

There we go. Y Combinator company. Top-end team, and by all accounts making excellent progress. They got bought by General Motors. They became the GM autonomy program. GM got a lot of praise, at least in the tech circles, for being the legacy automaker with the biggest investment.

I called Peter before it was announced. I said, "Hey, Cruise just got bought" — he's also GM family, we're both GM families. And Peter guessed: "Was it Nvidia?" I said no. He said "Apple." I said no. I said "General expletive Motors."

Right. So that's surprising to people — that they were willing to buy, that they did it, and by all accounts they were making excellent progress. And then there was an accident — was that an injury or fatality?

It wasn't a fatality; it was a serious injury. Somebody was dragged for 20 feet. Serious injury, bad press. And then the GM CEO on board put a bullet in the Cruise project. At least some of the senior Cruise people were extremely upset by the aftermath of that. Was it surprising that they reacted the way that they did?

Full disclosure: General Motors is a customer, and I went to the General Motors Institute, so we have a lot of love for the company. But incidentally — I'm reading this very famous book which I had actually never read before, called On a Clear Day You Can See General Motors. DeLorean's book. Have you read it?

As one does. Have you read that book?

Years ago, I have. It's one of the great all-time book titles. We should just pause to say: John DeLorean was, like — he was the super genius of the car industry.

He was going to be the next president of General Motors. And then later on he started his own car company — like Back to the Future — and then that whole thing collapsed for a variety of reasons. But he was a legend. He was one of the main principal drivers of innovation of the Bob Lutz category.

Repeat the title of the book.

On a Clear Day You Can See General Motors.

Why was that the title?

Because it's a very large complex. It's like a nation state.

I mean, we say that almost flippantly, but these companies are extensions of the state. Hyundai is an extension of the state. Toyota is an extension of the state. Volkswagen — literally, Volkswagen board members are members of the government. There used to be an old saying: "What's good for General Motors is good for America." You cannot underrate how important General Motors is in the history of the American corporation. Sloan's My Years at General Motors and Adventures of a White Collar Man — if you run a large engineering organization, you should read that. This modern corporation didn't just emerge. Sloan and Kettering — Kettering, the head of engineering — created this org with levels and vice presidents, functional and matrix organizations. It really is like the source code.

Then along comes DeLorean. He says, "I'm going to be president" — and he's so fed up with the company. What was controversial was: GM was doing really well at the time. GM was number one, two, and three in the Fortune 100. It was everything, seen as the best company in America. So somebody to openly criticize the company — he writes this book out of how annoyed he was. He said, "I don't want that book published," and so he fights for years for his co-author not to publish the book. The co-author still publishes it. So it's a real true insight into a large corporation.

Incidentally, even though I worked at GM 20-some years ago and knew a lot about the company — what's shocking is it's not only about GM. Most of the major manufacturers actually still operate that way on the inside.

So the point for everyone to take away isn't that the people who run these companies are stupid. They're not stupid. It's kind of like — when you're selling to the Department of War and people say, "Well, why are you doing that?" It's like, well, the distribution defines the business.

This is a consumer product. When I worked in safety systems 20 years ago, I remember GM used to pound into your head: of the top five consumer lawsuits in American history, three are automotive. So you have to be extremely careful. But we had these weird things — like inside the company, it wasn't red/yellow/green, it was like purple, or you'd always have to decode. Why? Because when they go to lawsuits they're like, "You let a safety system that was marked red go to production." "No, it was marked magenta." Can you imagine how infuriating that is? Every time you're like, "What does orange mean?"

So fast forward — the Ford slogan for a very long time was "Quality is Job One."

Safety is Job Two. Yes, exactly. That's the one-two punch of automotive: quality and safety. Quality really becomes because the Japanese really reset that stage — that's a whole separate automotive history. But the punchline is: you have the Silicon Valley company meeting this immovable object.

There is a parallel universe that Cruise is out there right now, even as a part of General Motors. So you always have to take it into the context of where the company is, where union negotiations are happening literally that year. If you're the union, you're like, "You can't make a billion dollars for us, but you're funding this thing that's killing people and it's sloppy." So I'm not saying precisely that's what happened — to be very clear — but it's a multivariate problem.

My other hot take: I worked at both companies — Google and General Motors. Those companies are way more similar than they're different. Way more similar. Literally, people don't need to know this — the Google leveling system is the same as the General Motors leveling system.

I used to say inside of Google meetings, "Hey, actually some of the engineers I knew at General Motors are better than the engineers here." And people would look at me like I'm saying there's no God in church. They'd be like, "How dare you, you metal-bending monkey from Detroit." No — actually, making a modern combustion engine is extremely complex. It's not simple stuff.

So the macro point: safety is always at the top of their list. We've hired lots of Cruise people. The way they dealt with that specific issue with the government — you got to dance a particular way when that happens, and they just didn't dance exactly right, and that just gives government bureaucrats more ammo to go after. And you're a big target like General Motors.

It reminds — you guys ever see that movie Goodfellas? One of the last scenes, "The House of the Rising Sun," all the old bosses go in the back of the courtroom. That's what happened. The board was like, "What are we going to do about Cruise? Kyle's a good guy, but..." And then it's like cue "House of the Rising Sun." People running through a San Francisco office. Just kidding. Don't make that an AI video. Gonna get a mean text from Kyle.

So a lot of what Applied Intuition does is that dance — how to be a great partner to these companies.

Exactly. Bearing in mind their own very real issues and constraints.

I think GM also had the topic of business model. Cruise was going after the robotaxi concept, but GM makes its profits from personal car ownership. And those things can be a bit odd. So I think that was also a bit of the equation.

八、时机与分销：核心的商业教训

You actually spoke at YC in 2013 — I was in the audience, I was a partner at the time. You said something which is very recursive here — we're feeding each other lines. The key thing in a new technology business — actually, everyone kind of figures out the technology, though that's still hard. It's still hard sometimes to build really complex things. It's when and how you deploy them into the market. The when becomes really important. You're two years early, you're doomed. You're two years late, there's too many competitors. You have to hit it at the right spot.

A controversial thing to say: I actually think Cruise was moving at a much faster pace than Waymo. They started way behind, and you're talking about neck and neck when the plug was pulled. So who knows what happens in the long term.

Our hypothesis in that same equation is actually the distribution — you let the manufacturers do that. We run self-driving trucks right now in Japan. They carry commercial loads. There are safety drivers, but they're autonomously running. You won't know that, because the brand is Isuzu. That's the customer.

Why it's so good for us to partner with Isuzu in that case: that company's been around for almost a hundred years — pre-World War II company. They know the government, they have test tracks, they know their own trucks very well. So when we go and provide them with the intelligence and the integration into their physical machinery, that's a fantastic one-two punch.

I think today the world is ready to consume AI in the real world — and that's a lot because of ChatGPT and Anthropic and everything that's happened. People are no longer like, "What's a self-driving car?" — that's because of Waymo and Tesla. So the market is ready to consume, and you just have to meet the market in the best way possible.

Our view has always been: you go through some of the people who run the economy right now — whether it's a mining operator, a Department of War, or the manufacturers — and we work within each vertical with the right partner. That's a fundamentally different view than a Tesla or a Waymo, which are going to be vertical. We're really playing the horizontal.

Our companies — we're kind of like a chipmaker. We look and talk and walk a lot like a silicon company, except we don't make chips. We have design wins, and then we have really large long-term relationships. And once we're in, we're in — it's really hard to take us out. So you need deep trust. Our partners have a lot of deep trust, and we know their markets really, really well. The thing that Jensen knows is: he knows his customers. That's why Nvidia does well, beyond the fact obviously they make a very complex technology.

九、传统车企如何应对

How are these legacy car companies preparing for the future? Are they making acquisitions? Are they building? Partnering with you? How are they going to compete with tech-native companies?

It's like saying, "How are governments dealing with AI?" It's such a broad topic. Each manufacturer — take Honda, Nissan, Toyota, three Japanese manufacturers with long legacies. They all approach it very differently. They're roughly in a spectrum of "we're going to build" to "we're going to buy." Both extremes.

More than ever, "we're going to buy" is the common answer, because they've been trying and we've been there the whole time. For the folks that are going to build, we provide them tools — that's what we talk a little bit about with our new product that we're announcing. And on the ones that just want to buy, we sell them the actual intelligence that goes on the machines. So we meet the customer wherever they are in their journey.

The more nuanced version of that: every product is a different product. And so the amount of silicon, the amount of dollars you can put towards it, towards sensors, what the customer is willing to pay — all that depends on what actually gets in. In the long horizon, all these things will be fully autonomous, but the intermittent steps are very much what we saw in the PC — you have this slow step-up. One day, nobody really looks at laptop specs, and maybe even your phone specs — but that's not the case from basically '85 to 2002-2005, where finally people stop actually spec-ing at all. There's a similar kind of 20-year horizon there.

Broadly, when we talk about machines becoming intelligent — fundamentally a machine is a collection of these different components that are integrated. And whoever does that final integration is oftentimes the company that puts their badge on it — the brand name. But many companies are building technology that goes into that machine. So we now have a bunch of technology components and platforms that can go into these machines, but we also sell the core technology that can be used to develop [and] help them as well.

If you look under the hood of a dirt mover or a combine or diesel truck, they'll have Cummins engines in them. But nobody says, "Well, because all these guys buy Cummins, this means that Caterpillar is not a good company." That's just a component that they buy. They have a different role. So in any of these verticals, there's a complex web of folks. That's why I always say the chip analogy actually works quite effectively — because none of those companies make chips, but they all buy chips.

十、自动驾驶：从 DARPA 到今天

So self-driving cars. We've all been talking about self-driving cars for — I think the whole thing started around 2005 with the DARPA Grand Challenge originally. And then Google engaged in the program shortly after that. Late double-O's. So almost basically around 20 years. There have been lots of predictions over the last 20 years of like, "Self-driving cars are imminent at any moment."

The bad news is: we're sitting here today, and most cars are not self-driving. The good news is: there are now self-driving cars. Most cars are driving all over — you know, in the places they're deployed, it's become routine. People in San Francisco treat it now as routine that they get into [a Waymo].

I think you can call — it's kind of like the AGI thing. If we're talking 20 years ago, everything we're seeing right now is mind-blowingly AGI. The post keeps moving. The Tesla stuff's amazing. You can look at a bunch of manufacturers — Blue Cruise, Super Cruise, BMW, Volvo's Pilot — they're all quite impressive systems. They're not full self-driving.

Well, it's full self-driving X, whatever remote monitoring is happening. The Tesla — we have a home in Los Angeles, and you guys may recall there was a large fire in Los Angeles. And the California power grid was buckling even before that. So it actually turns out among the things Cybertrucks are good at is they're very good batteries.

And so literally we have Cybertrucks as our backup battery for the house. And as of last year, whatever the FSD released — I forget the exact one, but there was one where a lot of people thought it really turned the corner. Version 14. And that thing drives you. I talked to somebody yesterday — has a Model Y — who let the thing do the full route all the way up Highway 1 through Big Sur.

Miles per disengagement are really high. I think miles is like in the thousands. Which is very impressive.

The Big Sur / Highway 1 / Big Sur — that's a stress-filled drive. He said it was great the whole way. Anyway — I wouldn't have been talking to him had it not been [for that]. Would have gone right off the cliff. Because he unbolted the steering wheel.

So Tesla is rolling out their robotaxi — starting to show up in the wild. On the one hand, those exist. On the other hand, 99.9999999% of cars are still not self-driving.

Then I would say maybe just one other would be self-driving trucks. There's been this recurring panic in the press of like, "The trucks become self-driving and all these truck drivers will be out of a job." And sitting here today, I don't know — are there any trucks on the road that are self-driving that don't have at least a safety driver in the truck? I think the answer is probably [no].

十一、L2++、成本与消费者车辆

Let's split this. On personally-owned vehicles: why are they not more ubiquitous? Part of that is the manufacturers are not good at deploying technology. Part of that is they want to be safety-conscious. But most of it is cost.

What you're seeing in China — China is kind of a different EV ecosystem, mainly because they don't care about profits. When you're talking about a business that doesn't care about profits, it changes the entire calculus of the entire industry.

But what you're seeing is L2++ systems. We can simplify the entire self-driving conversation to: is there a driver behind the steering wheel? So the driver behind the steering wheel is still there, but generally — like Tesla drives everywhere. They're like sub-$1,000. There's an aggressive [price point] — that's chip, sensors, the package, the software, everything.

We anticipate that once you get to like $500, the automotive OEMs will actually subsidize it for free. They'll just give it to you. This happened in nav systems — if you guys remember, nav systems used to be a big thing. You'd pay $4,000, $3,500 to get a nav system, and then suddenly it became free and it just became default.

There's a weird thing — actually getting into a subset of your cars costs X dollars, and to get into all the cars costs X plus just a small incremental amount, because it's just a fixed cost — the way the assembly line comes, the way you have homologation, all this testing regimes. So you'll have wait, and then — every single OEM, without exception, even the lowest-dollar OEMs, are working on an FSD competitor.

So it'll come. But it is like — a good analogy for self-driving in the personally-owned ecosystem is mobile phones. We had the satellite phones, then we had the Qualcomm brick phones, then we had the Motorola Razrs, and from the late '90s to the late double-zeros everybody was like, "When's mobile going to come?" And then it comes, and by '07 from the iPhone launch it's four years when you get Uber, Instagram, WhatsApp, Snapchat. Those are the killer applications.

So I think there's a very similar kind of wait, and then it's just basically ubiquitous in every vehicle. If you had to ask me for what that number is: '28 SOP (start of production), '29, '30, and then by the early '30s it'll start becoming very cheap to free.

Routinely by the early '30s, you would just buy a car and you just assume self-driving.

Exactly. Or it has the driver-in-seat L2++ system. Being very specific — like Cybertruck or Tesla — what Tesla owners have today will become common. Will be default.

十二、Waymo vs Tesla：架构与经济

So the other side of this: why don't we have a bunch of Waymos everywhere? Specifically, Waymo has a different technology. Without getting into the nuances here — Tesla and many of the Chinese and Applied were very much in this end-to-end model architecture. This is a new way of doing self-driving. Waymo, for the lack of a better word, is not that. It's not one end-to-end system. It's not one monolithic model.

One of the proclivities of their approach: it does depend on HD maps. Therefore there is a geo-fencing concept. Waymo is trying hard to remove that bottleneck so they can expand geographically faster. But the reality of today isn't there.

The other thing: when you have researchers — which they were, coming out of an Alphabet research organization — they didn't put commercial constraints. So the sensors are bespoke and expensive. The cars and the compute that are in there — they're just not economically feasible. And they've tried a lot to get that down. But it's a lot easier to go from something that's really cheap and make it more featureful than something that's overbuilt and then trying to trim it to make it really cheap.

That's the big debate — who's going to get there first: Tesla with full self-driving, or Waymo with cost and geographic ubiquity? But you know what we're not debating about? Is it gonna happen. You know what we're not debating about? Is there a big technical breakthrough that needs to happen? None of those things.

So now we're clearly in the engineering side of self-driving, which is just this grind down to dollar-per-mile efficiency. And the moment that it's cheap — guess what? All the OEMs are smart, they'll just adopt it. It's not the OEMs are resistant because they don't think consumers want it or they don't understand the technology. It's because they want a price envelope which allows them to keep their razor-thin margins, at a scale which is deployed across 100+ countries in V1.

And if you're just doing a small deployment it's very different. The buyer of a Subaru or a buyer of a Suzuki have very different brand expectations than a buyer of a Tesla, including the age of the consumer and what they think will happen and won't happen. So if you're a Suzuki, you're like, "Well, my buyer doesn't want this stuff, so I'm not going to jam it into the car." It's not because they're not technically competent. It's just a different area.

When do you think it'll be routine — let's say the 200 biggest American cities — would it be routine to walk outside and just take it for granted that a robot taxi can come pick you up?

It's '26 now. Certainly by '30.

Yeah, certainly by '30. The big variable there really is — because what Waymo will say is that the dollars-and-cents per city already work. And it's like, well, a company that has basically unlimited capital — why are they not already in 200 cities? But then you see their launch schedule is pretty aggressive, and you're like, that can get there.

Maybe if I was being aggressive, I would say '28. I would say available in '30, but routine maybe like '32, '33. Because there's a scale-up, there's a volume.

Also, five years ago I'd go to LA, people would be like, "What's Applied Intuition? I don't know what self-driving cars are." In the last couple years now, they all know self-driving. And some of them even know Applied Intuition because they know from the other manufacturers. I think you fast forward another two to four years, everybody knows it. Does that mean everyone's taking Waymos exclusively? That answer is no, actually. There is a huge — if you look at the numbers, if you're Uber, you got to be scared. They're just eating into ride-sharing. But to get 100% ubiquity — that has to be extremely cheap.

十三、长途卡车运输：计算器生意

And what about long haul trucking?

So the passenger side [is one thing]. Long-haul trucking — completely different economics, completely different business model. There are many companies right now — probably north of five — that are running long-haul trucks with drivers carrying loads between [places in] America. And China — if you had China, it's probably getting into double digits. So it's there, but the reason you don't know it, and the reason it's not top of mind, is: it's not a consumer product.

Unlike Waymo and Tesla, where investors are willing to essentially give you some market cap adjustment for the potential — the trucking business is like: you buy a car with your heartstrings, you buy a truck with a calculator. It's a calculator business. It's pure dollars and cents.

So as the provider of self-driving trucks, if you're doing the whole thing like some of the companies are (which we're not), you have to show every mile "I'm going to save you this many dollars." And the buyer is unsophisticated. They're just like, "Well, I already got a staff that can drive." They're just not inclined.

Where we're playing in Japan, it's not random that we're doing trucking. Demand — there's a massive labor shortage today, and there's an imploding demographic situation. There's a demand from almost every sector. That's why we've picked that market to really grow.

But even more obscure — like when will all quarries [have autonomy]? Literally like where you're moving cement, you're moving dirt. Quarries — Q-U-A-R-R-I-E-S. Quarries, rock, stone, cement. When are those — I can tell you: the people who own those things and run those things want it today. So you don't have a [problem there] — literally, we can't make the stuff fast enough.

The macro point though that people don't talk about: I think all this stuff is going to happen, and happen fairly soon. The macro point — in legislation and in the political economy of this conversation — AI is really: you have this big pushback in digital AI, because accountants are like, "I don't know, this is going to happen to my job," and VCs (I'm sure all of your associates are very scared). Like, they're debating whether they need us. In our universe, it's the other way around. I'll meet these operators and they're like, "We'll give you everything. If you can do this, we'll give you everything." So then it's just up to us to get there aggressively.

十四、卡车司机的现实

Well, you know, the fear for a long time has been — for trucking, for some reason, [it] triggers the press's imagination on apocalyptic levels of job loss.

But that's so wrong. Go ahead.

There's not enough truck drivers, and guess what? Nobody wants to freaking be a truck driver.

Why is that? Explain that.

Because it's a terrible job. It's like — you're asking me — this is like talking to my kid who's like, "Well, why can't I put my hand on the stove?" It's like, "Because it's going to burn your hand." "But why?" After the third "why" it's like, "Come on, buddy, let's do this the hard way."

So what's hard? Why is being a truck driver a difficult job? Or why would kids not want to do it when they grow up?

Let me use a parallel analogy. People will say, "Nobody wants to work anymore. McDonald's has all these job openings." No, actually — what it is is those people that used to work at McDonald's now DoorDash and Uber. Because it's better for them: they can start their hours and end their hours, and there's no boss, they don't have to stand on their feet, they can surf their phone in between orders. That's the reason. It's not random. The market is efficient.

In the truck-driving example: why does somebody not want to be away from their family for 4 to 8 days in a row doing long-haul trucking? The sharper example: in Australia, why don't people want to literally buy a plane [ticket] to go to a mine and work on it, or you go offshore oil rigs? Those jobs exist. If you want a job that pays six figures, they exist. Even with such lucrative pay packages, it's not enough — because people are like, "You know what, I like kind of being around my family, and I'm willing to take an incremental decrease in cost / how much money I make."

Today more than ever, things like back pain, being exposed to the sun, cancer — people care about that.

Tell me if I have this right — I believe commercial long-term drivers' life expectancy [is] 10 years less than their peers. People say it's a consequence of several things. One is some combination of nutrition and sleep. It's very difficult to eat well and exercise. What's your sleep score if you're a long-haul trucker? Let me guess — eight-sleep on that. Exactly. And so obesity, heart disease, hypertension are all very high.

Two: the vibration is very difficult, stress in the body. And the third — you mentioned cancer, but I think truck drivers have a much higher rate of melanoma on their left arm. Exactly. There's photos of a truck driver who's been driving for 30 years — one half their face vs. the other half's face, because they're exposed to the sun on one side.

A more stark stat: mining is 1% of the labor pool globally, 8% of work-related fatalities. Do you think people are rushing to work in mines when they hear stats like this? Most major mines have a fatality regularly — once, twice a year, three times a year. If you ever visit a mine, you'll see that everything is based around safety, because once you experience one of your co-workers dying, then you're like, "What am I doing here? There's other jobs I can take."

Truckers don't even want their kids to become truckers — at the very least, safer line of work.

Notwithstanding all that — how long will there be safety drivers in long-haul trucks that are self-driving? Or somebody in the cab to deal with what happens when they arrive?

We know multiple companies that have driver-out goals right now. They are working to get drivers out right now. Without going into our own details — to be honest, it's not long. We're talking a few years.

On the long end. And the thing is — there's a software technology thing which is one part of the problem. But the other part is the redundancies that you need in hardware, and the validation necessary for those redundancies. In many cases that can actually be a long pole. It's like, "Oh, they're productionizing a fully redundant steering system, fully redundant braking system — that's not in high-value production yet." Once you get that in high-value production, now you got the quality up, and then that's validated, and now you can actually do the price downs.

十五、Dana：物理AI 的开发平台

Do you look at the little delivery robots? Is there a world where there's a billion of those running around?

Yeah, I think so. The product that we're announcing — I think it's probably come out around this time — is called Dana.

You can simplify everything that Applied Intuition does into two buckets. We've been talking mostly about the models that go on the machines — we call this "onboard software" or "onboard AI." Then there's "offboard AI" — this is the tools to design and develop these same systems.

For the models that actually go on the machines: our vision — and the delivery robot is a great example — is like, a high school kid or a middle schooler, they can make iPhone apps, they should be able to [make] autonomous systems. So why can't they? That's a very simple question. Why can't a ninth grader make a delivery robot in their home?

Well, they don't have the actual environment that they would first develop the scenarios in. They would define the requirements — "Hey, I want this robot to go on my high school campus around these, let's say, four buildings." Once you define the requirements, then you have the scenarios get made — where are all the scenarios that can be made by using, let's say, a satellite image of the high school. Then now you have to train the robot. So you need some data. Where do you get that data? There's maybe enough publicly available data that can actually train a fairly rudimentary robot. Okay, now you got that data from online, maybe YouTube videos, couple other places. Suddenly the robot's not [working]. Now you need to deploy it onto the actual machine. So then you deploy it onto the machine, and then the robot runs into the wall. Okay, what happened there? The loop closes.

That platform for designing and developing is what we're launching. It's called Dana — which is the street that Applied Intuition is headquartered on. This comes from our tooling background. If you look at how tooling has changed in the digital AI world — what Claude did — we all remember from Mixpanel to GitLab, GitHub, all these — now everything has moved into a very different, almost IDE, frankly speaking. We think the same thing is going to happen in the physical world.

We already use it in-house to develop our autonomy systems, which are the most scaled, complex systems on the planet in all these different verticals. So we're pretty confident that it's actually quite useful. We've seen massive productivity gains.
