But also, we think other companies will use this to build their own systems — because it gets to that mission of building intelligent machines.

Fundamentally, Dana is our agentic platform for physical AI, and everything that we've built and developed over the past nearly a decade — every tool, every technique — is available in Dana. It's actually quite easy to use with the agentic interface. So workflows that used to take days or weeks to run, you can now run those in minutes in many cases. This just lowers the barrier to entry to building these systems.

Autonomy is still actually quite exotic in the scope of software. It's not, because of the things that we've talked about. And we've just brought that down very aggressively. It's kind of like the old adage — how do you make a great product in software? You either increase safety, convenience, or cost. We want to try to do all three of those things with Dana.

Our hope: just like kids can develop robots for their own use — and that extends to humanoids. So we're not just talking about landbased systems. You can do drones. The fact that right now writing drone software and deploying it is quite obscure and almost hobbyist — we want to just make that maybe not child's play, but like teenager play.

So this points to a world of a lot more experimentation and entrepreneurship — like agriculture bots, construction, defense. You just all of a sudden have a much larger number of people who are applying creativity and coming up with ideas and making things that move.

Yeah. Have you seen — with Claude — it's one thing just to make the engineer more efficient, or bring more people into engineering. But then when these agents really run, you're getting into — it's just like the iPhone example. You couldn't imagine Instagram before the iPhone. In 2005 on laptops, you're like, "In 10 years there's going to be this app where you can put photos." "Well, the phones don't have cameras." "Yeah, but it's going to be like social" — "What the hell?" It's hard. So we think by lowering that barrier, you're going to get way, way more creative autonomy products.

十六、教育与自主性的祛魅

I will definitely decide whether to include this or not. My kid is building autonomous bots in Factorio.

Oh, nice.

Is one of his projects. He's been rolling because the tool kit's not available yet. So he's actually training models. He's gathering data in the game. He actually has a whole army of bots that he's developed. And then his mother is like, "Why are you playing that game so much?" And he explains, of course, it's a purely educational process and experience.

Like, there's no reason autonomy should be this obscure, difficult, alchemistic technology. And not only does that have a huge impact on society, it also allows people to understand that these systems are not magic. If I can develop a Roomba for myself in my house on a weekend using Dana, then it's not suddenly so scary. That's important.

And it can support people in all kinds of ways that we haven't even imagined yet.

Absolutely. You think about folks with disabilities. We always think about humanoids as this very important task of folding laundry. So we focus on the important task, but when you allow these tools to exist — I feel so importantly that tools are what separates advanced civilizations from less advanced civilizations. Our first mark for the company was a monkey's head — and then we got a designer who said, "This is stupid." I was like, "I thought it was pretty good."

十七、Dana 的应用畅想：从人形到娱乐

So you were talking earlier about how, when the technology got so good in mobile, there was a wave of these companies — Uber, WhatsApp, Snap, Airbnb, etc. — that emerged in quick succession. And so now that the technology is getting there for the infrastructure for physical AI — what are some use cases or companies you're most excited for? What could we be talking about the equivalent of here?

Midterm — we want Dana, if not the short term, to really make humanoids way more real. There's like a thousand core tasks in a home. If you talk to people who work in these [humanoid] companies, everything is difficult every step of the way. Collecting data is difficult, cleaning that data is difficult, training those models or deploying the model is difficult. The bar being "I want a high school kid to make a humanoid" — that's our path.

There's some core ingredients that we're bringing together in Dana. We're making it way easier to actually get imitation learning to work, way easier to make reinforcement learning work in combination with that. We have pre-trained models that can be used as a baseline. World models, advanced simulation tech — all these things come together, and then you're sort of limited by your creativity. Well, what do I want to do?

If you think about any kind of physical AI task — you are understanding the world and you're manipulating something. That can be built now much more easily in this tool.

Sometimes people ask — us being a tooling company — take self-driving trucks: we deploy self-driving trucks, and many of the self-driving trucking companies use our tools. Sometimes people ask, "Oh, look, with Dana, are you going to enable all these competitors?" That's great! If you look at Google and what Google did to web applications — there was a massive internet, [and] Google still succeeded through search and YouTube and other web apps. Other folks learned and used open-source products and ultimately closed-source products and ultimately venture-backed products. We think the same thing could happen here.

I was at a robotics startup a while back that you guys know well. They were going through a training process, training one of their arms to do a particularly killer app that I thought was very appealing, which was: picking up dog poop. Training over and over again. And so — I don't know why not. Why not have the little robot follow you around when you walk the dog?

I know somebody who built a little lawn robot that would go around and individually pick up individual leaves. Because you got that problem — you rake your yard, it's completely clean, and then two hours later there's like 14 leaves and you're like, "Send up the little bot to pick up the leaves."

If development costs are zero, then people will do that. You guys remember the early iPhone apps? The hits were like the beer one, or the fart app. If you imagine that in '98 with the Symbian mobile OS, that'd be impossible. You need a team of 50 people to develop a beer thing for the BlackBerry. So there's a similar type of thing happening. We really want to be a part of that.

If you took the app store analogy — the number of kinds of bots that could be deployed in healthcare, home care, construction, all the physical trades — it's like us sitting in 2007 saying, "We should have an app store, what types of apps?" We would come up with a list of eight — a messaging one, a camera one. Now you look at the app store and there's an app for the hotel you go to, to order food off the menu.

十八、世界模型：从物理仿真到神经模拟

We were talking earlier about the differences between digital AI and physical AI. We were sort of hinting at LLMs, but world models are in vogue right now. Why don't you talk about the state of them as it relates to physical AI, and how we should think about them?

So first off — "world models" means about 100 different things. We had a team at CVPR recently, and I was joking with them about just how many different ways you can define what a world model is. When we're thinking about a world model, we're typically thinking about it in the context of a simulation — something that is effectively —

Started as a sim company. Yeah.

Yeah. Something that is sufficiently able to represent the real world, and is reactive in the sense where you can actually have, let's say, an autonomous agent that's acting in this world, and the world model is behaving appropriately in response to that autonomous agent.

Maybe, Peter, I think it's worth being super explicit here — just go one level lower. The determinism in simulators, kind of the sim-to-real gap, physics-based rendering, all the way to this generated world — where do we fit? Describe the landscape.

So, let's say simulation broadly. There's so many different ways of doing simulation. The more classical approaches of simulation are very physics-based. You can decompose physics in all different ways and all different levels of abstraction. You can simulate with sensors or without sensors. Is it just a body simulation, or are we actually simulating, for example, the light in the environment or the — almost think about the way CGI is done. If we literally had technical artists — and we have technical artists — who would create assets that would go in the simulator, which would mimic real road signs and have reflectivity and material properties that you would see in the real world. But as you guys know, Hollywood is going through its own fundamental change, and now you've generated — the same thing is happening in our universe as well.

So that's at the far end of physics-based simulation. And then the opposite end is purely neural simulation. But within that spectrum, there's many different things you can do that are each useful. One of those is a Gaussian-based simulation — where you have effectively a representation of the real world that has a 3D representation. And that 3D representation is consistent, meaning that if you have some reference point (let's say a camera), and that camera moves within that 3D world, because the Gaussian is actually representing the 3D geometry of that world, you'll actually get very high-quality output from that. There's a lot of value in that. That's let's say one type of world model.

But when you go further on that spectrum into really neural simulation, then you get into these [systems] where you're actually generating the video feeds. You can think [of it as] a neural network that's actually outputting a video as what's actually coming out of the neurons. And that can be reactive, which gives you some very interesting properties.

"Reactive" is in — the ego does something in the environment, and the other agents respond to the ego.

Exactly. However, you're not guaranteed in that reactivity that it's accurate. Now it's a question of: well, how can I align this simulation, this world model, with the real world and the way that the real world would actually react? And if you have perfect alignment between the real world and the world model, I think you've just sort of solved the universe roughly. That's impossibly difficult problem. But as we make progress towards that, it makes training physical AI models much easier because you can do more of that in simulation.

The hardest part though — we're always talking about performance. The labs, they have it easy because they can make models that are trillions of parameters, and those models can be super slow, and that's fine. But we don't have that luxury in physical AI. We deal in real time — like the actual clock real time. So we have so many milliseconds before we have to do something, and those performance constraints actually constrain the problem in a lot of ways. We can have very large models — and we do have very large models that are used in the offboard environment — but once you go onboard, all of those constraints are very real. Now we need to train a much smaller model that has these safety constraints, these determinism constraints. And that's the hard part about physical [AI]. It's also the moat — it is what makes our tooling and our competencies valuable, because it's just really hard to meet all of these constraints in a physical system.

十九、世界模型 vs. GTA6

When will you — which will we get first: a perfectly simulated real world environment for training autonomous devices, or Grand Theft Auto 6?

You know, as long as they keep putting out great trailers — I feel like I'm getting entertained without paying a dollar, and reintroduced to Tom Petty because of [the trailer].

Will you give us some timelines? So this for a second.

Go for it. Look, the whole thing with Grand Theft Auto — the big innovation was open-world sandbox gaming. So it's a simulated city, and at least in theory on that spectrum. I mean, we hire so many people out of the video game world. On that spectrum, it's absolutely real.

Well, tell us about that. What's the spectrum?

Here — this is speculation — but I think Grand Theft Auto 6 will be perhaps the last major real-world video game that's still really developed in that legacy era of traditional computer graphics tooling. Technical artists — yeah. I think Grand Theft Auto 7 will much more likely be a world-model-based video game. Where you could imagine, as AI tech evolves here, you have this concept of this video game world model, and there's some sort of baseline data store that represents the real world somehow, and then you have a translation layer that's actually turning that data store into something that you can see and run around in.

The game as a consequence could be the real world. You could have a complete recreation of the real world in the game. This has kind of happened with flight simulators, hasn't it? Isn't the most recent flight simulator literally the entire planet rendered accurately, at least from the air?

Yeah. That's where our bread and butter is. When we started the business, we hired so many people out of the Microsoft Flight Sim organization.

When you fly over New York or Duluth in the flight simulator now, it is the real city.

Exactly. But there's some tricks that they play there. A lot of that is fidelity. The real world — the more you zoom in — it stays a certain level of fidelity. The tricks that you play there: you basically are downsampling very, very aggressively. And then as you get closer, it becomes more high-fidelity. Where the real world isn't like that. If you were to try to rebuild the world with this level of fidelity, it would take all the energy of the universe. It's quite complex.

And that's probably, by the way, the best argument against us being living in a simulation. But of course, you would say, "Well, the simulator we're in doesn't follow the laws of physics that we're [seeing]." At that point, how do we know that the simulator that we're in is rendering all the stuff that we can't see?

As far as I know, everything happening outside this room doesn't even exist. I mean, Buddhism believes this. It's a different type of podcast. You know, when you open your eyes, the world is rendered, and then you close your eyes, the world [disappears] — that's literally religious.

I don't see why it's necessary for it to keep rendering if I'm not there. Buddhism from first principles. Exactly. That's what you should — that'll get a lot of clicks. That's what you need to call this.

二十、更多时间线：家务、娱乐机器人

Just on the timeline topic — you gave us timelines on self-driving cars. What timelines do you want to give us, if any, on other interesting things worth tracking — like when we'll get laundry folded, or other things that emerge because of humanoids?

And also touching a little bit on world models — where we see world models going. It's fundamental to the work we do.

So, laundry folding. It's not terribly far from being solved, to be clear. There is a lot of interesting research being [done] —

And then humanity can rejoice. That's in Proverbs 4:16, I think. Well — here, I do think housekeeping is a killer use case for physical AI.

Peter's two [obsessions] that he always talks about in the company: one is housekeeping, and it's entertainment. Peter's long on humanoid entertainment.

What kind of entertainment? I would 100% agree — I think entertainment is robotics' killer app. I don't think anybody knows [what form it takes].

I mean, like — some of these Midwest white guys are really into [it].

I'm just saying what I think. I just want to know when I go to Westworld. That's all I want.

I actually have a little tiny little Chinese robot dog that's just literally — it just roams around and it just does [things].

Would you pay to see Circus LA with robots?

Yes. Yes. I want to see kung fu trapeze swinging — spoken by like a compiler's guy here.

I know. But I want Westworld. I want Westworld.

The funny thing is I was just saying — people in the suburbs of Detroit — actually that passes the test. I bet you people in Sterling Heights would actually pay to see that. It's actually true.

I stand corrected. I stand corrected.

But back on laundry folding for a moment. It's actually not far from being folded if you remove the time constraint. If you look at the latest research videos, they'll say "play it at 8x real" or whatever. And that's for you to make it watchable. So the question is: when can you actually reach human parity of performance? That's further off.

When you decouple models from just the hardware — the hardware can do it now. That used to be a constraint. So the hardware is very fast and accurate now, which was actually — there's still overheating issues that are still being dealt with — but it's not terribly far off.

I mean, it's far off from — when I was a mechie, that was like fantasy. Like, nothing can [do this].

二十一、电影中的未来机器人愿景

What's the movie that has the most realistic future vision of robots?

Oh, man — Bicentennial Man.

Is it? Okay.

Yeah, he's realistic. Why that one? I actually haven't seen [it recently].

I like — I think it's iRobot — when Will Smith jumps in the car, and his companion's in the car, and he puts the car in manual. She's like, "What are you going to drive this thing yourself? Are you crazy?" That's Applied Intuition's goal.

Well, by the way, I haven't seen this movie in a long time — probably since it came out. So my recollection is probably a bit incorrect.

Don't worry, the internet will correct you.

But I think Bicentennial Man has fully self-driving cars. And it also has the housekeeping robot, played by Robin Williams — the friendly guy that will clean up and also babysit your kids. And it seems like it's in the not-terribly-distant future.

I got a different answer. You guys ever see that movie Moon with Sam Rockwell?

Oh, yeah.

The setup — I don't want to [spoil it]. It's a great movie. Don't watch the trailer, just watch the movie. The tagline of the movie is "250,000 miles from home, you find who you are." And it's one guy who works in an energy harvesting base run by Applied Intuition — run by "Lunar Technologies." I don't want to be Weyland-Yutani (that's from the Alien franchise) or Tyrell Corporation from Blade Runner. I want to be Lunar Technologies in the Moon franchise.

To this one guy who works on this — and the base basically runs by itself, and he's just there to mind it when things — you know, some error signal.

Yeah, the reason why it's so accurate — because the state-of-the-art for AI systems is: they just need the occasional grounding. They'll just go off and do something crazy, and then you say, "No, no, stop doing that."

LLMs are like that too. That's what coding bots are like.

And other reasons I think it's quite accurate: Kevin Spacey is the AI smiley face, and he's just there to placate the human, to assist. But like — "Oh, you seem like you're sad, Sam." That's the [dynamic]. But really, it's the one running the base.

Hopefully — I mean, I shouldn't say we want to be Lunar Technologies, because I don't know that they're quite a positive force in that [film]. But I think massive energy farms that are completely autonomous — that's going to be the future.

Everyone reacts to things like that with fear. And it's like — guys, that's amazing. That means energy costs go way down. That's an incredible positive thing.

二十二、技术进步与社会：太阳无法被遮挡

I just did this commencement speech at my undergrad.

Did you get destroyed?

No. You know what? Unlike Eric Schmidt — Yeah, yeah, listen, listen. This is the true story. My wife started watching. She said, "I feel like you're yelling at me. I can't watch this."

I basically — I'm not going to say which tech leaders — who just basically avoid it by punting and saying, "I'm not going to talk about it." I talk about this stuff. And partly it's the General Motors Institute. No one's — these are — I don't want to throw judgment on the people we recruit out of MIT and Stanford, but I say GMI people are a little different. They're pragmatic people. They understand — you don't go to a place like GMI if you believe a superficial view of what corporations do. Corporations are just people working on projects together. And by the way, people working on projects together in government, people working on projects together in non-profits — they all screw up. It's too simple to say AI corporations are terrible. And you can't say the other side, which is like, "It'll all be great."

So you have a role to play — that's basically what my message is. If it — the obvious abundance that comes from self-driving trucks, self-driving cars, and the fact that people don't die (which is amazing), and then you also get this efficiency of cheaper energy, etc. If all those things don't still satisfy your fear, then it's your responsibility as a person to really learn about that technology. You can't just say, "Well, I'm afraid of it and my reaction is shut it down." That's not [enough].

And I don't say this just to say that we're competing with the Chinese, but there's a confusion. The saying by Confucius is: "No hand can block the sun." And the sun is technological progress. If we as a society don't embrace technological progress, we will be left behind. Somebody else is going to do it. And it might — it's not [just] the Chinese. Who knows? Maybe it's an Uzbek — you know, it's another country that is recognizing, "Hey, my citizens are suffering, and I'm going to use this technology to remove [that suffering]."

It is, honestly — it's because we live in such a great society that we can have these — I would say, stupid conversations. There still are people who can't get food.

And someone will immediately quip, if they were debating me, "Well, there's plenty of food. It's the capitalist system that doesn't [distribute it]." No — let's be very specific. There's plenty of food, but getting that food to those people is difficult. So that means we should let robots get that food to them faster. That's just how it is.

So I think we as technologists — sometimes it's an inclination just to say, "Leave these people behind." I think you have to bring them along. You have to explain it to them. But we also have to treat folks like adults, and say: if you don't get it after I explain it a couple times, then you just don't get it. So there's a middle ground. It's not "everyone's an idiot," or "technology will just be perfect." There's a middle ground. Let's have that conversation to a point, and then we just move forward, and we make society better, and then the results show it.

There's people who still shockingly believe communism is the right answer. I just want to say — why? And I am a capitalist. I cannot admit that. But there's 70 years of history there. That's not even a debate anymore. If we're sitting here in 1965 and having a debate — you say, "Okay, maybe centrally controlled systems work better." There's no debate anymore, folks. Systems where individuals make decisions on their own interest actually work better for society. That doesn't mean everything is perfect. And you can't extrapolate that same thing with AI — it doesn't mean everything is going to be perfect, but net-net it's definitely going to be better.

That's roughly what my commencement speech was, without the booze. Mark and these guys were booing so they just cut it out. Eric was booing. He's throwing stuff and they just edited it out.

二十三、全球化与主权AI

You mentioned the Japan market earlier. Why don't you talk briefly about the global ambitions and how these technologies interplay?

So I think America particularly is still the most advanced in terms of — when you take account the business model. The second thing: for a company like Applied Intuition, we're an extremely global company. We work with everybody — minus, we don't have an office in China — but really everyone else on the globe. And we're a horizontal company. We're a technology provider.

I think more Silicon Valley companies can employ a little bit of what we do — which is work very collaboratively with the local economies as sovereign AI becomes more of a real thing. We have to build businesses that take that into account. By the way, we're not the first ones to do this. If you look at the history of America, you read the history of Standard Oil — you'll see that was the history of companies. You'd work internationally. Aramco is not a random company — you build based on the real geopolitical realities of the time.

I think we've navigated it quite well. I've lived in Japan, I lived in Germany, I lived in Dubai. Being Pakistani by birth — I think that's also influenced our company. Peter's only lived in Michigan and here, but he is a German. So innately we're more — we think about the globe more.

I was always surprised, both at Google and at YC, at how almost myopic the companies are — just always looking at the market that's just within the 30 [miles] between San Jose and San Francisco. Actually, the market is really big. Physical AI — the nature of it being physical — I think we have to be a very international company. And I think we've had a lot of success being very international.

Yeah. Cool. I think it's a good place to wrap.

Okay. Peter, Kazer — thanks so much for coming on the podcast, and congrats on the big launch with Dana.

Yeah, thanks for having us.

Awesome. Great to see you.

Great.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的反直觉真相：**"物理AI 的最大敌人不是技术，而是律师的调色板"**
Kasar 讲了一个让我拍案的细节：GM 内部的安全评级系统不用红黄绿，而用"紫色"或"品红"——因为一旦上法庭，"你放行了标红的系统"会成为致命证据，而"品红"就能在庭审中制造歧义空间。
这是整段访谈里最刺眼的观察：**决定自动驾驶何时能落地的，不是神经网络的参数量，而是律师写给工程师的颜色词典。** Cruise 之死不是技术败于物理世界，而是败于"未能与政府按正确节奏共舞"——他把这一步类比为《好家伙》最后一幕黑帮开会。
这里藏着一个跨领域连接：**汽车行业的"品红"，与金融行业的"标准差单位"、医疗行业的"不良事件报告"、AI 行业的"model card"是同一物种——都是在诉讼/监管高压下演化出的语义防御层。** 一个组织越成熟，它的语言就越多"品红"。这也解释了为什么 Waymo 慢——它继承了 Alphabet 的法务基因，而 Tesla 快——它把"品红"直接绕过成了"beta"。
## 二、出人意料的模式联系：**物理AI 与"McDonald's 招不到人"是同一条曲线

item.title: [Business of Sport] Rebuilding the Reputation of Athletics | Seb Coe
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"镁光弹"经济学：为什么博尔特之后不是"下一个博尔特"，而是"投资组合"

Coe 用了一个特别精准的词形容博尔特——magnesium flare（镁光弹）。这是一个来自军事照明弹的比喻：短暂、极亮、把整片黑夜照白，但它本质上是一次性的，无法用来做持续照明。

有意思的是，Coe 的应对策略不是"寻找下一颗镁光弹"，而是悄悄把田径的明星经济学从单点爆发切换到了投资组合：Noah Lyles 的社媒、Duplantis 与 Sony 的音乐合约、Kipyegon 与 McLaughlin 的多元人格……

这几乎是 1:1 复刻了唱片工业 2015 年之后的转型——从"造一个 Michael Jackson"到"运营一个 Spotify 播放列表"。Coe 没有明说，但他实际上把田径从"巨星驱动"重构成了"IP 组合驱动"。一个真正被忽略的观察：Coe 从来没试图重建博尔特神话——他在重建的是承担博尔特功能的整套系统。 单一超级 IP 依赖曾经是拳击的诅咒（阿里之后拳击花了 30 年才恢复），Coe 显然读过那本失败教科书。

二、莫迪的悖论：印度总理和印度电信运营商站在了对立面

访谈里最容易被忽略的一句话，是 Coe 引用莫迪说的："让年轻人放下手机（off their mobile phones）。"

这背后藏着一个奇怪的对齐：一国总理的健康目标，和该国最大产业（数字经济、电信、短视频）的商业目标是直接冲突的。 Coe 半开玩笑说"印度电信运营商不会同意"，但他其实在暗示一个更深的定位：

田径正在把自己重新品牌成"反注意力经济"的物理解药。

这是一个惊人的商业转向——过去 20 年，所有体育联盟都在争抢屏幕时间（NBA 做短视频、F1 做 Netflix 纪录片、英超做抖音）；而 Coe 却在悄悄押注一个反向叙事：田径卖的不是屏幕内容，而是让人离开屏幕的理由。 这和瑜伽产业、冷水浸泡、"多巴胺戒断"这些消费趋势属于同一条隐藏赛道——Coe 可能是唯一意识到田径可以卡进"wellness 经济"而不只是"sports 经济"的联盟主席。

三、"三夜赛制"的真正意图：这不是电视改革，是日历殖民

The Ultimate（三夜 × 三小时）看起来是给电视做的短平快版本，但如果你把它放在 Coe 描述的四年周期里看——两届世锦赛 + 一届奥运会 + 一个"空档年"——你会发现真正的战略动作是：

Coe 把体育日历当成了地产。

那个"空档年"是一块无人认领的黄金地皮，Coe 冲上去插了一面旗。这不是电视格式创新，这是日历殖民（calendar colonization）——他很清楚，如果田径不占领这个时间段，网球（ATP Finals）、F1（收官站）、甚至新兴的沙特体育项目会占领它。

四、一个几乎没人注意到的结构性矛盾

Coe 说了一个数字，值得单独拎出来：东京世锦赛 100 米决赛的 8 位选手中，只有 3 人年收入超过 10 万美元。

参考一下——一个上到 ATP Tour 前 100 名的网球运动员，最低奖金池起步就是六位数。也就是说，田径的"顶级"经济学不如网球的"腰部"经济学。

这里有一个 Coe 没有明说但暗含的判断：田径参与人数最多（10 亿跑者）、覆盖国家最广（83 个国家进决赛）、奥运奖牌最难拿——每一个"最"的维度都指向"这应该是最有价值的体育资产"。但商业变现却在食物链底端。

这几乎是教科书级别的"参与度—货币化"背离案例，类似于： - 维基百科（人类知识第一入口，收入不如一个中型 SaaS） - 播客（覆盖 5 亿听众，广告规模不如户外广告牌） - 开源软件（跑着一半的互联网，开发者拿不到钱）

真正的启示是：Coe 不是在"重建田径的声誉"，他是在解决一个跨领域的元问题——当你的产品是全人类共享的公共品时，怎么给它安装一个私人商业模型？ 这才是访谈标题背后的真问题。

📝 转录文本

Opening — Why This Conversation Matters

I've always found that the arguments against paying the athletes properly, they've often been propagated by people who've never really worried about where the next meal's coming from. I need them to know, as a federation president, that those sponsors come to the table because they know we've got athletes that they want to get behind.

Usain was bigger, in a way, than track and field. He's our ultimate ambassador. We've got more athletes now in the top 20 in terms of global visibility than we've ever had. I do say to athletes: look, your passport is your performance, but the journey is in large part about your personality.

A lot of people assume that if you are an Olympic winner, you are catapulted into the same echelon as someone that wins a golf major or a tennis major. You're not. If you look at the 100 meter finalists, Tokyo last year, their prize money alone — it is probably only three of those finalists that are over $100,000 a year.

Warm-Up: A Family Reunion in the Hot Seat

Seb, it is so good to have you in the hot seat. We've known each other for years — it's sort of a family gathering really, isn't it? I wouldn't have anticipated this 10 years ago, sitting having Sunday lunch. I have to remember you with your chicken sliders and your baked beans, and I remember going to Lantico with you — yeah, pre-Chelsea game, for anyone that doesn't know this.

I was thinking the last time we were all with microphones in front of us was at Mum's wedding. I think I had to say a few words, as did Harry — went off script, actually, that wedding speech. It was a perfect prep for doing this show, because you have all this stuff nicely put out and then you give it to him and he wouldn't read any of it and say anything that was on it. Hopefully I won't get cancelled after this one.

But no, it's great to have you in. It's about time. No, I'm very happy to — and congratulations, guys. You've made a big impact in this world. Thank you. It's been a lot of fun.

Athletics as One of Two Truly Global Sports

We've done a bit of athletics, but when I was listening to some of the stuff you've done before, there was just one thing that you said a few times and it was great: it's one of two global sports. Why?

Well, if you just look at what we do and you look at the number of member federations — i.e. countries — that are actively involved within our own structure at World Athletics, there are two global sports. I think we have to concede, particularly at this moment with the World Cup, it's football, and track and field.

Africa — extraordinary powerhouse for our sport now, played an involvement in track and field in pretty much every of the 50-odd countries there. And if you look at the global numbers, more people do what we do — which is running — than any other participatory sport. There are over a billion people. In the space of 10, 12 days, they identify themselves as participant runners.

And just to finish off — if you're looking at the universality of the sport, off the Tokyo World Championships in '25, just literally a few months ago now, we had 83, 84 countries came out of those championships with a top-eight finish or a finalist. There's no other sport that gets even remotely close to that.

And statistically, at an Olympic Games, winning a medal in track and field is the hardest gig of them all, because you've got so many countries competing, so many people, because you've got so many countries sending 100-meter runners or 1500-meter runners into that landscape.

So that's why I say that our sport is practiced everywhere in the world. Over the course of this year, I've been to Oceania for their congress and their championships, I've been to Africa, I will by the end of the year be at all the European gatherings and the Asian gatherings towards the end of the year. We've got the Asian Games there.

Beyond the Field of Play — Modi, Health and the Attention Economy

It's an extraordinarily impactful sport, and it goes way, way beyond the field of play, because governments across the globe are also using it to support some of their alignments around health and education.

I sat down with Narendra Modi a year ago. What was the biggest ambition he was setting himself in India, outside of the normal challenges that a country faces? He wants more young people fit and active, and — his words, not mine — off their mobile phones. I'm not sure that some of India's mobile providers would sort of share that.

But there is a growing acceptance that the sport that is probably most suited to doing that is athletics — because it's a low-cost entry point, it's accessible, it's truly global, and frankly, if you have a body, you're an athlete.

The Job of Running World Athletics — The Holy Grail Question

So you've been running World Athletics for over 10 years. With all of that that you've just outlined, what is the job? How do you balance where you can be most valuable and what you need to do?

You've mentioned participation there — there's a commercial element, there's a viewership element. It's brilliant having all of these people running, as we'll see on the streets. How do I make them a fan of the sport? How do I get them engaged in it? How do I ensure that kids coming through actually want to compete and see a viable career in it? Where do you come into this, Charlie?

It's the holy grail of all questions, and every sport is trying to figure that out — and we do it on a daily basis. The International Olympic Committee are going through a new initiative created by the new president, Kirsty Coventry, called Fit for the Future. Everybody is trying to figure out how they keep their sport front and center, that they are engaging with new audiences — and many spectators that may not even realize that it's a sport that has something for them — and that remains relevant, salient and exciting in the lives of young people.

"The Ultimate" — Rebuilding the Format for Television

How do we do that? Well, we do it across a number of initiatives that we're running. We've got a global event at the end of this year called The Ultimate, and that is a really quite a big departure from what we've done in the past. And again, it's unashamedly designed for television.

Our traditional world championships are over nine days, and it's a complex, lengthy process both in bringing them to the table and getting them operationally up and running. Tokyo was a great example — probably our most commercial world championships of all time. We sold 630,000 tickets over nine days of competition.

But The Ultimate at the end of this year is a very different format. It's effectively a world championships in three nights, and it's only three hours a night. And it's not every athlete, and it's not every discipline in the field and on the track. It is really aimed at unlocking new and younger audiences, but being in a way traditional enough and holding on to things that people would recognize — that our traditional fans that have grown up on the sport would recognize.

But it's a recognition of a mantra I've always driven, and that is: our activity is sport, our business is entertainment. And we've got to do more in that space.

Why This Format, Why Now — The Missing Year in the Olympic Cycle

So these are all the things that we're challenging ourselves to do at the moment. And the reason we've gone for this format is that over a four-year cycle — which, well, it is the Olympic cycle — you have two world championships, you have an Olympic Games, but we have a year… I don't say nothing happens, because there's a Commonwealth Games and there's a European Championships in it. But we have one year where we don't, at the most broadcastable moment in that year, have your famous billion eyeballs on the sport. And we can't have a year where we don't do that.

So we looked at that. We thought: what can we put into that? Let's not just make it another world championships — let's really test ourselves and do this differently. And it's a very different model, where the host city pretty much picks up almost the entirety of the cost. We take a hosting fee, but we then give them all the uptick on the tickets, on broadcast, on sponsorship. So there's a real incentive for them to be involved in that. And we're going back to Budapest, which was of course the scene of our hugely successful '23 world championships.

Personalities — Why Bolt Was Bolt, and What Comes Next

Is it not quite obvious that we just lack personalities? Why is Usain Bolt Usain Bolt? Yeah, because he's the fastest man on earth — but if he had the personality of Sinner or Djokovic, people wouldn't care so much.

Look, Usain is Usain. He is to us what Muhammad Ali was to boxing — and far bigger than boxing. And Usain was bigger, in a way, than track and field. He's our ultimate ambassador. In Budapest, I sat with him — just the other day at Wimbledon we were chatting — he's very focused on that and really wants to give back.

But actually, we've got more athletes now in the top 20 in terms of global visibility than we've ever had. And you're right in one respect. It's one of the mantras that I always try, without sounding too dogmatic or lectury about it — but I do say to athletes: look, your passport is your performance, but the journey is in large part about your personality. And you do have to fill a room, and you do have to communicate, and don't shy away from the media. They're going to be tiresome and irritable on occasions, but they are the way that you unlock interest in our sport.

If you were asking me — as you could have done 10 years ago, when Usain started leaving our sport — it was the same question that probably the boxing fraternity were getting in the 70s: "Oh my god, what are we going to do? There's no Muhammad Ali." Well, the reality of it is you're never going to replace a Muhammad Ali, and you're probably not going to replace a Usain Bolt, just simply because of the stature and everything that he achieved.

But we do have Noah Lyles, who actually has a huge social media following. We've got Mondo Duplantis, who is a rock star in our sport — he's on Spotify and got contracts with Sony and producing music as well. So there are many, many more athletes in different disciplines that are capturing the attention of the media than we did when we had Usain there.

Usain was the magnesium flare, but we've now got a lot more stuff going on around that. Mondo, Noah, Faith Kipyegon, Sydney McLaughlin, Femke — they're all big names.

The Calendar Problem — Extending the Season

But what you're doing here as well, and making sure that the calendar is consistently got events around it that can showcase these talents — you're giving them the opportunity to be consistently in the public eye. We had this conversation not when Sebastian Sarway broke the marathon record. I was saying, you know, as amazing as it is, I would love to — if I hadn't just said his name now — I'd love to go back to the audience and say: how many of you can remember his name? Who won the marathon? And where do you next see him run?

And that is of course a challenge, and one of the things that we're addressing at the moment at World Athletics. We've been discussing it — well, discussing it but acting on it — for some time. Our season is relatively short. We're a 12-month-of-the-year sport, because we have cross country, we have road, we have marathons, we have indoors, obviously outdoors, we have junior events.

But if you look at the seasons for somebody like Mondo — yes, Mondo will do an indoors — but effectively, the public are following Mondo from June through to September. And we've got to extend that. That isn't a long enough season and a long enough focus for fans, both in and those fans you want to suck into your sport. That's too short. And certainly for a professional sport.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"镁光弹"经济学：为什么博尔特之后不是"下一个博尔特"，而是"投资组合"
Coe 用了一个特别精准的词形容博尔特——**magnesium flare（镁光弹）**。这是一个来自军事照明弹的比喻：短暂、极亮、把整片黑夜照白，但它本质上是**一次性的**，无法用来做持续照明。
有意思的是，Coe 的应对策略不是"寻找下一颗镁光弹"，而是悄悄把田径的明星经济学从**单点爆发**切换到了**投资组合**：Noah Lyles 的社媒、Duplantis 与 Sony 的音乐合约、Kipyegon 与 McLaughlin 的多元人格……
这几乎是 1:1 复刻了**唱片工业 2015 年之后的转型**——从"造一个 Michael Jackson"到"运营一个 Spotify 播放列表"。Coe 没有明说，但他实际上把田径从"巨星驱动"重构成了"IP 组合驱动"。**一个真正被忽略的观察：Coe 从来没试图重建博尔特神话——他在重建的是承担博尔特功能的整套系统。** 单一超级 IP 依赖曾经是拳击的诅咒（阿里之后拳击花了 30 年才恢复），Coe 显然读过那本失败教科书。

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] The 2028 Natural Gas Crisis No One Sees Coming
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最深的洞察：这不是能源危机，是"信念结构"的时间套利

表面看这是天然气供需失衡的故事，但真正令人震撼的是Matt揭示的认知层的错配：物理世界里，2028年的短缺几乎已成定局（LNG合同、井筒物理、管道审批周期都是刚性的），但金融世界里，2028年之后的天然气远期曲线仍然是"平的"。

这是一次由"复盘偏差"造成的集体幻觉：过去15年美国人被页岩革命宠坏了，形成"天然气永远充裕"的直觉锚点。市场并非不理性，而是被"最近15年的样本"锁死了先验分布。这跟2022年前的欧洲天然气市场、2020年前的芯片产能、2007年前的房价预期是同一种认知病理——用"稳态期"的方差去定价"相变期"的风险。

真正的机会不在天然气本身，而在"人们何时会醒过来"。Matt说的"6个月内曲线会开始移动"——他实际上在交易一个认知拐点的期权，而不是一个商品。

二、令人意外的跨领域连接：AI训练的Scaling Law，正在撞上地质学的Decline Curve

这里有一个几乎没人注意到的深层反讽——

AI界最神圣的规律是Scaling Law：投入越多算力，模型能力指数级提升。这是一条向上凸的曲线，象征着"未来无限"。

而天然气井有一条同样铁律的曲线：Decline Curve（页岩气井产量的陡峭下滑曲线，前两年可衰减60-70%）。这是一条向下凹的曲线，象征着"物质世界的熵"。

整个AI繁荣，本质上是把一条向上凸的数学曲线，架在一条向下凹的物理曲线上。

更深一层：Scaling Law属于"信息领域"，边际成本趋近于零；Decline Curve属于"物质领域"，边际成本随枯竭上升。当二者被强行耦合（每GW算力对应150 MMcf/day的Bloom燃料电池），你其实是在用信息经济学的乐观去支付物质经济学的账单。

Matt说的"能源占超算成本从10%变成30%"——这不是数字问题，是范式冲突：软件时代的商业模式（零边际成本、指数增长）正在被强行拽回工业时代的成本结构里。

这或许解释了另一个谜团——为什么Sam Altman同时投核聚变、投Helion、说"智能的价格取决于能源的价格"。他知道这场战争的真正战场不在GPU，在原子。

三、最颠覆的系统洞察："BYOG"是一场分布式的公地悲剧

超大规模厂被要求"Bring Your Own Generation"（自带发电）看似聪明——每家解决自己的问题。但从系统层面看，这是教科书级的公地悲剧的翻版：

  • 每个hyperscaler独立部署Bloom燃料电池、Caterpillar柴油机、Solar Turbine——局部最优
  • 但每一台设备都从同一个共享的天然气储库里抽气
  • 没有任何单一决策者需要为"集体总抽气量"负责
  • 每一次press release都在给集体账单上加一笔——"just think more gas"

更狠的一层：这些"分布式"燃气机组的热效率远低于大型联合循环电厂（高热耗率 = 更多气才能出同样电）。也就是说，"时间到电力"的紧迫感，正在制造结构性的能源效率倒退——用最浪费的方式烧最紧缺的气。

这跟疫情期间"每家囤卫生纸"、跟半导体产业"每家抢产能"、跟18世纪英国公地放牧是同一个博弈论骨骼。区别只是这次赌桌上是全国电价。

四、隐藏的政治时间炸弹：2028恰好是大选年

Matt反复提到"middle of 2028开始击穿历史储气底部"——这个时间点值得单独观察一下：

2028年11月是美国下一次总统大选。

想象这个剧本：夏天（7-8月储气该补库）时Henry Hub从$3.5冲到$10+，居民电费飙升30-50%，同时新闻天天报"美国LNG正在送往日本和欧洲"。这将成为保护主义与AI监管最完美的政治催化剂。

Matt作为投资人只谈了"contract law复杂"，但历史告诉我们——当消费者电费和地缘政治合同冲突时，政客从不选择合同。1973-74年尼克松就撕过能源出口协议。这意味着：

  • 长期LNG合同看起来是"稳定现金流"，实际上藏着巨大的政治期权空头
  • 日本韩国台湾的电力效用公司正在无意识地做多"美国政治稳定性"
  • 更微妙的：这可能成为倒逼AI监管的完美借口——不是因为AI危险，而是因为它抢了老百姓的电

五、给你的操作性思考：三个"反直觉"押注

  1. 住宅光伏正在从"意识形态商品"变成"避险资产"——Matt说的最反常识的一点。当电网午间峰值电价飙升，屋顶光伏第一次变成纯粹的经济计算而非环保表态。这是一场没人预期的角色转换。

  2. 核电股的真正催化剂不是AI，是天然气短缺——大家在讲"AI需要核电"，但Matt指出真正的机制是：天然气必须先痛，人们才会认真建核电。所以Cameco、BWXT、Westinghouse的最佳买点，可能恰恰是天然气恐慌爆发之后，而不是之前。

  3. Expand Energy的4x EBITDA是"认知折价"而非"基本面折价"——市场用平的远期曲线为它定价。如果Matt是对的，这不是"低估的股票"，而是"错定价的期权"。区别在于：股票会缓慢重估，期权会突然重估。

要不要我继续深挖某个方向？比如： - 住宅光伏+电池 在你个人层面（如果你在美国）的经济学 - 认知拐点交易的历史案例研究（DRAM、铀、铁矿石都走过这条路） - AI公司真正应该问自己的三个问题（Matt结尾提到的counterparty risk框架）

📝 转录文本

The 2028 Natural Gas Crisis No One Sees Coming

频道：Invest Like The Best | 嘉宾：Matt

开场：一个惊人的结论

Natural gas is well supplied today, 26 to 27. When you get to the middle of 2028, we start to break very materially below in a historical way where gas available in storage has ever been before. And by 2029, we drop below all known historical storage evidence.

You're going to start to see a knife fight to secure natural gas physical in 28 like we really haven't seen before. And the biggest losers of this would be the US consumer.

[music]

主持人引入

So Matt, the last time we did this was I think during COVID — kind of crazy that it's been 6 years. I've always loved talking to you about energy markets. You've been working in this space for 20 years. You're about as encyclopedic on this stuff as anyone I've ever met.

But you've also been acutely studying the current energy situation in the US — rebuilding, sort of in a way that you'll describe, from the well-level up, a picture of what's happening especially as AI is creating all this new demand through data centers etc, of what is going on over the last 18 months of concerted effort.

You've reached fascinating and somewhat scary conclusions. I'd love you to just start with a conclusion and then we're going to talk through how you came to this conclusion, who the winners might be, the losers might be, what's to be done about it. But before we get deep into all the component parts, just tell us what you found after 18 months of study.

一、核心结论：历史性的天然气赤字正在逼近

We are headed into a place where we see a historic deficit in natural gas supply available in the United States, which which does portend some pretty serious consequences.

Natural gas, which is over 40% of US power generation, has become or is imminently going to become the most important fuel in the country. It's overtaking petroleum given the amount that we use now for generation.

Our work suggests that 26–27 natural gas is appropriately supplied. But as we get into 28, and you plug in this compute and you assign gas to very specific assets as they're plugged in as well, and you continue to export LNG as we're planning to do with known projects, we start to eat into our working gas storage — which is the nexus of supply and demand in the country.

I think we will come to the conclusion that the upside risk price of natural gas is both unbounded and convex. Where you will feel it the most acutely will be electricity prices in 28, 29, 2030 based on our work.

这仅仅是数据中心的问题吗？

主持人： How much of this is just attributable to data centers — like, just purely we're building a lot more data centers, that's just for AI — like is it that simple, or is there something else going on as well?

Matt： The die was cast long before AI compute came to the scene.

二、页岩革命之后的天然气版图

If I may set the stage a little bit — US gas was plentiful starting in about 2010 when shale started to really change, come to the scene and change things. We had been importing natural gas to satisfy consumption on top of what we produced domestically. Shale started to be very productive, surprised to the upside, and became this abundant source.

And as natural gas became more abundant, we started to export it. Starting with Cheniere, we've gone from that early Cheniere exporting to today we're exporting about 15 BCF a day of nameplate US export capacity.

Now that 15 billion cubic feet is on a base of about 110 to 112 BCF a day of natural gas production in the US. So if you think about it, it's become about 12 to 15% of the US daily ability to supply the market.

LNG 出口的加速

As this abundance continued, more and more facilities projects have been announced. As of today, we're scheduled to export up to 35 BCF a day by the end of 2030 — and in that case the die has been mostly cast.

To build an LNG project you need various approvals. They're project financed, you site and permit many, many years in advance. Most of these projects that get you from 15 or 16 BCF a day nameplate to 35 are well on their way. That's the primary incremental demand driver in the country over the last 10 years, and will be at least for the next five.

需求侧的转折点

We had moderate population growth during the 2010s and teens into the 2020s. We went through a period of stagnating electricity demand — energy efficiency and some other things driving down electricity demand. You had more demand for gas-driven generation, but it's really in the recent past where compute has started to pull incrementally. Before that, you had LNG as the main driver of demand.

三、供需缺口的量化

Now let's put those together.

I just shared that we're going to go from about 15 to 35 BCF a day of incremental LNG exports. After evaluating every producing gas well and the entire pipeline and processing and gathering system, we have the capacity to add about 20 BCF a day of gas production.

Even without AI compute, we had sources and uses matched between our ability to deliver new natural gas from Appalachia, Haynesville, Permian, and that which is supposed to leave the door through LNG.

AI 算力的叠加

Now, in AI compute — so many different power-generating ideas in order to power compute, given this value of "time to power" that folks talk about so much. It sort of goes from the large-scale most efficient assets — which are Geova combined cycle — all the way down through the distributed generation assets, which we'll call fuel cells (we'll adieu Solar Turbine or Caterpillar solar turbines). There are various local field-level behind-the-meter assets that are also relevant.

We have had to assign, with an outside partner, probabilities to all of this stuff.

P50 基准情境

So we'll start with our base case, which we'll call P50 — everything with a probability of 50% or more (50% being: they have some approvals, they have usually a PPA — someone planning to buy power from them under contract — they usually have some sort of interconnection agreement or they're in process with an interconnection agreement). Those are the assets we've taken seriously in our base case.

When you do that, it's about 5 BCF a day of very credible incremental natural gas demand associated with mostly AI compute.

Now importantly, there are multiples of what we are considering seriously in our base case that have been proposed that will consume natural gas. Every solution today involves more natural gas.

6-series Bloom Energy — kind of latest-gen fuel cell — will take 150 million cubic feet a day of gas per gigawatt. The market has been assigning a high probability on them obtaining 2 gigawatts a year of manufacturing capacity, and that's likely to ramp to 5 gigawatts, for which there isn't gas unless you take it from something else.

主持人： In the extreme case, how high does that number get?

Matt： If you start to move it down and say P30 or P20, that number can more than double and be 12 to 15 BCF a day by the early 2030s if unmitigated.

四、为什么不能"直接关掉出口"？

主持人： One naive way to approach this is to say, like, this doesn't sound like that big of a deal. Like — 12 new [BCF] in the extreme case, just shut off the exports. Like, who cares? We didn't export natural gas for a long time. People domestically are not going to tolerate skyrocketing energy prices, especially when they think the simple solution to this is just to stop shipping it out of the country and just use it for ourselves. Like, why is the solution not just shut off exports?

Matt： It's more complicated than that.

Contract law. There are rules. There are really good reasons why we're exporting, and these projects have tens of billions of project financing and contracts that are attached to or associated with these LNG projects. As the US will be about a third of global gas supply in several years, our allies and other FTA and increasingly non-FTA countries are reliant on…

主持人： What's FTA?

Matt： Free trade agreement.

So the answer is that it's both: because it's a third of the global supply that's really important for the rest of the world, and domestically there's just contracts and investments — and it could be stopped, but it'd be very complicated.

建模的出发点

Let's step back for a moment. You have to have a starting place for a base case, which typically starts with signed contracts. What do the words on the page say? What's allowed? What's not allowed?

When we set out to build the firm, we've had about 16-plus months to start to model almost every asset at the atomic level. Along the way, there are numerous constraints and rules and regulations and contracts. When we set out to build this, it was about acknowledging those constraints for what they are, assuming that contract law would be followed.

Then as we go through and build, all of this can flex up and down based on the choice to send less LNG out the border or out of our terminals, for instance — or slow AI compute growth, which is one other solution which we're not really willing to propose because we know that there's insatiable demand. It's not popular to say "slow AI compute growth," but to the extent that would happen, that would be another lever to reduce the pull or strain we expect in the system as the decade goes along.

[广告间歇：Ramp / Rogo / WorkOS]

Ramp is the only platform built to make your finance team leaner, faster, and better, saving businesses 5% annually on average, so you can stay focused on growth. Ramp customers grew revenue 3.2 times faster than the average American business. Visa, Vercel, Cursor, Stripe, Notion, 11 Labs, Shopify, and 70,000 other businesses all run on Ramp. Mine does too, and so should yours. Learn more at ramp.com/invest.

Felix by Rogo is a personal finance agent that turns a single prompt into finished client-ready work using your firm's own templates, context, and standards. Send Felix an email like: "Take these comments and turn them for me," or "update my tracker with the context of these emails," or "run the ability-to-pay math on this buyer," and Felix sends back finished PowerPoint decks, Excel models, and sourced research. Felix works the way your team already does, delivering work quickly and accurately around the clock. Learn more at rogo.ai/felix.

The best AI and software companies from OpenAI to Cursor to Perplexity use WorkOS to become enterprise ready overnight, not in months. Visit workos.com to skip the unglamorous infrastructure work and focus on your product.

五、地下有多少气？——资源 vs. 交付能力

主持人： It's your view that in the bad-to-worst case scenarios, this is like a full-blown crisis. This is not a small thing. This is like the story in the country.

So underneath the United States right now or North America, there's a certain amount of gas just objectively. And I'm trying to understand — how much of this is that we are literally going to run out of the gas that's under the ground, versus it's just a problem of how quickly we can find out where it is, get it out economically, process it, store it, transmit it, use it, etc.?

Matt： Starting with what we'll call resource in the ground —

There's tremendous data availability. We can measure where we are in the exploitation of most of the major gas-producing basins. It would be:

  • Appalachia — primarily the Marcellus plus the Utica
  • Haynesville — a key swing basin
  • Permian — and to a lesser extent the Eagle Ford, which are oil-directed plays where the decision to drill and produce is driven by oil, and gas is a byproduct

In each of these plays there are some stacked pays or zones where well penetrations' output can be measured with a lot of data. When you digitize the acreage controlled by each one of these companies with polygon shapes that use a bunch of lat-longs to drop in and associate a well with an area that's controlled, you can figure out what's left.

The reality is: there is gas. As part of our analysis we've produced the gas that is logically captured and can be produced from wells from existing acreage positions of all these companies. There is gas. We're assuming it gets developed here. That's how you get to our 20 BCF a day of growth. But there are other constraints.

主持人： It's unbelievably cool that we can literally know at this precision what is underneath the ground — often deep underneath in hard-to-reach places. It's a technology story. But it doesn't sound like the actual problem is that we are literally running out of the stuff underneath the ground. We've also had a history of just finding new stuff that we didn't know existed before. So it sounds like the problem is more our ability to serve the demand in this kind of time frame, not that we're literally going to run out of the resource over the next 20 years.

存量 vs. 流量：一个关键区分

Matt： So, it's a little more complicated than that.

We get through most of the existing captured inventory of companies in the next four or five years. When you bring on a new well, it has a decline rate, and each well as it's stacked on an existing companywide portfolio decline… a lot of these companies' decline curves are maturing.

主持人： They're pretty steep in natural gas, right?

Matt： It's steep initially in natural gas, but Expand Energy and others have such mature portfolios, the replacement is less costly today than it would have been 5 years ago.

When you stack all of these wells based on existing acreage on these companies, assuming they're going to drill optimally based on their forward curve (which is depressed and we'll talk about that), you get to this 128, 130, 132 BCF a day of maximum deliverability.

We are assuming that all of these companies develop the rest of their acreage.

主持人： But that's a flow metric, not a stock metric.

Matt： It's a flow metric. What is possible when you use known well performance parameters to maximize production before you get to midstream and other surface-level constraints — which we'll talk about.

更高价格能解锁更多资源吗？

There's resource. We are depleting the known resource. If you were to assume prices go up meaningfully, you may unlock additional basins that are legacy known basins. We know a lot about most of the rock in the US. There are other known gas basins, but they have been uneconomic. And furthermore, there isn't infrastructure to really accelerate drilling and activity in those basins.

This is [not] software — you don't press a button to solve this.

常见反驳：库存真的够吗？

A common pushback as we have gone through this is: "there's plenty of resource available to us in the Permian. There's plenty of resource in Appalachia." A number of companies describe themselves as having a lot more inventory of wells to drill than we can justify with facts. And I'll just leave it at that.

When folks meet with companies, they should ask to understand exact engineered locations on a map — where do they have not just the ability to produce, but plans to have infrastructure on the surface to allow it to flow, for instance? And the ability within financial parameters to invest and produce the resource in the ground?

We're fairly far along in understanding it. We've accounted for all of the major productive basins in the country, and I do not think we're likely to be surprised by some new major shale find. At this point, knowledge of those things is pretty mature.

主持人： So to say it back — there's a lot of resource, but at this rate, we're depleting the known resources quite quickly.

Matt： We are advanced in depleting the known resources. Especially as we move to the next layer, which is infrastructure.

六、地面基础设施：处理、集气、干线管道

主持人： There's a bigger longer-term question about, is this just a parenthetical period of time between 2010 and now when we were awash in gas? Prior to that, we really weren't, and maybe after this, we really won't be. Let's talk now about: OK, we get gas out of the ground. It still has to go get processed and transmitted and used. What are the most important rate limiters in that part of the equation?

1. 处理（Processing）

In some cases it's processing — the natural gas flows to the surface with natural gas liquids embedded therein. In some cases there's sulfur or nitrogen that has to be dealt with. In some cases it comes with oil, so you have to have surface-level infrastructure to produce the oil, which is different.

Gas primarily has to be produced into a pipeline system, and there's a certain spec on regulated pipelines — kind of 1030 BTU is the spec. So you have to remove enough of these other hydrocarbons to get it to pipeline spec to be able to produce it into the system to be consumed by folks downstream.

Processing is the first major constraint. There are a couple of basins with a little bit of extra processing. We'll fill it up pretty quickly. We do not have processing yet to get to our assumed 20 BCF production target that's necessary.

Processing would be something it takes 2 or 3 years to build at the midpoint. We generally know what processing investments are being made and projects have been announced by Permian processors or by Appalachian processors. We know where the materials- and liquids-handling throughput capacity will be in 27–28 at least.

You really would need, in the near term, even to get to our 20 BCF of incremental gas production we're willing to estimate, you need to have more processing built.

主持人： Build stuff now.

Matt： Build it now, imminently.

2. 集气（Gathering）

Gathering — small-diameter pipes. Gathering is what takes it from the wellhead to processing or the pipeline system. There is a fair amount of disclosure around processing systems being expanded and built, and we would posit — we have put all those on a map on top of every one of these wells at their lat-longs — in order to grow even a fraction of where we must have natural gas production go in the US, gathering has to be invested in very materially over the next imminently to get to the place where we can achieve 130 BCF/day of production in the US.

3. 干线管道（Interstate Pipelines）

The last one is interstate gas pipeline system. And this is where I come back to your answer on LNG — lots of rules and regulations around these things. Pipelines are monopolies for the most part. Local distribution companies that deliver gas to your stove — those are monopolies or oligopolies.

In the last 10 or 12 years we've really built one interstate gas pipeline — that was Mountain Valley Pipeline, connecting Appalachia to Mid-Atlantic. The various environmental permitting, the regime changes…

主持人： Things are a nightmare to build. Yeah.

Matt： It's been made very difficult to build interstate gas pipelines. This administration has been trying to reduce the barriers to building interstate gas pipes. We've started to see some more progress to that end. But there is an urgency to build more connectivity to wield gas around the country to serve this incremental AI compute load if it's going to happen. That's necessary.

七、如果什么都不做，2030 年会是什么样？

主持人： Before we keep going through this sequence, can you just say what you think the state will be? Let's assume there's roughly inertia in the system and nobody listens to this. Nobody does it. I think hopefully some people, a lot of people will listen to this and have ideas. But what is going to happen in the world? What will the state of the world be like in 2030 if none of this starts getting addressed sooner than later?

Matt： There is a tremendous inertia around natural gas being the primary fuel to power AI.

The market has been focused on understanding the power shortage and trying to solve that and generation generally — power generation, which could be solar and batteries, wind, nuclear (whether large scale or small modular reactors — SMRs), or natural gas.

Natural gas is well supplied today, 26 and 27. The result is that nobody is investing in gas. In fact, EQT is shutting in natural gas right now because they think it'll be more valuable later. The rig count, the things that we can see real-time to figure out if the market is on to this tightness in 2029 or 2030 —

主持人： No one's on it.

Matt： It's not apparent today. It's perpetuating this view that most Americans have — which is: there's plenty of natural gas, because for 15 years it's all we've been [seeing] — abundance.

复杂性的沉睡

There's a complacency that's developed, and we think that complacency is going to take us right up to the point where it's too late.

We do think the die has been cast where gas — which is currently at $3.50, $3.60 — going out to 26, 27, in 28, the curve is flat. 2029, 2030 the curve is flat because people believe the gas is abundant. That's despite all these AI compute announcements, despite what all of the companies are doing for their investments. Gas has lulled everybody to sleep.

2028 中的临界点

But what happens is: these structural things start to fall in place in 27, 28, and we start to draw meaningfully in the middle of 28, as early as early 28, on the gas system like we've never drawn before.

As we look at 2028, 29, 2030, we start to cut into the US working gas storage — which is about 4 TCF total gas storage. There's a range of high and low for that storage seasonally as we draw in summer and winter and then build in the shorter months in the spring and fall.

  • Middle of 2028: we start to break very materially below in a historical way where gas available in storage has ever been before.
  • By 2029: we drop below all known historical storage evidence.
  • By 2030: we get pretty close to where we think, ceteris paribus, gas storage looks very, very low.

At that point in time, because it's not happened before, we're forced to look at where gas price has gone during shortages.

历史参照与"结构性"的区别

We can look at Russia–Ukraine — gas went to $8, $9, $10 an MCF because we send a lot more externally to Europe. We've seen various weather anomalies — polar vortex in '14, in December of '22 — and those prices have gone to $6 or $8 or $10. But those have been transitory.

What we're talking about are structural drivers of demand against a known possible production of gas, and they don't match up. You pull in a very historic way starting in 28 to the point where the deficit gets really convex and unbounded.

主持人： Meaning the gas prices could be 20 or something like this?

Matt： I would hesitate to even put a price target on it. But at $8 or $10, we think you potentially shut off some of the US exports where there are spot cargoes and they are leaving the border to capture uplift in Europe or elsewhere. Those spot cargoes may not be lifted, and then that gas is left in the system, and we've tried to account for that in our model.

But spot cargoes alone can't solve this. You would have to get into shutting off contracted cargoes leaving our border via LNG to really start to mitigate some of this. And it's hard for us to count on the choice to shut off contracted cargoes where there's some Japanese utility counterparty who has counted on it for provision of electricity.

八、赢家与输家

主持人： In crazy convex outcomes like this, can you tick through who you think the biggest winners and losers are?

上游赢家

There are some clear natural gas producer winners.

Expand Energy is probably at the top of that list. They probably control 70% of remaining core Haynesville wells of the very closely known parameters of rock where we know it to be very productive. So Expand we think is far and away the biggest winner.

Uniquely, Expand is CEO-less right now. There was some turnover in the year — they're going through a search. The stock has plummeted over the last six months as part of that search, and it's trading at 4x EBITDA on a forward curve where no one believes what I'm telling you to be the case.

Even though we think modeling the facts gets you to a much higher gas price, the stock has dropped. The assets have not changed. It has some of the highest quality rock in the country.

Highest quality upstream company in Appalachia is probably Range. Range has significant room to grow production and materially grow returns to investors. So those would be the upstream companies.

太阳能：意外的边际收益者

It's not going to be obvious in the first pass through this equation for most, but natural gas sets the marginal fuel for the next-in-line power-generating asset in each power market. As natural gas goes, power prices go in the country.

If you think about the dispatch curve of different generating assets in the country, there are some where the fuel is free — that would be solar, to a lesser extent wind, hydro. Solar assets have been growing meaningfully — they've been 90% of the interconnect queue with batteries in the last 10 years in terms of new assets coming on, other than gas.

Solar assets stand to benefit from a windfall where electricity prices are going up because gas is setting the margin of plant taking fuel where the price is increasing, while sun costs the same.

We think there are some companies positioned very well for margin expansion for no incremental capital cost:

  • XPLR (ticker XIFR, formerly NextEra Yield Co) — an interesting set of assets. They have a windfall coming in the latter part of the decade, in early 2030s, ceteris paribus, because they mark their PPAs to market at much higher values without any capex.
  • Clearway Energy — another similar circumstance.

So solar assets at the utility scale, especially, stand to win.

住宅光伏的静默复兴

Maybe more interestingly as it relates to some of our discussions of the past: residential solar, which has been suffering from really the first removal of tax incentives to install residential solar since the late '70s.

Residential solar assets are really one of the only ways to protect yourself from what's going to happen during the time of 10:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m. once gas gets really tight in the electricity markets — what you pay for electricity at your house.

We think residential solar grows exponentially from here. Even without tax incentives for the first time, it's very economic with where electricity prices are likely to go — to install solar, especially when it's coupled with batteries which make the electricity much more available round-the-clock.

核电：唯一的长期解决方案

主持人： I think it's important to say — you're an investor, you have money behind this work. Beyond those two categories, are there any other surprising winners? What about nuclear? What about Westinghouse or places like this?

Matt： What we're talking about is a complex dynamic system where there will be choices to consume electricity or not at different times. As I go through this, I want to make sure I acknowledge that there's no silver bullet solution for what I described as a convex situation with natural gas — and therefore electricity prices — as the decade closes.

There's no bridge fuel other than solar and wind, because currently natural gas is the only flex fuel to get us to when we can bring on nuclear.

We have spent a fair amount of time in the nuclear ecosystem. To us, large-scale nukes are the only solution that makes sense — which points us primarily to the AP1000 Westinghouse units.

主持人： Don't those take like 5 years to build or something?

Matt： More than that. But at least they have a story.

Past and very brief history: we've built two nuclear reactor units in 30 years in the US — Vogtle 3 and 4. Around that time we also tried to build one in South Carolina called VC Summer — another nuclear project at the time nearly bankrupted SCANA, which was later pushed into the arms [of Dominion] and the project was shut.

The muscle memory from trying to build large-scale nukes — especially in the back of Fukushima in 2011, Chernobyl, Three Mile Island — for three decades, nuclear engineers and scientists and companies…

主持人： Gone to do other stuff.

Matt： And then the Vogtle 3 and 4 experiment, where it cost 3x as much and took, I don't know, 15 years from birth to commercial service. That's the recent memory of building these nuclear units.

But if you go out to the 2030s, what I'm describing in terms of gas deficit only gets worse in 31, 32 and beyond. So in our mind, the only viable solution is to build large-scale nuclear as fast as possible — which would mean it needs to come on in 2033 or 2034, which is as soon as it can come on.

Regular utilities, hyperscalers, regulators should all align around that goal. But because people don't really believe that gas is in short supply as the decade goes along —

主持人： They don't believe in the problem.

Matt： — and they don't like the solution. So they need to be convinced of the problem.

The country has a history of building pipelines to solve problems that exist today, not problems that will exist in 5 or 10 years. So we're trying to get out ahead and see where the puck's going. And where it's going is: we are going to need large-scale nuclear by 2033, 2034.

SMR 为什么不是解？

主持人： You don't think SMRs can be a solution — where you use smaller reactors to power individual data centers behind the meter, and this just never touches the system?

Matt： Many of the SMRs are still science experiments. The NRC and the US government are actually doing a fair number of things to break down the barriers to bringing those to fruition — to see if they work or not and what the cost will be and whether they can be scaled or not.

But many of these SMR companies are not set up to manufacture and truly scale for the solution that's needed to solve this problem, which is tens of gigawatts as you go into the 2030s. That points us to these AP1000s.

Since Vogtle 4 came on, Vogtle 4 experienced very material improvements from Vogtle 3. In Georgia today, China is building 39 plus-or-minus nuclear reactors. 34 of them are 1 gigawatt plus. I think a third of those are modeled after the AP1000. We know a lot more today about building large-scale nukes than we did when these mistakes were made.

Large-scale, to us — where it can be commercialized on a known timeline, where the costs are probably better — vs. where we don't even know if we can scale the businesses yet in terms of SMRs. Large-scale versus small — probably wins in our mind.

Two companies most levered to that would be:

  • Cameco — owns 49% [of Westinghouse]. Brookfield 51%. You'll probably find that the US government agrees with what I'm describing. They seem to really be lining up and trying to facilitate commitments and early procurement, which will de-risk some of the supply chain and help put timelines on this. When the Westinghouse comes public, and it's deeply undervalued within Cameco today — so that's an interesting one.
  • BWXT — a super interesting company. They're the primary supplier of nuclear for the US Navy. They significantly benefit from the coming nuclear cycle as well, and lots of dollar content in the AP1000s.

输家：消费者、涡轮机制造商、分布式发电

主持人： Who are the big losers do you think in this future?

Matt： Sadly, the biggest losers of this would be the US consumer.

If we're even partially right, electricity prices rise — which you can see some of on the forward curves in these different markets. As electricity prices rise, you start to think about the trade-off:

  • Are we going to export natural gas to foreign buyers?
  • Are we going to use it for AI compute?
  • Or are we going to try to keep consumer electricity bills [low]?

It's an awful trade-off. And I think it will probably start to contribute more to the public dialogue — the NIMBYism that we're seeing already pop up in some places.

We think AI is tremendously transformational. We're not anti-AI. But it consumes a lot of power. We need to really focus on the 2030 to 2035 period. The US consumer is probably going to pay the bill in the meantime.

Please note that most of the solutions being proposed by the government are to consume more gas because everybody believes it's enough.

"Bring Your Own Generation" (BYOG) is a thing today. That's what the hyperscalers are being asked to do — to site their data center in a certain lat-long. Well, that means more gas, not less.

Every time you read a press release from Bloom or from GE Vernova —

主持人： Think more gas.

Matt： Just think more gas. And you can use the energy efficiency of each one of those units and understand exactly how much more incremental gas beyond the base case that I just shared is dangerously tight.

涡轮机制造商：又一轮周期泡沫？

Another loser — I want to be respectful — some of the biggest winners so far, at least in the stock market, have been the manufacturers of gas turbines or distributed power gen sets.

When you think about those companies, it's been somewhat boom and bust. In the early 2000s, there was a boom to build as many gas plants as we could. The capacity was overbuilt, and the industry really languished for a long time — until now. You've had just tremendous profitability and equity returns come from these companies over the last two years.

But as you look at 2028, 2029, most of them are adding more capacity again just like they did in the early 2000s.

  • Caterpillar — I think they're doubling their Solar Turbine capacity between now and the end of 29, which I would judge is just at the exact wrong time, when people may be questioning whether they even want to deploy those assets because the gas is much more expensive than they planned.
  • Bloom Energy — has been topical recently because of other things folks are talking about (the rare earths they use in their manufacture, for instance). For us, we don't think that Bloom Energy's assets at 2 gigawatts or more will be able to get natural gas in competition with all of the other assets that are being deployed that will consume gas, given the scarcity that we see.

Those are two we'll call manufacturers of distributed generation or BTM generation that we think are probably more poorly positioned than investors appreciate.

And then it really doesn't make sense to us beyond 2029, 2030 to build large-scale natural gas generation until we ramp up production meaningfully and you can make sure we have the security of deliverability of supply of gas that's consistent with our model.

We could see orders slow very meaningfully for natural gas generating assets even at large scale as 26 progresses. Those could be some of the losers — it may just not make sense to use gas for power generation for new or incremental assets after a certain point.

超大规模厂商：类比 DRAM 短缺

主持人： It seems like sort of like this whole memory shortage thing that we're going through right now, that hyperscalers might also be people that are in trouble here if this is a key input to what they're doing. Do you think that's a big problem for them?

Matt： So as we've been socializing this a little bit — trying to learn more and have people poke holes — shared this with one of your recent guests. He listened. They said, "Well, this sounds like DRAM 2 years ago."

Slowly at first, and then all at once. The lack of investment in capacity expansion is going to come up to bite us. I think that's where the analog starts. When we think about the way this plays out and other analogs, that's probably the best one.

When we think about the cost of the hyperscalers right now, energy is budgeted to be about 10% of their cost. Depreciation's the highest. Certainly memory and other things factor into that as well, but total cost of energy is supposed to be about 10%.

If you plug in all of this compute and it's gas-powered, and we think gas could double or triple structurally even without weather, it could end up being 20 or 30% of the cost of compute by 2029. So we do think it becomes a much more material issue.

The levelized cost of energy (LCOE) as it's referred to takes into account capex, cost of fuel. Everybody making decisions in this moment is using the forward curve for natural gas — which is flat, a little backwardated, mostly flat out to the 2030s, in the mid 30s. That is a very attractive low-cost fuel for the hyperscalers to commit to when they're focused on solving everything else, like how do I get compute in place to manifest in this revenue growth in Anthropic or elsewhere.

For us, we're just focused on modeling objectively — when you plug in this compute, or this power gen source here and there, what does — how exactly does it pull on the system of companies that we focus on?

九、魔鬼代言人：什么情况下 Matt 会被证明错了？

主持人： If you're forced to play devil's advocate and come up with the set of circumstances such that this is all much ado about nothing, and we're sitting here in 2030 and gas costs three bucks — what do you think is the most likely reason? Is it that data center power requirements are much lower because we make performance breakthroughs, or AI demand isn't what we think it's going to be? What is this most sensitive to such that they might be wrong?

Matt： So after we did most of our work, we went on a bit of a listening tour to target conversations with who we think are the subject matter experts in that thing — energy storage, hyperscaler compute deployment and energy consumption.
