Oh, we then need this approval and that approval. Oh, and I forgot there was some doc that said we always have to do this checklist." Um, and it relies so much on human behavior and redundancy. So much of that can be automated and refocused on what actually moves the needle for our business.

And I think this move towards software factories is a move towards: how do we figure out what are the actual inputs that determine what features we need to build? And that might be inputs from the customers, inputs from the market, inputs from product leaders at the company. And let's be very clear these are the signals, the inputs that we are taking in here. Okay great, we have those signals. Then what is the process by which we build this?

Um, and really mapping out the assembly lines of how you are building software is really important, because then you get to close the loop and say: did this actually deliver outcome for our business?

公司即 AGI：反向传播 vs 直觉

Talking before about the tokenomics — if you're that CIO and you're faced with that question of "where do you put every incremental token?", really the question two years from now is going to become: where do you put every incremental dollar? And so you're going to have to be asked: do you put that incremental dollar towards headcount or towards tokens? And if tokens, to where in the org?

And these are things that you can only really know when you have these kind of feedback loops that give you examples of like, "Hey by the way, we made those decisions based on this data. And it did not matter at all. We added these new features and no one cared. It didn't create more retention. It didn't create more usage or whatever metrics that business is looking to optimize."

And the only way to do this is: like, you need kind of more rigor and more process. It almost feels like 10 years from now we're going to look back at this previous era of software and it's going to feel like businesses in like ancient times where they didn't do accounting.

It's going to be like — it's going to be like marketing in the day of Mad Men, right? Where it's like all creative and you have no idea what's actually working.

It makes no — like it's like, "Oh yeah, let's ship that feature. Oh, I think it went well. Yeah, we had — I got some metrics on that. Yeah."

It's like, no —

If you guys read the blog post that Jack Dorsey put out about how every company is like an AGI —

Yeah.

There's also this degree to which if your company is an AGI, you want to optimize the weights.

Yeah.

You want to figure out what nodes are doing what things, which are load-bearing, which are not, which need more tokens, where do you need more nodes? And in order to do that, you don't train a model by vibes. I mean, okay, actually you kind of do — but [laughter] you don't — I guess more importantly, you don't do back-prop in a model by vibes. Like you are running those actual calculations and you are seeing when we change this node what happens. Now you might be making bets on how to change the model by vibes, but you are — it's pretty mathematical in what you are doing.

Meanwhile at companies, you know, people are determining token budgets just by shooting from the hip. People are laying people off by shooting from the hip and just being like, "Oh yeah, like 20,000." There is no way there is science to laying off 20,000 people. That is just like, "Here is a chunk and let's just see what happens."

Instead, I think in these organizations, the way they can do things is much more mathematical of like: this part of the business matters a lot and does better if we give it more tokens. It doesn't actually matter if we give it more humans, so let's give them more tokens. There might be other parts of the business where actually giving them more tokens doesn't matter, but more people matter, because if we build more relationships with our customers and deeper relationships with our customers, that matters.

But these are things that we're going to need like quantitative insight on, and you need a software factory to do that. Otherwise, you're just like shooting from the hip and just guessing, which won't work as well.

Token 与 Headcount 的极限比例

In the limit, how much do you think people will spend on tokens versus on engineering headcount?

It'll depend on the business. I think every business will have a balance and it just depends on — like an easy example is: generally salespeople, they probably don't need that many tokens if they're good salespeople. Because generally the — where they provide the most alpha is when they're in the seat face-to-face with their customers talking about the customer's problems, understanding, you know, how they build software in our case and how we can make that, you know, more efficient, more productive. They can use tokens a little bit of like, "Oh whatever, generate them some, you know, AI debrief, take some notes, votes, like help them with the follow-up," but that's so minimal, the number of tokens it basically doesn't matter.

Like if you add more tokens to the sales team, it probably won't change their output. If you add more humans to the sales team, it probably will. Meanwhile, engineering teams are pretty different, where engineering teams generally — it seems like you want people to own an outcome end-to-end, but then if you give them more tokens, they can produce a lot more.

And so it seems like there — and then there's a lot of kind of places in between, of like operations, finance, marketing. These are places where they're neither here nor there, where I think they're somewhere in between and it kind of depends on your business. But I think every business is going to have to ask: what is our core competency?

核心能力回归：披萨店不需要工程师

Something that we see a lot in the market — or we used to see, and now they finally kind of hit reality — what we used to see is, "Oh, like we're going to build our own software development agents." And we're like, "Okay, like you're a like a consumer uh logistics company, are you sure you want to do that?" Like, "Yeah yeah, we have to do this." And it's like, "Okay."

And then six months later it's like, "Wait, actually this is not a core competency for our business. We don't want to hire, you know, AI engineers to be doing this. Our core competency is, you know, consumer logistics. That's what we want to focus on."

And I think this is an opportunity for every business to double down on their core competency and what matters for them, and then procure externally whatever it is that doesn't matter for them. Like a trivial example of this is — I don't know — in the days of the early internet, you probably had to be a programmer to build a website. And like websites generally help if you're a pizza shop, cuz you want to have people come to your pizza shop, they want to be able to — or like whatever. At that time would you say it was a core competency of a pizza shop to have engineers? Like certainly not. That is kind of a byproduct of a brief moment in time. But then there were companies out there that helped you build a website, you don't need to be technical. And this is why we live in a world where like most pizza shops don't have an engineering department, which I think is probably a good thing.

Um, and I think similarly a lot of businesses have dealt with the reality of: if you want to do XYZ other thing, you have to bring in people of this type of role. But I think that's been like something you had to do, not because it's a core competency of the business. And allowing businesses to focus and double down on the things that they are best at, I think is going to be good for the consumers of their business.

And so I think we're just going to see a lot of ruthless refocusing on what actually matters. Um, which is going to be cool to see.

企业如何转型：全员黑客松 + 允许失败

On that — so you know, every company kind of has to go through this process of reinvention. You know, 10 or 20 years ago people talked about digital transformation. And I don't know if anybody's given it a buzz word now, but "AI transformation" something of that sort. Couple years ago you ran into a bunch of organizations that just weren't ready to deal with autonomous agents. Things you've seen your customers start to change — and so the question is, when you look at your customers as they kind of go up this maturity curve and sort of reinvent themselves for the future, um, any good tricks or techniques that you've seen them use to repot themselves a bit?

Yeah, I mean, I think um, surprisingly, like the companies that have been doing companywide hackathons really end up doing well. It seems relatively trivial, but just setting aside a day where everyone in the workforce is just like, "Build with AI." It really sets the tone and sets the pace.

So just give me a look —

I tried to force him to build stuff [laughter] with coding agents. It didn't go so well.

We'll work on it. We'll do after this.

You know, we gave it a great effort.

But that's it. Like, it literally just setting aside the time to do it. And like even if it fails miserably, like it's fine. And also like the orgs that are okay with failing.

Like it feels like there are some who are like, "We need to do it exactly right. But we need to make the right decision from day one." No, like you're going to make mistakes. Everyone is going to —

And the orgs who are kind of leaning into it and embracing it to a certain degree, I think, are succeeding. Like one of our largest customers is EY. EY is not necessarily known to be at the absolute frontier of AI. But I think for them, they were just like, "Look, this matters. There have been other transformations that we relate to. We're not going to be late to this. Like we're just going to go in. We might mess up." But like obviously respecting like SEC, the things that you're not allowed to mess up and put those aside —

But like —

Let's go and get our engineers to mess around and build this stuff and see where it breaks and understand what they like and what they don't like. Um, I think that really matters a lot in the ones that we're seeing succeed. And also the ones who are pretty bold in reinventing the processes that they've put in place, and just saying, "Hey, there's no sacred cows. Let's put this aside, try something out. If it doesn't work, we'll put that sacred cow right back."

Um, and I think that's been kind of a determining factor there. Um, and when it comes from within — if it comes from the board, probably not going to go well.

Yeah.

If it comes from within like the tech team or the ICs or the leadership, that's when we see it go better.

12 个月预测：从同步到 90% 异步 Token

Do you have any predictions for the most important changes that are going to happen in your space over the next call it 12 months?

A lot of AI consumption is going up like crazy, and everyone's super super excited because the revenue is going wild. Like a lot of this is synchronous usage. In other words, if everyone woke up sick tomorrow, like a lot of Claude Code usage would be zero, because it's all just, "Hey Claude Code" or "Hey Codex" or "Hey Droid," right?

I think in 12 to 24 months like 90% of tokens will be asynchronous tokens. So these are going to be, you know, Droids on their own autonomously being like, "Hey, here's some signal that I found from a customer. Let's go fix it or let's go create a first-pass solution to this."

And I think that is going to be where the real agent-native stuff begins, because right now we're still kind of in copilot mode. Like if you're going to an agent and say, "Hey, go do this for me," it is more agentic because it's not going to come back and ask you a ton, but it's still like you are kicking it off.

Like if you guys have ever been to Tesla's factories, which is one of the sources of inspiration for the name — it's just robotic arms everywhere going and doing stuff. Like it's not like there are people there going and, you know, attaching the widget to the thing. Um, and this idea of a "dark factory" where the lights are off and things are just happening — that is where software development is going.

That's kind of where the name came from is like — you know, Elon was always talking about "the factory is the machine that builds the machine."

Yeah.

And that's been something that we took to heart. Um, and I guess also that combined with his whole thing about how you're destined to become the opposite of your name.

Um, and in our case, you know, Factory becomes artisanal. It's kind of a good flip there.

乐观愿景：短期阵痛，长期解放

So what's your most optimistic version of the future both for Factory and for the world at large?

So I think short-term there's going to be a lot of turbulence, because I think a lot of companies have misallocated resources pretty poorly. There's been a lot of bloat. Um, and I think the correction that's going to happen there is going to be really painful for a lot of people. And I think that's something that I think every AI CEO should really bear much more responsibility than they currently are for. Um, and also figuring out ways to address and kind of ameliorate in some way, because this is something that is going to be very painful for a lot of people.

Now, I also have optimism that we can actually address that faster than we think. We just need to start now in terms of addressing that.

Now the longer term — and why I think this is a good thing, and why I don't believe at all like, you know, the BS that people are saying of, "Oh, engineers are going away" — generally there is a huge number of problems in the world, a large subset of those problems can be solved with software, a small subset of those problems are currently being solved with software.

And so in the short term this means that, okay, for a given problem that was over-allocated engineering resources, okay, we need to reallocate those. "Reallocate those" is a kind of cold way of saying some people are going to lose their jobs.

But I think the thing that's going to happen in the longer term is: we need engineers. Engineers are some of the best systems thinkers and the best problem solvers. And there are so many problems that can be solved with software that are not being solved with software. And so that means that we are going to take those engineers and have them go and solve problems that previously were not being solved. That is such a net good for the world, because again there are so many of these problems that we are not solving, and also there's so many problems that we are maybe solving but with really shitty software. And like this is going to enable people to solve it with incredible software.

And you know, the vision for Factory is that we are kind of the factory that allows them to go and build this incredible software to solve these different problems. And these problems range from things that are trivial to, you know, like government software typically is not very good, whether it's like DMV or IRS. Web — like all that stuff is generally a pretty poor experience. Um, we don't need to live like that. We can live in a world where all software is really fantastic.

Um, but also things like, you know, pharmaceutical research. Like so much that goes into solving diseases is not just like a biology problem. A lot of it requires the best software engineers in the world. And previously those problems haven't allocated the right dollars to attract the best engineers. But now because of what's happening, I think we will be much more closely allocated to like: these are the biggest problems, let's get the best minds and the best problem-solvers to solve that.

Um, I think it's kind of our job as an industry to do that relocation, reallocation as quickly as possible, so it's not 10 years but maybe like 6 months or a year.

收尾

Wonderful. Matan, I think the clarity and consistency of your vision over time has just always been very inspiring. And then just seeing how much you've grown as a leader and how much Factory has grown as a company, even since the last time we did this training data episode. It's truly all inspiring. So, thank you for joining us again to share what you're up to.

I appreciate it a lot. Thank you.

Thank you.

[music]

[music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"付钱送客"是最反直觉的护城河建造术
Matan 最令人震惊的决定不是那些看似大胆的技术选择，而是主动把已经到手的 $2M 收入退回给客户。这在传统商业逻辑里近乎自杀——但它揭示了一个被大多数创业者忽视的**"信任的负熵原理"**：
在企业软件销售中，信任是一种熵增系统。一旦客户开始怀疑你的产品，衰减速度远快于建立速度。他实际上做的是**用现金购买未来的谈话权**——花 $2M 买下了"12 个月后我可以再次敲门"的期权。这种反向嫁妆式的操作，比任何 NPS 调查、任何 CS 团队都更能刻画一家公司的品格。
更深层的跨领域连接是：这个动作在物理学上等价于**"退火"（annealing）**——为了让系统跳出局部最优，你必须先主动提高能量、破坏眼前的稳定态。绝大多数创业公司卡在 $2M–$10M 之间死掉，恰恰是因为他们不敢做这种"负梯度动作"。
## 二、Harness 的悖论：多模型让系统更强，而不是更弱
这是整场对话里最反直觉、也最少被注意到的技术洞察。所有人的第一直觉都是"模型和执行框架协同设计（co-design）"会带来最佳性能——就像苹果的软硬件一体化

item.title: [Bits + Bips] The Stablecoin Rails War: Who Actually Wins?
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、稳定币战争的真正战场：不是技术，而是"自我颠覆的意愿"

表面上这集在讨论 Stripe 收购 PayPal、Circle 被挑战、USDG 借 Robinhood 上位——听起来是产品与分发之争。但真正被反复触碰的隐藏结构是：PayPal 不是败给了技术，而是败给了自己的合规律师和董事会。Austin 说了一句极其耐人寻味的话——"PayPal chose to fail"。这不是竞争失败，这是主动选择的失败。

这里有一个反直觉的观察：在稳定币这个赛道，拥有最多用户的公司反而最难成功。因为用户越多、监管暴露越大、律师越保守、"母舰保护逻辑"越强。PYUSD 的失败不是产品失败，是组织免疫系统排斥新器官的故事。这也解释了为什么真正跑出来的往往是 Robinhood 这种"体量足够大能给分发、但小到还敢冒险"的中段玩家。

跨领域连接：这和生物学里的"体型-代谢-创新窗口"规律惊人一致。大象不能跳跃，不是因为它没肌肉，而是因为它的骨骼-质量比不允许。PayPal 的组织质量太大，反而失去了做稳定币最需要的那种"跳跃动作"。

二、开源中国模型 = AI 版的"稳定币时刻"，但方向反了

这集把 Kimi K2、DeepSeek 和稳定币讨论放在一集里绝非偶然——它们其实是同一个结构性事件的两面：基础设施的去溢价化。

一个让人意外的对照：美国前沿实验室现在的处境，正是 2022 年 Circle 眼睁睁看着 USDT 抢占市场的翻版——你更合规、更"负责任"、有更多护栏，结果对手直接把产品免费开源出来。David Sachs 引用的那个例子简直是黑色幽默：Hugging Face 想用美国模型分析真实的网络攻击载荷，被 guardrails 拒绝，只好切到中国 GLM——过度合规反而成了国家安全漏洞。

这里藏着一条更深的规律：任何试图通过"道德溢价"来收租的行业，最终都会被开源+新兴市场组合拳击穿。稳定币是这样，AI 是这样，未来量子计算大概率也是这样。Chris 顺口提到的"apps on quantum stacks"其实不是随口一说，而是同一个模式的第三次演出预告。

跨领域连接：这个模式在制药行业的仿制药冲击、汽车业丰田对底特律的降维打击、乃至印刷术打破教会的知识垄断中都出现过。每一次都是"高护栏方"低估了"低成本+高分发"的组合。

三、被所有人忽略的暗线：这一集其实是一部"内部人如何被自己组织出卖"的合集

如果你把这一集所有故事排成一列，会发现一个惊人的共同结构：

  • Riot 数据中心事件：被竞争币种资助的 Greenpeace 通过环保活动家操纵地方舆论
  • PayPal 的失败：内部想做事的人被合规和董事会挡住
  • Anthropic/OpenAI 的困境：自我施加的 guardrails 反而让中国模型接管了实用场景
  • Trump 加密项目的伦理麻烦：家族的 10 亿美元曝光成了 Clarity Act 通过的最大障碍
  • 国会议员交易个股 vs. 加密项目：Austin 的神来一笔——"如果国会议员不能做加密项目，为什么可以炒个股？"

这不是五个独立故事，而是同一种"内耗动力学"的五种表达：真正的敌人从来不是外部竞争者，而是组织内部那个"为了保护现状而牺牲未来"的机制。

一个出人意料的洞见：在快速变化的行业里，"合规成熟度"和"战略死亡率"呈正相关。这不是说合规不好，而是说当整个行业的底层技术在重写规则时，把力气花在优化旧规则上就是慢性自杀。PayPal 花了几年把 PYUSD 做成"区块链版 PayPal 余额"，而 Robinhood 直接把它做成"发钱给你用的收益产品"——一个把稳定币当成负债管理，一个把它当成用户获取工具。

跨领域连接：这和罗马晚期军团为什么打不过蛮族是同一个道理——不是罗马变弱了，而是它把太多资源花在维持既有秩序（护墙、边防、税制），最终连打仗的钱都没了。今天的 PayPal、Anthropic 的 guardrails 团队、以及那些花几百万请律师而不是给开发者付费的银行 stablecoin 联盟，都在演出这场戏的现代版。

📝 转录文本

开场：跟着钱走 —— 一个数据中心背后的加密币暗战

was on board until they weren't. Out of nowhere, there was a lot of Facebook opposition to the data center that was being built. And we traced exactly where this opposition was coming from. As it turned out, it was coming from an environmental activist who didn't even live in the city. She came from the city next door.

And we looked into what she was doing in her background. And we realized that she had actually partnered with Greenpeace, which at the time Greenpeace had received a huge infusion of cash from a rival cryptocurrency to Bitcoin — that the explicit purpose of this Greenpeace movement was to flood or cast fear, uncertainty and doubt on Bitcoin itself.

And so from that experience, that's when I realized we need to follow the money. And in doing so, I was able to translate that follow-the-money ethos to this national project we're doing right now.

进步派为何反对最"进步"的技术？

What continues to bother me is that it's very easy to feed into this natural fear of technology that many people have, this natural fear of change. And you see across crypto, across AI, like the people who should be supporting this technology the most are fighting it.

And who am I talking about? I'm talking about the progressives. Crypto on its face is the most democratizing approach to finance you could have. It gives people real time access. And yes, there need to be client protections, but it's a very progressive technology.

AI is absolutely leveling the playing field overnight when it comes to information and education — huge, huge opportunity for people who have not had access, who haven't had that opportunity for on-the-job training because they didn't know somebody. So this is very progressive technology.

And so it's just crazy — when you see some of those progressive technologies coming out with this incredible progress, some of the progressives saying, "no, no, no, we don't like this because it's dangerous." I think we've got that backwards.

Clarity Act 的赔率与"意外的力量"

Polymarket odds were at 75% in May, and now they're a little under 40%. And they've come down kind of linearly, which just speaks to this — every day that it's not done is a day wasted.

The ethics question, it's hard for me to not think of that as a delicious political nugget that some people want to kind of wrap up and take home with them to eat another day — to point to the president's personal interests in digital assets. And of course, the disclosure a couple of weeks ago — he's made well clear of a billion dollars between himself and his family — is red meat for the Democrats.

It's important that it gets done. I do think at this point, the reason that it would be a good support for the market is it would be a surprise. I do think the market would take it as a surprise that passage takes place. It's not going to be a "yep, thought so, no big deal, we expected this." I don't think it's priced in right now.

And so surprise is one of the most powerful emotions that can help fuel either up or down moves in prices. So passage would be a surprise. And I think it would be hard to imagine a market that doesn't experience some lift.

Robinhood 链的早期数据与代币化未来

Well, you know, I think for us, what's exciting to begin with is the traction and the early activities of browsing on the chain. I think it's great to see developers building, you see users engaging, and you see the chain performing very well, like what we were designing. So not only it's super fast, but also the gas is pretty low.

And so, so far we are seeing a ton of excitement from the customer about just that — and the fact that the chain is really working like we intended. We just launched Shipper recently — so it's only two weeks ago that we announced some of these changes — and we're seeing the TVL actually increasing quite a bit. It's past 370 million.

A lot of that is stablecoin because we are connecting directly to the Robinhood app to the chain for our own product, where you can get approximately 7% on your stablecoin. And I think that was kind of the main hero moment that we were trying to push on when we announced all these new products in London.

And my hope is also that at some point we can reduce the amount of jurisdiction limitation. I think in the US, for example, we're waiting on this Clarity Act that we've been discussing. The SEC has also been vocal that they do believe that tokenization of securities will be helpful for the markets.

I think in a lot of regions across the world, they could actually benefit from tokenization because right now it's very hard to interact with some of these exchanges — and so liquidity is therefore limited. But in a world where you can access all these exchanges and all these assets across all the countries, you can actually largely increase liquidity and therefore largely increase the economical benefits of the local region.

So I think right now we are kind of at the beginning of the tokenization world, and I wouldn't be surprised if in a few years everything is tokenized and you can access any exchange 24/7 because the benefits kind of outgrew the old regulations that are in place.

总统对独立机构委员会的新权力

So the president now has greater leverage over commissioners who are already serving. So if you have a commissioner who the president thinks is blocking the administration's agenda, the president now has significantly more ability to replace that commissioner with someone who is more aligned with his administration's priorities.

So what that means is agency policy — right? So everything from rulemaking and enforcement priorities to granting exemptive relief, that can all become much more closely aligned with the White House. And these things could potentially change a lot more quickly from one administration to the next.

So I think what you'll see happen is that presidential elections will now have a much more immediate effect on crypto regulation and just regulation in general. So instead of waiting years for agency leadership to gradually turn over when commissioners' terms expired, a new administration could come in, replace all the commissioners presumably, and start reshaping regulatory policy from very early on. So I think that could potentially have a really big impact on policy.

战场上的 AI：从"距离"到"闭环"

What changes the distance between having the information and being able to act on it — because the more you understand agents and loops, the better you can get and making it work for you.

And this was not just like one fighter here and there using a trap bot. They have decided to devote significant resources to build an industrial operation where they have specialist AI units, some of them who just sit in vehicles near the front trying to figure out how to make the next move.

So they are doing AI governance in a way that a lot of startups should be doing, but in a horrible, like the worst possible way. And they're learning how to do all this through jihadist networks — so terrorists can talk to each other and share information and they were trained by other terrorists.

So you may have heard that LLMs have these safeguards — right? Like if you ask about a weapon it will say "no I can't answer that." Those are super easy to get around. There's a concept in complex systems like a "no is now I'm just going to prop the door open overnight just to make sure that the mouse doesn't get in" or whatever the hell the problem is. Or like there's birds in there.

ICO 遇冷：如何对待利益相关方

Long story short, market tanks. We started a winter. It's not great. So we postponed the ICO and we did it now recently. And in the end, that 5% value is no longer worth 11, 12 million — we were able to raise around $4 million.

So $4 million was our new budget. Now we have a new budget is much less than the original. And we debated a lot what to do. Do we give tokens? Definitely not in the plans, because then we go to zero. And then what's the point of doing this at all?

Need to take care of your stakeholders. And so we decided to stick to our 5% budget and we debated it with our investors, with our users. And we decided that instead of giving some people maybe one or two percent reward, where some people were losing 90...

CAPE 广告

And SIM swaps are still one of the easiest ways for attackers to drain accounts. That's where CAPE comes in — America's privacy-first mobile carrier. Same premium service, but CAPE rotates the identifier on your SIM every…

…time you contact people with fresh perspectives from different backgrounds, whether it's tech, UX, or product design. Fidelity is an equal opportunity employer.

回到 Riot 案例的完整版本

And I actually saw this play out in a job that I had before I went to the Treasury Department. I worked at Riot Platform. Some of your audience may know Riot. It's a Bitcoin mining and AI HBC company.

When I was at Riot a few years ago, we were building a data center in Corsicana, Texas. Everything was going swimmingly. It seemed like the community was on board — until they weren't. Out of nowhere, there was a lot of Facebook opposition to the data center that was being built. And we traced exactly where this opposition was coming from.

节目正式开场：市场快照

Hey, everyone. Welcome to Bits and Bips, where we explore how crypto and macro collide one basis point at a time. We're here to discuss the latest stories in the worlds of crypto and macro.

And as a quick market snapshot of the day: - Bitcoin's around 65k as I'm reading this - ETH around 1890 - SOL around 77.5 - Hype is back to 62 - WTI is at 83, Brent's at 89 - SpaceX at 120, Circle at 65.5

However, before we begin, a quick commercial break.

CAPE 广告（重复）

This episode is brought to you by CAPE, America's privacy-first mobile carrier. Same premium service you'd expect from any other carrier, but designed so your number, your location, and your data actually stay yours. Get 33% off six months at cape.co/unchained.

频道迁移提醒（重复）

Heads up, everyone. We've now fully transitioned Bits and Bips to its new dedicated channels. So if you're not yet subscribed to the new Bits and Bips channels on X, YouTube, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or wherever you get your podcasts, go there and subscribe now.

For the next few weeks, we'll play segments of the full interviews on Unchained as a reminder. But for now, you'll only hear those short segments of Bits and Bips on Unchained. No more full episodes. You can get links to all of the dedicated channels with the full episodes at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips, spelled B-I-P-S. Thanks again.

嘉宾介绍：Alex Wilson of Cyclops

All right, everybody. Welcome back. As always, I'm your host, Austin Campbell, High Scholar of Zero Knowledge Group. I'm here with my usual two co-hosts, Ram Aluwalia, Maester of Wealth, Leader of Lumina, and Chris Perkins, the Golden Hand of Franklin Crypto. But today, we have a very special guest for our opening segment, Alex Wilson, Founder of Cyclops, previously Head of Crypto at Payments Giant Shift4, Co-Founder of The Giving Block.

And before we begin, a quick reminder that Bits and Bips has its own channels now separate from Unchained. If you're not subscribed here you're gonna stop seeing these episodes, so if you hate us stay off — but otherwise don't lose your regular dose of the sharpest takes in crypto. So you can subscribe on X, YouTube, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, or wherever you're watching, so every show keeps showing up in your feed.

Now today, we're going to talk about stablecoins first. But Alex, as somebody who's been an operator in this space and a guest on the show, can you give us a little background as to what you've done and orient our listeners as to why you're here?

Alex: Yeah, thanks for having me on, guys. And yeah, I mean, Austin, your intro was a good starting point. So this is actually my second stab at things starting Cyclops. Back in 2018, started the Giving Block where we help charities accept crypto and stablecoin donations. And you can imagine in 2018, telling charities to pay attention to crypto wasn't that easy. But we stuck with it and we ended up working with most of the biggest charities and had really dominant 80–90% market share.

That business really took off and we sold it to Shift4 about four years ago. When Shift4 acquired us, that was really their first foray into crypto and stablecoins. And I became the head of crypto and stablecoins there. We stuck around for four years — which is actually, I think, longer than we probably would have expected to — because we really were learning a lot and really enjoyed it there. And so we built these different products for stablecoin settlement, for pay-ins and payouts for the hundreds of thousands of merchants at Shift4.

And then just about a year ago, we started Cyclops because we were pretty unhappy with all the solutions in the market and we really wanted to build a company that was purpose-built for the payment space. So that's a quick overview.

Stripe × Advent 对 PayPal 的收购：分发之战

Austin: Awesome. Thank you. So then let's start with some news and stablecoins payment space and let's just lead with the big one. So Stripe and Advent have launched a bid for PayPal, where essentially they valued PayPal at about $53 billion. I think that's about a 28% premium over the close at the time, though PayPal is traded up from there. And this is Stripe's second run at PayPal, really the first serious one here.

PayPal's board — as boards always do in these things — says the offer is inadequate and undervalues PayPal. You will never once as a listener hear a board be like, "you know, honestly, that's too much money, you have overvalued us." This is always what they say.

And where we are is looking at this from the perspective really of distribution. PayPal has 400 million plus active accounts. Branded checkout is just a cash cow for them — it processed 1.8 trillion in 2025. If you bolt that onto Stripe's merchant network, they have a much more two-sided platform.

So it's not a surprise this bid came out. At the same time this is going on, Visa is launching support for the Visa stablecoin platform, OpenUSD, support for USDC and USDG. That allegedly is going to reach 200 million merchants and 15,000 FIs. Visa settles like, I don't know, 15 trillion annually.

So let me start with the PayPal and Stripe dance. What do we make of that? What do we think of what Stripe is doing here and what this says about where the payment space is going and stablecoins put pressure on it?

Alex: On my side, I mean, I think it's funny because I think the last time, Ram, we talked and I was on your other show, we were talking about private versus public valuations. And that's, of course, a big driver of this. So I think Stripe is taking advantage of their private valuation being a lot stronger than the multiples we're seeing in the public markets.

And ultimately, it's a good idea. I mean, Ram can probably talk more numbers on why it's a good idea from a valuation perspective and things like that. But I do think it makes sense, right? For as long as they can have such a premium in the private markets, they might as well buy up payments companies that are trading at historically very cheap valuations right now.

Ram: Yeah, I think you got it exactly right. I mean, the funny thing is normally you see liquid public markets firms using their currency to acquire private companies — which is exactly what happened with you in the Shift4 acquisition. But now you're seeing the reverse because VCs are trying to avoid mark to market. They can enjoy the management from that. And Stripe has maintained an elevation on its valuation despite public markets firms in the payments category correcting. So they're taking that private markets kind of funny money currency and using that to drive acquisitions of real businesses that have lots of free cash flow. I agree. It's a smart move on Stripe's part.

为什么 PYUSD 失败了？—— "PayPal 选择了失败"

Chris: So from the integration perspective, what have we seen over and over again in stablecoins? It's distribution — in any business, frankly, distribution is king. And, you know, Austin, how are you thinking about the integration with Tempo, with the distribution potentials with PayPal? PYUSD never really got off the ground and really never had that material product market fit. I always wondered why. I mean, they came to the table with distribution, and I think maybe some of their public compliance held them back — maybe they moved a little bit slower than others. But I guess, why didn't that distribution work for them and how's it going to work for—

Austin: So I think the first part, I'll start with some thoughts on PayPal, because this also relates to how they got here and why they're being targeted at this valuation. I think PYUSD has been a failure from a distribution perspective because PayPal didn't choose to make it succeed, right?

And what I mean by that is you have a platform where you're holding an immense amount of user funds, you have them on your legacy ledger — and you could have had, had you embraced stablecoins, the second or third largest stablecoin overnight by actually embracing them. And they refused to do so.

The other interesting part is until recently — which is when you started to see some degree of growth in PYUSD — they were only offering it to the people who least needed PYUSD, which was in the United States and at relatively small values. So not really usable for institutional trading. You could mint like thousands to tens of thousands, which if you're Cumberland or Wintermute and you need hundreds of millions to crypto trade, what are you going to do? Wait a year to ramp that up at those limits?

So I think PayPal very much did the classic, call it early innovation thing of quote-unquote "taking the newspaper and putting it on the internet" — by which they literally took their current product framework and put it on a blockchain. And that doesn't work on a blockchain. The blockchain demands are different, the needs are different, the customer types are different, the market opportunities are different.

Alex, I'll throw that over to you after I finish what I'm saying here. But I think what happened is PayPal chose to fail, as weird as that sounds, by just not evolving or iterating and kind of putting the old thing up there in the way that they were used to, Chris. Like that was not really innovation in the meaningful sense. And that, I think, is the missed opportunity.

I do know there were voices at PayPal who wanted to do significantly more. I don't know if it was the CEO, I don't know if it was the board, I don't know if it was risking compliance — but clearly they could not get over the hurdle to actually do those things. And so when I look at this from Stripe perspective, given that they've already integrated Bridge and they're starting to work on stuff out in the wild, I wonder if they're seeing the exact same thing I'm saying here and being like, "well, we'll just do that."

Alex: Yeah, I mean, it's interesting you say that because it's exactly what we see at a lot of these other big payments companies we're working with — where they often get so frustrated, I think, these crypto teams internally, that they can't get much done. And so they'll take sort of whatever they can get. And often that's something that's really easy or easy for the leadership to wrap their head around when they're not super crypto or stablecoin native. So they look for something really simple — but it's, you're kind of set up for failure from the beginning.

And for us, like the biggest opportunities are much more B2B, high, large transaction value, rather than these small sort of consumer amounts. But so often we hear from them like, "oh, we want to start with something really simple, like a pay-with-crypto feature at checkout." Even if we tell them, "you know, it's kind of a novelty, that's not where you're going to really see the traction and the benefit of adopting stablecoins," but so many of them want to start there anyway. And then you're kind of stuck, right? Because then there's not great traction and they don't want to do anything else.

Chris: When you have a legacy business model, it's oftentimes very difficult to adapt it and move into a new space that's very uncomfortable for you. I think part of the challenge with PYUSD is its lack of international accessibility and DeFi accessibility. And I think some of that was probably driven by some of the lawyers and the compliance folks who said, "you know what, we got to protect the mothership guys. Like we can't take this kind of risk and bring this kind of scrutiny."

And that was the last cycle. I feel like things are different now. And I feel like it's different now because I'm seeing companies be much more aggressive in saying, "no, no, no, no — we have risk if we're not in this space," which is a lot different. It could have been a timing issue as well. And of course, execution. Part of what's the right way to say that?

Austin: I do think there was a timing issue. I do think there was an execution issue. I think you've identified both of those correctly. But I think Alex is right that there's an element of the innovator's dilemma here, right? That is to say, if we do these things, we risk disrupting our own business, so our teams don't want to do it, so we instead take the things we can get. Instead, you just sit there while other people disrupt your business. And by the way, if you don't think that's happening to PayPal, go look at their stock price from the early 2020s to present.

And so you end up in this world where by not doing it yourself, you let other people do it for you. I may have said at a couple of meetings at previous stops I was at: we should either do the whole thing or not do this at all. The worst thing to do is the half-assed thing, because then we're going to get stuck. Right? Like actually truly transform yourself with stablecoins, or actually skate away from them as far as you can.

Now, Alex, you mentioned the B2B angle where it might be easier to get traction there. And you've been in the payment space significantly. How would you view a combined Stripe plus PayPal from a B2B and business-side angle of that platform?

Alex: Yeah, well, I think first of all, Stripe out of all the payments companies is doing the best job when it comes to crypto and stablecoins, right? That's pretty obvious, I think for all of us. I don't know how much of the PayPal stablecoin and crypto business they would even necessarily want to keep, right? I think they would stick to the newer things they've done with Bridge and Privy and the other acquisitions. And they would just refocus the priorities probably more around things like stablecoin settlement rather than some of the consumer checkout products, at least when it comes to stablecoins. For the broader business, I mean, I think it's just more merchants that they can sell these stablecoin products and rails to.

Austin: Ram, how would you look — all right. So say you're a PayPal shareholder right now. You've got Stripe coming. I agree to some extent with the public/private stock divergence, but I think it's also pretty clear that Stripe cannot buy PayPal with stock. How would you look at this from a markets perspective? If you're voting on a merger, what is going to move you one way or another?

Ram: Number one consideration is price. At a certain price, there's a willing buyer and there's a willing seller. PayPal has free cash flow. That is attractive and Stripe needs to show growth in free cash. PayPal's free cash flow yield is around 11%. As an aside, I'm not sure why you'd want to own sovereign debt when you find assets like this yielding 11%. And that's true for a lot of payments companies, by the way, that are on sale.

So Stripe can take PayPal's earnings, which are fetching a low multiple — 10 and a half times — and Stripe can apply its higher multiple and cause a re-rate in their future stock price, just by wrapping that earnings stream. So it makes sense for Stripe. I think PayPal is just playing a bit hard to get. They've had a lot of headaches around this thing — like a board member stepped in the CEO role, payments competition is intensifying. It's an incredibly difficult category. I think they're holding out for a reasonable price, but there's a transaction to get done at the right price.

稳定币赛道格局：OUSD、USDG、银行联盟

Austin: Okay, so I want to move slightly on from just specifically talking about the PayPal angle here and think about the distribution battleground in general. Open Standard is coming. Mizuho has cut Circle to underperform with all of the competition. JCB, Japan's largest card network, signed an MOU with Circle. Other places have started to build on this. Pix has, of course, integrated a few things. And what I want to ask here is, as we're looking at the landscape right now, as we see the PayPal/Stripe dance, as we see Visa/MasterCard, as we think about what banks are going to do — Alex, I want to start with you because you've had a viewpoint here. What do you see as the emerging winners and losers the more this dance goes on?

Alex: I mean, Circle stands to lose the most, really, right? But whether it's OUSD or some of these other consortiums or newer ones that aren't even out yet — I mean, it's really hard to know which one's going to be the winner or even a serious competitor. It's still just so early for OUSD and a lot of these other sort of bank-led consortiums, which do a lot of innovation theater, I would say, right? They have a fancy announcement, a lot of names, and then you really don't know for six months, 12 months, what's going to actually happen.

But I do think at least the group they've assembled for OUSD is certainly threatening to Circle and certainly interesting. But it's going to be hard still. I mean, Circle still got a lot of market share and they're pretty deep in a lot of these relationships. It's not going to be easy to disrupt them. They might have some new competition with OUSD, but I don't think it's so certain that they're really going to get disrupted very quickly.

Chris: What about your alma mater Austin, USDG — didn't mention that.

Austin: So I think USDG is going to be a fascinating test case of what happens if you show up with your own installed user base in the first place. Right, because Circle as we know is not really deeply retail-facing — they use Coinbase for that, that's been a majority of their distribution. But USDG just showed up with a small company that has a handful of users called Robinhood attached to it. And I think that is a game we have not seen played in the stablecoin space before.

Which is to say, a company coming with a large user base and pushing stablecoins to those users. Essentially, Robinhood appears to be doing what PayPal probably should have done, which is just take every single user they can get their hands on and offer them USDG at certain terms. Now they also, of course, Chris, this is more complicated — they launched their own blockchain, they have integrations with a lot of like the major DeFi people, like the Uniswaps of the world building on there. They have a whole earn product, right, where they're giving a call it promotional 7% yield right now.

So they've got a whole ecosystem push behind this to try to solve that Circle problem of like, what do you do with the thing? And the answer is, well, they brought you the whole meal. So I think that's a business model we haven't seen. It's a reason why I'm actually optimistic about some of these broker-dealers getting in — where if they really choose to do it, they have the product, they have the architecture off the shelf. Another good example we haven't seen exactly where they're going yet would be Fidelity with their stablecoin.

Chris: Yeah, I think the USDG story is fascinating. If you want to come to my arcade, you got to use my token. The question that I want to see is: can I use that token at everyone else's arcade as well? And we'll see. This is a little bit different than the payments use case that we were talking about earlier. These worlds blend, but for the big exchanges, the big clearing houses, stablecoins are now your net interest income, which is a core, core part of your business model.

And I think everyone's looking at a stablecoin as, "hey, that's my net interest income." And it's just a fascinating place to be. Now, does USDG transcend itself into a payment stablecoin? Will you see a difference between net interest income, collateral on my exchange, float, whatever, versus payments use cases? The same asset — really, their gene. At some point, they'll be Genius-compliant, you can use them for everything.

I personally think you're going to see massive consolidation. Anytime you have regulated industries, you will see consolidation because the take is going to get squeezed more and more and more, and then it's just about scale and size. Don't know who those two or three are going to be. Don't know if it's going to be regionalized. Yes, I think there's going to be certain cases where if you come into my arcade you got to use my token, and people want to come to the arcade. I don't know, we're at the beginning of the story, fellas.

Ram: Alex, who do you think has got a better shot — JP Morgan with their stablecoin, or a Tempo offering from Stripe, or like a niche specialized stablecoin provider?

Alex: That's a good question. I mean, out of all the ones out there, I would think something like an OUSD has the best chance of at least appearing more neutral than a lot of the other ones. I think it's going to depend on how the governance really works for something like OUSD. But I don't know — we've seen a lot of these bank-related ones for a long time now and they've never really gone anywhere and they haven't really played nice with the outside world. So if they are willing to go in the direction of being more like a public blockchain and have some better interoperability, then maybe they have a chance. But until then, I'm pretty skeptical of the bank-led ones.

Ram: And what's your view on Stripe and where it stands today? You know, they've got a blockchain play. They're doing more M&A. They were priced really richly in 2021. What's your overall assessment on where Stripe is? Do they have more to lose, more to gain from where they are today?

Alex: Yeah, I think they're just a different stage than they were, right? They're just a more mature business than they were a few years ago. So that's why they're looking at new areas of growth, whether it's M&A, going deeper into stablecoins and crypto. But I can really just speak to their crypto and stablecoin plans — they're doing the best job by far out of any payments company. They're the only ones who I think are taking a shot at really trying to disrupt themselves before someone else disrupts them, and you got to give them credit for that. But I think they see where things are heading, and good for them for really making some serious bets there.

Austin: That's the right answer to get an acquisition offer from Stripe, so congrats there. I think that's all right. They're going to have to wait a few more years before that. So I'll close out — Alex, I know you need to step off here, but we appreciate you taking the time. And everybody listening, please pay attention to what Alex just said: disrupt yourself before others do it to you. This is the key to getting out of the innovator's dilemma. Spoken by somebody who's done this once already and is now doing it again. So Alex, thank you for joining us here. For everybody else, we're going to take a quick commercial break and we'll be right back.

Alex: Appreciate it, guys.
