股票、SpaceX IPO 与"周期顶信号"

Austin: All right, everybody. Welcome back. Let's talk about stocks. So there has been record U.S. equity issuance, which is often a late-cycle tell. And the SpaceX IPO — is that the round-trip poster child? It's already back below its 135 IPO price. It's trading slightly under 120 the last time I checked before this show, meaning about one trillion of market value is gone from the peak where it had traded up significantly right in the first days after the IPO.

What's going on underneath here? The AI capex boom is soaking up cash that companies would otherwise use to buy back their own stock. And we've been seeing, call it a structural drag on U.S. equities as a result of this. 2026 Q2 was a record quarter for U.S. equity issuance from Goldman. The Wall Street Journal framed this as running near the 2021 peak. It's one of the highest levels on record. The last comparable extremes were '21 and then 2000.

So SpaceX did the round trip that we just talked about. Securitize had originally jumped and now has fallen down from there as well. So Ram, I want to start with you on this. We're seeing record issuance, we're seeing buybacks being crowded out — are we seeing late-cycle top signals here? What is going on?

Ram: This isn't late cycle top signal. This is what happens after you have a couple events, one of which you pointed out, which was a SpaceX IPO. We had other events that were taking place also. The bicentennial is one of these mini euphoric moments — people get really excited and then they get less excited afterwards. Same thing happened after Trump's inauguration. If you remember, he was talking about nuclear space, rockets to the moon. You had this little mini euphoria and then some weakness after.

You also had Kevin Warsh, which had his first press conference, and he led with a more hawkish tone, which he has to, to instill credibility. So you had a lot of things going at the same time. And the other which really popped the bubble was Micron earnings. One day after Micron's earnings, you started to see deflation in the semiconductor category, which had an extraordinary momentum run-up.

On the issuance, it's not excessive yet. The pace of issuance is still considerably less than what we saw in 2021 with all the SPACs and the IPOs. You still have reasonable deal underwriting. Like I'm not going to go buy SpaceX — stock is overvalued. It's still low float, though, so it hasn't unlocked onto the market yet. So that can't be the driver. I think it's all of these events together. And then, of course, you've got the post-July seasonality that's showing up now.

You are seeing rallies in quality stocks like ICE, which I own, in Broadridge, which I just bought today. Insurance companies are making a comeback. So it's really a weakness in these year-to-date high momentum categories which sucked up so much capital and attention, and now that's released and flowing into other categories.

I saw an article in The Wall Street Journal today about this 19-year-old kid who said he sold Microsoft and Nvidia and he wanted to buy quote-unquote "aggressive," quote-unquote "high growth" stocks — names like in quantum and space. That's what's happening. That kid's probably not making the best decision now. I expect we're going to see dramatic cloud business growth from Google and Microsoft and Amazon. They've been left out in the cold. I expect their CapEx decisions are in fact productive, and there are opportunities in the quality parts of the market.

Apple：不烧钱做 AI 的另一条路

Austin: Another interesting one I've seen on that angle has been the recent performance of Apple. Ram, it's one of the companies that essentially did not burn a bunch of cash on AI and instead can just continue buying back stock. They seem to have let everybody turn right and continue going down that road and just continued on the path they were staying on. What do you make of that?

Ram: You're correct. I would say this: Apple's navigated it quite well. Tim Cook, who's handed the wheel and his job was not to mess it up. He bought back stock, had good capital allocation. He actually cut CapEx by scrapping Apple Car. He didn't blow a lot of capital on building the super AI Avengers team like Meta. They didn't spend a bunch of money on data centers. They did a deal with Google Gemini around how to reinvigorate Apple Siri product. So they did it in a capital-efficient way. It's a very thoughtful approach.

I would say this on Apple — it's now at a 35 times forward P/E. The last time we saw this was at August 2020. It's rarely been this pricey. Now, I doubt there's much upside left in Apple from where we stand.

Chris: We've seen the same theme in the crypto space for a very long time. How often have you seen TGEs or even IPOs with the crypto companies that we've seen of late spike on the narrative and then ultimately come down materially? I think investors are starting to realize that the narrative works until it doesn't. And I think you're seeing some just cyclical factors now where you believe and you believe and you believe. And then all of a sudden it's like, okay, that press release was amazing, I love the storyline, but let's get down to brass tacks.

Ultimately, investors need to focus on fundamentals and that's what will prevail longer term. Look, the economy is doing pretty good right now. We saw much softer CPI last week, softer PPI. Despite the fact that it looks like the war is starting to heat up again, oil hasn't gone to 200 overnight. It feels like we're fine. The war is the war — you can have uncertainty up or down, but it seems like that uncertainty level, the markets are getting used to this war.

And so it feels very constructive. I know I've said that for a while now — because again, between macro and some of the fundamental projects that we're seeing across our space, stay focused on fundamentals, not narrative. And that's the play.

Austin: So I guess as a final point on this one, what I'm trying to keep an eye on is, Ram, your point on valuations like Apple. Usually when valuations are this high on a forward basis, things don't look great in the United States, but we'll see where it goes. I'm not 100% certain that's the end state here. So time will tell.

AI 战场重估：Kimi K2 与开源中国模型的冲击

But speaking of things that have been disruptive for markets, let's talk a little bit about AI. So Beijing's Moonshot dropped Kimi K3 — an open-weight coding model — and we saw AI and chip stocks sold off across Asia and the U.S. Excuse me, the real drama, okay, this is the second case of cheap open-weight Chinese models pressuring the U.S. labs' moats. We all remember DeepSeek questioning the scarcity thesis given all the hundreds of billions of AI capex.

Is there going to be a rotation out of mega-cap AI? And there are nation-state level concerns here. On one level, there are a lot of contested points around this, right? The benchmarking story — allegedly they topped Anthropic and OpenAI on one leaderboard, but K3 also lands behind on many general tests. Did they optimize it for a specific test or specific benchmark? So we'll have to find out. Full public release for the model's weights are set for July 27. Anyone can download it, anyone can run it, and we'll see what happens there.

There's also a debate around moat and compute. So Chinese labs have figured out with equally great talents and cheaper access to usage data, they've been able to make similarly powerful models. I'm expecting a lot of competition from this playbook from Quixia Yin — apologies if I butchered that.

And then Peter Fenton was saying cheaper open models are bullish for the infrastructure layer — it's almost a thermonuclear battle to provide the compute. On the other hand, Gavin Baker via CoinDesk: K3 may be an important inflection point, potentially negative for Anthropic and OpenAI while being net positive for essentially every other company in the world.

So, Chris, we saw this with DeepSeek and CapEx and markets kept climbing. We also know the level of nation-state concerns here has been rising exponentially as models have improved in quality. What are you making of what's going on here?

Chris: First, I've been kind of blown away by the acceleration of innovation and the outputs that we're seeing. Every time there's a new model release, for a while they're just leapfrogging each other. But when you step back and see where these models and their outputs were a year ago, a year and a half ago, the pace and acceleration — we're in the middle of an arms race in certain ways. That's really — I mean, the technology is improving very very fast.

As a free markets person, you like to see competition, of course. To the extent state actors are involved, sometimes it's always not clean competition. I think it's just fascinating how the Chinese have gone open source, which is not normally their tact. And they're doing so because they have to. And the fact that they're able to create innovation in an environment where they don't have the compute tends to result in outputs that are highly optimized for what they're good at. And so I think there's a lot to learn.

Look, and I think America across corporations, they're grappling. They're like, "damn it, these token costs are so, so expensive." I mean, I'm seeing that in real time across the companies I deal with. It's unsustainable. And so to the extent that this puts pressure to optimize, this is not a usable technology if it blows through your tech budgets. We're already seeing rationing in many places because of the expenses here.

So hopefully in time, these expenses go down, the utility goes up, the deflationary impacts in the economy are realized. Very interesting volatile moment. And, you know, I can't wait to see how this plays out.

Ram: Yeah, no, I agree. It is a war with China for the next technology that is transformational. And China is playing warfare by offering these low-cost models — like this model, a 2.8 trillion parameter model, 1 million token context window, which rivals Google Gemini, and it's priced at a hundredth of what OpenAI is. So it is significantly negative for the equity of OpenAI, Anthropic.

There's a cloud business directly indexed to OpenAI — it's called Oracle. Oracle's got a big contract backlog where they face off with OpenAI and their stock price dropped 4% today. It's plumbing new lows as we speak — down 50% in the year. So it's not going to lower demand for semiconductors, though. We still need semiconductors. But it is creating credit risk if you face off with OpenAI, who signed up a trillion dollars in these revenue obligations. That's why Oracle is feeling the pain here.

There was a bit of an own goal here. The founder of that company is a Carnegie Mellon University graduate. So we lost this amazing talent to a geopolitical competitor, which is unfortunate. But I do agree with Gavin Baker's take. The cost of intelligence is dropping. That's a good thing. We need more intelligence. My wife would be the first to tell you that — we need it in our household, everywhere, with the real world. The next mission with AI is going beyond intelligence and touching workflows.

So overall, it's a great development for humanity, but there are local costs at these frontier labs that are going to face the brunt of greater competition.

AI 价值捕获：从"卖铲子"到"应用层"

Austin: So let me go to something that I have been observing here and sort of provoke a few more questions out of this, hopefully. If it is true that open-weight models are going to become highly performant and we can run them locally, what does that ultimately say about where the value capture in AI is? From an investment thesis perspective, where are the companies that created value — not just for the world, because I think all of us agree that AI models have created value for the world, but capturable long-term value for themselves?

Ram: I'll put an idea out there. Everyone knows semis are direct beneficiary of CapEx spend — that's the kind of CapEx receiver thesis. But they've re-rated, they're fully priced, now they're coming back down to earth.

I believe the next leg will be the AI beneficiaries leg — companies that can demonstrate strong productivity growth through the use of AI. Within that, financial services stand to benefit and healthcare. There are so many use cases in financial services. We're going to see an AI-native bank that's built front to back — from customer acquisition to customer servicing to underwriting to loan documentation execution. That's going to happen. That company doesn't exist. And you're going to see that in healthcare around diagnostics and sharing of information, proactive guidance to the patient. We haven't seen that take hold yet.

The ChatGPT moment was in Q4 2022. And we've still mostly been in the picks-and-shovels part of the investment thesis as opposed to AI beneficiaries.

Chris: So in crypto speak, you're talking about the app player.

Ram: Yes.

Chris: Right. That is the value accrual. In crypto land, we had the fat protocol thesis — where does value accrue to these blockchains? It's kind of like it rhymes here. And I agree around the value ultimately accrues to the app layer. And I think you're going to see some interesting differentiation on top.

And it's not going to be dissimilar to quantum. Ultimately, you're going to have these massive, expensive, potentially state-controlled quantum stacks. And guess what? Apps are going to probably be highly controlled by sovereigns who eventually launch them. And again, it's going to be the apps that end up driving that value. So it's kind of a similar thesis when you look at apps on blockchains, apps on AI stacks, ultimately apps on quantum stacks.

消费者会拉动医疗 AI 采用吗？

Austin: So I think one interesting point about the AI adoption thing is I wonder how much of this is going to start happening because they will be pulled along by consumers. And I say that specifically, Ram, because you raised healthcare. I know tons of people who are now taking things their doctors say to them or conditions that they have and putting it into AI and trying to get a first cut — not just from the perspective of like, "hey, diagnose me," but "before I go to a doctor, I want to understand more of what's going on here and have a vastly more informed conversation with the doctor than I would otherwise have."

I think there's also been a lot of testing of doctors' claims in ways that maybe were not there in the past. And essentially, my doctor thinks this, but let me put it into AI. Let me shop for a second doctor if I don't like it or don't think they're right. So I'm wondering — and I don't have an answer to this — if that behavior is already observable in the healthcare space or if we're too early in the AI adoption curve to really see the impact here.

But to me, there is a possibility industries will be forced to start adopting things. Because part of what I'm seeing in healthcare in particular — and I, as you can tell from my hair, was a mathematician by training originally. So if nobody was paying attention during the World Cup, apparently Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 solved the Jacobian conjecture, which is for non-mathematicians, I'm going to make this real simple: it's a roughly 90-year-old problem in algebraic geometry, it was proposed by Keller. And it basically was talking about how spaces behave.

And what Claude was able to do was provide a counterexample to the conjecture. It actually came up with a specific knowable computable counterexample to the conjecture saying "this cannot possibly be true in general, because here I found a specific time where it's not true." That is a huge result. This is one of the 25 most important problems hanging out there unsolved in mathematics right now.

How long until we're seeing that with the quality of these models with regard to healthcare? Ram, you know, good example: if I got cancer right now, I'm probably going to try to get it sequenced to go slam it into one of these things and be like, "okay, what do I do about this?" We may already be very close. And so I guess I'm asking the question of what is the pull function to increase the rate of adoption here?

Ram: Well, the doctor is using AI now for diagnostics. Doctors can get the skill level of like House now, right? So it has powerful diagnostics capabilities. So I think that that's there, but we still have so much more to go. The promise is still ahead of reality. Even now with the latest models, there's still opportunities for these models to improve.

And the Jacobian conjecture — it's a great accomplishment for math. Well, what real-world impact does it have? Real-world impact is: we found a way to target this cancer, or here's a way you design a fusion reactor that produces net energy, or here's a way you keep your telomerase longer for adenaline gel. We still haven't seen that kind of impact, which is actually what matters in the world.

Austin: I mean, have we not found some of those impacts? Now I'm shooting from the hip here, but haven't we had various results in healthcare already found by AI?

Ram: We had incredible protein folding, you know. Great. What's the consequence? We've had novel stories — the guy with Australia and his dog, there's another guy who diagnosed something with his daughter. There's some novel stories that are emerging and they have some nice local impacts. But we still have more to do.

Chris: Oh, there's tons more to do. But I think the acceleration is profound. I mean, across the board, every single discipline is being impacted.

Austin: I also wonder how much of that — what is the right way to say this — is adoption-based. Like doctors — I'm not trying to insult you guys, I have a lot of doctors and nurses in my family — but doctors are old. And so I'm wondering how much of this is just an adoption question, because I do think some of these impacts are large, like the AlphaFold thing which is what Chris is talking about.

Ram: For you, what it does is allows faster, cheap molecule design, novel proteins and specific like custom therapies for various diseases and mutations. So the impact is real. The problem is we've got to do all the studies or get emergency authorization to use these things. It seems like it's more the bureaucracy holding that up.

I think the adoption is self-financing because if you're using AI, you have an unnatural competitive advantage. For example, Eli Lilly is using AI and they're driving results. I know we're using AI in a lot of what we do that drives productivity. So if you are using AI, you're going to grow and take market share. And if you're not using AI, you're going to get computed out. So that's going to force adoption.

Chris: It's also evident on the battlefield — just ask Darriel.

战场 AI 与国家安全

Austin: Yeah, and I mean, not just there. As I've been observing this, right, like the battlefield iteration — as we start looking at the nature of warfare, if you look at what's going on with the front lines with Ukraine pushing Russia back, largely using almost entirely a drone-based strategy, some of which are human piloted, some of which are probably AI-like piloted, even if only over short frames right now. I think we're seeing more and more of that iteration.

Like today, all right, it's a cartoon, so let's not get overexcited here. But did either of you guys see the Anduril ad that dropped on X today? No, not yet. All right — so we've got self-flying helicopter drones that are now engaging in both target interception and disabling anti-air defenses to protect manned U.S. military craft. These things I think are coming pretty rapidly as people adopt.

So I'm going to bring this all the way back to where we started. And Chris, this one's for you to start. Oh good. If you're looking at what's going on in the frontier model space and you've seen the special pleading from OpenAI and Anthropic to restrict these things and keep them out of people's hands, but then China just drops an open-weight model that will do it all — how are you thinking about this from a national security perspective?

Chris: It's tricky. We're in a national race. Ram said it beautifully earlier. We have to be a leader in this technology, full stop. And we also have to build in the controls and the — at the end of the day, your technology reflects your ideals. And we need to make sure that it powers our ideals, and doesn't isn't used to change our ideals or go in the opposite direction of our ideals.

So, look, we have to accelerate. We have to be able to outperform our adversaries on the battlefield. There's a massive race going on. Talk to anyone in the defense space. And again, it's not only just AI — I think crypto has important national security aspects and there's also an arms race going on in the quantum side. Why? Because AI is accelerating all this stuff. So you got to own it.

How do you defend it? I don't know. Like Ukraine is a big laboratory. Everybody knows that. Lessons are being learned every day. And it's a very very dangerous world, but we have to really accelerate these technologies to understand them and to win.

Ram, what do you think?

Ram: Yeah, no, I agree. I think you just need policies to embrace and advance AI. There's a post by one of the members of the All In podcast who works for the admin. He's highlighting the regulatory constraints on AI adoption. He's a part of the administration. So you have to have the right policy in place to accelerate adoption.

China is very serious about this opportunity. The United States has the edge on capabilities. But there's a lot to do. And as we discussed earlier, the promise is significant, the potential for human impact is significant and positive.

In finance, we found that lit, predictable, regulated markets attract a lot more liquidity — because there's controls, there's a common set of rules. And that's where we found the most success in finance. In AI, there's a lot of concerns about sharing your information with some of these Chinese models. Is regulation going to be important? Yes, the right type of principle-based regulation will be. Because if you have models ultimately that you can't trust or hallucinate or you can't trust your data — I do think a thoughtfully regulated principles-based industry wins, based on my lessons in finance. But it's a different industry. Maybe I'm wrong.

Austin: So I'll give a point here on how this is so complicated. Pulling up some quotes from David Sachs right now on Twitter:

One: Kimi K3 just fixed 15 critical security bugs that Codex and Fable refused because of, quote, "cyber guardrails." There's no reason to limit American models on tasks that Chinese models handle without issue. We're only making ourselves less competitive.

Following tweet:

Here's another example: Hugging Face tried to use American frontier models to analyze an AI-powered cyber attack, but the guardrails blocked requests containing real exploit payloads. So they switched to GLM 5.2 running locally. The guardrails actually impaired defensive security.

And I think this gets to the point that both of you are raising, which is there's no way out of this race. It's going. You can choose not to run, but that doesn't mean the race isn't happening. The race is still happening. You just chose not to be involved.

Clarity Act：最后的窗口期

All right. So to get to our final topic where people also may or may not be choosing to not be involved — the Clarity Act. So, Chris, you and I have both been hearing things about Clarity today. I think let's start with the framing. Senate is on the clock. They go into recess first week of August. They need to get things moving if they want to get anything done. Once people get back from recess, I'm skeptical anything happens because of timing, must-pass bills, and the fact that a lot of them are going to be campaigning. So it's hard to get the votes. I think we agree this is the critical window. So I'll ask you, where do you see the bill right now?

Chris: I think we're going to get one shot on goal. And what I'm hoping to see is a vote. Prediction markets have pretty much given up at this point — I think they're in the 30s at this point, maybe even dropped closer to 30. They've given up.

But I don't know — I feel like there may be a comeback on the horizon. I remain hopeful because it's the right thing for the country. And a lot of these politicians will — you know, things get weird towards the end. We all know that. And I hope in the end, like even Senator Warner, who's a Democrat, had some generally positive things to say recently. So ethics is where it's at.

And will ethics ever be good enough for the Democrats vis-à-vis the president? No, they're going to continue to want to push it. But I'm very hopeful that we get something that's palatable. Because do you really want to be an individual who's heading into a very, very, very tight midterm race and you're on the run and you vote against something that sets fair shake and the crypto folks off?

If you're Schumer right now, you've got one thing on your mind. That's how do I get a majority? How do I respect control? And it all comes down to that electoral calculus. That's why if we get something to the floor, we get a vote on the books. That's where that electoral calculus comes into play. That's what I want to see. That's our best shot for succeeding. There were a couple of paths where we would have had a couple of shots on goal. I think we're down to one. I want to see it come to the floor.

Austin: So I'll say I agree on the one-shot-on-goal framing. We're going to get one good chance at this. And that's really all we're going to get given the timing. That's certainly true. I think you're right that ethics is where the rubber meets the road. I want to be clear and say the following: I think the BRCA — there's a place to get to yes from the Democrats on a bill that is actually good. I think as they've gotten more up to speed on it, they've realized some of the lines they were being fed were probably not totally accurate. If you look closely at things like Tornado Cash and the DOJ and FinCEN literally disagreeing with each other, we do need clarity on how these things work — pun not intended.

I think that's solvable. I think on the yield compromise, if I'm being honest, the big banks have alienated so many people now they've just moved on. That's over.

Ram: They haven't moved on. They want clarity. They want clarity badly.

Austin: Well, now they want clarity because they've realized if they don't get it, they're stuck with Genius, which is even more permissive than what they were —

Ram: It's more than that though, Austin. They want clarity because of federal preemption. They do not operate well in state-regulated things.

Austin: See Illinois slapping a transfer tax that doesn't work on crypto, right?

Ram: They want federal preemption. They're going to press the throttle down. We get this bill, you're going to see banks come in. We get this bill, you're going to see Basel start looking better. And then once we have bank liquidity, it's a brand new world for crypto.

Austin: And to the electoral math, I actually think one of the things that could be helping the bill that people have not thought about is how badly the Democrats seem to be doing picking functional candidates in Maine and Michigan. By which I mean, if the Republicans are looking at it and being like, "we like the territory, we're going to play offense not defense," they may force a vote for that exact reason.

I think the key to getting it through is going to be: can we get an ethics provision that is stiff enough that the administration probably doesn't like it, that the Democrats will vote for, but is not so stiff that somebody is going to throw their body in front of it to stop it? There is some sort of pivot point there. And to me, Chris, you tell me if you disagree — I think that efficient balance is probably forcing the Trump family out of their crypto endeavors, but without punishing them for things they did before the ethics guardrails were in place.

Chris: I haven't seen the text. I know blind trust will have something to do with it. Yep. You hate to have individuals specifically targeted for any political reason whatsoever. It needs to apply to both sides. I hope they compromise. And then if we have something good on the ethics side, well, let's adopt it for every other market because we have equal problems there.

Austin: I was going to say that may be the other playback from the Democrats that I do think would be helpful — is if congresspeople can't run individual crypto projects, why are they trading individual stocks?

Chris: All right. Yes, sir.

收尾

Austin: So on that note, we will know in a couple of weeks whether Clarity is going to move or not. But for this week, today, we are out of time. So thank you as always for joining us for Bits and Bips.

One last reminder before you go — Bits and Bips has its own channels now. The old feed won't carry these episodes anymore. Subscribe so you don't miss a single one. Now, thank you for joining us. We'll be back in one week to discuss more about how the worlds of crypto and macro are colliding.

Until then, everyone — thank you for watching and hope you enjoyed this episode of Bits and Bips. Just remember, nothing we say here is investment advice. And please check unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips for more disclosures.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、稳定币战争的真正战场：不是技术，而是"自我颠覆的意愿"
表面上这集在讨论 Stripe 收购 PayPal、Circle 被挑战、USDG 借 Robinhood 上位——听起来是产品与分发之争。但真正被反复触碰的隐藏结构是：**PayPal 不是败给了技术，而是败给了自己的合规律师和董事会**。Austin 说了一句极其耐人寻味的话——"PayPal chose to fail"。这不是竞争失败，这是**主动选择的失败**。
这里有一个反直觉的观察：**在稳定币这个赛道，拥有最多用户的公司反而最难成功**。因为用户越多、监管暴露越大、律师越保守、"母舰保护逻辑"越强。PYUSD 的失败不是产品失败，是**组织免疫系统排斥新器官**的故事。这也解释了为什么真正跑出来的往往是 Robinhood 这种"体量足够大能给分发、但小到还敢冒险"的中段玩家。
跨领域连接：这和生物学里的**"体型-代谢-创新窗口"**规律惊人一致。大象不能跳跃，不是因为它没肌肉，而是因为它的骨骼-质量比不允许。PayPal 的组织质量太大，反而失去了做稳定币最需要的那种"跳跃动作"。
## 二、

item.title: [a16z] American Dynamism Presents
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、三个"下潜"：硅谷正在从平面撤回 Z 轴

三段发言看似跨越海洋、矿业、核能三个毫不相关的领域，但如果把它们叠在同一张坐标系上，你会看到一个惊人的方向性一致——它们全部在往"Z 轴"走：Ulysses 向下潜入海底，Moriana 向下钻入地壳，微反应堆则把人类反向（深空）投射。过去二十年硅谷把力气花在 X-Y 平面上的"信息流铺展"（社交、SaaS、消费应用），而 American Dynamism 这批公司在做的其实是同一件事的镜像：放弃平面，重新征服深度。这不是"硬科技回归"这么简单——这是硅谷第一次公开承认，抽象层已经吃到了天花板。

二、隐藏的产业三角：这三家公司其实是一个闭环

大多数人把它看作 a16z "投了三个不同赛道"，但它们其实构成一个自洽的工业环路，出人意料地相互喂养：

  • 微反应堆需要铀和稀土 → Moriana 挖
  • 深海多金属结核矿床需要 24/7 水下自动化作业 → Ulysses 潜
  • 自主水下机器人集群需要长时供电与离岸能源 → 微反应堆供

换句话说，你以为这是"分散配置"，其实是一盘垂直整合的棋——一个不依赖现有石化-电网-港口体系的平行工业基础层。这个视角一旦打开，你会发现这不是三家公司，而是一个尚未命名的"新工业操作系统"的三个原生模块。

三、语言的秘密：他们全部跳过了"产品"，直接讲"文明"

一个几乎没人注意的细节：这三位创始人没有一个人提到客户、市场、收入或产品功能。Turner 说的是"整个行业"；微反应堆创始人直接跳到"人类作为文明离开出生的恒星"。这是一种典型的目的论式路演（teleological pitching）——不卖功能，卖必然性。

跨领域联想：这种语言结构与其说像科技创业路演，不如说更像 19 世纪的铁路大亨宣言，甚至像宗教劝进书。它标志着一次语义转向：核能不再是"清洁电力"，而是"星际移民技术"；矿业不再是"大宗商品"，而是"文明基础设施"；水下机器人不再是"作业工具"，而是"海洋神经系统"。当一个行业开始用这种终末论口吻说话，通常意味着资本正在为它准备一个远超当下市场规模的估值框架。

一个反直觉的收尾观察

有趣的是，"American Dynamism"这个词本身就在暗示：美国正在把创新的语法从"颠覆（disruption）"换成"动能（dynamism）"。前者是破坏性的、面向存量的；后者是牛顿式的、面向真空的。这不只是词汇变化——它意味着这波公司默认前方是空的（海底、地壳、太空），不需要打败谁，只需要率先抵达。这是一种极其罕见的、类似"新大陆心态"的投资叙事，上一次出现是 1990 年代的互联网。

📝 转录文本

Ulysses — Underwater Robotics

We started Ulysses 3 years ago in the effort to build underwater robots to solve a very specific problem.

The broader vision of what we're doing is that you have a network of surface and undersea vehicles, and they're operating 24/7 to solve these critical tasks all around the ocean.

Moriana Minerals — Modernizing the Minerals Industry

There's this minerals industry that no one has yet really taken today's modern technologies and applied it holistically to the industry. And so we see a bunch of opportunity to go and attack it.

My name is Turner Caldwell. I'm the CEO of Moriana Minerals.

Nuclear Micro Reactors — Leaving Our Star

Nuclear micro reactors that are mass produced, that are reliable in the most wild scenario, enables humans to leave the star that we were born at — the sun — and go to other ones.

And it's imperative that we do it as a civilization.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Institutions Holding Off WWIII – Sarah Paine
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：和平的建筑师，是被战争碾碎的一代

Paine 在这里悄悄埋了一个心理学的炸弹——UN、IMF、NATO、WTO、EU 这些今天被视为"精英俱乐部"的国际机构，其实是一群创伤最深的人在集体 PTSD 里搭出来的避难所。他们一战被送去越壕沟，大萧条中养家，二战又把自己的孩子送上前线。三次死亡循环之后，他们得出的结论不是"要更强的军队"，而是"要更多的律师和外交官"。

这里藏着一个让人后背发凉的推论：当一代人没有经历过体系性的崩溃，他们就会开始厌倦维持体系的成本。 今天欧美对多边机构的疲惫、对"规则"的嘲讽，本质上不是政治问题，而是代际记忆的自然衰减。Putin 之所以敢动手，某种程度上是因为记得战壕的人全死光了。

跨领域连接：陆权 vs 海权 = Web2 平台 vs Web1 割据

Paine 说陆权玩的是"负和游戏"——你抢邻居的地，抢的过程中把地打烂了。这套逻辑在数字时代有个精确的镜像：封闭生态 vs 开放协议。

  • 陆权 = 苹果生态 / 微信小程序 / 抖音闭环：控制你能触达的每一寸"领土"，通过内部通信线（interior lines）榨取租金。
  • 海权 = TCP/IP / 集装箱 / SWIFT：不控制节点，只控制协议。任何人接入都能受益，网络效应产生正和增长。

更妙的是，Paine 提到"maritime powers 通过 exterior lines 触达全球"——这几乎是平台经济学的地缘政治版本。海洋国家不是征服海洋，而是把海洋做成了低摩擦的交易层（transaction layer）。今天美国跟中国的真正竞争，不在于谁的芯片更快，而在于谁的"海"更多人愿意航行——美元、SWIFT、英语、普通法、云计算，都是这片"海"的组成部分。

出人意料的暗线：乌克兰战争其实是 Mackinder 的"复仇"

结尾处 Paine 一句话轻轻带过——冷战的热战都发生在"Mackinder 内边缘新月带"（Intermarginal Crescent）——海权与陆权的摩擦生热带。但如果你打开地图看：乌克兰正好躺在这条新月带的最中央。

这里有个让人不安的洞见：Putin 打的不是乌克兰，他打的是"新月带的物理属性"。 只要地球还是这个形状，只要黑海还在那儿，只要顿巴斯的黑土还能通粮食走管道，这场战争的地理逻辑就会一次次地重演——不管沙皇、苏联还是俄联邦在克里姆林宫。

更深的一层反转：Paine 暗示，冷战期间"全球增长率上不去"不是因为技术不够，而是因为陆海两派都在新月带"按秤盘"——一方按下去，另一方按下去，秤永远不平，但秤上的东西一直在被消耗。这解释了一个经济学长期困惑的现象：为什么 1945-1990 年的技术革命（半导体、抗生素、喷气机）没能带来匹配的经济起飞？答案可能不是"技术不够好"，而是"新月带的血在偷偷抽走复利"。

一个衍生的、几乎无人讨论的推论：今天 AI 革命的经济回报，很可能同样会被下一轮陆海冲突的"新月带征税"稀释掉。 台海就是新的顿巴斯。

📝 转录文本

The End of Cold War I: A Miracle of Our Lifetimes

Uh, if you look at the end of the last Cold War, too — sadly we're into the second one — but look at how world trade just takes off. It was a miracle of your lifetimes. It lifted hundreds of millions of people out of poverty.

Uh, the problem for the likes of Putin is that this wealth-producing trade was not happening at his house, but the houses of those vested in the trading order, not in extortion. But with the second Cold War, we're into more inefficient things again.

Interior vs. Exterior Lines: The Third Distinguishing Characteristic

All right, this brings me to the third distinguishing characteristic of continental and maritime powers. And it has to do with the transportation revolution.

So, continental powers historically have leveraged interior lines of communication that allow them to garrison their empire, deploy their armies against neighbors, and then to form a contiguous alliance system. And that worked well when uh, power was a function of land. Well, that's not true anymore.

The maritime powers uh, are using these exterior lines of communication, the ones by sea, to give them access not just to the people right next to them, but to the entire globe — which opens the possibility for them to form a global alliance system. And from this reliance on interior versus uh, exterior lines of communication, it gives you the possibility of what sort of alliance systems that you can have.

The Industrial Revolution Puts Continental Empires on Notice

All right, the industrial revolution put uh, continental empires on notice. Not simply because land transport is so much uh, more expensive than sea transport, but but but because it opened the possibility of a positive-sum world order.

The land empire MO of beating up all your neighbors — well, it's negative-sum. What do I mean by that? Is you're fighting over territory, you're damaging the goods while you're fighting. So, whatever someone lost in pristine territory now looks like this. And it's negative sum. You're destroying wealth at a very rapid clip.

Whereas, the maritime world is: "No, boys. Don't do it that way. You want to have trading partners. You want to have it win-win. So, everybody makes money. So, we have compounded growth." And this maritime order is focusing on freedom of navigation, free trade, international laws and institutions that facilitate trade in order to minimize transaction costs so we can maximize the money.

Alliances as the Ultimate Insurance Policy

And the insurance system for all of this is alliance system. So, most countries aren't maritime by geography. But if they all gang up together and they coordinate diverse instruments of national power when they're dealing with these rogue continental states that want to upend the trading system, they can turn out in force to protect the rules that protect them all.

And here's the ultimate insurance policy that was created by the greatest generation — which were the conscripts of World War I. The people who were told to go up and over trenches and to see how that would go. And the ones who survived came back and tried to raise their families during the Great Depression. And then by the time they were of the age to be strategic leaders, they're off sending their own kids to fight World War II.

And they concluded — and this is, this generation is in Europe and the United States both — they concluded that the solution to Great Depressions and World Wars was institution building on a global scale. And they built institutions that have lasted to the present and held the peace, until in the industrialized world until Vladimir Putin decided to word cuz magic.

But they create the UN, the IMF, NATO, the predecessor institutions of the World Trade Organization, and also of the the European Union. And their idea is: we must hash out our differences with diplomats and lawyers, cuz when you send the soldiers in, whatever gains you think you're going to get are going to be inferior to what you would have done with the diplomats. It's — it's too ruinous fighting each other.

The Cold War's Hot Edge: Mackinder's Intermarginal Crescent

All right. So, if you look at where the fighting that did go on in the Cold War — cuz it was cold in the industrialized world, but it was really hot elsewhere. A lot of it's on this Mackinder's Intermarginal Crescent, which is where the maritime and continental worlds hit.

So, if there's civil wars going on in there, uh the one side of the Cold War wants to put its finger on the scale, and the other one wants to put its finger on the scale, and there we go. And that is all wealth-destroying, and helps explain why growth rates and things weren't as uh — or globally had problems — cuz you're destroying wealth. All right.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here, and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：和平的建筑师，是被战争碾碎的一代
Paine 在这里悄悄埋了一个心理学的炸弹——UN、IMF、NATO、WTO、EU 这些今天被视为"精英俱乐部"的国际机构，其实是一群**创伤最深的人**在集体 PTSD 里搭出来的避难所。他们一战被送去越壕沟，大萧条中养家，二战又把自己的孩子送上前线。三次死亡循环之后，他们得出的结论不是"要更强的军队"，而是"要更多的律师和外交官"。
这里藏着一个让人后背发凉的推论：**当一代人没有经历过体系性的崩溃，他们就会开始厌倦维持体系的成本。** 今天欧美对多边机构的疲惫、对"规则"的嘲讽，本质上不是政治问题，而是**代际记忆的自然衰减**。Putin 之所以敢动手，某种程度上是因为记得战壕的人全死光了。
## 跨领域连接：陆权 vs 海权 = Web2 平台 vs Web1 割据
Paine 说陆权玩的是"负和游戏"——你抢邻居的地，抢的过程中把地打烂了。这套逻辑在数字时代有个精确的镜像：**封闭生态 vs 开放协议**。
- 陆权 = 苹果生态 / 微信小程序 / 抖音闭环：控制你能触达的每一寸"领土"，通过内部通信线（

item.title: [YC Root Access] The First Dedicated YC GPU Cluster - With Together AI
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的角色反转：YC 正在变成"算力工会"

这场对话表面在讲 GPU 集群，真正发生的却是 YC 商业模式的一次静默突变。Jared 自己点破了那个关键词——"founders union"（创始人工会）。这不是修辞。传统 VC 卖的是"钱 + 建议 + 网络"，而 YC 现在开始卖 集体议价权：用 8000+ 组合公司的规模去谈一个单个 seed 阶段公司永远拿不到的算力合约。

这背后的对标不是任何一家 VC，而是——医疗行业里的 GPO（Group Purchasing Organization）。医院联合起来集中采购药品和器械，因为单个医院议价能力太弱。YC 在做的事情结构上一模一样，只不过采购的是 H100 而不是心脏支架。一旦这个类比成立，你会预感到：下一步 YC 大概率会把这套"集采模式"扩展到能源合约、数据标注、甚至合成生物学的湿实验室时间。VC 正在悄悄变成一个 共享基础设施运营商。

二、最反直觉的观察：AI 公司正在"反向变成制药公司"

Vipul 顺口提到自己 2018 年做的是 pharma 公司，Jared 顺势说"现在连保险、医疗都成了软件公司"。但对话里没人点破的是——方向是双向的。

软件行业过去 20 年最大的骄傲是"零边际成本"：写一次代码卖一亿次。但你仔细听 Vipul 说的那句话："compute is going to be the biggest expense in some ways for development and product。" 这句话在传统 SaaS 语境里是荒谬的——研发成本怎么可能是最大开支？可如果把它翻译成 pharma 语言，就完全自然了：训练一个模型 ≈ 一次三期临床试验；FLOPS ≈ 试剂消耗；GPU cluster ≈ pilot plant。

于是我们看到一个惊人的对称： - 传统行业（保险、医疗）在软件化，从资本密集走向零边际成本 - AI 原生公司在制药化，从零边际成本重新走向资本密集

"软件吃世界"这句话在 2026 年正在被改写为"世界重新吃软件"。而 YC 的这次动作，本质上是承认了这个转向——它开始像 Big Pharma 的 CDMO（合同开发生产组织）一样为 portfolio 提供共享"生产设备"。

三、被忽略的定价曲线：Jevons 悖论在 AI 里正在爆发

Vipul 说了一句技术味道很浓的话，但含义远超技术："as models get better, the value of tokens is increasing, which has an impact on the value of flops."

这句话的经济学翻译是：算力不但没有因摩尔定律而变便宜，反而因为下游价值提升而变得越来越贵。这是 19 世纪煤炭经济学里的 Jevons 悖论——效率提升不减少消耗，反而放大消耗，因为每一单位的算力现在能变现更多。

跨领域连接：这跟纽约地租的逻辑完全一样。曼哈顿的写字楼没有变得更"好用"，是华尔街的产出让每一平方英尺的租金水涨船高。GPU 集群正在成为 AI 产业的"曼哈顿地产"——供给曲线几乎垂直，需求曲线在无限上移。

于是隐藏的商业洞见浮出水面：Together AI + YC 这个合作最有价值的部分不是"便宜的 GPU"，而是"把 24 个月的地租承诺切成几周的短租"。他们在算力市场里发明了 WeWork 模式——只不过 WeWork 输在了办公空间需求缩水，而算力需求还在指数级扩张，所以这次"WeWork for GPUs"可能真的会成。

四、一个可能被低估的信号

留意 Jared 提到的一个具体数字："公司为下两年算力所需的预付款 超过了它们当前的现金余额。" 这不是一句轻描淡写的话——这意味着一大批 AI 原生 startup 在传统资本结构下 数学上不可能存在。YC 这次实际上是在用组合基金的信用给单个 startup 做算力层的债务证券化。

如果这个模式跑通，下一步逻辑上必然出现——GPU-backed securities（算力资产支持证券）。就像 2000 年代的按揭证券化一样，会有人把"未来 GPU 使用权"打包成金融产品在二级市场交易。你今天在这条 YC 播客里看到的，可能是一个全新资产类别的胚胎。

📝 转录文本

Intro

[music] Hey everyone, I'm here with Vipul, CEO and co-founder of Together AI, and we've got some exciting news. YC and Together are partnering to bring online the first dedicated YC GPU cluster. This gives our portfolio of AI-native startups easier access to the compute they need to build and scale.

Vipul, thanks for being here.

So great to be here.

What Together AI Does

Vipul, why don't you start by telling us a bit about Together AI. What exactly do you guys do?

Yeah. So, you know, we started the company almost exactly four years ago and, as you know, my co-founders are professors and academics, and we were quite convinced at the time that generative AI was going to be a very foundational technology. And I think we also had a sense that, because of the capital structure required, we may see large concentration, and we really wanted to create a platform for innovation — for, you know, the sort of participatory side of Together.

So what we've built at Together AI is really a cloud service that's designed for the whole life cycle of generative AI, which is everything from building models to post-training open models to serving them at scale.

Who Uses Together

Nice. And could you tell us a little bit about how companies actually work with you? You know, YC — we acquired the first GPU cluster for YC itself to share to our companies. Tell us a little bit about the types of companies that have worked with Together in the past.

Yeah, you know, our focus is really on AI-native startups, and we have over 8,000 customers today. And this ranges from, you know, small research groups who are sort of still figuring out what they want to do and experimenting — and this is just such an empirical field that we enable them with access to compute or access to inference services — all the way to some of the biggest names like Cursor and Cognition and 11 Labs. And these companies are training models, and we're serving their models at scale for them.

Yeah. One of the really incredible things from my perspective over the last year has been all the different types of what we would call AI-native startups that have been built, right? You have companies that are training their own models from scratch, where they're now able to use this GPU cluster to get access to compute, right? Especially in this environment.

You have AI-native services companies that are often building on top of models and increasingly building on top of open-source models hosted at places like Together. And then you have kind of everything in between, from data providers to application-layer companies. So there's this whole landscape of companies in the YC portfolio, and they all have kind of very heterogeneous compute needs. And so I think for us, having a partner where there's different ways to work with you guys has been a big win for a lot of companies.

Yeah. And I think that flexibility is particularly important because you're trying to be efficient with your capital, and being able to deploy it behind the workloads you want — and you want really rapid sort of iteration. I think we have sort of really helped create a platform that allows companies to be able to do all these things efficiently.

Building an AI-Native Company Today

Building a company in today's AI-native world is quite different, I imagine, from the days you started your company.

Uh, what are you seeing? What do you think are the main challenges that YC startups are experiencing?

Yeah, I think there's a bunch of different ones all at once. It's a very exciting time at YC. I think one of the really cool things is way more things look like software companies now than ever before. Like when I ran my company, we were a pharma company — this was back in 2018. At the time, saying that you were a software-first pharma company was sort of an absurd concept.

Now I think it is actually very common to think of industries in which things you don't necessarily think of as software companies — whether it's insurance or healthcare or pharma or whatever else — are software companies for the first time. Which expands just the pie of things that people might work on in Y Combinator.

At the same time, it means there's whole new challenges. Right, there's whole new industries in which people are facing bottlenecks that they didn't face before. So on compute — right, one of the things that's very different, you know, eight years ago when I was running my company, we could just like go to AWS and get a thousand, you know, instances with GPUs, and yeah, they were like maybe expensive or whatever, but it wasn't that hard to get them. Like, you could get them, and you can get them on spot instances, and we don't have to do long reservations and whatnot.

I think now what we see is, as people's compute needs go up, actually just having access to capacity is a really big problem — let alone great pricing. And I think, you know, for us, with this partnership, being able to secure capacity, secure it at a really great price, have great support also available, and make it so startups don't have to commit to two years — they only have to commit to a few weeks, right? Makes it so that for startups that have these heterogeneous computes, you can kind of solve for that.

And so it kind of solves for this bottleneck that can help them access all of these weird new markets that are now for the first time software problems.

Compute as a Fund-Level Differentiator

And do you see this becoming a differentiator from the perspective of the fund? It is becoming harder and harder, and I feel as models are getting better, right, the value of tokens that are being produced is increasing, which sort of has an impact on the value of flops and the cost of compute. And you may have this curve of, you know, ever-expanding cost of compute. So finding ways of enabling companies seems important.

Absolutely. Especially because, you know, YC has become probably the biggest seed funder of research-style companies. You know, probably the very first real machine learning seed investment was literally OpenAI.

Uh, OpenAI was built as YC Research.

Um, when Sam was president here. And then since then we've funded all sorts of companies developing their own models in a bunch of different verticals. There's companies like Deepgram making voice AI models. There's companies that are then distributing those and building their own companies, like Vapi for example, or Retell — voice AI companies. There's a new one called Miso Labs — just launched a few weeks ago — that has an incredible new emotive voice model.

In other domains, like in language, you have companies building new edge device models or different variants of model architectures that aren't the ones that are currently already being scaled up. And then you have this whole long chain of other modalities in which people are doing research, whether it's in biology or in healthcare or kind of you name it.

And so I think what's really exciting right now — and actually I didn't even say anything about hard tech, where there's actually tons of interesting robotics companies that we're funding these days. And with a lot of these, um, they are research bets. They are not companies where we expect them to commercialize in significant ways immediately. They're ones where they have to solve a hard technical problem, and access to compute ends up being a big bottleneck for a lot of them.

And so yeah, we very much see securing access to compute as a really important thing — to attract the best founders to do, and then also to enable them to succeed both during the program and afterwards. Like what's great is companies can get on this cluster, or you know, our future ones, basically during the batch, and then continue to grow with whatever their compute provider is for a very long time.

Yeah, yeah. And we are definitely seeing that. It's been pretty exciting to track the journey of some of these companies that we've onboarded.

Why Together for the Partnership

What was it about Together that led you to do this partnership with us?

[YC's next batch is now taking applications. Got a startup in you? Apply at ycombinator.com/apply. It's never too early, and filling out the app will level up your idea. Okay, [music] back to the video.]

There was a few things. Um, one is we really prioritize speed. I think being able to work with a partner who is excited to get a cluster up really fast, and then also build it in a way that works with startups with lots of different needs.

You know, we have some companies who are just looking for a single node and they just want access now, but they're in the scaling mode where you could totally see them becoming a lot bigger. There's others who are buying 256 GPUs on day one and looking to scale to several thousand very soon. And being able to offer a product that actually supports all of those use cases was a big ask.

And then the second was having a great engineering and research team that could help actually enable the best practices in these companies. You know, a lot of the founders — they have really great engineering backgrounds. They have trained models in other places where there's been managed compute available to them. They haven't necessarily had to manage their own compute cluster. And so having a partner that can help with just showing the ropes, so that, um, yeah, of course there's a lot that's managed by Together directly, there's some parts that the founder themselves need to figure out, but they can make it so it's not a zero-to-one right away, right? And there's a lot of best practices to encode. I think that was a big part of it.

And then I will say, like, just lastly personally, I've actually followed a lot of the research from Together for many years. Like I'm friends with Ce and I've, you know, seen some of the work that they've put out in research papers in the past.

Research and Product

And so actually maybe something I'd be curious if you want to share a little bit more about — like how do you guys think about all of your research efforts, you know, work like FlashAttention for example, and all the other work you guys do, and how that fits into your product offering. Like how do you balance those two things and how do companies benefit from that?

When we founded Together, we felt that there was going to be quite a bit of research and effort that's going to go into making models better — you know, reducing loss and building better and better models. And there was this entire space of production AI, which — it's a science and it was understudied, and it's really kind of, you know, linear algebra executing in these novel accelerators. It's a new form of computer science, and we felt it was going to be very important, because this is a very, very scaled endeavor, and [snorts] you are deploying large amounts of capital. The cost of training and the cost of producing tokens is very high, and you want to make it really efficient.

So a lot of our research has focused on AI systems — from, you know, approaches to doing attention, and that continues to be a really sort of rich field, as now, you know, attention is going after longer and longer context — to, you know, architectures. We also did work on the Mamba architecture, which is now making its way into, you know, long-context attention like in the Nemotron.

I was going to say that the latest Nemotron models also seem to use it, right?

Yeah. And compilers. And so this is a very interesting and productive area of research for us, and for the companies that work with us, because we can accelerate their workloads, make them generally achieve the best unit economics possible on these workloads. And that's really — it's very useful to early startups, but then also the scaled ones who care a lot about the economics of their applications.

Preparing for the Compute Shortage

Yeah. I was going to ask you on that latter point — like, you know, when you think about a lot of the research companies you've worked with, you've seen a lot of them scale on Together platform. What should early-stage companies who are starting to use the Together cluster or a different compute provider think about now to prepare for what's coming, both in terms of the compute shortage and how to sort of set themselves up for success as their company starts to work and get product-market fit?

Yeah, you know, there's so much infrastructure now, right, relative to even a couple of years ago. From really frontier-class open models that are accessible, there's so much machinery and [clears throat] tooling for, you know, training models, post-training them. There's rich sources of data sets. So I think you can be incredibly efficient in terms of kind of using this machinery and deploying it, and, you know, we do see many of the YC startups we're working with are very proficient with the current set of tools that exist.

I think the compute planning is definitely something that — it's complex, right? When you are a seed-stage company, you have a certain amount of capital and your sort of perspective of what you're going to do with it is very different from, you know, once you get to a next level of scale. And so I think part of what we are trying to do is build products and price and package them in ways that allow companies to be able to scale up to the next stage. And I think some of the collaborations that we are doing are going to be sort of essential in, you know, helping companies underwrite. And I think this is where companies are very different from software companies — because you really have to think about this is going to be the biggest expense in some ways for development and product.

Yeah, yeah. I mean, I think a big challenge we see — and I think we saw it before, having what really motivated setting up this cluster, was a lot of companies… just to put into numbers, you know, the upfront they would need to pay in order to secure the capacity for their next two years of compute was greater than their current cash balance. And so you just could not do that unless you go and raise and basically put all of your money into a compute partner.

And so a big thing we look to solve with this is, you know, use some of YC's scale so that we can secure access to that compute, so an individual startup doesn't have to make a 24-month bet. They can make a few-month bet instead, which is much more doable with access to capital dollars in the way they currently flow. And so they can kind of continuously scale and not have to lock up all their cash basically, and make it so, you know, they raise at more effective terms for themselves.

Feedback From the Portfolio

Yeah, yeah, absolutely. What is the kind of feedback that you're hearing from these companies?

Oh, it's been great. I mean, I think — well, what's very cool is, you know, there's lots of different companies who have different needs. You know, some with training clusters versus inference clusters. I think they've really loved working with your guys' support team to build the right kind of workflows to work really well and understand the kind of realistic underlying compute setups to actually have really good performance and availability.

And then we've made it so that they can sort of book in advance, and now companies can plan out their compute several months in advance, which has been very helpful for them, where some of them might know that they need to do a big training run three months from now, and they can choose to buy a relatively small amount of compute for a while and then a much larger amount. And that flexibility is great for them.

And, you know, across the portfolio of companies, there's still enough to service all the demand at all times. So the cluster is at 100% utilization, but for any individual company who doesn't have, you know, a fixed utilization, it's really helpful to have this sort of lever they can pull and plan out in advance.

Looking Forward

Yeah, yeah. I'm really looking forward to expanding this. I think it's working. It's been a very creative way of solving this problem, and I think it's working really well. And I think this is going to become essential to startups who are thinking about creating AI products.

Yeah, I think you're going to be hearing from me quite a lot about how we can make this thing even bigger. I think in general, you know, at YC, we think of ourselves as sort of a founders union. You know, I think a lot of how we — what we push to do is, regardless of how someone's building technology, whether it's a research company or an application company or whatever else, is to give the shared resources to allow the founder to have a lot of the leverage that a much bigger company might have — whether that's in terms of raising capital and having things like Demo Day to support that, or a GPU cluster in the way that a bigger company might have, or access to certain advisors or whatever else. And, um, yeah, I'm excited to bring compute as one of the levers we can pull to kind of enable that for people.

I'm really excited for where this is going to go.

Awesome.

Cool. Thanks so much for coming. This is going to be awesome, and excited to keep working with you guys.

Thanks for having me.

[music] >> [music]

观看原视频 →

## 一、隐藏的角色反转：YC 正在变成"算力工会"
这场对话表面在讲 GPU 集群，真正发生的却是 **YC 商业模式的一次静默突变**。Jared 自己点破了那个关键词——"founders union"（创始人工会）。这不是修辞。传统 VC 卖的是"钱 + 建议 + 网络"，而 YC 现在开始卖 **集体议价权**：用 8000+ 组合公司的规模去谈一个单个 seed 阶段公司永远拿不到的算力合约。
这背后的对标不是任何一家 VC，而是——**医疗行业里的 GPO（Group Purchasing Organization）**。医院联合起来集中采购药品和器械，因为单个医院议价能力太弱。YC 在做的事情结构上一模一样，只不过采购的是 H100 而不是心脏支架。一旦这个类比成立，你会预感到：**下一步 YC 大概率会把这套"集采模式"扩展到能源合约、数据标注、甚至合成生物学的湿实验室时间**。VC 正在悄悄变成一个 **共享基础设施运营商**。
