item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1455期】7月大轮动，谷歌决定芯片生死，财报展望✨20260719#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"现金流反转"：芯片与云厂的角色互换

视频里最容易被一带而过、但其实最锋利的一张图，是那条紫线（半导体未来12月现金流冲到4500亿美元）与黄线（超大规模云厂现金流从3000亿跌向近零）的十字交叉。

这不是普通的板块轮动，而是产业链话语权的一次结构性倒置。过去十年，云厂是"甲方"，芯片厂被采购、被压价；现在的图景变成——云厂正在把自己所有的自由现金流"倒进"芯片厂的口袋里。用生态学的比喻，这不是共生，是寄主与寄生虫的暂时性反转：芯片是宿主，云厂是被抽干的宿主。

出人意料的推论：市场担心"大科技会不会砍资本开支"，其实问反了方向。真正应该焦虑的是——当云厂现金流真的归零，谁来接盘下一轮资本开支？ 现在托底的是"未来AI收入"这张远期支票。这张支票的兑付节点，恰好就是主播提到的"Google Agentic Commerce落地"。所以谷歌财报的关键根本不在capex数字，而在C端AI变现的第一声硬币落地声。

二、Russell 2000的"身份漂移"：一个指数正在悄悄背叛自己

主播提到罗素2000因为里面的AI权重股（如Bloom Energy）大跌而拖累整个指数，这句话背后藏着一个指数认同危机：

罗素2000被投资界作为"小盘股、市场风险偏好、经济健康度"的代理指标使用了三十年。但现在它已经不再代表小盘股情绪——它代表的是"AI基础设施的能源边角料"。

这有点像语言学里的"语义漂移"——"gay"这个词一百年前意思是"欢快的"，现在几乎没人这样用了。指数也在经历同样的过程：同样的名字，实际所指已经变了。

跨领域连接：这与生物学的"生态位替代（Niche Displacement）"惊人相似——一个物种占据某个生态位的名号还在，但里面已经被入侵物种偷偷替换掉了。如果你还在用罗素2000做"小盘/宏观风险"的判断，你实际上是在读一份被篡改过的地图。 真正代表美国小微企业情绪的指标，可能反而藏在等权标普里那条"横着走的线"里——那条线才是真正"没有AI叙事加持的美国经济体温计"。

三、英特尔的"隐形悖论"：越基本面向好，市场越不给面子

主播用了很克制的一句话："我只能看到英特尔的基本面在变得越来越好，无非就是价格贵和便宜。"

这句话里藏着一个反直觉的现象：在AI叙事主导的市场里，基本面改善反而不是最强的股价催化剂。为什么？因为当整个市场的定价机器被"故事完整性"（narrative coherence）驱动时，一个有多条平行叙事线、每条都不算最锋利的公司（英特尔同时讲代工/CPU/封装/美国制造/新CEO），反而不如只讲一根线但讲到极致的公司（英伟达=AI训练算力）值钱。

这在心理学里叫"分类流畅性偏差（Categorical Fluency Bias）"——人类大脑对"一句话能讲清"的东西估值更高。英特尔的悲剧不在于业务不好，而在于它"太丰富所以难以被一句话总结"。

由此推出一个反直觉的操作洞察：主播反复强调的"大科技持仓可以稍高一些更安全"，其实和他对英特尔的判断构成了同一枚硬币的两面——当叙事拥挤时，避险不是去买"没故事"的公司，而是去买"故事最粗的那根"。这与传统"高PE=风险"的教科书直觉正好相反。真正的杠杆压缩（台湾杠杆ETF已跌去22年那种幅度）不发生在故事最弱的地方，反而发生在故事线最集中的芯片板块——这才是市场当前最深的一层不对称。

📝 转录文本

开场与市场概览

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场奉选小陶。

今天期内容首先我们回顾一下7月份到现在为止市场的这个走势，包括截止到上周五看一下市场的参与度，看一下CNN的恐慌贪婪指数，主要就是给市场把个脉；然后我们看一下FactSet数据对于第二季度财报的一些指引；最后我对于本周将要发布财报的一些公司稍微去做一个展望和聊一下，包括特斯拉、包括英特尔，好吗？

那我就废话不多说，直接开始今天内容。在开始之前，大家记得留言点赞加转发。我在上周四还是上周三的时候接受了168X 他们这个频道的一个采访，那个采访差不多一个多小时吧，大家感兴趣的话可以登录我的一个X，关注我的X账号，然后在X账号上面可以去看一下那段采访的一篇总结。其实是一个比较好的方式了解一下我对于下半年市场的这个看法，包括现在当下的一个仓位，好吗？

一、7月份至今各类资产走势（QTD）

看一下QTD，就是从7月份到现在为止资产价格的走势：

  • 美元小幅下跌
  • 美债收益率：两年期几乎是没有动的，也就是市场对于加息和降息这个预期整个7月份是没有动的；但是长端这一块上涨的幅度比较大，十年期上涨了8个基点，TLT下跌了2.2%
  • 美股：三大指数都出现了下跌。道指其实几乎没有下跌，跌0.3%；标普下跌的幅度也比较小 2.6%（注：应为约0.6%）；但纳指下跌的幅度最大 2.6%
  • 芯片板块从7月份到现在为止下跌了将近19%，那么YTD从最高的110%吧，现在下降到了73.3%
  • VIX：出现了14%这个上涨，主要是来自于上一周了，过去一周时间里面VIX是上涨了25%。那VIX的一个上涨也和一部分原油的一个上涨有关
  • 原油：7月份上涨了19%
  • 比特币：7月份也走的不错，上涨了9.3%
  • IGV（软件板块）：上涨了2.4%
  • 罗素2000：出现了下跌，下跌的幅度不小

主要是因为罗素2000里边的一些权重股是和AI相关的，比如说Bloom Energy也在里面，那Bloom Energy下跌的幅度比较大，所以罗素2000也下跌的幅度比较大。所以现在罗素2000其实已经和中小盘股或者整个市场风险偏好有一点脱钩了，因为里边那些权重股是和AI相关的，就有点类似于标普500是一样的一个道理。

那么所以我们看到其实YTD到现在为止，罗素2000上涨19.4%还是走的非常的好，遥遥跑赢了三大指数，为什么？那跑赢和芯片板块是一样，就是因为罗素2000里面的权重股是和AI相关或者是AI的一个获益股。

7月份到现在为止，大科技股们看一下上涨9.3%，出现了比较好的这个反弹。相比之下，标普的等权指数几乎是在横盘。

二、市场参与度与恐慌贪婪指数

截止到上周五，市场的参与度我们来看一下：

  • 纳指：现在是处在一个中性稍微偏悲观的一个位置，20均线和50均线 41%和45%
  • 罗素2000：55%和63%，处在中性偏乐观的位置
  • 标普500：58和63，处在一个中性偏乐观的位置

整个市场现在处在一个中性偏乐观的位置。主要这一波的一个回调是来自于芯片。

在上周日和大家分享CNN的恐慌贪婪指数里面的这张图，上周日我记得当时CNN的恐慌贪婪指数正好是在一个中性的位置。那随着过去一周的一个下跌，我们看到CNN的恐慌贪婪指数来到了悲观的位置，现在是在37左右。

在上周我和大家更新的时候，我特别挑了这张图出来——CNN恐慌贪婪指数里面市场的一个避风港需求。那避风港需求它主要衡量的是股市和债市之间的一个回报差。当这一个回报差在比较高的位置，就是代表过去20天里边股市跑赢了债市。当时我说如果画这条线的话，接下去我们可以期待的是股市和债市之间的一个相对回报差可能债市这边要走的好一点。

那其实在过去一周里面，我们看到债市基本上是没有动的，那股市出现了下跌，所以这条线出现了回调。那回调完之后，这两者之间的回报差接近于零了，也就是说其实已经处在一个比较中性的位置了。

三、芯片板块的杠杆去化

前面讲到了芯片板块的这个回调，来看一下台湾这边的margin loan，台湾这边的杠杆，这边是日交易的一个杠杆，可以看到在过去一段时间里边这个杠杆下跌的幅度非常的大。

那其实X上、在社交媒体上已经有人在讨论韩国那边的杠杆现在处在什么那个位置，我看完之后基本上还是处在一个相对比较高的位置。和最高幅度相比可能下降了20%左右，但是绝对值来说还是在一个相对高的一个位置。

然后我看到美国这边和芯片板块相关加杠杆的ETF在过去一段时间下跌的幅度堪比22年的这一波下跌。所以整个芯片板块我认为比较艰难的这个时刻可能在我们身后，但你说市场马上就要再回去，我觉得不大现实。通常来说，在一波去完杠杆之后，你再要回到这个新高的话，这中间有很多事情要发生。起码这个故事线，就以前的这个故事已经结束了，结束完之后你需要一个新的故事线，可以把价格带到更高。

那现在我们在第二季度这个财报，马上本周将会有一家大科技、两家大科技发布财报。其中一家谷歌，谷歌的这个财报，特别是资本开支，对于整个芯片板块的情绪面会起到非常大的这个影响。有任何资本开支增速放缓的这个信号，对于芯片板块都不是一个利好。

四、现金流预测：芯片 vs 超大规模云厂

然后我们来看一下这张图，这张图很有意思，是未来12个月现金流这边的预测。

图里紫色这条线是Semiconductor芯片板块的一个现金流，你可以看到在过去这一段时间上涨的幅度速度非常的快，而且这边是衡量的体量已经达到了4500亿美金，$450 billion。

黄色这条线Hyperscaler，所有的这些大云厂商，他们的现金流从3000亿美金现在下降到几乎为0。

从这个角度出发，可能市场不是那么的偏好Hyperscaler后偏好这些芯片板块也合理吧，因为芯片板块短期特别是你看未来12个月，它现金流的这个增速非常的确定，其幅度也非常的高。然后主要的这个现金流是来自于大科技这边的花费。

但是反过来也是一样，也是现在市场担心的：就是大科技有没有可能放缓资本开支这个增速？就我觉得主动权是掌握在大科技这边，这也是我手里现在拿的大科技，我完全不担心市场出现回调。那有任何资本开支放缓的这些增速就来自于这些大科技的话，我认为对于大科技的股价应该都是一个利好。所以我觉得还是应该有一个相对比较平衡的这个仓位。那在当下的话，我认为持有大科技或者大科技的仓位过高一些，我认为会更加的合理和安全。

五、Mag 7 与标普493 的盈利增速对比（FactSet数据）

讲到Mag 7，我们从FactSet数据去出发，看一下第二季度Mag 7的增速：

  • EPS同比增速：31.1%，非常的高
  • 剩下标普493：22.8%
  • 整个标普500 Q2市场预期的增速：24.7%

那这一个季度EPS增速主要来自于哪里呢？其主要来自于5家公司：

  1. 美光：上年的同期才1.9亿，然后今年来到了25.1亿
  2. 两家能源相关公司：因为能源价格的一个上涨，所以两家和能源价格相关的公司EPS的增速幅度也非常的高
  3. 博通：上年是1.69，今年预期是3.2
  4. 英伟达：上年是1.05，今年是2.08，就是英伟达和上年去都对比的话，EPS同比增速1倍

如果我把两家芯片公司（美光和英伟达）去掉的话，那整个标普500 EPS增速从24.7就直接会降到16.8。

在第一季度，我们看到就是过去的这个季度，我们看到Mag 7同比增速63.2%，493是7.9，这个差距非常的大。

第二季度我说现在市场预期31.1，其他公司是22.8，那我认为这个数字最后可能会停在40%左右。因为基本上大多数的Mag 7他们最后出来的结果、财报出来的这个结果都会比市场的预期要高。那么当然了，493也会比市场预期要高，但是493高的这个幅度可能不会有Mag 7会更高。

然后第三季度我们看一下这中间的这个差距会越来越小：第三季度Mag 7是在31.8，493是25.5。那么从第二季度再次有了一个台阶的一个上升。到第四季度，Mag 7还会开始出现增速上的一个放缓，22.8%，标普493仍然保持在25.3%。

那如果大家担心说Mag 7这个同比增速和标普493之间可能会缩小，是不是应该去投资标普493？其实这个问题在上年年底、上年第三季度第四季的时候市场就已经讨论过。原本市场预期在2025年（口误为2026）又会出现EPS增速反转的这种情况，但是我们在2025年完全没有看到，相反，第一季度这一个同比增速的一个差距在越来越大。

那么而其实如果今天不是有Mag 7的话，我们单去看标普493 EPS的同比增速18%、23%、26%，其实已经非常非常的高了。那只是在这个前提下Mag 7它的一个增速会更好。所以我觉得拆开看可以看，可以拿Mag 7和标普493去做对比，但是我们单看标普493——因为标普493也算衡量整个标普500的一个健康程度嘛，或者营收的一个盈利的这个增速——所以单看标普493的话，这个盈利增速其实也相当的高。

整个2025年现在市场预期EPS同比增速24.5%。在年初的时候市场预期是15%，也就是和年初做对比，现在已经比年初要高了10%。那随着第二季度财报出来之后，相信这一个差距会越来越大。

六、估值水平

最后是估值。下面处理蓝色这条线Forward PE，那FactSet上面的一个数据，截止到上周五是在20.5倍左右吧。那5年的这个平均是在20倍，也就是现在略微高于5年的这个平均，高的幅度多少，1%~2%左右。10年的平均在19倍。所以现在基本上标普500这个估值是处在一个中心的位置。

七、本周财报预告

最后我们来看一下本周将要发布的财报。发财报之前，本周其实没有一些宏观数据。然后在下下周就会有下一次这个美联储会议——一晃，我记得凯文·沃茨（Kevin Warsh）第一次美联储会议好像就在上一周呃，不知不觉马上要来到他下一次的一个第二次的美联储会议了。

所以本周下半周开始吧，美联储的成员就会进入一个禁默期了。那因为上一周公布的CPI数据、PPI数据基本上已经确定7月份是不会加息（口误，应为降息），即使现在市场还有15%的这个概率在那边，但7月份是不会降息，9月份现在降息这个概率仍然是在67%、65%左右。所以本周没有非常重要的一些宏观数据，那主要就是财报了，接下去财报季度将会越来越精彩。

周二盘前：MSCI

下一周我们将会看到周二的盘前会有MSCI。

周三盘前：Moody's

包括在周三盘前的一个Moody's。这两家公司主要是通过这两家公司可以看到，可能要几个月之前马世钟在讨论像SPGI（S&P Global）这一类的公司，他们会不会受到AI的这个影响。那我个人的看法是，掌握这些独有的数据，他们在评级这一块的一些业务，包括核心业务本身不但（应为"并不"）会受到AI的这个影响。而我前面特地去看了一下SPGI的这个走势，在过去这段时间其实走的还是不错的。虽然还没有把前段时间可能要大半年之前的那一波下跌的跌幅还没有完全擦掉，但是过去这段时间做的还是不错的。所以MSCI包括Moody's如果财报如果不错的话，可以把SPGI一类的标的都会往上带。

周三盘前：GEV

然后是周三的盘前GEV，那这家公司从订单、从很多个角度去看，GEV的财报不会有任何的一些风险。那对于他们来说，更多的还是产能的这个提升。

周三盘后：特斯拉 + 谷歌

周三的盘后就非常的精彩了，几乎是整个市场的一个焦点。那在周三的盘后——我记得我在App上应该有个直播的，是不是公开的直播我要确认一下，我记得应该是一个公开的一个直播——周三的盘后会有特斯拉的财报，然后还有Alphabet。

特斯拉：其实我有一段时间没有去跟了。最近我有看到有些Robotaxi开始上线了，可能现在在多少 40多辆还是100多辆，那么从上线的这个速度来说可能让一些人比较失望。那我个人仍然认为今年是累计特斯拉仓位的时候。我现在还没有开始累计，但如果有机会的话我可能会加仓特斯拉。现在还没有看到，我个人认为特斯拉的一个爆发点是在他们下一代芯片要推出的时候，或市场可能会提前去走嘛，或者提前去布局。

谷歌（Alphabet）：那谷歌应该是整个周三盘后市场最关注的一家公司的一个财报吧，没有之一了。为什么？因为谷歌的资本开支这个体量基本上和亚马逊是持平的。在上一个季度的财报里面，管理层有聊到他们已经给了2027年的一个资本开支，当时他们用的一个词我记得非常的清楚叫"substantially higher"。这个词的一个解释就起码在2027年的增速也能够达到40%至50%。但他们26年的增速是非常的高了，但我不知道市场能不能够满意。

那我个人猜测在这一次财报会里面，在资本开支这一块，谷歌可能不会透露从数量上去看，可能不会透露任何会放缓的一些信号，从体量上去看。但是我不知道从言语上来说他们会不会给自己稍微淡化一些，或者我说到这个痛稍微把它压低一些。为什么？是因为大科技现在疯狂的巨大资本开支，其实会反射他们、反射就是整个成本上升。

然后另外一点就是谷歌这边的一个margin，云服务这边的一个margin。谷歌的云服务的margin整个这一个季度吧，起码大科技这些云服务，我期待他们的一个margin会比上一个季度要更好。从Anthropic向另外两家公司去购买算力，包括购买算力的这个成本就可以看到，这个supply的这个价格就可以看到现在整个市场里边算力仍然是短缺。

另外一点，谷歌的财报里面其实我希望听到他们Agentic Commerce，那么在AI Agent这一块的一些落地，在C端这一块有没有一些落地。所以我基本上周三盘后会非常关注谷歌的这个财报。那谷歌的财报对于市场的这个影响，我认为也是非常非常的大。

周三盘后：ServiceNow / IBM / TI

然后接下来这两家公司ServiceNow，那主要是软件这一块。ServiceNow我个人对于他们的业务本身没有那么的担心。然后IBM，最后是TI（德州仪器）。那后面两家公司在市场里面现在讨论度也是相对会高一些。

周四盘前：诺基亚

最后是周四。周四的盘前会有诺基亚。诺基亚这家公司我会和大家跟一下，然后在周四那期视频里面我和大家稍微聊一下诺基亚这一次一个财报。财报本身我并没有太多那些指引和展望。

周四盘后：英特尔

周四的盘后会有英特尔。那过去这一段时间嘛，两三周，英特尔下跌的幅度会比较的大，现在价格在多少 92、93。英特尔财报里面的一些重点：

  1. 良率：第一点最重要的就是良率
  2. CPU的出货：然后第二点。在上一个季度财报里面，他们把压库存的这些CPU全部给卖掉了，这一个季度其实没有多余的CPU可以去卖了。从增速这个角度去看，不会比第一季度要好，这是在上一个季度财报里面就已经做出过的这个展望
  3. 封装业务：看一下他们封装这块会不会有些额外的客户

其实在所有AI的一些相关的标的里面，我个人认为英特尔其实是比较稳，而且不单是从一个从各个角度：从CPU也好，和AI相关也好，从代工也好，从美国这边的一个制造业的这个角度去出发也好，包括从陈立武的执行力也好，包括他们的良率也好——我只能看到英特尔的基本面在变得越来越好，无非就是它的一个价格短期之内贵和便宜的问题。好么？

所以周四的盘后和大家聊两家公司：诺基亚和英特尔。那我自己持有的公司特斯拉、英特尔。

八、活动预告与结语

最后今天的活动来自于Investing.com，主要是他们量化的一个选股策略。下面有3个以前成功的例子，分别是英伟达、SMCI、AppLovin，这三个成功的例子回报的相当的好。

7月份，Investing.com正在举行一个大促销，高达60%的这个折扣。Pro一档加币是9.49美金的话在8块钱不到；Pro Plus加币24.49美金的话应该是$17.8左右。

那这个平台本身我建议大家即使不订阅的话也可以去参考、也可以去用，因为这里边有很多的一些数据、财报数据、包括财报会议、包括一些纪要在上面都有。所以这个平台本身就已经是一个非常好的一个投资工具了。大家如果感兴趣的话，包括如果用我的这个链接的话，在这个基础上可以再便宜15%。所以我会把链接放在置顶的留言里面，大家感兴趣的话可以自己去订阅。

那今天这期内容就到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的"现金流反转"：芯片与云厂的角色互换
视频里最容易被一带而过、但其实最锋利的一张图，是那条**紫线（半导体未来12月现金流冲到4500亿美元）与黄线（超大规模云厂现金流从3000亿跌向近零）的十字交叉**。
这不是普通的板块轮动，而是**产业链话语权的一次结构性倒置**。过去十年，云厂是"甲方"，芯片厂被采购、被压价；现在的图景变成——云厂正在把自己所有的自由现金流"倒进"芯片厂的口袋里。用生态学的比喻，这不是共生，是**寄主与寄生虫的暂时性反转**：芯片是宿主，云厂是被抽干的宿主。
**出人意料的推论**：市场担心"大科技会不会砍资本开支"，其实问反了方向。真正应该焦虑的是——**当云厂现金流真的归零，谁来接盘下一轮资本开支？** 现在托底的是"未来AI收入"这张远期支票。这张支票的兑付节点，恰好就是主播提到的"Google Agentic Commerce落地"。所以谷歌财报的关键根本不在capex数字，而在**C端AI变现的第一声硬币落地声**。
## 二、Russell 2000的"身份漂移"：一个指数正在悄悄背叛自己
主播提到罗素2000因为里面的

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 台積電表現明明超好，股市為什麼突然變得很悲觀？【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"Token Maxing"是一场KPI的自我催眠——但它的历史原型不在硅谷，而在1990年代的日本商社

Chamath 讲的"公司比谁用的 token 多"这个荒诞现象，表面看是新问题，本质却是一种古老的组织病理学：把成本中心伪装成利润中心的语言游戏。

真正让人意外的是：这不是"dot-com 眼球经济"的翻版。眼球经济的用户是免费的、外部的；而 token maxing 是企业主动把自己的钱包烧掉、还把账单挂到荣誉墙上。更接近的历史类比是 1990 年代日本商社的"売上高至上主義"——为了做大流水，不惜赔钱做转手贸易，因为组织内部的晋升信号被绑在了"营业额"而非"利润"上。当一个指标脱离经济现实，它就不再是测量工具，而是变成组织内部政治的筹码。

一个更冷的观察：AI 时代第一批被"AI 替代"的白领，可能不是程序员或客服，而是 CFO——因为传统 CFO 的成本控制直觉，正在被"我们要拥抱 AI"的政治正确所压制。Uber 三四个月烧完全年预算这件事，如果发生在云计算时代，早就有人被开除了；发生在 AI 时代，反而变成了"战略投入"的证据。

二、Alex Karp 真正指向的不是"数据主权"，而是一种被忽视了 150 年的经济结构——数字佃农制（Digital Sharecropping）

Karp 说的"alpha 被锁进平台"，用商业语言包装了一个非常古老的东西。19 世纪美国南方的佃农，把土地上的经验、种植技巧、气候直觉一年年灌进地主的土地——最终地越种越肥，但佃农永远无法把这份"土壤记忆"带走。

企业今天把 SOP、客户偏好、行业 know-how 喂进 OpenAI 或 Anthropic 的模型，本质上就是数字时代的佃农。但这里有一个魔鬼般的细节是原始佃农制没有的：

Alpha 泄漏是不可逆的、无声的，而且具有"网络负外部性"——你上传的数据不仅让平台更懂你，还让平台更懂你的竞争对手。奶茶店 A 教会了 AI 台北人的口味偏好，奶茶店 B 明天就免费获得了这份洞察。这在经济学上叫"共有池悲剧的反转"——不是所有人过度索取毁了资源，而是所有人过度贡献喂肥了中介。

跨领域连接：这和 Google 与新闻业的关系一模一样。报纸把内容喂给搜索引擎索引，本以为换来流量，结果是 Google 学会了"新闻"本身，最终用 AI Overview 直接跳过报纸。AI 平台不是在租用你的数据，它是在把你的行业知识"资本化"到它的资产负债表上。

三、台积电悖论：这不是"利多出尽"，而是一次教科书级别的"胜利者诅咒"——但有一个所有人都忽略的变量

台积电赚翻却股价下跌，市场评论家喜欢说"利多出尽"，这个解释太懒了。真正发生的是资本密集型垄断的悖论：当你是唯一一家能做的公司时，客户的所有 FOMO 都会以"更多资本开支要求"的形式转嫁给你。

这不是"资本开支拖累毛利"这么简单——航空、航运、面板、DRAM，每一轮周期都上演过。但台积电这次有一个独一无二的、被市场低估的风险变量：

它的下游客户，正在用它的芯片，去摧毁自己的定价权。

DeepSeek、Kimi 用少得多的算力做出接近前沿的效果，直接把"barrel of intelligence"的价格从 26 美元砸到 50 美分。这意味着 NVIDIA/微软/Meta 的资本开支的边际回报正在崩溃。台积电现在的订单看起来火爆，但订单的信息熵已经开始变化——客户不是在为"确定的高毛利业务"买单，而是在为"军备竞赛式的恐慌"买单。

跨领域类比：这非常像 2008 年之前的美国次贷市场。评级机构（NVIDIA）说"这些 CDO 是 AAA"，投资银行（Hyperscalers）说"我们要买更多"，房地美（TSMC）说"订单强劲，我们要扩产"。每一环看财报都完美，直到最下游的房主（企业 AI 用户）开始违约。Arthur 说的"2028 年检验"，就是在计算这条链上的支付到期日。

一个让人不适的收尾观察：这支视频本身，就是本文分析的最完美例证——邦妮花了前 15 分钟解构 AI 泡沫的下游变现困境，然后花了后 5 分钟卖 OKX 的美股代币账户。她本人就在做"卖铲子"的生意——不是给淘金者卖铲子，而是给害怕淘金失败的观众卖看着别人淘金的门票。这是"卖铲子模型"迭代到了第三层：卖对不确定性的对冲工具。当媒体本身开始扮演金融中介，你就知道这个循环已经进入了自我指涉的阶段。

📝 转录文本

开场：一个巨大的矛盾

Something has gone completely wrong. A massive contradiction has just emerged in the AI market, let me explain.

TSMC just delivered a practically flawless report card. Second-quarter revenue exceeded 1.27 trillion NTD, with net profit reaching 247.8 billion NTD—a 77.4% year-over-year increase, setting a new all-time high. TSMC is earning an average of 7.8 billion NTD every single day, and they even project continued revenue growth for the third quarter. Judging by chip demand, this AI arms race is still sprinting at full speed.

However, also recently, well-known Silicon Valley investor Chamath and Palantir CEO Alex Karp have consecutively raised concerns about the current AI business model.

Hi, I'm PNY. If you enjoy content that doesn't just look at stock fluctuations, but breaks down the entire supply chain, business models, and risks, remember to subscribe to the channel and like this video.

We are going to unpack this seemingly major contradiction. TSMC's performance is stellar, yet the stock price dropped sharply, and the concerns raised by these Silicon Valley tech titans actually involve different stages of the AI supply chain.

AI 供应链的层次结构

The AI supply chain can be divided into several layers.

At the front end, the chip and infrastructure layer, companies like NVIDIA and AMD are responsible for designing AI chips, while TSMC is responsible for manufacturing them. Then, tech giants like Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta buy these chips to build data centers, providing the computing power needed to run AI. Of course, some companies also design their own chips and develop their own AI models.

But moving further down, these models and computing power then enter enterprise software, various AI tools, and consumer applications, eventually reaching the hands of companies and everyday users like you and me.

So, TSMC, Chamath, and Alex Karp are seeing crises and opportunities at different points in the supply chain. But our question is: why do the signals from both sides seem completely opposite?

台积电财报：一份令人惊艳的成绩单

Let's start with TSMC's earnings call on July 16th.

TSMC's report card this time is super impressive. Second-quarter revenue exceeded 1.27 trillion NTD, and net profit reached 247.8 billion NTD—a 77.4% year-over-year increase. This is once again a record-breaking achievement. Note that this is a 77% year-over-year profit growth, and the gross margin surged to 67.7%. What does this actually sound like? It means that for every 100 NTD of revenue TSMC receives, after deducting direct production costs, they are left with 67.7 NTD.

There was another signal this quarter that caught everyone's attention: 2-nanometer technology has started contributing to revenue for the first time. Moreover, TSMC projects that its revenue will continue to grow in the third quarter.

In other words, looking at TSMC's order book, there is no sign of customers hitting the brakes; everyone is still buying chips, scrambling for capacity, and moving toward more advanced and expensive processes. TSMC is one of the companies that has most clearly and directly turned the AI arms race into actual profit.

那为什么股价反而跌了？

But it's strange—despite such a stellar earnings report, TSMC's stock price actually plummeted. What is the market worried about?

They are worried that to meet the demand for these AI chips, TSMC will have to pour significantly more money into its operations than originally anticipated. The company raised its capital expenditure for this year from the original $52-56 billion to $60-64 billion. Coupled with the inherently higher production costs of overseas plants, what will that do to gross margins in the coming years? People are starting to wonder if there will be pressure.

But this is a different level of concern compared to what we are about to discuss. What TSMC faces is whether it can maintain these high margins after expanding capacity. Meanwhile, downstream companies face the question of whether, after buying so many chips, so much computing power, and so much AI, they can actually generate enough revenue or reduce enough expenses.

危机之一：Chamath 谈"下游有没有赚回来"

TSMC has already made astonishing profits from this AI arms race. But for the downstream companies that have been buying chips, building data centers, and paying for computing power all along, are they really earning that money back? Recently, tech industry leaders have started to express some hesitation and reservation.

For example, one noted on a show, "Oh, AI is so useful that companies are all rushing in, but no one has had a chance to stop and calculate the basic math of their company operations."

The ecosystem has ultimate buyer tokens, and at some point, one has to ask, if two or three companies are going to generate $400 billion a year and it's doubling and tripling every year, where is the rest of this money going to be made, and who is implementing it in such a way where these profits are to be had? And I think you're starting to see a little bit of the wheels come off, like, just at a very basic level.

"一桶智能"的比喻

If you think AI is like oil, let's just use this analogy to make it simple. A barrel of crude is what? $86 bucks right now.

Right now, unfortunately, it's gone up. Yes.

No. No. WTI is 80, WTI 81. Okay.

Let's say a barrel of intelligence, which is a million tokens. Okay. You can buy that barrel for $26 bucks from Anthropic, a really good model. You can buy from OpenAI for $26 bucks. Anthropic's latest model costs you $56 bucks. Elon is selling you a barrel of intelligence for a buck. Zuck is about to sell it for a buck fifty. Demis and Sundar are trying to sell it to you for a dollar. The Chinese will sell it to you for 50 cents.

So this rationalization has to happen. We have the same input that has this crazy cost. If you made a bet on one of these folks that are selling extremely expensive barrels of intelligence and you try to pass that through, you run into some downstream difficulty.

Token Maxing：一个被扭曲的 KPI

Now, AI is not just about encouraging employees to use it more; in fact, some companies have even started competing over who uses the most tokens. When you analyze these things, it seems absurd, because shouldn't companies be tracking or comparing how much money AI has actually made for the company or how much manual labor it has saved? It turns out the metric being used is how much money is spent, which shouldn't be the benchmark for a successful AI integration; it's the wrong indicator, the wrong KPI.

J calls this strange phenomenon "token maxing."

Reality is everybody in this ecosystem, me included, we're all struggling to figure out how do we price stuff so that the ultimate buyer is making more money, growing faster, and that is still a question mark.

He believes many CEOs and CFOs might not even realize how massive this bill is, and some may even suddenly realize that AI hasn't immediately boosted their profits but has instead driven up their operating costs. For example, it was reported this April that Uber burned through its entire annual budget for AI tokens and infrastructure in just three or four months. Instagram head Adam also mentioned that they had to shut down some practices they considered quite foolish to finally bring their AI costs under control.

So, the first crisis can be simplified as enterprises mistaking AI usage volume for the value created by AI, and now they are starting to reflect on whether this phase of spending was actually worth it.

危机之二：Arthur Hayes 谈"定价权"

The next issue is that the prices these enterprises are currently paying for tokens are too high and may be unsustainable in the future. A month ago, when I interviewed Arthur Hayes, he talked about very similar content. Many people who watched that episode were quite impacted, as it challenged everyone's preconceived notions about the AI market.

The question Arthur raised is: Can these US AI companies really maintain their current pricing in the long run?

One of the big things that we don't understand, we don't appreciate, is number one, do these AI companies have pricing power? So, if there's a model from DeepSeek or Kimi or one of these Chinese models, and it's just as good, it costs 1/15th the price of what it costs to produce a token in the West. Does the marginal consumer say, "Oh, okay, if I'm just building some like [__] travel app or I've got some agents coming my email, do I really need like the best top-tier model that costs so much more money from a US lab and probably is harvesting all my data, sell it back to me in some way, shape, or form? Or why don't I run a much smaller model on my own device or my own network securely and I pay way less and do this by, you know, do it the Chinese way, right?"

And as long as you don't badmouth the Chinese Communist Party, like, they don't care what you do on these on these apps. So, I don't think many people care about the politics in China, and the price is right. And we've seen with every other vertical that China has entered, the initially people think, "Oh, the brand of the Western company matters." And then you present the customer with something that's just as good for a much cheaper price, and the customer says, "Okay, I don't really care about that brand." We can look at automobiles as a prime example of like, do you rather have the BMW or the BYD? And so I think we already know the answer to that.

Now, this is, of course, a very good thing for users, because as models become cheaper, enterprises can adopt AI at a much lower cost. But for US AI companies, this is a massive problem, because their current valuations, investments, and capital expenditures are all built on the assumption that "a large number of customers will be willing to pay a very high price for top-tier models in the long run." If this assumption fails, even if model usage continues to skyrocket, the price per token might keep falling. At that point, while the demand is real, the revenue might not be enough to cover the massive investments made earlier—which brings us to another issue.

I think the AI bubble, given the amount of capital considered misallocation that is happening, obviously the market's wrong at this point, will be bigger than subprime, and there is a lot of credit time bombs ticking globally. I can take a chip that goes with years. under.

Arthur believes that by around 2028, all these assumptions will begin to face a severe test. If you haven't watched the full interview between Arthur and me, I highly recommend you watch this episode and then check that one out.

危机之三：Alex Karp 谈"Alpha 与数据主权"

Up to this point, Chamath and Arthur were talking about costs, pricing, and return on investment, right? But American corporate leaders, over these past two years, have been circulating this video of Alex Karp's passionate...

unpack that, what do you mean by that? Well, you have so when you're when you're using large language models they are it's like at this point everyone technical realizes they're like a critical resource to make them valuable in enterprise like battlefield context or regulated context or manufacturing. You have to have what's called an application layer. It makes it safe and useful and precise. So safe because it doesn't touch your unlearning data. Safe it prevents the large language model from caching your data and replicating your business. Uh safe because it doesn't transfer your

什么是 "Alpha"？

Have you heard the term "Alpha"? It's used to describe the areas where a company truly outperforms its competitors. It could be higher yields, more precise risk assessment, more efficient logistics planning, or perhaps years of accumulated customer insights, industry expertise, and decision-making methods. These capabilities are usually scattered across various corporate databases, employee experiences, and operational workflows.

So, what is it that Alex is actually worried about, anyway? IP.

奶茶店的例子

Let's take an example: suppose I have a bubble tea chain and I use AI to decide how much inventory each store should order and when to offer discounts. After using it for a while, my bubble tea company will keep feeding its own experience into this AI, such as what flavors customers in certain regions prefer, or how long before a holiday I should start building up inventory. The longer I use this AI, the more I'll invest my own sales data and experience, making the system understand this business better.

So where's the problem? The problem is that the AI model system and the pricing power are both in the hands of the AI platform company. So, my bubble tea business pays token fees, invests its own data and industry knowledge, and in the end, years of accumulated experience and competitive advantages are all locked into this platform system, unable to be extracted.

We need to rebuild trust and that trust is going to happen where everyone gets to ask and answer basic questions. Who owns the data? Where is it cached? Are the prompts secure? Is this being transferred to you? Are you being—

Okay, if it was so valuable, let's say I can make you a billion dollars right tomorrow. Wouldn't I say I'll make you a billion dollars and I want 30%? Why are they charging for tokens if it's so valuable?

国家安全层面的延伸

Moreover, Alex believes this also involves national security issues. The military is already using AI for intelligence gathering and assessing battlefield situations, and it is impossible to hand over complete control of something so critical to a few tech companies.

When the Department of War goes to you and says I need this application, do they get to control the weights to do it, or do you get to control the weights? Are we really outsourcing the battlefield of this country to the consensus view in Silicon? That is insane.

This narrative certainly aligns with Palantir's own commercial interests, since their service allows enterprises and governments to use AI while retaining control over their data, but that doesn't mean the risks Alex raised don't exist.

You can look at a graph of the financing created which then the VC are like lemmings they run and they're like I need to be on the right side of the super

And then they flip to the other two, which is: this is a complete weapon, shut the world down, and why don't you regulate everything? They just go back and forth. We had this game now the problem is, at the scale of a trillion dollars of market cap, that game has these ripple effects that touch everybody in the industry. This is why, by the way, I think what Alex Karp did he deserves a medal, this is incred-

CEOs, and he was on the right side of history.

结论：需求是真的，但回报存疑

So, is the demand for AI real? Technology is indeed changing the way businesses and consumers use tech, but the market is starting to worry that the revenue and profits downstream companies can generate from these investments might not be growing as fast as their current spending and valuations.

We still need to distinguish between two different levels. TSMC reflects the demand for chips and AI infrastructure, while figures like Chamath, Arthur Hayes, and Alex Karp are discussing whether those chips and computing power, once they reach these companies, can actually help them make more money in the end. So, strong upstream demand and downstream skepticism about investment returns can absolutely coexist at the same time.

It's a bit like the Gold Rush; those who sell shovels can make a lot of money, but just because shovels sell well, it doesn't mean everyone who buys one will actually strike gold.

广告环节：如何用 OKX 参与美股

Therefore, the market's greatest opportunities usually appear when there is market fear. Suppose you don't want to be tied to traditional U.S. stock market hours and want to participate in the price fluctuations of U.S. stocks 24 hours a day; what can you do? I will use OKX to demonstrate two ways you can easily participate.

开户与入金

Scroll down now, find the link in the information section, register an account, complete identity verification, then download the OKX app and log in with your credentials. Since U.S. stock tokens are built on the blockchain, you need to prepare some USDT before getting started. USDT is a stablecoin pegged to the U.S. dollar; you can think of it as the dollar of the blockchain world. Whether you want to buy U.S. stock token spot or trade perpetual contracts, you'll use USDT directly.

On OKX, the simplest way to get USDT is by using a credit card or Apple Pay. Go to "Buy Crypto" on the homepage, select "Express Buy," and you can purchase USDT directly. If you just want to familiarize yourself with the process using a small amount, this method is the fastest and can be completed in minutes.

If the amount is larger, say several thousand or tens of thousands of dollars, it is recommended to deposit U.S. dollars directly, as the overall exchange rate will be better. Through digital banks like LINE Bank or Nex Bank, for example, you can transfer U.S. dollars to OKX entirely online. The reason for using digital banks is that if you use a traditional physical bank, you might have to go to a branch in person for your first overseas wire transfer, which is a hassle. It's certainly possible, but digital banks are just simpler.

Once your USD arrives, transfer the funds from your Funding account to your Trading account, and you can then exchange them for USDT. We have already provided a complete demonstration of the more detailed process for opening an account and depositing funds to buy crypto. If you want to watch a detailed, step-by-step video on how to buy USDT, I've included the link in the description box below.

第一种玩法：美股现货代币

OK, once you have your USDT, you can choose between two completely different types of US stock products. The first type is spot US stock tokens, which means turning stocks like NVIDIA, Tesla, and Apple into tokens that can be traded on the blockchain. Because they are on the blockchain, US stock tokens can be traded 24/7, year-round; you don't need to buy a whole share at once and can participate with smaller amounts based on your budget. US stock tokens track the price movement of the underlying company, so you aren't holding the actual stock, but you still receive dividends after taxes. The dividends are then distributed by reinvesting them to increase the number of tokens you hold.

Open the OKX app, tap "Trade" on the home page, select "Spot," then "Stocks," and you will see the major US stock tokens here. You'll notice an "X" added to the front of the original stock ticker. For example, NVIDIA's stock ticker is NVDA, so the US stock token is XNVDA. Select the XNVDA/USDT trading pair, tap "Trade," and you can start buying or selling NVIDIA stock tokens. Limit orders allow you to set a specific price to buy or sell, executing only when the market hits that price; market orders allow you to buy directly at the current market price.

If you don't want to actively time the market but have found a good asset you want to purchase long-term, you can also set up recurring investments. Tap the top left of the OKX app, go to "Strategy," select "DCA Strategy," and enter the asset you want to buy through periodic investment. Suppose I want to buy $50 worth of XTSM, the TSMC stock token, every day; just tap "Create Strategy" and you're done. The system will automatically buy it for you daily, so you don't have to guess the highs or lows.

第二种玩法：美股永续合约

The second type of US stock product is called US stock perpetual contracts; you don't actually own the stock, you just decide whether you think it will go up or down. You can also use leverage to trade the same position size with less capital.

Let's say I want to go long or short on Tesla stock tokens. Open the OKX app, tap "TradFi" on the homepage, select "Stocks," find the TSLAA/USDT trading pair, and from there, you can choose your leverage and go long or short.

If you want to learn how to determine your position size and the five steps traders use to open orders, how do you actually judge that? This video contains pro-level insights that usually cost hundreds of thousands to learn; I've put the link in the description, so be sure to save it and watch it later, or you might not find it again.

Additionally, for stock token contracts, you can also use a contract grid strategy. Just set a price range, and the system will automatically help you buy and sell in batches within that range.

If you join OKX now via the exclusive link in the description and complete the designated tasks during the event period, you can claim a US stock contract grid with a position value of 1,000 USDT. If you have any questions, feel free to ask in our LINE group; all the detailed information is in the description.

留下你的看法

Do you think these companies can rely on AI to earn back the massive capital they are currently investing? Or has this arms race already moved too fast? Feel free to let me know your thoughts in the comments below.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"Token Maxing"是一场KPI的自我催眠——但它的历史原型不在硅谷，而在1990年代的日本商社
Chamath 讲的"公司比谁用的 token 多"这个荒诞现象，表面看是新问题，本质却是一种**古老的组织病理学**：**把成本中心伪装成利润中心的语言游戏**。
真正让人意外的是：这不是"dot-com 眼球经济"的翻版。眼球经济的用户是免费的、外部的；而 token maxing 是企业**主动把自己的钱包烧掉、还把账单挂到荣誉墙上**。更接近的历史类比是 1990 年代日本商社的"売上高至上主義"——为了做大流水，不惜赔钱做转手贸易，因为组织内部的晋升信号被绑在了"营业额"而非"利润"上。当一个指标脱离经济现实，它就不再是测量工具，而是变成**组织内部政治的筹码**。
一个更冷的观察：**AI 时代第一批被"AI 替代"的白领，可能不是程序员或客服，而是 CFO**——因为传统 CFO 的成本控制直觉，正在被"我们要拥抱 AI"的政治正确所压制。Uber 三四个月烧完全年预算这件事，如果发生在云计算时代，早就有人被开除了；发生在 AI 时代，反而变成了"战略

item.title: [Business of Sport] Is the FA really backing Infantino?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的"零成本反抗"悖论

大多数评论只看到"英足总在Infantino面前很弱势"，但真正诡异的事实是：英足总此刻恰恰处于一个近乎零成本的反抗窗口。

把台面上的筹码摊开： - 2035女足世界杯没有竞争对手，主办权已经等同于囊中之物； - 男足世界杯的门票，连Sepp Blatter都亲口说"别想了"； - FIFA给FA的收入占比约0.5%（对比某些国家的70–80%）。

也就是说，主持人真正指向的洞察是：当"顺从"几乎不再能换来任何东西，机构却仍然选择顺从。 这比"因为利益而妥协"要黑暗得多——它揭示了大型体育治理机构内部的一种"顺从的路径依赖"：顺从已经成为身体记忆，不再需要利益作为理由。换句话说，FA的沉默不是"利益计算"的结果，而是"利益计算能力的萎缩"。

二、跨领域连接：中世纪教皇选举、股权稀释与Infantino的"选票通胀术"

Infantino忽然抛出"扩军到64队"，节目里当作花边讲，但这其实是一部教科书级的政治工程。可以叠三层跨领域视角看：

  1. 梵蒂冈史视角：中世纪教皇通过大量增设小国红衣主教（cardinal inflation）来稀释欧洲大国的话语权。Infantino的每一次"扩军"，本质是同一动作——扩大选民基数、稀释老牌足球国家的相对权重。
  2. 创业公司股权视角：英格兰是这项运动的"创始人股东"，正在被后来加入的"投资人（FIFA新会员国）"通过增发新股逐步稀释。1966是他们最后一次拿到分红。
  3. 奢侈品牌视角：世界杯的价值来自稀缺性。扩到64队意味着"连意大利都能进"（节目原话的玩笑），这是典型的品牌稀释死亡螺旋——为了短期政治忠诚，杀死会下金蛋的鹅。

真正令人意外的一点是：Infantino不是在"改革世界杯"，他是在用世界杯的品牌资产给自己发选票。他把FIFA的"未来股权"当作"当下选票"来兑现——这在公司治理里叫"掏空型分红"（tunneling dividend）。

三、最反直觉的观察：Debbie Hewitt 的"身份夹层"

节目末尾一句轻描淡写：Debbie Hewitt既是FA首位女性主席，也是FIFA首位女性副主席。

这句话的隐藏结构非常残忍——FA若要反抗Infantino，就必须让自己派出的女性突破者当场自我打脸。 这是治理系统最擅长的自我保护机制：把最有可能成为改革者的人吸纳进体制的高层，让"反抗"在情感和职业层面都变得不可能。

这类结构在别处也随处可见： - 大型科技公司把最激进的批评者聘为"AI伦理主管"； - 政党把街头运动的领袖收编为议员； - 学术界给最尖锐的边缘学者一张终身教职。

吸纳（co-optation）永远比压制便宜。 Infantino不需要让FA闭嘴，他只需要在FA最重要的进步性叙事（女性领导力）里留一个自己的席位，反抗的道德成本立刻由外部转移到内部。

一句话彩蛋

节目讨论的表面是"FA该不该支持Infantino"，但转录里真正藏着的问题是——当一个组织的道德资本已经远大于其经济依赖，它却仍然选择政治顺从时，问题就不再关于FIFA了，而是关于英足总自己已经变成了它所批评的那种机构。

📝 转录文本

Opening: Why we're revisiting Infantino

Charlie, we spoke a little bit last week about FIFA in the wake of Donald Trump's intervention. Um, which naturally led us to a conversation around Infantino and his suitability for the job in the wake of everything that uh came out, but also I think in pretty simple explanations explain why we thought none of it really mattered. He was in position and no matter whether we think it's right or wrong, nothing was going to change with that.

We got a few — well, interestingly — from [laughter] from the people um…

we've been deluged by people pointing the finger at our — at England's football association — saying: correct, given how terrible the whole situation is, why is the FA supporting Infantino?

The voting-bloc reality behind Infantino's power

Well, so part of our reasoning for him not being removed or or not leaving was the power and influence that he has as president — by no means because he has so many votes from the blocks of countries that pull basically their votes together, i.e. from the Caribbean, from parts of Asia, from Africa, which gives him over half of the vote of voting nations. And with the kind of one vote, one —

so you know, basically all votes meaning the same,

his power was unlikely to be broken.

That was when we got messages going, "Hang on, don't forget it's not just those countries."

So from people relatively high, well, no, very high up in football within this country. I got a few messages uh saying saying just that.

We both did. We both did.

So, you know, this was something that I thought we should just cover, because the FA's potential reported support of Infantino just further highlights some of the challenges that I think we have with FIFA right now and and the broader game. Because what happened over the last few weeks is very easy to get very agitated about all of this. But there is a far greater conversation and story to have here around FIFA's role in football.

The reported FA letter and why associations play along

But on on the FA piece that you raised there — um, before the World Cup, it was reported, right, and I'm very clear on that, that a letter was going to be sent at the beginning or during the tournament at some stage, that the FA would support FIFA's —

Yeah.

— that the FA would support Infantino's re-election in the 2027 presidency elections, which last time he was unopposed in, just to be very clear. This time, there were rumors this morning maybe there'd be a candidate, but anyway…

Um, now for them to do that — like, why would they do that?

Well, generally for organizations like the FA, if you're on the right side of FIFA, let's be sweeping, it gives you a far better chance of hosting a World Cup. And that's across the men's and the women's game.

England last hosted a World Cup in 1966, right? So since 1966, with this World Cup now, Mexico have hosted three World Cups. England haven't hosted a single one.

Blatter's warning: don't expect a men's World Cup

Now Charlie, I got a little bit worried when uh looking at this and I saw the word — Sepp Blatter's made a comment on on the situation of uh English hosting of major tournaments — and he actually said that English football should not expect a World Cup anytime soon. It was too successful and there was still a perception of "nose in the air."

This is England. Well, if Sepp's having that kind of commentary, then I'm inclined to disagree with it. But I have to admit, as a man that has sat in there and given World Cups to various countries, I imagine on this he actually does know what he's talking about, because he also knows the types of people still in FIFA making decisions, as he did.

So if that's the main reason that you would support Infantino —

is to try and get a men's World Cup, right?

Well, I'm just going to say it's try and get a World Cup because — because I'm also trying to understand why the FA would support him, whether we agree with it or not. Okay. The reason I mentioned the women's World Cup is because hosting a women's World Cup is the reason the FA would try and stay on side of FIFA. Because that led me to think about the 2035 women's World Cup.

The 2035 Women's World Cup: a bid with no rivals

So that would that would assume that there was competition to host a women's World Cup.

Oh — again, so this is Charlie, this is my point. Okay, rather than us just being critical all the time. So okay, why would the FA do that? Why would why would they do it?

Yeah.

So the 2035 Women's World Cup — England, Northern Ireland, Scotland, and Wales wanted to host it, and that's been relatively public. So you go, "Right, we want to host that tournament. Maybe we need to do a little bit of a supporting act here."

Well, as you just highlighted, there weren't any other bidders for the Women's [laughter] World Cup in 2035. None.

So England's bid, England, sorry, I should say —

the home nations,

— the home nations' bid — once it was submitted was going to win.

Yes.

And therefore meant that the FA, if we're then taking Sepp Blatter's piece into account, have not a huge amount to gain from supporting Infantino. They could take the moral ground. They don't need to play the political game.

Mark Bullingham's revealing 0.5% number

Mark Bullingham said a really interesting thing — and he's the chief executive of the FA. So he made a statement that said, when asked whether they would support Infantino next session, he said:

"That's a board review that we will do in due course. There's a lot of countries for whom FIFA is their main source of revenue. Obviously, we're fortunate enough that FIFA money that we get is around 0.5% — speculative — of our money. For other countries, it's probably 70 to 80%. So if someone's increasing their revenue, obviously it allows them to do more of the sports development side, so they have different perspectives."

Yes. Well, yes. So again, if it's 0.5% of your revenue coming from FIFA, you need to support them far less.

Why then would you back a president that now has so blatantly shown that neutrality is not a key focus of this organization? And that to me is a problem. We are in a position as England to actually make a little bit of a stand, while many other countries have to rely on the revenue that they get from FIFA.

"The FA's game": why senior figures want a stand

Well said, Charlie. Well said. And you know, what I'm going to say next does not gainsay the fact that I'm glad you said that. And I think that's what — the gist of what the background to this discussion is — that both Charlie and I have been contacted by numerous very senior people in the football industry in the last week or two saying:

"For God's sake, guys, can you at least point up the FA should be making a stand on this?"

Right? We we did actually invent the game. It is called the FA — not the English FA — it's the Football Association, and this is Association football. It's the FA's game, and FIFA administer the FA's game. And therefore, if any organization in the whole world should be holding FIFA to account, it is the FA.

The counterpoint: the football-politician's world

That's what people have been saying. Now, all I can just put as a counterpoint to that — and it doesn't gainsay the truth of that, and I just want to make that clear —

is that when you are a football politician — what a thought that would be —

um, and you're going around these various different conferences and delegates meetings, and it's about who sits on which committee, who gets to actually have an influence over certain decisions… like, for instance, a decision might be coming up to expand the World Cup, to make it even bigger, for instance, and so on and so forth. It's not just about the money. It's about other areas of influence.

And once you put yourself properly offside — you put yourself in an "I am against this leadership" perspective, which effectively is how Infantino would perceive it, even if the FA was only slightly critical — then he has a lot of levers to pull, given all the various bits of support he does have from, as Mark Bullingham says, countries for whom the revenue that Infantino is giving them — or FIFA's giving them — is a massive percentage of what they get.

It's not impossible to imagine a situation where you do become really quite isolated.

"So what?" — the case for going rogue

Now, can I ask what the impact of being isolated is, if you're unlikely to host a major tournament anyway in the near future, and the revenue that you get is less than 1% of your revenue as an association? They're not going to ban me from playing in World Cups. Like… how? So what?

I'm going to spend the next 12 years — and maybe this — I'm going to spend the next 12 years trying to change this institution and garner as much support as I can. And I'd most likely be able to do so from the bigger footballing countries, right? Which doesn't matter at the moment, because as I said, every vote is proportionally equal to the same thing. But — England, Germany, Belgium after what's happened now, Spain, you know, these countries — we're going to pull together and we are going to try — we're going to try and do something.

And then I'm far more likely to maybe host a major tournament off the back of that, cuz I'm seen as the organization, the association that came in here and actually started to try and clean some of this up.

Stubbings for president. Stubbings for president is all I can say. The campaign starts here. The 32-year-old tyro who will in 20 years time be, as I would call him, the next Gianni [laughter] —

or the new Gianni —

the next Infanti [laughter]

The moral-conscience path

But no — right, so I totally take your point. And I think, you know, it's absolutely a point worth making that, even in as much as there is a mercantile reason for sucking up to Infantino, we don't have that reason.

We've already been awarded the 2035 Women's World Cup, which no one else wanted anyway. We're basically being told you're never going to get a men's World Cup under the current dispensation, so we might as well go rogue and and be the moral conscience of world football.

I don't disagree with that, Charlie. Of course it will be uncomfortable for England's — and to a degree the home nations' — political representatives on these councils and bodies. But frankly, they need to be able to make that sacrifice for the good of football.

But let's put it this way. I'm not uncognizant that it will be uncomfortable — and would be uncomfortable — and that in the short run it will be failed. Right. But what you're saying is if we really want to change things going forward, then ultimately it is up to the FA to take a stand.

Look, fair. All I'm saying is that you can sit on the outside and go, you know, "I wish it was different" or "I wish this would change." You know, if you believe that there's a way you can try and affect that change, and you're as powerful as English football is — again, fine on the voting system, maybe not, but in the broader game, there's a few needles that you can move.

Debbie Hewitt: the uncomfortable dual role

You mentioned Debbie Hewitt, first female FA chair, is also the first female VP at FIFA, and that's a four-year term. She is —

in exactly that position you described. It would be an incredibly uncomfortable one. However, that can't stop you from trying to push through the right things.

The 64-team World Cup: political engineering in real time

Charlie, you mentioned just one thing on there that I thought was interesting — because the Infantino 64 World Cup plan. You know, this has suddenly started rearing its head, and he started talking about it literally in the last week.

I mentioned the re-election in 2027. Not hugely surprising that he's suddenly, just after this disgraceful uh happening with Trump and Balogun drama, started talking about increasing it to another 16 teams — to encourage and garner more support from countries who he knows he's probably going to need if a few of the countries he's got suddenly start getting a little bit upset with the behaviors that we've shown in this World Cup.

If you say you're going to expand the World Cup, you're highly likely to get more votes. So suddenly that's true to the narrative.

Could you kill World Cup qualifying?

That's certainly true. I will say one thing about the expanded World Cup: I think we're almost at the stage now where, if we took it to 64 nations, could you get rid of World Cup qualifying?

No, of course you couldn't.

I think you can.

No, because you still got to have the hope for all the other —

Okay. So no, hear me out on this one. I think what you could say — because it's got to a stage really now where, once 64 teams are qualifying, effectively even Italy will qualify, right? [laughter] Gotcha. That's my Italian friends.

Um, we got to the stage where even Italy will be able to qualify. And so really, actually, what might be best is to say: look, okay, the top 50 in the FIFA world rankings qualify automatically, and then all of the rest of the countries play off for the remaining 14 places — um, however you want to draft it — because I think that would be… since the World Cup's already become this big bloated thing, actually adding an extra 16 teams to it probably doesn't make a vast amount of difference.

What inspires those teams to vote for Infantino in this model?

It's hope, right? It's hope that they can reach it. And if you have 14 spots up for grabs, or 64 as a narrative, right —

I'd be maybe worried if I was one of the other teams that this is actually narrowing my opportunity if there's only 14 spots that I could qualify for. So that would be my push on that.

I think he is trading this purely on the hope that these FAs of very small countries can do the impossible, do the Curaçao, do the Cape Verde, and get to a tournament and benefit from it financially.

Closing

Either way, I think the very difficult thing is — it's a much longer discussion. It's a huge discussion.

观看原视频 →

大多数评论只看到"英足总在Infantino面前很弱势"，但真正诡异的事实是：**英足总此刻恰恰处于一个近乎零成本的反抗窗口。**
把台面上的筹码摊开： - 2035女足世界杯**没有竞争对手**，主办权已经等同于囊中之物； - 男足世界杯的门票，连Sepp Blatter都亲口说"别想了"； - FIFA给FA的收入占比约**0.5%**（对比某些国家的70–80%）。
也就是说，主持人真正指向的洞察是：**当"顺从"几乎不再能换来任何东西，机构却仍然选择顺从。** 这比"因为利益而妥协"要黑暗得多——它揭示了大型体育治理机构内部的一种"顺从的路径依赖"：顺从已经成为身体记忆，不再需要利益作为理由。换句话说，FA的沉默不是"利益计算"的结果，而是"利益计算能力的萎缩"。

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Rise of Sinner | Why Britain’s £300M Tennis System Isn’t Producing Champions
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、辛纳的"教练组合投资学"：把自己当作一支基金来运营

大多数人看到辛纳频繁更换教练团队，第一反应是"这人不好相处"或"太苛刻"。但仔细看他的换人逻辑，会发现一个惊人的模式——他不是在换人，他是在做资产配置。

Piatti 带他从少年到职业球员，功能兑现后被替换成 Cahill（因为 Cahill 有培养大满贯冠军的经验）。体能教练因为在多巴胺失利事件中"未尽把关之责"被开除，但服完禁赛期后又被请回——因为他技术层面依然是最优解。这是纯粹的功能主义人力资本管理，情感权重接近于零。

跨领域看，这更像顶级对冲基金经理管理团队的方式，而不像传统体育明星和团队的关系。有意思的对比是拉杜卡努——她换教练是"混乱型换人"（emotional churn），辛纳是"结构型换人"（portfolio rebalancing）。这两种"换人"在表面统计上看起来一样，但底层完全是两种物种。

二、"大满贯东道主诅咒"：拿到 Wimbledon 反而毁了英国网球

这是整段访谈里最反直觉的洞察，但主持人只是掠过没有点破：举办大满贯的四个国家，在过去 20 年男子网坛几乎零冠军产出（澳洲男子上一个冠军在 2002 年，法国 1983 年，美国 2003 年，英国只有一个 Murray 且完全体制外培养）。

这在经济学里有个名字，叫"资源诅咒"（Resource Curse）——就像石油富国往往发展不出制造业。LTA 每年从温网拿到 4800 万英镑，这笔"外生现金流"让整个体系丧失了寻找真正稀缺资源（教练、俱乐部生态）的动机，转而把钱砸向可衡量、可宣传的东西：公园里的球场数、参与人数、职业赛事场次。

意大利没有大满贯，反而被迫用"内生方式"建设——4500 家俱乐部对英国 2000 家。俱乐部意味着教练网络，教练网络意味着人才涌现的毛细血管。这是典型的匮乏催生结构，丰饶催生虚胖。

三、Draper 和 Raducanu 的"伤病同频"：温室化育才的生物学代价

这段访谈里最容易被忽略但最重的一击是：LTA 体系培养出的两位当红球员，都在生涯早期反复受伤。主持人只是提出疑问，但没有深挖。

我认为这不是巧合，这是过度专业化+过早高强度训练的必然产物。对比辛纳——他在阿尔卑斯山滑雪长大，直到十几岁才转向单一项目。运动科学近十年有大量研究表明：多项目童年训练培养出的运动员，成年后受伤率显著更低，运动寿命更长。

这跟当代教育的一个核心悖论完全同构：你越早"集中投入"，长期回报越差。硅谷创业圈也在验证同一件事——早期就"all-in"某个方向的创始人，往往在 5-10 年后崩盘；而绕过弯路的"晚成者"反而走得更远。

LTA 的模式本质上是"工业化温室"（hothousing 这个词主持人用得很精准），而意大利的俱乐部体系是"分布式生态"。前者产出脆弱的高纯度晶体，后者产出可以承压的复合材料。

四、一个更深的隐藏结构：英国正在用"网球"的钱补贴"高尔夫"的模式

最后一个 aha：主持人无意中点破——英国高尔夫强，因为高尔夫的物理形态强迫你必须走"俱乐部+驻会职业教练"模式（你不可能在公园里开一个高尔夫球场）。

这意味着：当一项运动的基础设施必须"重资产化"时，它自动过滤掉了公共参与幻觉，只能走精英培养路径。网球是"轻资产化"运动，因此可以逃避这个约束——而 LTA 恰恰选择了"逃避"这条路。

换句话说，英国网球体系的失败，不是执行问题，是它选择了运动本身允许它偷懒的那一面。这在企业战略里叫"路径依赖的舒适陷阱"。

📝 转录文本

Opening: A New Sinner Fan

I think you may um be a fan of someone after some pretty exciting tennis at the weekend.

Yeah. Well, so I sort of feel guilty in retrospect when we spent part of the first part of this year sort of looking a bit sort of worried about tennis and saying, you know, if SA becomes the next big thing, he's not the most charismatic guy around. Um and and you know maybe there's an element of truth to that, but I watched the Wimbledon final um from start to finish — and that was Yanick SA against um Alexander or Sasha Zerev um the recent French Open champion — and first of all I really enjoyed the match, and secondly I became a bit of a Yanick SA fan. I think that he's the real deal, isn't he?

He's the real deal.

He's the real deal. The guy has got a lot going on, right? He is a brilliant athlete. You're watching literally an — it's an on camera transformation here.

Yeah, he's a brilliant athlete. He's an amazing competitor, and he has that star quality of being able to pull out his very very best just when it's really really needed. And I just thought it was absolutely compelling watching — it was a champion, true champion's performance, cuz ZV brought his A-game.

Sinner's Background: Skiing, German, and Piatti

And it inspired me, Charlie, um my new found fandom for Yanick S — um it inspired me to look a little bit more deeply into the guy, um because he does seem like a sort of mild-mannered kind of guy, you know, good guy, nice guy, etc. Um, but uh it it's not quite as simple as that. And this is a guy who is phenomenally driven.

The extent of my lack of curiosity about Yanik extends even to the fact that, ve at various times in the last two years since he became prominent, I thought bit odd for an Italian to have a German name. So I mentioned this [laughter] I mentioned this to my wife who is German, and she and she sort of clouted me around the head affectionately and said, "You do know that there's part of Italy which is on the Austrian border where they where they speak German." And I looked it up and found that Yanik in his first language is German, and he comes from a ski village um on the Austrian border and grew up skiing — not ski jumping, very just in case you get excited about his recruitment prospects for [laughter] for naturalizing him as a Norwegian ski jumper, Charlie. Um a downhill skier.

Um and uh but clearly a young player of immense promise on the tennis side. He was taken in by a very prominent Italian tennis coach um as a teenager um —

Ricardo Piatti —

who coached him through from that young age through to being a top tennis player.

The Systematic Firing of Coaches

But since then, and as I relate in in my memo for those Substack members this week, he's been quite a hard taskmaster. You know, he fired Piatti despite all the success he's had with him um and replaced him with Darren Cahill, who's an Australian who had been coached — coached multiple Grand Slam champions. So when um in other words, when SA decided "I need to make my next — I need to get to the next level. Thanks a lot, Patty. Nice to see you. I'm now going to switch to Darren Cahill because he coaches Grand Slam champions."

He had a physical coach — bulleted him. People may remember there was a controversy over Rafel dope test, and SA fired his his fitness coach for holding him accountable for that.

Quite a serious controversy. Yeah.

Serious controversy. He then um had two new um fitness coaches, bulleted them because one of them he thought spoke a little bit too much in in the media about things and this type of stuff. Didn't want that, etc.

So, this is a guy — he's not going through coaches like Emma Radukanu does in a sort of sort of hell sort of sort of rather odd way. In a very systematic way, he is making sure that every single person who works for him is utterly dedicated to turning him into one of the all-time greats.

Well, yeah, Charlie, I actually say the opposite because Darren Cahill was meant to leave him last year and he managed to persuade him to stay.

Yeah.

So, I think what he — what he's incredibly good at doing is understanding exactly who he needs in his box.

Yeah.

And you look up at that box and you see those people and you can — you just know that every single one — Yeah. It's — Yeah, it it's in it, right?

Um and well, interestingly, the guy that he fired, Ferrer, for the — uh who was supposedly responsible or partly responsible for his failed test — was Claustable, it's very very specifically banned by Wada, and any physio really should know that. Even though, you know, he's fired him, he he's actually brought him back because he's after — after he's obviously served his ban.

Yeah. Which didn't involve him missing any grand slams and confused a lot of — cause a lot of confusion around the tennis world. Anyway, that's a — that's a deeper hole to go into.

Italy's Tennis Miracle

Anyway, the point is: this is a guy that in weeks where we've been talking about what it is to be professional and what it is to be absolutely elite at the top of your sport, this is a guy that does it. And actually, where I've been most interested in tennis over the last few years from a national perspective, which leads us into another part of this chat, Charlie, is how good Italy have been over the last few years at developing.

They've got four top 20 men's players. Um which in a truly global sport is a really significant achievement.

You got S, Kaboli.

Yeah. Berrettini.

And there is another one. Um —

Arnaldi also, didn't he — got to the semis of French. Yeah.

And then you've got Jasmine Paolini on the women's side.

Yeah.

So this is a system which is working.

The Grand Slam Windfall — and Does It Work?

Um now we've spoken in recent weeks in the whole debate over um pay at the Grand Slams — how c- certain of the top players are wingy about how much they get paid by the grand slams and went on a rather pathetic sort of not-even-a-media-strike, a sort of media downscaling of their media commitments. Um —

It was nothing. No one noticed anything.

Yeah, forget it. Um so um well, but what one thing we have noted is is that those um associations that host the grand slams — which is Tennis Australia, the French Tennis Federation, the Lawn Tennis Association that's here in England, and the US Tennis Association — so those tennis associations that are lucky enough to host a grand slam hand a very significant amount of money — each of them — to the elite tennis development schemes of their country, right?

So, it's a real windfall for those countries who who happen to be lucky enough to host one of the Grand Slams. It means that their elite systems get vast amounts of money chucked at them basically to subsidize the development of elite players.

Does it work?

Well, based on the data, you'd have to say it doesn't work.

It doesn't work. Charlie, when we — we were just chatting about this, I was thinking, hang on a second —

Because just to be clear, I've been —

Well, we we've certainly had our views on on that pay discussion, and and one of it being, you know, very much so — if you take more money for the players, then you're taking it away from the places that actually go and develop players. But then actually, like if you put the counter to that, you go, well, yeah, develop players in a very specific region, actually in the richest countries because they're getting all of this revenue anyway. So, what I would naturally want to see from that is: if that money is going somewhere, I want to see some results.

The Grim Statistics

Yeah. And if you look across — we we did a, you know, we had a look at those uh four countries and what — how many real champions they produced. The the statistics are quite frightening.

So the UK, let's call it the UK, you know, has had Murray and Radicanu in the last 20 years. France — could you could you name how many Grand Slam champions France has had in the last 20 years?

Um I don't think there's been one.

Emily Mauresmo — 2000 and — that is 2006 she did it, so I'm thinking, you know —

And that was Wimbledon, so it's actually —

Australian Open — she won two.

Actually more than 20 years ago.

Australia — Sam Stosur and Ash Barty.

US — obviously both Williams, Serena and Venus Williams, Sloan Stephens, Sofia Kenin, Coco Gauff, Madison Keys.

So a couple of things stand out there: no men — no man from Australia since Hewitt in 2002, no man from France since Yannick Noah in 1983, no man from the US since Andy Roddick in 2003. So Andy Murray is the only man from the Grand Slam countries to have won in the last 20 years.

Andy Murray Was Not an LTA Product

And guess what? What do we all know about Andy Murray and his training?

He had absolutely nothing to do with the Lawn Tennis Association. His mother didn't believe in the Lawn Tennis Association, and his family, who were reasonably well-to-do, sent him to the um Sanchez-Casal um academy in Barcelona at a very young age. As also happened to Johanna Konta, who's one of the very few British women to have um reached an elite level in in in the last 20 years.

And if you look back at other great British players — Tim Henman grew up in a tennis middle-upper-middle-class tennis family and was sent to a specialist private tennis school called Reed's near Cobham. Again, not in the LTA set — that that was run by, I think, by John Lloyd or David Lloyd. Um Greg Rusedski grew up in Canada and, you know, transferred to England once he was already a professional tennis player. Then you've got um Cameron Norrie, who is one of the other players who's reached the top 10 in recent years, who grew up in New Zealand and then went to to tennis — um played tennis in in the US college system.

So — you where you get to amongst — even forgetting the Grand Slam champions, even talking about British players who have really made anything of themselves, I've come across two: Kyle Edmund and um Jack Draper, who have what you might call been LTA kids. Now Jack — um Jack Draper, it should be noted, is the son of the former chief executive of the LTA, Roger Draper. Um, so it's, you know, again, from a very upper-middle-class background, it's slightly hard to imagine that he wouldn't have had the resources to make it even if the LTA had not eased his way.

So, we're really looking at Kyle Edmund being the only example of a successful British male tennis player — all female really, I think, maybe other than Radukanu — who has been helped through the system by the LTA.

The £300 Million Question

Now, the LTA spends 15 million pounds a year on their youth program. Sorry, by their youth program, I mean their elite youth program. So that's on these kids, right? 15 million pounds a year. And then on top of that, they spend another 35 million on putting on professional events to — with with the express purpose of helping these elite players have events to play in so they can climb the ladder.

So in that time — 15 million at the elite — 20 years — criky. So 300 million, let's say, has developed —

No one. Well —

Radicanu maybe to — to win the top tournament. Because we can't — because we can't call Murray, as you said, coming — you can't say that that money has gone to him.

So suddenly you're looking at that going, wow, okay, well — where's that going?

It's — it's a quite extraordinary allocation of capital.

The Italian Comparison: Clubs, Not Courts

Now, if I — I sort of dug into it a bit looking at the Italian system, right? Because actually the Italian tennis federation, despite not having a grand slam, still generates about the same amount of money as the LTA. And I wondered why that was.

So, I looked into it and I found that we've got 2,000 tennis clubs in this country. In Britain, in Italy, they have 4½ thousand. Now, if you want to try and work out why Italy is producing so many top players — it's a country of almost identical size to the UK — and it has more than twice as many tennis clubs.

Clubs. Interesting.

Now, clubs is important. I should tell why clubs — because it's also important in golf as well. So, the LTA puts a huge amount of money into tennis courts, right? That includes many, many, many thousands of courts in public parks, right? So, this is a court where if Charlie and Charlie want to go and have a game of tennis, we can go and play tennis for either free or or for not very much money.

But that isn't going to develop an elite player. Develop an elite player, you have to have a coach. And the coach ain't going to be in the — in the course in the park. The the coach is going to be at a club. And this is, I think, where the problem lies and why — why Italy are doing so well is they've got many more coaches.

The LTA's Defense: Participation

So that will be it. Because my counter to any critique that it was leveling was going to be: the real mandate of the LTA is to get participation up in this country. Last year, I think you had 5.8 million adults playing in the UK. You had 4 million children, 3,000 park courts renovated is your point — judged on those, that was the most successful year.

So for the money that — let's say — I think participation had — I had here 26 million which is court funding versus schools competition. That's doing great.

That's doing really well.

That's doing really well.

And therefore, you know, Grand Slam Champions we all want, but the most important part of their allocation is to get people playing the game. Okay, they're doing well.

That doesn't mean they get a let-off for spending 156 — sorry, £50 million pounds a year on an elite division that doesn't throw out anything. And, you know, cumulatively over the 20 years, if I was just going through it — and that, you know, worked out that basically 2025 they got 48 million from Wimbledon which was about 90% of Wimbledon's profits, as we know previously. So, you know, just cumulatively over the years, the account comes out — let's say they've received about 700 million pounds over the 20 years.

Yeah.

From Wimbledon.

Of which they're spending about half on the professional game — the elite game.

Yeah. Right.

Yeah. A third. Yeah. Because 16 of — let's say — no. Because if they're paying £35 million on professional events and 15 million on the on the elite program.

Oh, you're saying professional events. I'm saying professional events is a — I wouldn't — I wouldn't class that as a contributor to us developing Grand Slam champions.

That's how they see it. That's how — that's why the LTA do it. So that's their excuse for spending a lot of money on elite events is to help develop elite players.

Wow.

In in this country, because that's what gives them the ability to give them wild cards and to get more British players playing. And —

That's just like — it just gives — it's just cannon fodder to some of the [laughter] other — Arthur Fery.

Yeah. [clears throat] New exception to the rule, Charlie. [laughter] No. How — how many wild cards were there across —

Yeah. Yeah. I know, indeed.

The Italian Control Study

So, so anyway, but I thought it was interesting just looking — looking at the Italians um and saying it's a really good control study where you've got a — a country which is the same size in Europe, both developed tennis economies. Um both of them would have aspirations to producing elite players. Both of the national federations are making kind of quite a bit of money, can afford to do things properly.

And the Italian system is pushing money into clubs and keeping the club system alive and kicking. And the English system is not pushing money into clubs. The English system is pushing money into, on the one hand, public participation courts; on the on the other hand, what you might call hothousing.

The Injury Question

Now there's one other bit I just want to just raise, and this may be circumstantial, but the top two current players who have come through that system are Jack Draper and Emma Raticanu. What is the thing that is regarded as holding both of their careers back?

Injuries.

Terrible injuries. I mean, like catastrophic series of injuries as quite young players. We're not talking about players in their mid-30s. We're talking about players in their early to mid-20s. Injury after injury after injury. I [clears throat] wonder if there might be some attention which the LTA needs to pay to their performance program.

Interesting point. Yeah.

Um because it seems a bit too much of a coincidence to have your two main stars that you spent so much money on unable, basically, to get themselves on the court in one piece — hobbling around on crutches and casts all the time.

Well, they should be in the best years of their career. Well, coming into the best years of their career.

Absolutely. Yeah.

The Golf Contrast

Um so look, it's an interesting thing. And if you contrast it with golf, where Britain over-indexes and does exceptionally well, what do we have many more of than other —

Golf clubs?

Golf clubs. Yeah, golf clubs with qualified PGA professionals who will pick up a young player, teach them the basics, get them up to a certain level.

Now, you're kind of limited, aren't you? Because golf, you can't have a park court in the same way that you can have a — golf course generally has to be a golf club. So, so the system, you're almost forced into that model without having to make a decision.

That that is true. So, yes, we have both many more golf courses and many more golf clubs, cuz in golf the two things are basically the same. Um but I would probably again think about — and do you remember one of the most interesting things you said about Norway and football last week was how many coaches they have.

Yeah. The sheer number of qualified coach — 300 — sorry, there was one coach every 325 people.

I'm going to come back to what the number of qualified golf coaches is in Britain. I'm going to guess that it's pretty much the highest per head of population of anywhere. I mean certainly in Scotland — I mean it's quite extreme, that — I mean Scotland I think have got some sort of golf pro factory somewhere and they roll them out wherever you go in the world. If you go and play golf at some obscure place in Thailand, it'll say um "PJ golf professional Hamish Matagot" — it's quite extraordinary, they get everywhere. So anyway, we'll come back to it a — non —

Closing: Prize Money Position Unchanged

Listeners, I'm just saying — although we're criticizing, I still am not convinced that the players deserve any more money than they've been getting at the moment. The tennis players —

Oh right, yeah, no, absolutely not.

Even — even if we're questioning whether that distribution of cash. I still think a 19 or 20% increase on your Wimbledon prize money was — was pretty good. So, yeah, we're not changing — I'm not changing my position on that. But I do think having a look at the allocation of that, and actually then holding to a standard of — um of success — is is important.

item.description:
## 一、辛纳的"教练组合投资学"：把自己当作一支基金来运营
大多数人看到辛纳频繁更换教练团队，第一反应是"这人不好相处"或"太苛刻"。但仔细看他的换人逻辑，会发现一个惊人的模式——**他不是在换人，他是在做资产配置**。
Piatti 带他从少年到职业球员，功能兑现后被替换成 Cahill（因为 Cahill 有培养大满贯冠军的经验）。体能教练因为在多巴胺失利事件中"未尽把关之责"被开除，但服完禁赛期后又被请回——因为他技术层面依然是最优解。这是纯粹的**功能主义人力资本管理**，情感权重接近于零。
跨领域看，这更像顶级对冲基金经理管理团队的方式，而不像传统体育明星和团队的关系。有意思的对比是拉杜卡努——她换教练是"混乱型换人"（emotional churn），辛纳是"结构型换人"（portfolio rebalancing）。这两种"换人"在表面统计上看起来一样，但底层完全是两种物种。
## 二、"大满贯东道主诅咒"：拿到 Wimbledon 反而毁了英国网球
这是整段访谈里最反直觉的洞察，但主持人只是掠过没有点破：**举办大满贯的四个国家，在过去 20 年男子网坛几乎

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Can the AI Industry Regulate Itself? Stripe Wants PayPal, China Catches Up, NY Bans Datacenters
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的"元模式"：AI 治理正在复刻互联网的中国化路径——只是方向反了

大多数人把 Demis 的 SRO 提案、Anthropic 的州级"棘轮战略"、纽约的数据中心禁令看成孤立事件。但把它们叠在一张时间轴上，你会发现一个反常的镜像：中国用中央集权制造了 AI 的"确定性护城河"，而美国正在用"分权 + 恐惧"制造同样的护城河，只是收益者从国家变成了两三家私营实验室。

Sachs 说 Anthropic 想要"FAA for AI"，Chamath 说要防止"少数玩家用资产负债表把梯子拉上去"——他们其实在描述同一件事的两面：监管资本主义 (regulatory capitalism) 的自我组装。真正让我意外的观察是：Anthropic 的策略并不是"游说政府"，而是把政府当作分销渠道。它不需要说服华盛顿，只需要在 50 个州同时点火，让州级立法成为它的 API——一个把"安全焦虑"转换成"竞争对手合规成本"的 API。这是一种前所未见的商业模式：把外部性货币化的反向 SaaS。

跨领域连接：PayPal 收购案、Bending Spoons、以及"数字文艺复兴"的共同物理定律

表面上，Stripe 出价 PayPal、Bending Spoons 收购 Evernote/WeTransfer/AOL、Ryan Cohen 盯上 eBay，是三个不相关的故事。但把它们放进一个热力学框架里看——这些"僵尸互联网资产"就是被困在低能态里的高熵系统。它们积累了巨大的用户网络（4.39 亿 PayPal 账户），却因为组织熵（咨询公司文化、创始人流失、产品停滞）无法释放能量。

AI 不是在"改造"它们——AI 是在扮演麦克斯韦妖：一个能以极低成本对内部混乱进行分类、重排、再激活的信息机器。这解释了一个反直觉的现象：为什么 AI 时代第一波真正的巨额价值捕获，不会发生在纯 AI 公司，而会发生在"AI 原生运营者收购老互联网资产"这类交叉地带。Bending Spoons 从米兰远程重启十几家死掉的 Web 2.0 公司，本质上和 SpaceX 收购发动机公司让 Colossus 绕过清洁空气许可，是同一个操作——都在用"新一代物理"来套利"上一代监管/组织摩擦"造成的价值真空。

最出人意料的连接：Russia Today、反 GMO、反数据中心、以及一个正在成型的"认知供应链攻击"

Freeberg 提到 Russia Today 与反 GMO 情绪的时间相关性时，几乎在无意间揭示了本期播客里最深的系统性洞察——认知层是新的供应链，而供应链是可以被污染的。GMO 情绪曲线在 RT 进入美国后翻倍上升，在被驱逐后回落。同样的曲线正在数据中心议题上重演。

但真正让我意外的是这个连接：Anthropic 用"AI 灾难叙事"驱动州级立法，与外国影响运营用"数据中心叙事"减缓美国基建，在信息流形态上是同构的。它们都利用了同一个心理学漏洞——Sachs 说的"道德恐慌 (moral panic)"是免疫系统外的一种寄生模式：先制造焦虑，再提供"解决方案"（无论是新监管、新政策，还是让对手停下来）。

这里有一个极不舒服但也许更真实的假设：在信息生态里，"内部监管资本主义"和"外部认知战"不是对立的两股力量，而是共生的——它们互相为对方提供合法性和素材。Anthropic 的恐惧叙事让外国影响操作显得"有道理"；外国操作又让 Anthropic 的监管游说有了"民意基础"。这就是为什么 Chamath 说"必须尽快确立规则以避免这个 off-ramp"——他真正担心的不是任何一方，而是两者共振时产生的驻波：一个能让美国自我阉割 AI 竞争力的稳定态。

一个被所有人错过的连线：Token 价格差 100x = 主权代价

播客里所有话题——SRO、纽约禁令、开源之争、Apple 起诉 OpenAI、SpaceX 的数据泄漏——最终都指向同一个数字：每百万 token，Grok 是 1.5 美元，Fable 是 56 美元，中国模型是 50 美分。这不是价格差，这是一个国家愿意为"AI 主权幻觉"支付的税率。

当 Ramp 的 CEO 说 token 支出一年 21 倍增长、CFO 们即将迎来"错过财报的一天"时，他其实在描述一个宏观现象：美国企业正在集体为一个尚未证实的风险模型 (existential risk) 支付一个 100 倍的溢价。这个溢价的最终受益者不是纳税人，不是安全，而是那两三家品牌自己是"安全 AI"的公司。Sachs 说的"品牌自己安全，禁掉不安全，赚钱"这个三步战略——其实是对整个西方 AI 生态系统正在支付的隐性 GDP 税的精准描述。

Freeberg 详解 SRO 概念

I think it's worth putting a little definition around this proposal, which is to form an SRO, self-regulatory organization, because they're not purely independent. SRO's like FINRA and the National Futures Association, they exist in the financial markets and they were created to allow the financial institutions to set their regulatory rules, how they check each other, how they make sure that everyone is being safe because they're obviously all trading risk with one another. So the industry doesn't want to have exposure and they certainly don't want to have things get slowed down because that would make the markets inefficient.

So the analogy with AI is pretty appropriate here which is that there are many players in the industry. They are all trying to progress AI technology and no one wants to have a single regulatory body that comes in from the government or outside that says here are the tests you guys have to pass with your models in order for them to be appropriate. As we saw in California when California tried to pass AI legislation, I think it was about a year a year and a half ago, none of what they wrote even made sense at the time. But fast forward a year, none of those kind of rules and requirements actually map to the technology of the day.

So the purpose of an SRO like FINR run NFA is they can adjust how tests are being run, who is actually running the test and make sure the right experts are involved in doing this, independent experts that is to do the testing with federal government oversight but not control. So in the case of FINRA NFA, they report up ultimately to Senate committee and House committee that gives those committees oversight of those governing bodies that are supposed to be doing the work to make sure that they're doing their frigin jobs.

So the SRO concept would be that experts could be brought in from industry that know how to assess models for things like cyber risk, for things like biorisk, for things like weapons risk, social manipulation, etc., etc. that independent body can get voted on, can get changed over time, and because they actually have expertise in running software valves and running tests like this, they can operate at a faster pace than setting up a new government agency.

So, it's it's kind of a very elegant solution. And I think it's why everyone, to your point, Jal, and I I'll say this is right, the industry recognizes that there needs to be some degree of oversight and checkpoints here. And I think that this could actually solve that problem. So that's why I think everyone's kind of climbing on board with it because it doesn't actually hand stuff over to the government. It says, "Hey, we're going to get the right people to to take a look at these things and the government is going to have oversight ultimately."
