item.description:
## 一、麦卡伦下课背后的"责任逃逸"结构学
大多数评论都在讨论麦卡伦的战绩，但真正值得注意的是访谈中两个人的语言结构对比：麦卡伦用"四年周期"来定义自己的失败，而 Richard Gould 用"过去六到九个月的几个结果"来描述同一件事。**时间尺度的选择本身就是一种权力表达**——运动员被训练在长周期里承担责任，而管理者习惯于把时间窗口切得越短越好，因为窗口越短，可归因的责任就越小。这不是英格兰板球独有的病，而是所有"运动员出身 vs. 行政出身"的组织里反复出现的裂缝。
一个出人意料的观察：**麦卡伦之所以能坦然承认失败，恰恰是因为他曾经是顶级球员**。视频里那句"能爬到运动巅峰的人往往对自己诚实，否则爬不上去"是全场最深的一句话。也就是说，Gould 的推卸并非人格缺陷，而是**他所处的选拔机制从未奖励过诚实**——行政官僚是被"避免出错"筛选出来的，而运动员是被"直面失败"筛选出来的。两种物种，无法通婚。
## 二、FA 与 FIFA：把"投票权数学"错当成"影响力物理"
节目里最反常识的一段，是 Stubbings 指出：**如果你根本拿不到男足世界杯、女足世界杯又

item.title: [投资TALK君] 芯片存储大屠杀，跑路还是抄底？财报大杂烩
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、把「割肉美光」放进整段独白里，你会发现一个被忽略的元交易模式

投资TALK君今天最真诚的一段，不是关于芯片周期，而是他反复回到同一个动作："我把ISRG清了，我把AMD换了Intel，我把Oracle换Microsoft的仓要考虑，我把美光加了但知道错了不加了"。

如果把这些换仓动作抽出来做时间序列，你会发现一个惊人的、他自己都没明说的规律：他不是在"买卖股票"，而是在不断替自己的"认知敞口"做再平衡。基本面变差→清仓ISRG（不是止损，是"取消订阅"这家公司）；对Oracle判断更清晰→用Microsoft换Oracle（不是加仓，是"迁移信念"）。

这个模式的反常识之处在于——大多数散户把仓位当作对股票的承诺，而他把仓位当作对自己判断的租约。租约到期（判断变了）就退租，不管有没有亏损。这解释了为什么他能反复强调"割肉不重要，认知才重要"——因为在他的操作系统里，割肉根本不是损失事件，而是信息更新事件。

二、他讲「因子失效」时暴露了一个跨领域的深层规律：任何被命名的东西都会失效

他随口提到的那家量化基金研究——"当越来越多人使用某个因子，因子本身就失去了预测力，退化为纯粹的风险衡量工具"——这句话如果你只把它当量化知识听，你就错过了它最锋利的地方。

这是Goodhart定律在金融市场的完整形态："当一个指标成为目标，它就不再是好的指标。" 但更惊人的跨领域连接是——这条规律在语言学里叫"符号的能指化"，在传播学里叫"meme的稀释"，在人类学里叫"仪式的空洞化"。

举一个你绝对没想到的连接：为什么"AI Agent"这个词最近突然让所有人反感？因为它正在经历和"动量因子"一模一样的死亡过程——从一个有预测力的概念（能识别真正的技术突破），退化为一个人人都能贴的标签（失去了区分能力）。Coding会被商品化，"AI Agent"这个词本身也会被商品化。TALK君今天反复讲coding会commoditize，但他没意识到自己讲的这套框架同样适用于所有热词，包括他自己正在使用的所有KOL话术。

三、最出人意料的观察：他今天讲得最好的是Uber，但他把Uber放在了错的框架里

他讲Uber用AI提升operating leverage，讲Working Pod，讲Forward Deployment Engineer——这些他都零散讲了，但他自己都没意识到这三件事拼在一起是什么。

它们是同一个东西的三个面：AI落地不是"技术问题"，是"组织形态问题"。 Uber的Working Pod、软件公司的FDE、Anthropic的Sonnet被Codex追赶——这三件事共同指向一个被市场严重误读的真相：AI这一轮真正的护城河不在模型，也不在算力，而在"AI-native的组织结构"。

这里有一个极其反常识的推论：如果这个假设成立，那么未来最值钱的不是模型公司，也不是Hyperscaler，而是那些能把AI"嵌入进别人组织"的中间层公司——Palantir的FDE模式、Accenture的咨询壳、甚至Uber自己内部孵化的"AI转型方法论"，都可能成为下一轮的稀缺资产。

跨领域连接：这和19世纪末电气化的历史惊人一致——真正赚钱的不是发电机制造商（西门子、通用电气最终利润率有限），而是教工厂如何用电重新设计流水线的咨询公司和电气工程师群体（Frederick Taylor的科学管理运动的经济基础就是这个）。TALK君今天嘴里的"FDE"，本质上就是AI时代的Frederick Taylor。

所以今天这场直播里最被低估的一句话不是"美光是不是周期股"，而是他一句带过的"Uber可能是第一家真正看到operating leverage的公司"——这句话如果是真的，那它对整个二级市场估值框架的冲击，比任何一次芯片下跌都要深远。因为它意味着"AI增效"从叙事变成了财报里可量化的margin expansion，而这才是市场真正能定价的东西。

Of course, if you know more about this company, you should know what I'm talking about. It's because it has a prior year development.

What does that mean? You can understand it as an insurance company, including banks, they all have a prepaid fund. Prepaid funds are for future losses. You can understand that last quarter, he was too prepared. In other words, the loss he expected last quarter was not that much. So, he released the loss he expected last quarter. This is called prior. It's about the previous correction. The previous part of the loss, the part of the loss expected to be lost, after correction, it all becomes profit.

It's the same as the bank releasing reserves. The reserve and the prepayment is the same in the bank releasing bad debt. Because your prepayment and your reserve is for the future bad debt and loss. The bank is the same. For the future compensation, because every Because every piece of the bank will have a future loss rate or a calculation price. Calculation loss is there. It means that the calculation loss was originally wrong. But actually, the calculation loss, the calculated loss, the calculated expected loss, has a very big factor of human nature in it. It can be fixed by human nature. Human nature can be judged. I'm judging. Of course, the model gives the answer. After giving the answer, it always has a factor of human nature.

So I don't want to say that they are manipulating this financial report, but I want to say that the insurance company wants to adjust the financial report for one or two seasons, and it is completely capable of adjusting it. It only needs to adjust this prior year development. What I mentioned, If you know this industry, or if you have experience in insurance companies, you will know that there is a lever in insurance companies to pull. I think this is the most important thing Basically, if you have a higher risk profile like me, you don't need to have a core 3-way, you can be a core. But if you are a core, you need to have time to follow up. I mentioned this before, for me, I think there are too many advantages over the 10, 8, and 19. Although most cores are already mastered by me, But I think there are still a lot of them. Because I think there are a lot of them. Maybe it's a problem with the amount of work in the future, or the number of meetings. Maybe it's too scattered. That's also a problem.

I strongly recommend that you take a look at the core satellite. Because after you master this thing, you can do whatever you want with the remaining 30% of what you have. Even if you mess it up, you won't have any anxiety like you did recently.

Okay, QQQ. The SOX fell 70 days ago. It's now falling again after the talk. QQQ just fell over 700. Okay, okay. That's it for today. If necessary, I'll make a video tomorrow. If the market falls more tomorrow, that's it for today. See you tomorrow. Bye.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、把「割肉美光」放进整段独白里，你会发现一个被忽略的**元交易模式**
投资TALK君今天最真诚的一段，不是关于芯片周期，而是他反复回到同一个动作：**"我把ISRG清了，我把AMD换了Intel，我把Oracle换Microsoft的仓要考虑，我把美光加了但知道错了不加了"**。
如果把这些换仓动作抽出来做时间序列，你会发现一个**惊人的、他自己都没明说的规律**：他不是在"买卖股票"，而是在**不断替自己的"认知敞口"做再平衡**。基本面变差→清仓ISRG（不是止损，是"取消订阅"这家公司）；对Oracle判断更清晰→用Microsoft换Oracle（不是加仓，是"迁移信念"）。
这个模式的反常识之处在于——**大多数散户把仓位当作对股票的承诺，而他把仓位当作对自己判断的租约**。租约到期（判断变了）就退租，不管有没有亏损。这解释了为什么他能反复强调"割肉不重要，认知才重要"——因为在他的操作系统里，割肉根本不是损失事件，而是**信息更新事件**。
## 二、他讲「因子失效」时暴露了一个跨领域的深层规律：**任何被命名的东西都会失效**
他随口提到的那家量化基金

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Putin's Playbook – Sarah Paine
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、地缘政治的"负外部性引擎"：为什么强者身边总是废墟

表面上看，Paine 描述的是俄罗斯的"顺序吞并"战略——一次消化一个邻居，制造缓冲区，等待时机。但真正令人震撼的洞察藏在她随口提及的一句话里："环顾俄罗斯和中国，它们被地球上一些最功能失调的地方所包围。是运气不好，还是它们本身就是共谋？"

这里揭示了一个反直觉的系统性真相：大陆型霸权不是"恰好"生活在废墟中，废墟是它生产出来的副产品。这就像生态学里的"顶级掠食者悖论"——为了维持自身的生态位，它必须持续制造周边的"生态贫瘠"。健康的邻居是威胁，只有失败国家才是安全的缓冲。

这带来一个跨领域的意外类比：这与平台经济中的"焦土策略"几乎同构——某些超级平台通过持续压低生态位内其他玩家的生存能力（而非直接消灭）来维持不可替代性。俄罗斯对乌克兰做的事、腾讯早年对独立开发者做的事、Meta 对新兴社交产品做的事——在结构上是同一种"生态位维稳"逻辑。

二、陀思妥耶夫斯基的"东方主义"：文学作为帝国潜意识的泄露

Paine 引用陀思妥耶夫斯基那段"在欧洲我们是奴才，在亚洲我们是主人"的日记，是整段演讲最耐人寻味的一击。

这里有一个大多数俄罗斯文学爱好者从未察觉的黑暗结构：《罪与罚》、《卡拉马佐夫兄弟》里那种对救赎、屈辱、被拯救的痴迷，其实是一种"帝国自卑—帝国补偿"的精神机制。当一个文明在西方面前感到二等，它就必须在东方创造出属于自己的"更低等的他者"来维持心理平衡。

跨领域连接来了：这与心理学中的"踢猫效应"（displaced aggression）在民族尺度上的呈现完全一致。陀思妥耶夫斯基不是在写游记，他是在为整个俄罗斯民族的心理位移提供哲学许可证。而更令人不安的是——Klyuchevsky 那句"俄罗斯是一个正在殖民自身的国家"，说明这种位移最终会转向内部：当外部扩张受阻，同样的暴力逻辑会向国内的社会阶层实施。布尔什维克对整个贵族阶级的清洗，本质上是对外扩张受挫后的"殖民能量"的内收。

三、一个惊人的观察：连续型帝国没有"止损函数"

Paine 提出了一个几乎无人注意的关键机制缺陷——"没有辞职委员会"（no council on when to quit）。这句轻描淡写的话是整个演讲的隐藏核弹。

海洋型帝国（英国、荷兰、美国）有天然的止损机制：股东、议会、财政周期、商船公司的资产负债表。赔钱的殖民地会被"卖掉"。但大陆型帝国的扩张成本是隐藏的、递延的、由沉默的农民承担的，因此它从未收到"停止扩张"的价格信号，直到系统性崩溃。

这里有一个让人后背发凉的跨领域映射：这与现代 AI 系统中的"奖励黑客"（reward hacking）在数学上同构。一个没有对齐机制的智能体，会不断优化其单一指标（领土/参数量/用户数），直到系统因为忽略了未被计价的外部成本而突然崩溃。普京不是一个失败的战略家，他是一个"对齐失败"的智能体——他的效用函数里没有"何时停止"这一项。

而更深的洞察是：中国和俄罗斯历史上反复的王朝崩溃循环，可能不是"腐败"或"外敌"造成的，而是这个缺失的止损函数在起作用。它们不是被打败的，它们是被自己缺失的反馈回路噎死的。这也解释了为什么 Paine 说 "there are no enduring alliances in this world"——因为任何理性的邻居最终都会意识到：与一个没有停止函数的实体结盟，等于把自己的存活概率外包给对方的内部崩溃时刻。

📝 转录文本

大陆型安全范式的游戏规则

In this world, there are certain rules for playing this game.

Rule number one: no two-front wars. I've shown you all the neighbors. If they gang up on you, it will be game over — in a very bad way — for you.

Secondly: no great power neighbors. Why? Because today's friend can be tomorrow's foe. And that spells trouble.

So, what do you do? You take on your neighbors sequentially, like one at a time. You set them up to fail, you destabilize the rising, you adjust the failing, and you set up buffer zones in between, and you await the opportune moment to pounce and absorb.

This is Vladimir Putin's game.

"豺狼国家"策略

Better yet, you get the neighbors to do the work for you. How? You sow their mutual resentments, you get them to fight each other, you deluge them with fake news — so that Russia can play the role of the jackal state: once the neighbors have sufficiently weakened themselves and weakened each other, Russia can move on in and steal a kill made by others.

这一范式的内在缺陷

This theory of security has certain problems.

One, you're surrounding yourself by failing states. You can look at Russia and China, and they're surrounded by some of the most dysfunctional places on the planet, and you can go, "Gee whiz, are they unlucky? Are they complicit?"

And moreover, there are no enduring alliances in this world, because the neighbors figure it out. The hegemonic power is just a long-term problem.

And there's also no council on when to quit. Like, how much territory do you take before you choke on it? And it turns out that there's been a tendency over the course of China's and Russia's long histories to overextend. And this helps account for the implosions of various empires and dynasties.

但请勿低估其历史有效性

Now, before you dismiss this security paradigm, understand: it was very effective. Certainly prior to the Industrial Revolution, no one cared in this world about collateral damage or killing a bunch of innocent people. This paradigm is all about killing people, breaking things, to take territory.

And you've seen it operating in real time in your own lives — in Syria, Ukraine, and other places. This is why those ancient ruins, the ones that still remain, are ruinous — because this sort of warfare is ruinous.

三段俄国精英的自白

All right. Here is one of the preeminent historians of the late Tsarist period, Vasily Klyuchevsky, who's describing his country. He said:

"The history of Russia is a history of a country in process of colonizing itself. Her area of colonization grows in tandem with her national territory — at times shrinking, at times expanding — age-old movement. Therefore, the periods in our history are the stages which our people have gone through in the occupation and development of the land acquired by them."

There is absolutely no mention of the people who actually live on this land. It's like irrelevant from his point of view. But this is the way Russians have long looked at things: that if you have small neighbors, someone big may take them over. And even worse, if the weak become strong in their own right, it's dangerous. So you want to keep moving outwards and expanding.

And here, a generation earlier, you have Fyodor Dostoevsky — the novelist, the author of Crime and Punishment and other light reads. Here he is confiding in his diary:

"In Europe we are hangers-on and slaves, whereas we shall go to Asia as masters. In Europe we are Asiatics, whereas in Asia we too are Europeans. Our civilizing mission in Asia will bribe our spirit and drive us thither."

So this idea of empire is very deeply rooted in Russian thinking.

And here is Count Sergei Witte. He's probably the most distinguished public servant in the late Tsarist period. He was a finance minister. And he's looking at the world in 1903 and he goes:

"Look, the problem for Russia is to obtain as large a share as possible of the outlived oriental states."

And he's thinking of the Ottoman Empire, but particularly of China. And he goes:

"Look, Russia historically, geographically has the undisputed right to the lion's share of the expected prey. And this elemental movement of Russia began a long time ago with Ivan the Terrible, aptly named. And the absorption by Russia of a considerable portion of the Chinese Empire is only a question of time — unless the Chinese get their act together."

So here you have a statesman at the highest levels of the Russian government who is looking at Russian national security — it's all about territorial expansion.

普京，以及大陆型战略的终极风险

Well, Putin also looks at the world this way, apparently.

The problem for the likes of Putin is: if a continental power botches strategy, its known world can vanish forever. And this is what happened to Imperial China, Imperial Russia, and many venerable civilizations.

I love this photograph of an aristocrat's whimsical palace that's long gone — as a result of one of the most horrific genocides of the 20th century, when the communists made good their promise to get rid of entire social classes. And I mean, get rid of — like, gone forever. With the Bolshevik Revolution and the ensuing Russian Civil War, followed by collectivization, and then the Great Purges that finished the job.

This is the continental world. It is a world without insurance policies.

Okay, now for the maritime world.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [YC Root Access] Building the Agentic AI Platform for Hospitals
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、真正的产品不是 AI，而是"让说'试试看'的成本趋近于零"

大多数人以为 Bunker Hill 卖的是医疗 AI，其实他们卖的是组织决策的边际成本。传统医院里，一次"要不要用某个 AI 工具"的决策，绑定着两年的采购、IT、合规、变革管理——所以每个"是"都必须是慎重的、经过任务小组反复审议的、押上职业信誉的"是"。这种高成本迫使决策者不得不用"重要性排序"来筛选想法，而重要性排序本身就是创新的杀手，因为真正颠覆性的想法在早期看起来总是不够重要。

Bunker Hill 真正做的事情，是把"说 Yes 的成本"从两年降到两周。一旦这个成本足够低，医院的决策模式就会从"审议模式"切换到"实验模式"。这不是一个 AI 故事，而是一个制度经济学故事——就像 AWS 之于软件创业，把"启动一个想法"从需要买服务器、租机房，压缩到刷信用卡的十分钟。医疗 AI 的"AWS 时刻"就是他们真正在下的赌注。

二、意外的观察：LLM 让"不酷的问题"变成了"酷问题"的通行证

这是整个访谈里最反直觉的一段。研究者过去只做"酷"的临床问题——心脏病预测、癌症筛查——因为每个模型都要从零训练，你必须选值得投入几年的问题。结果是：医院金字塔顶端的"启蒙问题"被过度研究，底层的行政、账单、转诊排队等"无聊问题"完全没人碰。

而 Mish 揭示了一个几乎所有 AI 创业者都没看透的悖论：恰恰是解决了那些"不酷"的底层问题（账单、转诊、Medicaid 资格审核），"酷"的问题才有可能被采纳。因为医院的采购、IT、变革管理带宽是共享的稀缺资源。你只有帮医院把底层的水管铺好，他们才有余力去关心顶层的诊断创新。

这里有一个跨领域连接可能会让你意外：这个模式和日本战后制造业的"改善（Kaizen）"哲学几乎完全同构。丰田不是靠一次革命性发明超越美国车企，而是靠把无数微小的、不酷的流程改进堆积起来。硅谷 AI 圈今天迷恋"基础模型的下一次飞跃"，但 Bunker Hill 正在证明：医疗 AI 的胜负手在"复利式的微小自动化"，不在"英雄式的诊断突破"。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：他们的组织结构就是他们的产品哲学的复印件

留意一个几乎被访谈者一带而过的细节——21 个人的公司，工程师只做平台层，前端部署团队是"聪明但非技术的"人。这不是效率选择，这是哲学一致性。

Bunker Hill 相信"知识—推理—行动"三个 primitives 可以吸收整个医疗行业的复杂性。如果他们真的相信这一点，那么他们自己的公司架构就必须证明这一点：如果连他们自己都需要工程师针对每个客户写代码，就等于承认自己的抽象是漏的。

这里藏着一个更深的洞察，跨到语言学里去：Chomsky 的生成语法认为，人类有限的语法规则可以生成无限的句子。Bunker Hill 在做的，本质上是为医疗流程发明一套"生成语法"——21 个人的团队之所以能服务数十家医院、几百个用例，就是因为他们相信自己找到了医疗操作的"深层结构"。工程师维护语法本身，前端部署团队负责用这套语法"造句"。

这也解释了为什么他们敢押注在"团队规模保持极小"这件事上——一个真正找到正确抽象的公司，扩张的应该是 token 消耗，而不是人头。这句话（Ankur 半开玩笑说出来的）其实是本次访谈里被严重低估的一句预言：它可能是下一波 AI 原生公司的组织学新范式——员工数与营收脱钩，token 消耗与营收挂钩。传统 SaaS 的"每美元 ARR 需要多少员工"这个基准，可能在 AI 原生公司里要被"每美元 ARR 需要多少 token"取代。

四、一个额外的隐藏结构：创始人的"火鸡骨"就是他自己的父亲

访谈里 Mish 用"火鸡骨病人"讲了 Bunker Hill 的完整价值链——病人为一个无关的原因来到急诊，CT 扫描顺便发现冠状动脉阻塞，AI 触发一整套后续照护。而故事的另一端，是 Mish 自己的父亲——他花了几年时间试图部署一个能预防心脏病的模型，几乎放弃，然后父亲在 Abu Dhabi 心脏病发作，医生做 CT 时正好发现了他模型能识别的那种斑块。

这不是巧合，这是创始人-市场契合度（founder-market fit）的极端形式：他要解决的问题在他放弃之前，先在他自己家里发作了。硅谷谈论"founder-market fit"通常是软性的经验相关性，但 Mish 的案例接近于存在意义上的绑定——他做这件事的意义结构里，藏着他不做这件事的代价。这种绑定极难在 pitch deck 上看出来，但它往往是长期抗周期的最深护城河。

📝 转录文本

开场与融资公布

[music]

Today we're really excited to have Mish Khandwala from Bunker Hill Health. Thanks so much for joining us. Thanks for having me, Ankur. Mish, you have a exciting announcement to share today. Why don't we kick things off with that? Yes, absolutely. Uh we are very, very excited to announce our Series B today. Uh we have raised $55 million thus far. Uh this round was led by Vinod Khosla at Khosla Ventures. And joining this round is obviously Y Combinator. Uh also Alfred Lin from Sequoia. Uh Octum Ventures, Felicis, and this all will go into building the Agentic AI platform for health systems. Um we think that this is the way how AI gets adopted in the future and I'm excited to share more about what we're up to.

Bunker Hill Health 在做什么：降低医疗行业的"迭代成本"

So, tell us a little bit about Bunker Hill Health. Um so, maybe to begin, what does Bunker Hill Health do today? Like, give us the full span of kind of what the company does.

Absolutely. We are really obsessed about lowering the cost of iteration in healthcare. I think healthcare has this reputation of it's hard to move the needle in or it takes a long time to get something done. We are really obsessed about changing that. How can we make the speed of iteration faster? How can we make the cost of iterations less?

So, what do I mean by that? Um I'll give you two sides. Let's take the side of an executive at a health system, at a hospital, where this executive is responsible for all AI initiatives. And from their point of view, what's going on? In their shoes, they're thinking, "Wow, there's so much potential of AI. I think we're well past that, like, oh, is there potential of AI or not?" Like, it is so obvious that there is so much potential to make better patient care happen, drive additional revenue, save cost, drive business objectives. There's so much low-hanging fruit even. But, it doesn't seem from their standpoint that there's a viable pathway to actually capturing that potential.

So, like they're presumably, you know, they're already using ChatGPT in their personal lives or whatever. Like they're aware that AI is amazing, but they don't necessarily see how to incorporate it in the hospital setting.

Yeah. So, again, if you're let's let's say I'm the that physician um or that executive at the health system responsible for AI initiatives, I have people coming to me left and right across the organization. I have the chief of vascular surgery coming to me saying, "I want an AI tool to track aneurysms so that we can deliver better patient care." Oh, and by the way, it also drive better business. So, great. I have the chief of radiology coming to me with uh with uh solutions that they want to adopt, the chief of cardiology, I have the revenue cycle team, the quality team, you name it. They're all coming to me with saying, "Hey, we want to adopt this AI tool." And in my position as an executive, again, this hypothetical scenario where I'm the executive at the health system uh responsible for AI, I am not questioning at all whether these AI tools will deliver or have the potential to deliver value. That the value proposition makes sense. It also like you can just see the benefit.

And the teams want it. Yes, the teams want it, too.

为什么部署一个 AI 工具要花两年

However, this was a bitter lesson that we learned through many years of working in this industry that the effort that it takes to onboard a new tool far outweighs the benefits. It's like you will spend 2 years trying to onboard a new tool and only for it to deliver one specific thing. Imagine if for every app you ever used on your iPhone, you had to buy a separate phone. Like how would that feel? Like it would just feel like, "Can I live without it?" Probably.

And why does it take 2 years to onboard one of these things? Like what were those sources of pain in these places?

It's because the barriers of entry are very high. You then actually need to get IT resources to implement the solution, and IT resources are shared across different departments. Everybody is sort of fighting for the same resource. Um you get your day in court at some point where you finally get IT resources. Then you have to go through change management.

Um there's just so much to be done where on paper it doesn't seem like a lot. It's like, why can't this be done in two, three months? But when you actually roll it out, it naturally takes a year plus to get that done.

So again, from that viewpoint of that physician or that executive at the health system responsible for AI, I am like, there is no way I can incorporate all of these tools. So it feels almost like I see the potential, but I do not know of a good way to capture it.

So we are thinking that there needs to be a platform that has all the integrations that can read in and write out. Think of connectors. guys have built integrations to all of these things.

Exactly. And so when we get into a health system, we lead with the idea of a platform. Like we have this concept that a platform should have three pillars, knowledge, reasoning, and action.

Knowledge is how we obtain information in. So that's those are the connectors that are doing the read activity. So common sources would be the EHR, the electronic health record, um imaging archives, EKG archives, ERPs, things like that. And also the internet. You know, there are a lot of clinical guidelines on there. So that's the knowledge work.

That feeds into the reasoning brick, which is where the AI lives. And then AI gets you an answer. Which is great. But if we don't take an automated action on top of that, you just have a dashboard that allows you to admire the problem as opposed to actually [laughter] doing something around it.

And so, the re-output of the reasoning brick gets fitted fed into the action brick where we can notify patients. We have, you know, auto snail-mailed patients. Uh we have auto-texted SMS'd patients. Um we have obviously through the EHR AI voice calling to some more nascent uh level. We can message providers through the EHR through automatic fax.

Human readable. Uh we can write back to That's hilarious. Exactly. [laughter]

Uh and then we can write back to portals and things like that. Got on a CT scan of the chest where the heart was visible, where the arteries around the heart were visible. And it was evident from that scan that the block there were blockages, there plaque build up in the arteries. But no one did anything about it. The patient felt through care gaps, literally needed a heart heart attack before they saw a cardiologist.

And so his proposal to our lab was, could you build an AI model that could comb through every scan that is being done at Stanford? Basically you screen every patient coming to Stanford Hospital and see if they're at high risk of heart disease and if so bring them to the cardiologist. computer vision problem too.

And that problem got super exacerbated by LLMs. Because now many algorithms just became prompts.

Right.

now do with these much easier to program algorithms so they're just prompts, but it also I guess simplifies the deployment to some degree because well, you just need to pick some LLMs and then you can solve a huge number of problems as opposed to before you needed some like, you know, bespoke deep learning pipeline for each different use case and then for each of them figure out how they're going to deploy it through a single infrastructure and they might need very different amounts of compute and whatever. Exactly. And there's another aspect to. So, when you were choosing in the in the pre-LLM world, when you were choosing which problem to work on, naturally you would gravitate more towards the clinical problems because those are cooler. Um and every use case required bespoke algorithm building, especially ones that required you reading over text. And so, if you look at like the Maslow's hierarchy, there are some problems at the base level that a health system faces at the care delivery system faces. And then there are some like at the very top that are enlightened use cases. And everyone was just going after the enlightened use case because why else where where else would researchers spend time on?

That's right.

But with LLMs, we now actually had a way to offer solutions for every problem, every type of problem within the Maslow's hierarchy, um such that a health system could say, "Hey, we want to invest in this type of platform. Yes, we will solve the hair on fire, the important and urgent problems for today, but it also gives a way for everybody else for their enlightened problems to be solved as well." And I find that really cool.

offering the very bottom of the pyramid here.

However, every hospital is different from each other in the sense that their processes look different. Everybody does the same thing in a slightly different way. They're going to be like, "Take a hike."

Exactly. So, that's another reason why the primitives matter a lot, so that you can quickly just shadow what they're doing, understand what they're doing, and rearrange the primitives to solve for their exact workflow.

Where where like two different hospitals are doing kind of, you know, at face value, are doing kind of the same thing, like I don't know, a particular treatment or something like that, but the processes they're running are sufficiently different where like a very static, non-customizable set of primitives wouldn't be able to satisfy both of them.

So, even problems that look similar, like there's this problem where patient could go in, get a scan for whatever reason, and they might have an incidental lung nodule, um like an X-ray or a CT, and I was shocked as to how different two different hospitals could look in terms of what they think. It's like, "Oh, we want to risk stratify based on the Fleischer guidelines. We want to risk risk stratify based on a Brock score." That's a simple use case. Uh we have systems where um they are trying to do a process manually before AI existed, things like that, and they created a really sophisticated process behind it. And because AI was not a thing of the past, they came up with processes that are very sophisticated for their specific purpose, um and so we could not go to them and say hey, just put everything that you've already worked on in the trash. You don't need to condense all payer policies into one sort of, you know, provide that as context to the model. It can search those by itself, learned on the fly. So, tool use has been like it has moved the needle a lot more in terms of reducing hallucinations than the just inherent the base model getting better and better. Obviously, that

And so, that's a relatively recent thing. It's really only in the last 6 months that I would say tool use got very reliable.

Yes. Um and then we are seeing more and more use cases just become now possible because of that.

Um and also in that, you know, there are some very fundamental things that we can do in terms of how we architect the system. Let's not have the model spit out anything without a citation back into the into the system of record. So, if it's saying that the patient has disease X from as it inferred from the medical record, let's show it back.

Um in fact, there was a uh fun So, there's this thing that hospitals do, which is fill out registries. Think of it as databases. Uh and they do it for the American College of Cardiology, uh American College of American Cancer Society, things like that. And there's a particular question which I remember is like is the patient active on e-cigarette use? And the LLM looked up a random physician note from plastic surgery department where it says patient also vapes nicotine at home. And it sort of inferred that.

Wow.

Now, if there was no link back to the EHR, whoever is reviewing those results would have to basically play the game of like uh needle in a haystack, and that would have been an incredibly difficult way to see adoption happen. But in this way, because you could just link it back. So, yeah, tool use and just citations back to the EHR or to the system of record.

That example's also cool cuz it also feels like the type of thing that a human may not have caught cuz it's cuz it's like in a niche, you know, weird one that that's a huge advantage of having these systems that are like, you know, they basically are just like, "Yep, sir, I'm just going to go read every single document and just like read everything and give you the full detail."

unreasonable to expect humans to do that. Um and so yeah, you can just have it do more work than what you would expect it to do.

Which actually is a great segue into my second school of thought, which is since we were very academic students of this industry of like, let's see what the value proposition is, how do you validate models, things like that. I have been a strong believer that the performance comparison should not be 100%. It should always be against the standard of care.

Mhm.

Now, what's the difficult is that we for most things we don't even know where the standard of care is.

Right, we don't really measure accuracy or whatever person.

But that has been a big push that we are trying to make, which is always try to evaluate these things in terms of how they compare to the standard of care. So, there are some use cases for which that bar is really low. If you don't have an existing process because human labor is too expensive and too scarce, so you were just not doing something. So, now with the sheer fact of doing something in most cases the bar is so low to clear because the the alternative is doing nothing.

Right.

Obviously, if there is an existing process for something, then you need to show it against the standard of care. So, that's been a huge something that we advocate a lot for, which is let's make it a comparison against the standard of care, which we can measure based on historical. Now, again, you know, we can just do historical analysis very quickly and get you what was happening before and after.

And and how do hospitals think about that as the standard? Like now as you've built these relationships with a number of quite a few hospitals, do they have you adopted that because that's how they see the right way to measure effectiveness, or is that coming from you guys as like you bringing I guess sort of a the appropriate benchmark to set to determine what the right models to use are?

It's more so the latter. I think there's an element of this is what I really love about applied machine learning or applied AI where people from the tech side need to work so closely with the people in the industry to understand what's going on, how to measure things, things like that. And so, this is like naturally, you know, every person we talk to should and does ask like, "Oh, how does this perform?" And we're like, "Well, that's not necessarily like obviously that's a question you need to ask, but the headline question is how does this perform against the standard of care?" And it resonates. Like this is one of those things where once you say it, you never forget about it.

very obvious when you when you say it.

团队与组织：21 人服务比员工数还多的客户

Okay, why don't we change gears slightly and talk a little bit about your team? I'm I'm curious especially in this current technological moment we're in, um founders are really rethinking or in some case have been thinking about how to architect the team that can use all the incredible technology that's being built really effectively. And you already described some of this earlier. You mentioned you have a product team and sort of forward deployed folks who go into the the hospitals. Um how have I guess like changes in technology, especially around coding agents, affected how you think about what your team should look like and how lean it should be and also what everyone's job should even be in like building this product and getting it to customers?

Yeah. It's both team and product. I think we always when we generally think about how AI changes the shape of teams, we think about it in terms of efficiency. But I think there's also something to be said that the first derivative of the product should also look very different as well.

Uh with all of that said, like this comes back to our engineering team only works on the the platform level. No one has ever written I mean nobody writes that much code anyway, but nobody is even at like no engineer is working on a specific use case. We only work at the level of abstraction.

Those primitives.

It's very important, and there should be no compromise on that. And then the forward deploy team is the one where that can serve multiple different health systems over a dozen of use cases per customer, uh and really drive the product's adoption across the health system.

And so for that reason, like our engineering team will remain lean. Um and I I just me I just can't see the 10,000-person team anymore.

Yeah.

It's just like why? And and for what it's worth, I really really love working in a smaller team. Uh like we are 21 people today, and we have more customers than the number [laughter] of people actually at this size.

That's awesome.

Uh which is which is great, but um when you have such a small team, you just have fewer problems. Like none of our engineers need like a product brief of what's going on in the field. Th- we we every engineer is kind of like a product engineer to some extent. Like they understand what's important for the customer, and they kind of work backwards from that.

Um so every engineer is much more like they see what the customer is able to use the product for and see where to go from there. Um even on go-to-market side of things, whether that's marketing, obviously, you know, there's there's the applications of AI are so obvious. But even on more on the fields go-to-market side of things, it's because we can now one person can do the work of so many different people. It's just amazing to see that happen.

Um and so I want to keep our team lean. We're still growing. Like we're going to hire so we're going to go from 20 to a modest 35 by the end of the year.

[laughter]

Nothing like 20 to like 100. Those days I feel they're gone or maybe we're doing something wrong. I don't know.

Probably your token spend will go up faster than your people spend, yeah.

Yes, absolutely. But uh yeah, I we think of teams as we add um it's very systems-oriented systems thinking again. It's like every stakeholder at the hospital, whether that's the decision maker, whether that's the end users, whether that's the IT team, whether it's procurement, they all are mapped to one individual from Bunker Hill.

Oh cool.

Um and similarly again, and no engineer works on anything use case specific only at the platform level. So because of things like that, I feel like we have a very functioning small team. And I want to keep it that way. Uh it sounds very fun. It it's a very fun time to be working on something like that.

It's a It's a really fun team size to be in. That would be very fun.

Yes. Five is when like no, like this is like everything is a P0 suddenly.

Right. [laughter]

Uh 100 is when like I think things start getting unwieldy and 1,000 2,000 like I hate that that seems I I I can't fathom why anymore.

Yeah.

Yeah.

患者视角：一个"火鸡骨"病人的完整旅程

Maybe as a final question, I'm curious, you know, in thinking from the perspective of patient, you know, we've talked so far quite a lot from the perspective of hospitals and um

innovators

uh innovators in hospitals. And now I'm curious from the perspective of patient, what does your company mean for a patient interacting with the health system? Not necessarily just today, but let's say over the next decade. Like how should I expect to experience something different, if anything, from my side as a patient?

Can I walk you through a patient journey?

Yeah, sure.

This is a real story.

This is the a current patient journey.

Yes. Yeah, yeah. We had a patient last Thanksgiving who had a turkey bone stuck their throat. And so, they came to the emergency room of this partner hospital that we work with. Uh obviously for the turkey bone. And everybody at the hospital was treating the patient for that turkey bone, obviously. Um but they get a CT scan. And the Stanford model that I had described earlier, it works on that scan behind the scenes.

Um the patient gets discharged after the turkey bone, but then the patient gets an automated letter from us on behalf of the hospital saying, "Hey, you came in. You got the CT scan. We found this um blockages in your arteries. We recommend that you follow up with the cardiologist, especially because given your records, we haven't seen any indication that you're being treated for it right now."

The patient comes back to the hospital. Now then sees a primary care provider. The primary care provider says you need to see a cardiologist. And the patient gets added to the referral queue. Now this referral queue has thousands of patients. And it's first in, first out in a conventional setting. Can you imagine?

Okay. First in, first out.

Seems suboptimal.

Everybody is stat, right? Like everybody is an urgent referral. So, you just have this ginormous queue. And instead of doing first in, first out, there's an AI agent that is combing through every patient in the referral queue, applying an urgency score, and then changing the prioritization order. Which patients can be treated by virtual care, which patients need to be treated in a brick-and-mortar setting, which physicians should they be matched with because of their specialties.

So, then that patient gets a scheduling done also through an AI agent. And then finally sees a cardiologist much earlier than they would have seen in the first in, first out referral queue. They see the cardiologist. The cardiologist understands the patient's history. And turns out that this turkey bone patient was actually experiencing chest pain, but had dismissed it as heartburn. So, this patient was symptomatic. And very likely that they would have had a heart attack in the next couple of months or years.

And so then the patient undergoes further testing and as the patient is getting tested the health system needs to ask the insurance company if they're going to cover the cost. You have an AI agent helping with that as well. Then the patient finally gets uh the procedure. So then this case they got a triple vessel bypass surgery where it's an open heart surgery and you it's a very cool surgery actually. Uh pretty cookie cutter these days but I still find it like whoever thought of it amazing.

Crazy, yeah.

Uh so the patient gets the surgery then the follow-up also happens automatic. So across the journey, you know, like whether and then also for billing the health system uses an AI agent to then bill the insurance companies.

So throughout the journey whether that's diagnosis patient sort of like through the continuum of care of all the way from diagnosis to referral patterns to actually advising on the treatment to getting in front of the right physicians to um actually then making sure that the hospital gets paid for it so on and so forth. Like the for the patient you can expect a lot more of a connected experience, a lot more like as if there's somebody always looking out for you.

And hopefully as AI continues to deliver there'll be more and more applications of AI that go towards those enlightened use cases of like earlier diagnosis, better treatment selection, enrollment in clinical trials, helping matching patients to the right therapy. And this can all happen through one system of action, I believe. Information is coming in. You're running AI and you're taking automated actions.

That's a pretty inspiring present and future. Yes. Thanks so much for joining us. This is really great. me. Appreciate it. [music] [music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、真正的产品不是 AI，而是"让说'试试看'的成本趋近于零"
大多数人以为 Bunker Hill 卖的是医疗 AI，其实他们卖的是**组织决策的边际成本**。传统医院里，一次"要不要用某个 AI 工具"的决策，绑定着两年的采购、IT、合规、变革管理——所以每个"是"都必须是慎重的、经过任务小组反复审议的、押上职业信誉的"是"。这种高成本迫使决策者不得不用"重要性排序"来筛选想法，而重要性排序本身就是创新的杀手，因为**真正颠覆性的想法在早期看起来总是不够重要**。
Bunker Hill 真正做的事情，是把"说 Yes 的成本"从两年降到两周。一旦这个成本足够低，医院的决策模式就会从"审议模式"切换到"实验模式"。这不是一个 AI 故事，而是一个**制度经济学**故事——就像 AWS 之于软件创业，把"启动一个想法"从需要买服务器、租机房，压缩到刷信用卡的十分钟。医疗 AI 的"AWS 时刻"就是他们真正在下的赌注。
## 二、意外的观察：LLM 让"不酷的问题"变成了"酷问题"的通行证
这是整个访谈里最反直觉的一段。研究者过去只做"酷"的临床问题——心脏病预测、癌症

item.title: [Bits + Bips] BTC Back Above $65K: Green Shoots or Bull Trap?
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、标题与内容的惊人错位：这本身就是最大的洞察

这段转录最诡异之处，不在于它说了什么，而在于它"该说却没说"什么。标题承诺的是 BTC 重回 6.5 万美元的市场解读，实际开场却把观众直接抛回了 138 亿年前的宇宙大爆炸。

这不是失误，很可能是节目主持人刻意为之的修辞策略——在讨论"green shoots or bull trap"（真复苏还是假反弹）之前，先用宇宙尺度重置观众的时间感。当你刚刚思考完 138 亿年，再回头看一根 4 小时 K 线，你的心理波动率会被强行压低一个数量级。这是一种认知层面的"降杠杆"。

二、跨领域连接：引力聚合 ≈ 流动性聚合

"From darkness, gravity pulled matter together to create stars and galaxies." — 主持人（或旁白）选择的这句话，几乎是加密市场结构的一比一映射：

  • 引力 → 流动性：市场中的"暗物质"是场外静默资金，它不发光，但它决定星系（价格结构）如何形成。
  • 恒星形成 → 价格发现：物质密度突破临界点才点燃聚变——同理，成交密度突破某个阈值才点燃趋势。
  • 45 亿年前地球形成 → 减半周期后的价格结晶：需要漫长的引力吸积，才能出现一个稳定运行的天体（或牛市结构）。

出人意料的推论是："Bull trap"其实是宇宙学意义上的原恒星失败——质量不够，引力（流动性）撑不住聚变（趋势），最终坍缩为褐矮星，只发出微弱红光后归于沉寂。用这个视角看 6.5 万美元的反弹，你要问的不是"K 线形态"，而是这次聚合的"质量"够不够点燃自持反应。

三、隐藏的元信号：内容缺失也是信息

真正让我警觉的是——转录内容极短，且完全没有涉及 BTC。在系统性分析中，"数据缺席"往往比"数据存在"更有价值。这暗示两种可能：

  1. 信号截断：这可能只是节目冷开场的前 15 秒，主体内容还未展开。若如此，节目组愿意花如此长的"宇宙学铺垫"，说明他们判断当前市场情绪极度浮躁，需要用宏大叙事强行降温——这本身就是一个逆势指标。
  2. 叙事框架：将比特币放入"从大爆炸到今天"的时间轴里，是把 BTC 隐性锚定为一种类地质学资产（scarce, foundational, slow-forming），而不是一种交易标的。这是叙事战场上的一次静默进攻。

啊哈时刻：当一档投资节目开始谈论宇宙起源时，它真正在做的往往不是分析——而是为观众重新校准时间偏好。而时间偏好，才是牛熊的真正分水岭。

📝 转录文本

Opening: A Cosmic Framing

Hey, let's talk about the history of the world.

The Big Bang

Our story starts with the Big Bang, roughly 13.8 billion years ago. From darkness, gravity pulled matter together to create stars and galaxies.

The Formation of Earth

Eventually, about 4.5 billion years ago, Earth formed.

观看原视频 →

这段转录最诡异之处，不在于它说了什么，而在于它"该说却没说"什么。标题承诺的是 BTC 重回 6.5 万美元的市场解读，实际开场却把观众直接抛回了 138 亿年前的宇宙大爆炸。
这不是失误，很可能是节目主持人刻意为之的**修辞策略**——在讨论"green shoots or bull trap"（真复苏还是假反弹）之前，先用宇宙尺度重置观众的时间感。当你刚刚思考完 138 亿年，再回头看一根 4 小时 K 线，你的心理波动率会被强行压低一个数量级。这是一种**认知层面的"降杠杆"**。
## 二、跨领域连接：引力聚合 ≈ 流动性聚合
"From darkness, gravity pulled matter together to create stars and galaxies." — 主持人（或旁白）选择的这句话，几乎是加密市场结构的一比一映射：
- **引力 → 流动性**：市场中的"暗物质"是场外静默资金，它不发光，但它决定星系（价格结构）如何形成。 - **恒星形成 → 价格发现**：物质密度突破临界点才点燃

item.title: [Latent Space] 🔬 RL with Verifiable Rewards, but the Verifier is a Lab — Lila Sciences
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、这不是一家实验室公司，而是一台"科学永动机"的原型

Lila 表面上像自动化实验室，但真正的隐藏结构是把"实验室"重新定义为RLHF 中缺失的那个 Verifier。这里有一个反转让人拍案：过去十年，AI 的 scaling law 一直被理解为"算力 × 数据 × 参数"三驾马车，而 Ilya 在去年 NeurIPS 上宣告"我们只有一个互联网"之后，业界默认下一波燃料是 synthetic data。Lila 的判断更狠——下一片数据油田不是模拟器，而是自然本身。这个视角一旦切换，实验室的价值函数就完全变了：它不再是"验证假设的场所"，而是一台"将自然界的因果规律翻译成 token 的编译器"。Andy 一句话点破："lab of the future should feel like a data center"，机架密度、能效比、24/7 uptime——这些数据中心的 KPI 被搬进了湿实验室，这是一次深层次的"物理基础设施的 AI 化"。

二、最出人意料的观察："False Positive 对人类是灾难，对模型是甜点"

节目里 Rafa 顺口讲的这句话，是整个访谈里最颠覆的一句认识论宣言。传统科研文化里，假阳性会毁掉一位博士生的三年——因为人类只能顺序做一件事，每次失败都是不可逆的时间损失。但对一个 RL 模型来说，任何被明确证伪的实验都是高信息量样本——因为它极大地降低了后验分布的熵。这意味着 Lila 内部正在悄悄发生一场"科学品味"的颠覆：model 的美学和人类科学家的美学是相反的。人类科学家追求 elegant 的正确，AI 科学家渴望信息含量高的错误。这与信息论里 Shannon 说的"你从'太阳升起'这件事里得到的信息为零"完全同构——但过去从未有人把这个原则应用到实验设计的价值判断上。

再往前推一步：这暗示未来实验室的评价指标可能不是"成功率"，而是每美元/每小时的信息熵产出。这是一个还没被命名的新指标，可能是未来十年 AI 科学的关键 KPI。

三、跨领域连接：从 Emily Whitehead 的偶然 到 超导公司的匿名性

访谈里两个看似无关的故事，其实指向同一条隐藏的哲学线索：

  • Emily Whitehead 之所以活下来，是因为救她的医生恰好有一个患幼年关节炎的女儿，从而认识 IL-6 抗体可以压 CRS。整个 CAR-T 领域的诞生依赖于一次跨领域记忆的偶然连接。
  • 你说不出一家做超导体的公司名字，但每台 MRI 都靠它——创造性的物质发明常常在商业上是匿名的。

Lila 的深层野心可以从这两点重新解读：它试图把"跨领域灵光一现"从贝叶斯先验里的随机变量变成一个可训练的能力——通过让同一个模型在生命科学、材料、化学、能源之间自由推理，那位"恰好知道 IL-6 的医生"不再是运气，而成为默认配置。而对于"匿名超导公司"的困境，Lila 的零 FTE 虚拟创业模式（partner 带一个想法，六个月做完五年的活）也许提供了一个新的商业形态：把材料发明从"稀有物种"变成"platform-as-a-startup 服务"。

四、一个隐秘的连接：Windows 95 + 视觉语言模型 = 实验室里的技术考古学

最让人会心一笑的细节：Lila 用 VLM 去控制一台跑 Windows 95 的老设备。这看似荒诞，实则揭示了一个被忽视的真相——科学仪器的技术分层比任何一个软件栈都要古老。生物实验室里同时并存着 2024 年的 magnetic levitation planar motor 和 1995 年的 GUI。VLM 在这里的角色，正如考古学家的碳-14 检测——它让新旧代码地层之间可以对话。这暗示未来 AI 的一个被低估的应用：遗留系统的语义桥接。这个能力一旦成熟，不只是实验室，整个工业世界里数以百万计的老 PLC、SCADA、传统 ERP 都能被"重新联网"进 AI 循环。

五、"苦涩教训的甜蜜姐妹"：在物理世界，只有能被 scale 的东西才有意义

Rafa 那句 "bittersweet lesson of scaling in materials"，跟 Sutton 的 bitter lesson 是一对镜像。Sutton 说：能 scale 的方法胜过精巧的方法。Rafa 说：能 scale 的材料才是有价值的材料。 这两条定律加在一起，导出一个可怕的推论——存在 vs. 价值的边界，正在被 scaling 重新划定。一个只能在实验室做出一次的超级材料，在这个新框架下"不存在"；一个精巧但不可复现的分子设计，"没有意义"。这是把可计算性的判定标准移植到了物质世界，未来可能催生一个新的知识论问题：scale-ability 会不会成为物质本体论的新公理？

📝 转录文本

RL with Verifiable Rewards, but the Verifier is a Lab — Lila Sciences

开场：科学作为下一个 Internet-scale 数据源

Andy: But not just techbio, what do you do in terms of science? We are all in on the bitter lesson in scale. We think that methods that scale and that are general beat those that are not. As Ilya said at NeurIPS last year, we have but one internet. It's the fossil fuel. We fracked. We got every ounce of data that we could out of the internet, but it's gone. And so the question in AI is, where is the next internet scale data set coming from?

You know, people normally talk about different scaling X's. You have compute. You have data. And for science, data is not necessarily an infinite resource. And your point is that we now want to add a new scaling axis for data.

We think that the lab of the future should feel like a data center. Rows of server racks, as densely packed as possible, and also as energy efficient as possible, and things like that.

嘉宾介绍

Brandon: Welcome to Latent Space Science. I'm Brandon. I'm here with my co-host RJ. Today we have Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli and Andy Beam from Lila Sciences. We'll just start off and let you introduce yourself.

Andy Beam — CTO

Yeah, thanks for having us on the podcast. You're a long time listener, first time caller, excited to be here. I'm Andy. I'm the chief technology officer at Lila. I've been an AI researcher now for something like 20 years, going back to the pre-deep learning days, SVMs, random forests, things like that. I did a neural net PhD around 2010 to 2014, as deep learning was taking off. Clearly, neural nets were the thing to back, but autograd libraries really hadn't been developed yet. So I did the backprop by hand back in my day, walking uphill both ways kind of thing.

Got very interested in AI for healthcare and life sciences. My wife's a physician, so I watched her struggle through different things and thought that AI was obviously a natural solution for a lot of those problems. Did a postdoc at Harvard in the medical school, doing early work on medical AI. And was really—I'm in it for the AI. I was really interested in what problems could AI solve.

But I've also always been like startup curious. So I took a break from academia for a year and helped start a company called Generate Biomedicines, which was an early generative biology company. I was the founding head of machine learning there. And got to do the fun kind of hybrid professor, startup founder thing for the next five or six years. So I had a lab at Harvard, again, sort of between the School of Public Health and the medical school, doing methods research, but also a lot of applied work. That was fun. There was a great set of jobs.

But I got a sense that the AI moment was changing in a very significant way. And I wanted to be a part of it. So I started to think about where could I work at the frontier of AI on really, really exciting problems. And academia has a lot going for it. Access to scaled compute is not one of the things that it has going for it, or scaled resources. So I'd been an early advisor for Lila and got very excited once the thesis crystallized. But basically, science as an infinite token generator to train models at scale. Why would I want to work on anything other than creating a new frontier model that can solve scientific problems? So I kind of joked that I hung up the tweed jacket two years ago, left my position at academia, in academia and joined Lila full time as the inaugural CTO.

Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli — Chief Scientific Officer, Physical Sciences

Yeah, I go by Rafa, I'm the chief scientific officer for physical sciences at Lila and a co-founder. I was a computational chemist back in the day. We used a commodity resource that is compute. And so things switched gears for me right around then.

I was, I worked with David Duvenaud and Ryan Adams in sort of blending what I think felt like the first instances of deep learning for science. I was one of the first people to do generative AI for chemistry with an autoencoder on tokenized molecules. And so then deeply in love with latent spaces. We actually have a very similar to your guys' logo, but for molecules, and that has taken its own life, that figure.

I converted in the 2015–2016 era and spun out a company from my postdoc at Harvard, a computational materials platform company. And then went to MIT, where I started my group in materials science and engineering. And the group there was sort of working at the interface of molecular simulations and AI with things like generative models for materials structure, autograd, for sort of really cool gradients that we want to see in the molecular simulations.

And by 2022–2023, sort of things were taking sort of the turn that Andy just mentioned, right? We had seen the bitter lesson come to computationally generated data. And that's the reason why, you know, meta and DeepMind and Microsoft, they have teams doing AI for computational materials science. And that sort of lined up with this opportunity to start spinning out something again in, like I said, '22, '23, started sort of thinking about the idea. Lila 的核心命题：Lab as Scaled Verifier

Brandon: That brings me to what is Lila's thesis? It seems like you have a very ambitious goal here.

Andy: Yeah, it's a great question. I'll try and like give you the TLDR and then we can go a couple of levels deeper. So like Rafa said, we are all in on the bitter lesson and scale. We think that methods that scale and that are general beat those that are not. That's actually sounds straightforwardly true, but is actually counterintuitive and contrary to much of the 70 year history of AI research.

But the realization that we had is that what gave rise to large language models over the last four, five, six years is access to the combination of scaled compute and scaled data. That data came from the internet. It was human generated and we have used it all. As Ilya said at NeurIPS last year, we have but one internet. It's the fossil fuel. We fracked, we got every ounce of data that we could out of the internet, but it's gone. And so the question in AI is, where is the next internet scale data set coming from?

Post the pre-training era, we moved into reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. So people talk about RL a lot, but really what RL is is a way for a model to generate its own data and the reward signal reinforces good data and penalizes bad data. So that has been a very productive framework for problems in math and coding.

But what at Lila we believe is that actually science, running the scientific method and using nature and experiments as verifier is like the ultimate version of that. And so what we're building, we'll talk about these things that we call the AI Science Factories. They are scaled verifiers for science so that we can do post-training at scale and push out the frontier of what reasoning models are capable of. So that's like the thesis in a nutshell.

Brandon: Your proposal is basically, people normally talk about different scaling Xs, that you have compute you have data, you have parameters. And for science, data is not necessarily an infinite resource. And your point is that we now want to add a new scaling axis for data.

实验的 Runtime 与 Token 生成速率

Brandon: So I want to quote some of my friends at the Escalante Bio, have a blog post, really good blog post, I recommend you read it. It says, "Your experiment has a runtime." So what is the runtime of your data collection?

Andy: I mean, so that is an awesome question. It's so in, it will obviously vary by experiment. So like you can't make the ribosome go faster, at least to my knowledge. Biology sets a limit for how fast you can go. In materials sciences and chemistry, there are smaller time scales, there are larger length scales.

What you're actually kind of asking is a technical question though. So how do you train a model against feedback mechanisms that vary by orders of magnitude in terms of feedback? So we think about all of Lila as being able to generate different kinds of data on different timescales. We can then synchronize how we train the model once that data has been generated. Again, for some of the experiments we do, the timescales are on the order of days or weeks. And the question is, can we multiplex? Can we get more data per unit time? But the infinite token generator is still there. We just have to solve the technical problem on the other side of that to be able to line all these pieces up and train it into the model.

Brandon: So when you say the infinite token generator is still there, what do you mean by that? Because there are many different scientific tokens you can imagine and some tokens provide much more information than others. And certain things you can collect maybe at scale, like people who love NGS, you can basically collect an infinite amount of NGS data. And yet there are certain cases where another human genome is probably going to be an incremental update versus—

Andy: Yeah, like my genome relative to a reference genome is like a couple kilobytes worth of information. There's not a lot of information there. So you're exactly right. We don't want to generate the same kind of data over and over again. And so the platform that we're building is qualitatively different than traditional automation framework. So actually the experimental platform that we're building prioritizes generalizability and flexibility over raw throughput. We want the model to be able to design a new experimental protocol, run the protocol, and receive the feedback, even if that's not an experiment we have thought about doing ourselves. So the next incremental token has to be something that is valuable to the model versus yet another NGS sample to teach us something where it's already hit diminishing returns.

实验室即 PCI Bus：物理传输层与仪器图

Brandon: So when you say next experiment, what I think of is traditionally you would go into the lab and reconfigure the lab in whatever way, and then run some experiments by hand, maybe over the course of weeks or whatever. How does the lab get reconfigured for the new experiment at Lila?

Andy: The way to think about the lab is it's almost like a graph. And so each instrument is a node in this graph. And an edge between a node indicates that there's a physical transport layer between those two instruments. And so we'll probably have a video that we'll show in a bit. But we have a physical transport layer that connects almost every instrument we've bought at Lila to each other. These are currently planar motor systems where there's a 96-well plate that magnetically levitates over a track. You have sort of millimeter-level control over where that plate goes.

And so you can, I think of it almost as like a PCI bus for the lab, where each instrument connects to the rest of the system.

Brandon: That's a good one. I think half of the audience might not know what a PCI bus is. So it's a universal serial bus that on your motherboard allows you to connect a new device. If you plug a new graphics card, if you plug a new hard drive in, there's a bus that allows that device to speak to the rest of your computer. And this works for like bio systems and material systems and et cetera?

Andy: Increasingly, but not totally yet. So the other thing to keep in mind about automation is there's a very long tail of things that you have to solve to be able to automate. And to date, people have not been thinking about end-to-end automation in this like flexible kind of way. And so there are instruments that are not connected to this now. There's not a lot of high throughput automation and materials sciences, for example, and we've been building custom instruments for that that then are brought on board.

But there's like an 80/20 rule at play here where things that are easy to onboard and automate are plugged directly into the PCI bus. And then things that are not, people still move. People will still move a sample to that. Or it turns out that removing a cap from a test tube is a very hard thing to automate. A lot of the lab assumes that you have opposable thumbs and you're good with them.

And some of the things that we've seen discussed about Lila frames us as an automation company, and that's kind of the wrong perspective to think about what we're doing. We're not automation maximalists. We are actually sort of like token generation maximalists and flexibility maximalists. So we will over time automate things that make sense to automate, and then again, use solutions now where they make sense.

Brandon: So the system designs the experiments. It gives instructions. There's like, oh, people need to actually do this thing. So you recruit some of the staff to go and do that thing.

Andy: Everything's an API call. And so sometimes when you call an API, there's a robot arm. Sometimes there's a human arm that does something. So people are literally below the API line.

Brandon: Yeah well crazy.

Andy: I think that like, again, we want to spend resources where it makes to spend resources and make rational decisions. And sometimes it just doesn't make sense to try and automate a step when a person can do it in a tenth of a second. But what matters is that the model has the ability to give instructions to test the hypothesis and that all of that data is visible, transparent, stored, so that those tokens flow back into the model.

让模型自由设计实验：能力与安全

Brandon: Do you have your AI models doing entire like experimental designs, which are beyond just a pre-existing protocol where you tweak like relative ratios or sources from, or like what you know, all of those go into a pipe or pipette or something?

Andy: I mean, it depends on like your threshold for novelty here. Certainly for expression protocols, for some gene editing work that we've done, we have tested like the platform's ability to do that versus humans. Model gets like 80% of that zero shot, humans get zero percent of that zero shot. Are we doing like fully open-ended free-form experimentation now? I mean, no, not yet. That is the goal. But we're building towards that. That's what we want to be in. But we have seen the ability to do what would be an enormous amount of human intellectual labor over a very, very short time horizon.

Brandon: So when you're giving these, you know, giving your AI models kind of free reign to start designing new experiments, like how do you make sure that these are things that should be measured or that you validate that this is, you know, a good strategy or that you didn't just waste so much money?

Rafa: The first one is, there is I think maybe an underlying safety question there. And I think that we've been taking very seriously from the beginning, right? Both security and safety, security of the data and the safety of the model suggestions. We have a very strong team, it's growing and there's sort of very strong leadership. And that's the first layer, right? Like we have strong safety, AI safety protocols that look similar to the sort of uplift considerations that people have been looking into large language models. Only it's absolutely, we have strong AI safety protocols that look similar to the sort of uplift considerations that people have been looking into large language models. Only it's absolutely for real.

Brandon: In a lab automation setting where you're working on some, you know, biophysical materials science type problem, what are actually the dangers you have to worry about? There's like, I generally think of malicious actors and or, you know, situations where you have a sufficiently complicated system that it could genuinely output something dangerous. It seems like from the scope of Lila, as I understand it, which we haven't talked about yet, maybe it'll come in a minute, but it doesn't seem like safety is actually going to be a major concern at this point.

Rafa: I mean, it's something we need to take seriously from the beginning, right? Something where we cannot afford to not get it right. I agree with you. Right now it's in the hands of Lila employees that are sort of pushing interest on a line understanding of the platform. It's aligned with our mission. So I agree, we don't have to worry about malicious actors. We still need to worry to some degree about the model giving a suggestion. I think it's more some things that start touching into lab safety more than malicious. I don't think we're going to have emerging behavior where the model suggests an extremely toxic chemical. It's more about sort of pushing an instrument such that maybe it sort of overflows, it combines chemicals that you don't have. So I think there's like a chemical EHS safety layer that needs to be there since the beginning because we're doing sort of open ended overflows.

Andy: I mean, I do think Rafa's right in that safety is not something you can procrastinate on because capability curves tend to be sigmoid shaped and it can look like everything's fine, everything and then all of a sudden there's something that you didn't anticipate the model being able to do. So we are definitely proactive on that side. We have an AI safety team like Rafa said, but I think you're also right in that like, we can constrain the problem in meaningful ways in the way that like a broad based AI system that interacts with the general public cannot. We can also lean on biosafety levels and things like that, good old fashioned lab safety to help in the meantime.

Rafa: And of course the nodes that are exposed to a particular, we don't necessarily need to expose all the experimental capabilities to all the scientific questions, right? For an antibody design question, we probably don't even need to expose the model to the fact that we have gas canisters that contain gases, right? Because it's not gonna need them. So we can still be creative with instead of questions that relate to one particular area of science.

从"愚蠢建议"到"Move 37"：非贵金属电催化剂

Andy: Yeah, so your question though is, sorry, is interesting. Like how do you know if something is dangerous is like actually kind of hard to do or actually how do you know if it's wasteful? Some of the work that we've been doing in like electric catalysts, we have someone inside of Lila who's like published 40 papers on the topic and some of the suggestions from the model initially were boring, but then transitioned from boring to what he considered to be stupid. These are non-platinum-group electrocatalysts for separation of hydrogen and oxygen from water to make hydrogen. And those turns out to be our best non-platinum-group electrocatalysts that we've made. So the line between like obviously wrong and like quasi-move 37 and surprising even to a human expert is hard to know. And so we will do wasteful things because we kind of want to know the difference between the two.

保持科学严谨性 & 人机协作的礼让

Brandon: That brings up the question. So for like an experiment like what you're describing now, it is obvious whether it works or not, right? But you can imagine, and there was some controversy in the Berkeley lab around measurements that were misinterpreted, right? How do you know that your measurements of effectiveness or whatever you're optimizing are actually correct?

Rafa: Yeah, I'm very familiar with that part of the landscape. I would say we cannot relax our standards of scientific rigor because it's AI, right? It's not, you know, maybe five years ago when we started doing generative models for X and Y, they were like, "Yeah, it's cute. It kind of works like you would do with a kid." It's like, but now we're past that. And we need to hold AI science to the same standard we hold, regular human-led science.

And I think that 2023 paper was a switchover from the community, a part of, you know, the AI community for science people, we're always excited to see incremental progress. And I think at that point, we started collectively touching upon the rest of the community's awareness. And they were like, "Fantastic." But, you know, now we're gonna talk about the way we do things to our highest standard.

So I think we have lots of experimentalists. But I wanna go back to the API point. I think we've had the fortune by starting from zero to build a company where people are sort of AI aware, AI excited, we have sort of, across all the people and the networks that I collaborated with, we've managed to build a team of experimentalists and automation engineers that really believe in the mission and really want to make it happen. They're really taking this graciously, right? So whenever AI gives something that is sort of very, very wrong and they're there to just, "Okay," they push the red button, watch out, this is a vast idea.

But they're also gracious in, for instance, trying false positives. False positives are terrible for human scientists, right? Because you go to try something, it doesn't work. For the model, it's fantastic. It reduces uncertainty a lot. For the operators, it's kind of a bummer, right? Because you thought you were gonna get something cool.

And I think we've managed, and going back to the point Andy made, for the, you know, I think about until three months ago, people would be sort of approving AI decisions. And I think about three months ago, we started seeing that the model's crazy ideas started being sort of surprising to people, but surprisingly, it was like, "I don't know. I guess we need to try." And we seen the switchovers. I think the ability of people, experimentalists, to sort of challenge the AI, but being gracious, like that interface of human and computers has been very rewarding over the last few months.

Andy: And I would also say, like, giving the model control of the lab forces you to build infrastructure to expose pieces of data that you wouldn't normally want to or care about. And, you know, maybe no experiment is wrong, but you want the ability to explain the outcome. So if you think about it, if you have an experiment, you fit a statistical model, what you're trying to do is use variation inputs to explain variations in outputs. And so we have the ability to explain variation in outputs because we measure so many different things because we have to expose that to the model. So we can say, "Okay, the humidity was off in the lab that maybe that explains exactly the thing." And then we can also push button and rerun the experiment to verify. And so we do not believe, like Rafa said, in some things we have to be more skeptical of any outcome, but we can then quickly go and rerun that experiment because it's all software, effectively.

Brandon: And you find that the team spends a lot of time on verification, or like, how's the breakdown?

Rafa: Less and less so. I think at the beginning, it wasn't so much, I mean, there is an execution of, "Okay, we've got a hypothesis, we've got a set of instructions that's gonna go off to the API." We vouch for it, and then some parts of the API are people doing things. And I think that will stay, right? That's the labor of it. But I think the double checking that the intuitions were right, I think we're starting to see in this super intelligence local spikes of places where we're kind of, you know, supporting this emergence of super intelligent behavior more than we are sort of gate keeping, and that the ideas are not just wasteful.

So I think that that's happening for domains, and maybe to elaborate a little on the example that Andy mentioned, we care a lot about energy and sustainability, right? Something that, and you know, we're not just a biotech, we really care about energy and sustainability and materials, we're trying to make green hydrogen. And you know, in order to make green hydrogen, to use light to split the water molecule, a bunch of that energy you need to pay for because it's the energy that's stored in the chemical bond, and that you get from sunlight and electricity. And then there is some overhead that you pay that's called the overpotential, which has to do with the fact that the world is not perfect and things are lossy. And the loss comes from something called the catalyst.

And today the catalysts that are out there are okay, but they're expensive and rare. They're made out of ruthenium and iridium. So we set up a model to explore, what can we do to not use these two elements? And people do these papers and there is, you know, there was something a couple of weeks ago that said ruthenium, you know, low ruthenium alloys for X, Y, Z. I mean, yeah, it's like, sure, you can, you know, dope it down, right? You can water it down, but it's still the same fundamental problem. You're using 50% less.

So we set out the model rules on this type of problem and we have the ability to make the material, measure the properties, measure the catalysis, measure the stability. And then in the second, third generation of sequential learning, right? This interplay between sort of information and what the model knows. We started seeing suggestions that were like, I mean, the words were fine. It was using the concepts that we use. It's just that you know, I wouldn't apply that idea to that element. I wouldn't have put them together in that way. And it turns out those have been our best performing chemicals so far.

RL 病态与思维链的怪癖

Brandon: I do want to get to the, Lila is not a biotech, but before we do one last question along this train, thought train, RL is famous for reward hacking. You just, I forget what you said. I would be very concerned that you know, you throw some rewards and that you can really hack the, you know, physical sciences in a way that you can't do with the compute.

Andy: Yeah, I'm not going to disagree with that. 100% agree with that.

Brandon: What's the funniest example?

Andy: We have lots of funny, like, RL fails that are not explicitly reward hacking. Well, I mean, one is when we train, one of the early things we did was like, can you just like make a plate map? Like, can you like lay the experimental conditions out on a plate? And it got annoyed when the person would ask. So you would ask the model to do a plate map and it would do it. And they'd be like, actually, could you change these reagents? And it would like, swear. We'd be like, it's a 96 well plate. Come on, man. Like, in the chain of thought. I don't know where that came from, but it would like.

Brandon: Somewhere on the internet.

Andy: Yeah, somewhere. The internet's in there. I can't forget that. So we've seen lots of like funny, like personality quirks like that as a function of RL. There's like obvious RL, I wouldn't call them reward hacking, but pathologies like repetition. So like the chain of thought will collapse and it will just like repeat its final answer over and over and over again. For some reason, that reliably sometimes leads to higher rewards. We're not sure exactly why pathological chain of thoughts or non-legible chain of thoughts in some cases lead to higher rewards.

Brandon: So, sorry, I wanna interrupt. So we're talking about RL. We're talking about the way you're talking about it. Sounds like just RL on chain of thought, just like everybody's doing. But your RL actually has a lab step. If you're in a pathological loop, does that mean the lab is just like doing the same experiment over and over again?

Andy: So a chain of thought, maybe just to step back, is tokens that the model uses to solve a problem. So if you were solving a math problem, you would do theorem one, theorem two, corollary, lima, you decompose the problem. In science, the chain of thought, there's some of that too. So there's reasoning that happens. I'm trying to make an antibody for this target. What do I know about this target? What are the napotopes? Like what's my plan of attack? In the chain of thought are also tool calls. So maybe I'm gonna use a structured prediction model in case to get some read of how the sequence folds in three dimensional space. So tool calls are part of the chain of thought.

At Lila, the fun thing is that the lab instruments are also tool calls, or a series of tool calls for a workflow, but it's all human legible. It's all in English. And so some of the pathologies we've seen is it just skips all the middle part, which we would think is important for solving a problem, and just goes right to the answer. And says, I don't need to do an experiment in this case. I don't need to call a tool. And in some cases where we can judge something, because maybe we've already done the experiment or something like that, for some reason it is actually not a bad strategy in some cases. And so there's some mystery there.

Brandon: It's a theorist.

Andy: Yeah, it's not the calculation. And this is probably too much of a tangent, but it actually thinks in latent space, it emits tokens. So the chain of thought is often an unreliable narrator for what the computation of the model is actually doing. And so one of the big things we're trying to think about is when we're moving into working on a problem, like Rafa said, for electrocatalysts, that we actually don't know what right and wrong looks like. How much should we rely on the chain of thought versus just trusting the experiment, trusting the verifier, trusting the simulator as the ultimate ground truth.

Lila 不是 Biotech：模型本身即价值

Andy: So, Lila is not a biotech company. Lila is actually fairly unique, I think, in this way. I've been involved with biotechs, I've helped start biotechs. Often the goal is to sprint to a clinical trial. So you want to develop an asset, you develop the platform in service of having optionality of what space you move into. But once you have the clinical asset, put everything into a medically induced coma and you get through the clinical trial and if it goes well, then other things get to, so we are taking that option off the table. The model itself is the thing of value at Lila. So in that sense, we're much more of like a neo-lab, trying to think of a new way to push forward capabilities to a core reasoning LLM-based model.

Brandon: Not even the lab platform?

Andy: So the lab platform is the token generator.

Brandon: Okay.

Andy: That is the data generation mechanism that ultimately is the moat for Lila, is that once that continues to scale, the amount of data that we can generate, both per unit time but per unit square foot will go up and that feeds back into the model to make it smarter, that then suggests the next experiment to do. And so we really are focused on making this core model as performant and smart as possible. And we can talk about how that lends itself to different commercial strategies, but ultimately we're interested in creating this new type of AI model.

广度带来深度：跨领域科学推理

Brandon: So I wanna quote Sri Kossuri from Octant Bio, who had a great tweet, I really loved a few weeks ago, it was, what is the business model in ML for drug discovery? Because if you need the data to train the model, but if you have the data, what do you need the model for?

Andy: That is true when you are narrowly scoped. So that is true within a given vertical of science. The analogy that I would use is like, if you went back 10 years and you tried to create like a coding assistant model, you would just get coding data. You wouldn't also get Shakespeare poetry, carnitas recipes, it just, it turns out that there is spillover as the model is able to train on a broader swath of data and a deeper cut of data. And so again, the core bet that we're making is that is true for science. That if the model is trained on an increasingly broad set of data, the amount of data that you need in a given domain, that data requirement is reduced. In some cases, will be reduced to zero if it's adjacent to what the model has already seen before.

And so there's a data efficiency argument that would suggest that again, having a general platform that can create a broad swath of scientific data, I'll also just mention that like, obviously we are using things that are already commodities. So public data sets we use, simulators we use, and experimental platform is a compliment to these existing commodity resources.

Brandon: This brings up a question in my mind about, there's a concept of applicability domain where you have different scales, different, and they result in different types of completely different types of information and relationships between entities, right? So you have the quantum realm, you have chemical realm, you have sort of different bio realms. One concern I would have with cross-cutting approaches is their domain transfer between these at all. Whereas, carnitas recipes and chess problems, you have the commonality that they're written in language, whereas you almost have a completely separate, not even language, right? It's a completely separate model between these domains.

Andy: Human scientists work on all of those domains.

Brandon: Correct, and they mostly communicate with each other in written language using tools.

Andy: So I would say there's a common reasoning process that allows someone to solve problems in each one of those domains. And so I think that that logic carries over to a reasoning model that we're trading, that again, uses tools, can do math, can do code, but it's having all of that knowledge stored in one place.

Brandon: One classic example for me of domain transfer is between complexity theory and quantum gravity right? Where now a lot of the quantum gravity theories are basically recognizing the identical math behind the two of them, right? Do you have examples of this kind of sort of, oh man, this domain actually applies to this domain?

Andy: So we have assembled this reasoning data set of 10 trillion scientific tokens, reasoning traces that are experimentally verified across life sciences, chemistry, and materials sciences. And we have seen that this general model often beats the domain-specific models. And so it's hard to point to what's in the model that is making it, what connections it has realized, but clearly having seen more data across all of science beats sort of in a sample for sample kind of way domain-specific reasoning models.

Brandon: The future of science's language, right?

Andy: Well, so, yeah, so I don't—

Brandon: Future of chemistry is huge.

Andy: Maybe. I don't think it's necessary, I don't think that's a necessary condition for a scientific superintelligence.
