CAR-T 简史

So CAR-T has been worked on since like the late 80s or 90s, really caught fire around 2010 or so for cancers. The way it used to work is you'd extract some of those T cells, you would engineer what's called chimeric antigen receptor that goes on top of that, that tells the T cell to what kind of cell to go and kill. So you're basically modifying people's T cells, take them out, you modify it so that it has this weird antigen receptor on its surface.

Brandon: To seek and destroy tag.

Andy: Usually they use a protein called CD19, which is preferentially expressed on B-cells. When B-cells get malignant, they create blood cancers, they create autoimmune diseases, you wipe out someone's, almost their entire B-cell repertoire, we can do this, there's a lot of collateral damage. But essentially you're telling the T cell what to go and kill.

So this really started to catch fire around 2015. It was expensive and slow, you have to extract someone's T cells, you have to engineer them, it was like $400,000 per infusion and just like, it's still a miracle cure for lots of different types of cancer, too much of a tangent for this, but there's this child named Emily Whitehead, who was treated at the Children's Hospital of Pennsylvania, CHOP. She was one of the first cures in pediatric cancer by CAR-T. She was gonna be referred to hospice care, got CAR-T. Another was like, "Psychetangent." She almost died of a fever from this initial CAR-T treatment.

Brandon: Yeah, that's difficult.

Andy: The only reason she survived, because the doctor who was treating her had a daughter with pediatric arthritis and knew that this specific antibody would blunt her IL-6 response to CAR-T. So like, there's a lot to unpack there in terms of AI for science, all the serendipity that had to happen in that specific case for all of that to go right. And probably if you roll that dice a thousand more times, you probably don't get that doctor at that moment who knew exactly what antibody to give her to make that treatment curative instead of lethal. So again, those are the types of serendipity things that we'd actually like to automate.

In vivo CAR-T 的机制

So anyway, it's so inexpensive. People then realize that actually through just an infusion, if you take an mRNA that encodes for the chimeric antigen receptor, you put it in a lipid nanoparticle called a lipid nanoparticle, you put a CD8-targeting moiety on the outside of this ball of fat, it will then, you give them the infusion, it will go bind to the T cell, get ingested, ball of fat dissolves, mRNA comes out, chimeric antigen receptor gets expressed and presents on the top of the T cell. So you're just telling, you're reprogramming the T cells to express these weird antigens.

Brandon: Literally programming biology.

Andy: And then the T cell goes and does its thing and wipes out whatever has CD19 in this case. So malignant B-cells explain a lot of blood cancer. They also explain a lot of autoimmune diseases. B-cells often make antibodies to auto antigens and things like that.

Lila 六个月复刻 Capstan

So recently, six months ago, as the result of about six years worth of work, spun out of a Nobel Prize winner's lab and about $100 million worth of R&D, we saw, that's some good music man. We saw some of the most compelling preclinical data for in vivo CAR-T treatment of autoimmune diseases. It was by a company called Capstan, they were bought by AbbVie for like $2.1 billion.

So at Lila, we had been working on all three of those things in isolation. So about six months ago, a team of two or three people inside of Lila tried to see what we could do in the in vivo CAR-T. And what we had been working on was mRNA design. Like most RNA medicines, the biggest knob that you can turn is expression peak and expression durability. So how many proteins do you get per unit of mRNA when you give someone a vaccine or some other mRNA medicine? So we have developed some monster UTRs, untranslated regions, which flank the protein coding region, which dictate those expression properties. Something like 10X, the references from Moderna and Pfizer's reference sequences's reference sequences's reference sequences.

And over the course of six months, got to in vivo data in nonhuman primates, where B-cell depletion was significantly better than what was shown in the Capstan data. And the sort of like durability of that was also, all of the characteristics that we looked at were significantly better. Having more CAR expression is probably one of the most potent ways to improve a CAR-T therapy. The number of receptors that get expressed dictates how likely that T cell is to bind to the bad cell once it finds it. And T cells are literally serial killers and that they will kill a cell, then they'll go to the next one, the next one. And so how long they can do that is dictated by how durable the expression of the CAR is.

Again, we're not a CAR-T company, we're science nerds, we like to do cool stuff. So we got to that proof point in about six months, where again, all the way up to where you might think about filing an IND for a new clinical asset. We're not gonna do that, we're not gonna do a clinical trial, again, that would be all encompassing.

零 FTE 创业模式

But some folks who had been around Lila for a long time saw that as a way to do essentially like a two to three person FTE startup. Where there's a couple scientists who have domain knowledge and a combination of the model plus platform can do five years worth of biotech work over a six month period for 10% of the total investment.

And so a lot of the commercial relationships we're thinking about now are essentially like the zero FTE startup model. Where someone comes with an idea, they say, if there was a CAR-T in the market that could buy and do things, if it was a buy specific, or if it had these other properties, I know the hole in the market that that thing would plug into. And so a lot of our commercial engagements are effectively virtual startups running on Lila now, where someone comes with a very well specified problem, they don't know how to get there. There may be some things related to target identification and things like that too, but they can effectively run that entire program over a much shorter amount of time at a fraction of the cost.

So you can't have scientific superintelligence if you're just a good test taker. And so Ken has created or built and open-endedness team at Lila to sort of take the outer loop or the meta part of that reasoning challenge on. This is happening already on materials sciences because those capabilities just don't exist. And we often think about labs in terms of like their XY coordinates. As we integrate, like we'll have like a Z component too, because we'll be able to stack things. And again, tokens per like unit volume is like what we'll be thinking about then.

But we think that like the lab of the future should not be made for people to easily walk into it. It should feel like a data center, where you go and you see the rows of server racks. There's room for like a crash cart behind it to service the nodes, but it should be as densely packed as possible and also as energy efficient as possible and things like that. So yeah, so to answer your question, we're using commodity things now because it makes sense to get started, but over time, almost surely the form factors of those will change quite a bit.

Brandon: I see, I'm just a little surprised that you can come up with this common size of tray that kind of matches your needs for a good percentage of your problems.

Andy: Well, it's just working backwards. 96 well plates are the like atomic unit of experimentation in lab automation. And so we now do 96 well form factors for materials sciences as a result. Not everything fits into that form factor, but again, the coverage that you get from adopting a 96 well or 384 well plate format. 80 20. 80 20, exactly.

Rafa: Yeah, I mean, you can see some of those words, the pieces of deposited material were bigger. So we still use the plate shape to carry them over, but then the number of samples, right? That you have them are smaller. I think some of them are like maybe 12, four times three.

This takes me also to a point you focused earlier about scaling and sort of how, when you scale your programs are different and a problem I think we're looking forward to collectively at the company is the orchestration and the scheduling of a data center size AI science factory, right? When it's a full experiments, you could rank concurrently. How are you going to think about the, you know, the logistics and the orchestration of moving all these samples and interfacing all these instruments to create sort of the maximum value for our customers and maximum information for our model. And that's exciting part. That probably is going to look very different from some of the other problems we're thinking about now.

Andy: What we think about Rafa said is like orchestration on top of that is like a slurm queue or something like that, that lets you globally maximize throughput of the system that you have. But again, using those same abstractions to think about throughput scheduling orchestration. And as the system gets complex or it gets larger, the complexity and maximizing that throughput. So if you're like a, you know, a CSP, a constraint satisfaction problem nerd, we have like one of the coolest ones to think about.

Brandon: Are you thinking about scaling as like that one cluster and then you just cookie cutter that, or is it like I have all of my liquid handlers and all of my whatever spin coders and yeah, over in different parts of the lab or something.

Andy: I mean, currently what we have essentially is one big fully connected graph. And like that won't scale indefinitely. Just some of the material stuff use, throw off hazardous fumes. And that's isolated for safety reasons. I don't know exactly what the exact configuration layout of the scientific cluster of the future looks like. But I think that it will probably have fewer instruments on it than like you might guess you would need. You know, hundreds, maybe thousands. But we do think about scaling it in the same way that you would think about scaling a data center. And that it's a multi-level building, occupies millions of square feet. And it's like a lights out facility as they say. It's like running 24 seven generating data in real time. And you would want the same uptime you would expect of a data center. Now that's very hard to do. That's like an insanely hard thing to do. But that's the end point that we're trying to work backwards from. What problems do you need to solve on the way to that end point?

迭代速度 vs. 并行广度：Pooled Assays 的价值

Brandon: This goes back to my previous question though about the runtime of your experiments too. Because scaling means different things. And one of them is experimental design, which intrinsically scales, but maybe at the cost of signal noise ratio or some other idea. But getting broad data quickly and efficiently at some cost. Or scaling is lower throughput, but just paralyzing wildly. So in general, I would approach those as two different sets of problems. I don't think that the same strategy really works for them in general. So what type of scaling is more important for you as a scientist?

Andy: I would say round over round iteration is more important than a broad, hugely multiplexed, highly noisy kind of thing. So iteration time is really the single easiest thing.

Brandon: Yeah. Yeah. So does that limit the domains that you want to focus on? Now do you think if we are going to try to tackle a new problem, do we ask can we just solve this problem with fast iteration versus something where maybe the answer is will you scale up by massively multiplexing something, with like month long turnaround? But analyzing a multiplexing are somewhat different, right? Sometimes—

Andy: Yeah, that's the other point. I would say pulled—we love pulled.

Brandon: Yeah. Pooled, we love. Because you get fast and broad. So it's what is pulled made for. Pooled are things like DNA encoded libraries, where you use—G12 has a bunch of crap in it. Then you can sort out the crap after you do the experiment.

Andy: Somehow the form of the assay allows you to throw 1,000 or a million or a billion experiments in time, and the way the assay is set up, the readout picks the winner. So you try a million things in one plate, and you get one readout of 1,000 readouts of 1,000 winners.

Brandon: Multiplexed. There's a little joke that all value tech is just mapping whatever readout you want to an NGS sequencing.

Andy: I mean, yes. Tag it multiplex. There you go. You can get lots of data.

Rafa: The other argument would be that if the standard for a field is a month and it's going to take us four days, a four-day learning cycle is amazing, because it's really going to move the needle for that part of the field. This is where our automation engineers and our teams are thinking about other ways of measuring things. And in coolants and in catalysis, there are places where we just made different instruments that measure a different property that turns our response a thousand times faster.

For instance, in sorption—I can tell you folks a little bit—in gas sorption, people typically measure. They pressurize an amount of gas for the MOF and COF materials I was talking about, sucking CO2 out of the air. You know how much from the ideal gas law, if you remember high school, you know how much gas you put in the little box. And then you wait for the gas to be absorbed in the material. You check the pressure. And from the difference in pressure, you know how much went into the thing. Then you up the pressure again, and you see how much extra went. And if this sounds low, it's because it's very slow. It's called BET. This is about a day per sample. And it's very tough to parallelize because there's another gas line, another canister.

Or you can take all the types of proxy measurements from other instruments that are parallelizable. And that's something we built in the lab now. Instead of measuring pressure, we're measuring another property we care about. That is a readout for what actually pressure will tell us. But we can do 96 well plates for 96 metal-organic frameworks in like an hour. So it's like maybe 2,500 times faster. So this is a place where there's a little bit of room for ingenuity or just—and now we're still slow compared to other readouts, right? Other things in electrochemistry maybe we can do in a minute. But now we're sort of 1,000 times faster than the way we were doing it.

Andy: I think the answer to your question also, too, depends on how much we think the model is starting from a dead start versus a walk versus a jog. So if there's some area that we care about, some question, it's clear there's like zero knowledge in the way to the base—in the model that we're using. Then we may prefer a big slow thing to move it in. If we think that it's already relatively competent in that, then we would vastly persevere the rapid serial fast-iteration cycle. So we will do both. The bet is that as the model performance improves, the sample efficiency goes up. And therefore, the compound interest that you get from round over round experimentation will outweigh that you would get from a big, noisy, broad data set.

会不会耗尽问题？希望仪器成为 USB

Brandon: Do you have any concern? This is—I'm just thinking out loud here. But do you have concern that you're going to quickly sort of saturate the problems that you can solve using—

Andy: Concern or hope. [laughter]

Brandon: Like concern and hope, maybe. But maybe you have these systems that you're putting in place. And right now, because they're new, then there's a lot of green field. You can go and tackle all these problems that are amenable to high throughput experimentation. You're going to do that for a couple of years, maybe. And then all of a sudden, now everything is different. And you have to completely retool your gazillion dollar base.

Andy: I hope that that is true, to be clear. So I hope that we don't have to measure a binding KD again in two years. We didn't have to do that. I'm very pumped about that. Because the model has essentially mastered binding kinetics. So you would think that eventually you get to the point where the model knows how to do that.

Let's go back to the PCI bus again. So what we actually want to do is to reduce the amount of time it takes to bring a new instrument on platform. So you want that to feel a lot like a USB. I don't know how old you guys. When I was a kid, you got a new device. You got the drivers on a floppy disk. You had to beat your head against the wall to get the driver to install. And two days later, your printer only kind of works. So that's kind of what—Yeah exactly.

Brandon: And if you're a Linux hardcore person, you're going to still leave that experience today. Audio driver still doesn't work.

Andy: So that is what it's like to bring a new instrument on platform in biology and physical sciences now, is that we're in the driver on a floppy disk in the manual to try to get to work. So again, one of the things that we hope a unified platform enables is instrument onboarding time eventually goes to zero, where you have the spec from the manufacturer. The model reads it. The right APIs get abstracted. We're working with some instrument vendors to make this process easier.

But I think a lot of the way that we think about modulating a system is conditioned on how we do it now. And so again, we're hoping that a unified platform makes onboarding instrument two years from now, a 30-minute exercise versus a 30-day exercise. Again, it's a hard thing to do. It could be wrong. We might not be able to do it. But that's the future that we're pointing to, where currently, we actually can swap out existing instruments very quickly. So if we need to replace a Hamilton with a different liquid handler, that swap actually happens very quickly already. And so we do have some reasonable belief that onboarding instruments will get faster, better, more reliable over time. And again, we don't want to be doing 2026 science in 2036. And so we hope that some of these instruments get deprecated, or the way that we're measuring things changes. Otherwise, lots of assumptions we and everyone else made about the rate of progress in the next decade will have been wrong.

Rafa: They were wrong. And we're already benefiting from the instrument vendors. I wish the problem we have is what you're describing, that we've run out of science to do with the instrument we have. We used all the science. That we have the ante for the instrument vendors. The instruments we have now are as powerful as a beam line would have been 10 years ago. We're taking measurements today that 10 years ago would have requested you to ask the federal government for a time slot at 2 in the morning, somewhere out there, to waste a couple of nights of sleep, taking measurements that are really bright in neutron or X-ray sources. And today, the vendors make instruments like those that we can put next to the quantum dot or next to the protein expression. So I wish that that's an end state that is decidable, but very, very unlikely. And I'm sure there's going to be new science to be asking of the instruments we have.

Brandon: We've had guests who have had both of these themes of, first of all, none of the devices you buy are set up to do high throughput AI science. And also, that there are new scientific devices which come up every day, which just open up something which is impossible, like five, 10 years ago. Like inline NMR.

Rafa: There is lots of characterization, miniaturization, and more resolution, more bright sources. That are just transformational. And they might read really well with a kind of automated high throughput science we're doing.

Andy: So we're moving into this facility in Alewife, Massachusetts. And it's just a 3D rendering. It's 100,000 square foot space. And we will move towards AMRs, autonomous mobile robots, as some of the transport. And so you can see some of that there. We'll put it in the show notes.

10 万亿科学 Token：不是数据下载，是推理踪迹

Brandon: OK, so kind of switching topics a little bit. So you were talking about your scientific pile of 10 trillion tokens. When I hear 10 trillion, my first thought is, man, that sounds like a lot. Things like this is 30,000 human genomes, which would cost roughly $3 million to sequence. It is roughly 1, 2000th of several of these large foundation models, like Evo and Nucleotide Transformer and so on. So in some sense, it is a lot of data. In other sense, it's not a lot of data. And not all tokens are the same. I'm curious, what went into creating this? What were your thought processes? And then how much actual useful information is in 10 trillion tokens or 10 trillion tokens?

Andy: So it's tokens in the same way that we think about counting pre-training tokens from the internet or from post-training runs. So these are, again, the best way to think about RL is a data generation mechanism. It's a way to steer the model towards more and more valuable tokens, like better tokens. And so these are the result of running that process across many different scientific RL environments at MyLa, where the tokens are a mix of English tool calls and experimental feedback. So they're quasi-English tokens, as we've been talking about, tokenized by the tokenizer. So that's where they came from.

Brandon: So you're not tokenizing—

Andy: So we're not tokenizing those in general. Implicitly, because if the model's asked a question about DNA, there are DNA tokens in there. It's not like we downloaded dbGaP or the PDB or Swiss Prote or something like that in tokenized at the sequence level. These are reasoning tokens, model-generated, that are experimentally verified.

Brandon: On top of this, you also still have your alpha fold, your nucleotide transformer. You have all your sequencing data, which goes into this. So 10 trillion tokens is—

Andy: 10 trillion. 10 trillion. I like looking at 10T on my laptop. The reason why I think that level of data is important—pre-training corpuses are usually somewhere between 15 and 30 trillion tokens. And so that's the scale at which you see these emergent things happen. And so once you're in the trillion token regime, we feel confident that that's enough for the model to start to master and see emergent capabilities.

Brandon: Are you starting from scratch with your model, or you have some open source model?

Andy: Again, in the interest of being ambitiously over scoped, but not pathologically so. We have not decided to take on pre-training as well, just because the black magic that you have to do is insane. And we've been gifted something like a billion dollars worth of compute in the form of open-weight models. So we start with an open-weight model that has been pre-trained. And the assumption that we are making is that the model has been pre-trained on the internet in a large fraction of the scientific literature. Therefore, it's a good scientific prior over what is known, and therefore a good base camp to build upon.

Brandon: So it's 10 trillion on top of the trillion—

Andy: Add it in, too. Yeah. Been—Yeah. And we use Nemotron quite a bit because we have a partnership with NVIDIA. And I think there's like 30 trillion tokens that go into the pre- and post-training for that model.

Brandon: So in the process of these reasoning tokens, you are also creating what are arguably probably just rather useful data sets themselves. Have you thought about independently releasing some of those data sets open source and even in the absence of the reasoning model, which may still be quite valuable to the community, but doesn't actually deteriorate, break your mode at all?

Andy: So one of the things that we've developed along the way is a test suite of something like 1,000 unique scientific RL environments where you can drop in a frontier model. You can drop in your own model. We drop in our models. So almost surely we're going to open source a subset of that. Some of it based on data that we've generated, some of it that we have curated for the community to use. So there will be some open source version of the benchmark that we've assembled as doing part of that. And there will be probably some data, training data, that goes along with that.

Brandon: Do you have benchmarks internally that actually operate the lab? Like essentially like a benchmark for how well does—Maybe another way of saying, do you have automated experimental controls?

Rafa: Yes. I think for every of the—we've put together from the beginning of the company these multidisciplinary teams to work on specific close-ended problems. And the model super-anil has always been to benchmark, train something naively from zero, call in frontier, the frontier models that everybody would go use right out of the box, on our own internal. So everything we've done, we do have an internal benchmark. Now the domains are very specific. They're not as general and all encompassing as the benchmark that Andy was describing, because they are the things we really care about, and the products that we want to deliver, and sort of the places where we want to make a difference. But in all those places, we've typically seen that the scientifically pre-trained model that Andy is describing with access to tool calling typically demolishes, of course, anything else that we do.

Andy: I mean, it's worth thinking about what we're trying to do, how that is additive with LMs. If you think about an experimentally verified reasoning trace, how many of those do you think exist on the internet or in the pre-training corpus? Order of zero. Order of zero, I think. It certainly rounds down to zero versus the next order of magnitude. So we have just seen incredible lift from showing the model that even if we're at a parameter disadvantage relative to the frontier models, just showing it an experimentally verified reasoning trace, you see just immediate lift when we do that.

Lila 与 Flagship 的关系

Brandon: Lila is a flagship company. Flagship is basically one of the biotech incubators in the world. Do you have had something like, what, I think 30 successful IPOs? Or not you, but your parent. Including you yourself were just part of Generate Biomedicine and just had a successful IPO very recently. So Lila is very good at biotech. I would say from history, it's very much single asset. Traditional flagship. Flagship is very good at biotech. Flagship, that's not what I just said. Lila. Lila. Flagship is very good at biotech because historically, we've been very focused on single assets. I guess in the last few years with Generate, with I guess Expedition, VELO, there are some branching out into more platforming things. I'm curious about one, how does Lila fit into the broader flagship ecosystem? Was there a specific reason why Lila is now? Why this sort of pivot from single asset into scientific reasoning? And what is the broader interaction? In particular, you mentioned that you had a drug, or you had a CAR-T drug, which was at the level of IND. So you clearly have the ecosystem to make that into something. So I'm curious, where is this going?

Andy: Yeah, great question. Let me do a little flagship framing, and then I'll sort of talk about—so we all started the same pluripotent stem cell, but there's differentiation that we all take. The traditional path for a flagship company is there's—so the history of Generate is I was an early advisor to Generate, a consultant over 2018. There was this idea to use machine learning for protein engineering. Me and a couple other folks at Flagship and some other external folks who came in—Dartmouth professor named Gevorg Grigoryan was part of this—got seed money from Flagship to then go and spin that out. We worked on building the technology, and then usually the deal is that Flagship is the sole investor during a Series A. And then the Series B is normally the first point at which external capital comes into that.

To your point, they often end up being asset-based companies. Generate has a Phase 3 trial for monoclonal antibody to treat asthma, a Phase 1 program behind that to treat COPD. I think the recognition from some folks at Flagship, especially our CEO Geoffrey von Maltzahn been involved in creating a lot of these companies. And he's like hiring the same team over and over and over again. You need the ML team. You need the platform team. And so I think he saw shared DNA between all these companies. And let's have one company that can essentially support all these different things.

Year one of Lila was essentially like when 01 dropped. And so we had all these pieces in place, and it just became clear that we could create a platform to support a new kind of scientific model. In the early days, we didn't know how do you monetize that? What's the commercial strategy? We've gotten a lot of clarity over that over the years. But sort of the core conviction that we had two years ago was the bitter lesson is correct. Science could be an infinite token generator.

Operationally, the way that we're different from a normal flagship is outside investment came in before the Series A. Again, the lead of the Series A was not Flagship. So we do have that lineage. We do come from Boston. We do have a lot of the shared learning that a company that has created 110 startups that I think—So Generate was FL5657. It was actually a merge. In the early days, Lila was 9697. And so the Flagship has this long history of creating companies. So we have that network, and we have the learning of leaders who have created that many companies. But we're such a weird creature that we essentially went down a very different path very, very early.

Brandon: So why is it that when I hear you have a very promising CAR-T therapy, you said you had a DC. Why not just partner with that out, partner with that out? Or maybe this is on the horizon or something.

Andy: The short answer is that we are engaging in commercial partnerships around CAR-T therapies, for sure. Some of them are further development to increase or change some of the properties, like by specifics and things like that, going after novel indications. But we've used that one CAR-T to essentially launch several partnership programs.

Brandon: Okay, so sort of like the proof of principle, but it in itself was not quite exactly what a drug needed to be or something.

Andy: Well, so just to be clear, we could go and try and license or partner that specific thing. We found that it was better to take that and secure several partnerships around further development.

Brandon: I see, okay, cool. You're getting at basically, you're doing some sort of code development thing.

Andy: This is a virtual startup idea where company starts a virtual startup around one of these indications and they essentially pay us revenue to further development. And again, we have these milestones and things around it.

Brandon: Yeah. So like long-term, since flagship is specifically bio and is never really branching the materials, how does that sort of weight flagship or Lila strategy, does that play into it at all? Or is like, at this point you've kind of launched and sort of spun off. It's kind of one of the reasons why it's sort of differentiated into something different so quickly right?

Rafa: I think part of the breadth of the mission clearly was beyond biotech from day one. And the people we needed to hire came from different networks, the instruments we had to buy came from different vendors than the flagship vendors would have usually been. So I think that was part of sort of the reasons why it feels somewhat different. But it's also core to the mission, right? We cannot get this to work on a narrow field. By definition, we want to be as broad as we can possibly be because that's where the emerging behaviors are going to come from.

Andy: And I think if you looked at the composition of people who work at Lila now, it would look like categorically different than what you would expect like a median biotech company to look like. So we hire out of, or compete for, and sometimes win against people who are considering frontier lab offers. We have a heavy software engineering and tech presence. The amount that we spend on GPUs would be atypical for a biotech, I will say. I think that if we called ourselves a biopharma, we probably would have a top three GPU cluster in the world. It's true that that's part of our DNA, but we've been intentional about trying to make decisions that put us on what we think is the most promising trajectory for life. This isn't just kids rebelling against their parents or something. We think that the thesis is right, and it points towards a very valuable, but also important company for not just biotech, but for materials and chemistry.

材料 vs. 生物：哪个更难？

Brandon: OK, that brings me to what I think is my last question. What's harder, materials or bio biology?

Andy: They're actually very different. It's funny, right? I feel like we're about to do the Spider-Man meme in like—

[overlapping voices]

When I was around for the first merry-go-round of AI for small molecule drug discovery, the Atomwise, Generate, and insitro. So I think the hardest is the thing that is a small molecule. It has all the difficulties of chemistry, of reasoning about synthesis, and then it has all the difficulties of reasoning about biology and adverse effects and—

Brandon: Yeah, but the counterpoint thing, that we have so many tricks in our toolkit, which you can borrow from biology, right? So it's harder, but you also have—

Rafa: Well, I think materials are harder. So they have the benefit of great simulators that we don't have in bio.

Andy: Well, I think materials are harder. So they have the benefit of great simulators that we don't have in bio. In materials science, you don't have the mature high-throughput automation that you have in biology. For me, materials as a subject is interesting, because there's not a unifying principle like the central dogma. Materials means lots of different things. I actually still don't quite understand the unifying principle. When we say materials science, what exactly that means. And then the commercial dynamics are completely different. Again, with CAR-T, we know, if we wanted to, how to monetize that directly. With material, there's this supply chain, there are devices. The testing that you do in the lab is only partially predictive of the lifetime of how that material will be used, and the math is harder.

Brandon: In terms of supply chains, still matter for both. Maybe you replace clinical trials with some product validation and verification, qualification as the terms. So there are direct analogies, and there are hard parts for both of them.

Andy: The economics are very different. If you pass a clinical trial, you make money. That thing is valuable. And how much it costs to make it is very rarely the blocking element. It can be for the most—Much easier to underwrite an asset in biology than it is in materials.

Rafa: Do you guys know the name of a company that makes superconductors?

Brandon: This always comes up. Are you guys doing superconductors?

Rafa: Yeah, we care about magnets. We care about superconductors. They're really cool science. Do you folks know the name of a company that makes superconductors?

Brandon: Nobody knows. These things are super important. I know that they're using MRI.

Rafa: Exactly. That's the only commercial application. But it turns out, these things, when you succeed, you kind of are—when you make up a cool material that does something, you're kind of a nameless company that makes it same and is successful and has good cash flows. But you don't get to break. Everybody knows a big pharma. But you've got your three ends. And most of the big material companies are behind closed doors.

Andy: Most of commercial engagements look like getting them to tell you what the important problem is. And there's less of an open innovation ecosystem. There's a couple of things and materials that are obviously recognized to be valuable. But it's just, I think, very different than life sciences.

Rafa: And maybe one of the last things that we haven't touched upon a lot—and I want to flag out, I think, chemistry, and especially materials—government-sponsored research is a big driver. So in the same way that the government doesn't feel they need to do direct discovery other than funding NIH for early-stage, open science, hypothesis-driven science, the government and national security drive materials innovations in ways that are unique. And you see this in the way we engage with the British government. We have partnerships. We work with the US government. We have awards. We participate in developing materials and technologies, which is a different part of the ecosystem that drives innovation. That's also different. Yeah, definitely.

Brandon: Are you guys working in Mission Genesis?

Andy: We were one of the named partners. We've had an ongoing relationship with a lot of the national labs. And so we have been working on that. We did send 25 Genesys, like house proposals, and a lousy way. So yeah, we have a lot of—I was joking. He left academia thinking grant writing was behind him, only to have to write. I booked him. They expect about 20,000.

心愿：移除一个瓶颈

Brandon: The question that we like to ask all of our guests if you could remove a bottleneck in your domain and you can define domain by fiat, what would that bottleneck be?

Rafa: To me, I'm going to go to old time in Rafa that was doing physics-based simulation. I would say they sim-to-real. They sim-to-real for the people that come from the physics-based world. I think they sim-to-real. How do you explain what that means? What I mean—so these people have typically meant it in the context of robotics, your virtual simulations in 3D spaces, allow you to train robots that will move in physical spaces. But there is a gap. And they call it the sim-to-real gap.

For us in physics-based simulations is that we do molecular simulations of gooey stuff. We do electronic structure simulations of hard stuff. And they're OK, but they're not predictive enough. And this is the reason why—if it wasn't for that, maybe we will have to make a self-driving lab for materials, because we would have been able to just predict. So I think we know there's physics, but it doesn't quite go the way to being predictive, meaning that the models that we train on physics cannot possibly close the gap either, because they're still missing this. They're trained on approximations that are just not good enough.

So I think the thing we've been chasing for a decade in AI for materials has been sort of—if we train on computational data, can we answer real world experimental questions? And that would have been the place where if I get to also go back in time, in addition to taking the bottleneck out, it would be the accuracy of the underlying simulation that we've been training on all the time.

Brandon: So this is sort of like Heather Kulik said, there is no alfalfold for materials.

Rafa: Well, the funny thing is alfalfold was trained on experiments. So it's a different—I mean, that's not a funny—she and I would both come from doing physics-based simulations. And the fact that she called out something that had no simulations in it whatsoever, it's kind of admitting the same underlying issue, which is like all these—META has produced tens of millions, hundreds of millions of training data points. But they're all virtual simulations that just don't carry enough water for the thing we actually want to do.

Andy: This is going to be like a boring and obvious one. But there's a metric that you use to track how efficient your training runs are. It's called model FLOPs utilization, or MFU. So the GPU comes with an advertised peak FLOPs throughput, which is under the best situation doing a calculation that you don't actually care about. How many floating-point operations can you do per unit time? MFU is always a very small fraction of peak theoretical flops. And for reinforcement learning, it's always somewhere around 5% to 6%. So said differently, that means that we're getting like 5% of the actual GPU computing power that we're paying for. So if I could buy fiat, wave a wand, and make our stack perform at 100% model FLOPs utilization, I would do that because we would, one, get to the answer faster, but then also be able to buy fewer GPUs and redeploy that capital to the lab or something like that.

Brandon: That's interesting, though. Because your rollouts, aren't they constrained by the lab?

Andy: They are. But when we train a big model, all of that data—our RL training pipelines are very complicated. So one way to think about how you would do this at scale is just to have the model doing rollouts left and right, waiting for enough trajectories to pile up and then backpropagating that into the model. A different way to do that would be to factorize that, have a bunch of expert models that are trained in parallel that are either generating data or being trained themselves and then you distill that back into the central model. And second way is the most efficient, the more efficient way to do it. Because all those things are happening at different timescale. It's that big, when you have the 10 trillion tokens and you want to push them through the model as efficiently as possible, you're still going to be doing some reinforcement learning on top of that. So if we could get all the flops that we're paying for, I would by fiat declare that.

招聘：181 Fremont Street, SF

Brandon: Cool. Well, yeah, before we end, is there anything you want to leave the audience with?

Andy: Let me say why we're here. So we have an office in San Francisco now. It's 181 Fremont Street in downtown San Francisco. There's currently 20, 30-ish people who sit there, but we are looking to expand that aggressively. We're looking to pull from all areas of the stacks, so both post-training, obviously aggressively hiring for that. Folks have been working in domain AI, like life sciences and materials sciences. We're also hiring for that. No wet lab here currently, so it's all computational work. But if any of this stuff that people have heard about today sounds interesting, feel free to shoot either me or Rafa a message if that sounds interesting.

Brandon: Thank you for being here. It's been a really fascinating conversation. I appreciate it.

Andy: Thank you for having us. Yeah.

[music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、这不是一家实验室公司，而是一台"科学永动机"的原型
Lila 表面上像自动化实验室，但真正的隐藏结构是把"实验室"重新定义为**RLHF 中缺失的那个 Verifier**。这里有一个反转让人拍案：过去十年，AI 的 scaling law 一直被理解为"算力 × 数据 × 参数"三驾马车，而 Ilya 在去年 NeurIPS 上宣告"我们只有一个互联网"之后，业界默认下一波燃料是 synthetic data。Lila 的判断更狠——**下一片数据油田不是模拟器，而是自然本身**。这个视角一旦切换，实验室的价值函数就完全变了：它不再是"验证假设的场所"，而是一台"将自然界的因果规律翻译成 token 的编译器"。Andy 一句话点破："lab of the future should feel like a data center"，机架密度、能效比、24/7 uptime——这些数据中心的 KPI 被搬进了湿实验室，这是一次深层次的"物理基础设施的 AI 化"。
## 二、最出人意料的观察：**"False Positive 对人类是灾难，对模型是甜点"**
节目里

item.title: [Mercatus Center] M.R. Madhavan on Party Power and the Decline of India's Legislatures
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、反叛法的悖论：印度议会已变成一个"20人房间"

Madhavan 在对话中提出了一个惊人的思想实验：既然反叛法（Anti-Defection Law）+ 党鞭制度让每个议员都必须按党派指令投票，那么印度实际上不需要 543 人的下议院——只需要一个 20 平米的会议室，每个政党派一个人按按钮就够了。这不是夸张，而是对制度实质的精准还原。

让人意外的观察：印度是当今世界上极少数将"反叛法"写进宪法的重要民主国家（其他基本是津巴布韦、塞拉利昂这类国家）。这意味着，从制度形态上看，印度议会的"投票自由度"比英美德法日甚至韩国、以色列都低得多。我们习惯把印度视为"世界最大民主国家"，但在议会自治的核心维度上，印度其实是全球异类。

更深的悖论在于：一个原本设计用来"防止腐败叛党"的法律，最终把整个议会变成了"党魁的橡皮图章"——把小恶（个别议员叛变）解决掉的代价，是失去了大善（议会对政府的问责能力）。这与生物学上的"过度免疫反应"（如自身免疫疾病）在结构上惊人地相似：防御系统压制了它本应保护的宿主本身。

二、跨领域连接：GST 委员会 vs. 加密货币的"共识困境"

Madhavan 与主持人关于 GST（商品服务税）委员会的争论，本质上是一个去中心化系统治理的经典难题——它和区块链协议升级面临的问题结构完全一致。

  • GST 委员会要求 3/4 超级多数，但每个邦法律的实施仍需邦议会通过——这就形成了一个"共识层 + 执行层"分离的架构。
  • 一旦某个邦议会拒绝执行，联邦最高法院也无法强制邦议会立法——这就是加密世界所说的 "硬分叉"（hard fork）风险：共识失败时，网络会分裂。
  • 主持人认为这是设计缺陷，Madhavan 却认为"税率必须靠共识"是好事。

这里藏着一个被双方都没点破的洞见：印度 GST 之所以还没出问题，不是因为设计好，而是因为过去 9 年中央与多数邦执政党相同——制度的稳定性来自政治同质性，而非制度本身的韧性。这就像一个从未在压力测试下运行过的分布式系统，我们看到的"稳定"可能只是"未被扰动"。当政治格局分裂时，GST 委员会将面临与以太坊 DAO 分叉、比特币扩容之争同构的治理危机。

三、被忽视的深层病灶：印度选民正在把议员当"政治修理工"，而非"立法者"

对话最沉重的部分被藏在了看似温和的讨论中——Madhavan 讲了一个故事：某议员凌晨 3 点被选区打电话叫醒，问题是"我家的水牛"。

这背后是一个惊人的系统性反常识：印度议员在议会里的"缺席、鼓掌、按钮"式表演之所以没有被选民惩罚，是因为选民根本不期待议员立法。他们期待议员是跨越失败地方政府的"应急通道"——街灯坏了、垃圾没人收、家庭纠纷、儿媳带走嫁妆……这些本应由市政府、村委会处理的问题，全部涌向了议员。

这里的跨领域连接极具启发性：这与软件工程中的"泄漏抽象"（Leaky Abstraction） 完全同构——当底层抽象（地方政府）失败时，问题就会向上层（州立法者、议员）"泄漏"，最终污染整个架构层次。

更让人意外的推论：印度议会改革运动（延长会期、公开投票、废除反叛法）之所以推动不了，不是因为议员反对，而是因为选民不需要。选民已经在用脚投票告诉整个系统：我要的不是《威斯敏斯特式议会》，我要的是"能解决水牛问题的中间人"。真正的改革入口不在议会，而在恢复市政选举——班加罗尔从 2020 年就没有市政府选举、孟买同样瘫痪——印度议会的病，要从街道办事处开始治。

这是一个典型的"底层塌陷、顶层过载"的分形结构，与心理学上的"情感承载错位"（一个人在原生家庭得不到的东西，会去伴侣、老板、朋友身上过度索取）在动力学上惊人地相似。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Welcome to Ideas of India, where we examine the academic ideas that can propel India forward. My name is Shruti Rajagopalan and I'm a senior research fellow at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University.

Today my guest is M.R. Madhavan, who's the co-founder and president of PRS Legislative Research in New Delhi. We talked about the role and purpose of the Rajya Sabha (or the upper house of the Indian Parliament), the treatment of money bills in a Westminster system, how the anti-defection law and party whips have hollowed out parliamentary function in India, more general parliamentary procedure, the role of the governor, and much more.

For a full transcript of this conversation, including helpful links to all the references mentioned, click the link in the show notes or visit mercatus.org/podcasts.

Shruti: Hi Madhavan, welcome to the show. It's a pleasure to have you here. Madhavan: Thanks for calling me.

一、Rajya Sabha 的角色与目的

Shruti: So you are my go-to person for all things related to Parliament. But Parliament has two houses and we really over-index on discussing one and we rarely talk about Rajya Sabha. So I want to start by asking you about Rajya Sabha and what you think is the role and purpose of both Rajya Sabha as an institution, and then maybe you can talk about Rajya Sabha members and what their role and purpose is or should be.

Madhavan: So as an institution the constitution is very clear, right? Lok Sabha's exclusive rights on primarily two things: one is who will be the prime minister of India — effectively who will form the government — and second, related to that, on everything to do with money bills, because under the Westminster system if a money bill loses it's like a no-confidence vote, so it's connected to the first.

On everything else the two houses had equal powers. That's at least in theory through the constitution. The way it's now, Lok Sabha is slightly more powerful simply because if there is a disagreement on bills you're going to have a joint session, and 543 + 245 — obviously 543 has one member one vote, they have a larger say to that extent. But every bill has to pass both houses, other than the fact of the joint session. When it comes to a constitution amendment, there cannot be a joint sitting and therefore they are again equal in power.

So the primary role of Rajya Sabha as it was envisaged — and actually even Lok Sabha — is that any significant issue on legislation should have a second house looking at it and reviewing it before it is passed. So each house has to do an independent assessment. A bill could be introduced in Rajya Sabha, then Lok Sabha will be reviewing it and doing an independent assessment; and of course if it comes to Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha does that. So that is an important role that two sets of people are doing.

Another important part of Rajya Sabha in this process is the fact that Lok Sabha gets elected at one go. Every MP comes in at the general election — I mean I'm not talking about occasional violations, that's an outlier in numbers. Rajya Sabha is a continuous house. Every 2 years one-third of them come in. So there can be occasions when there has been some event and a country gets extremely passionate about it. Just think for example 1984, Indira Gandhi assassination — immediately after that there was a certain type of atmosphere in the country and one party can sweep the polls. That is moderated by Rajya Sabha. Or even 1977, the other side, the party swept the polls. Rajya Sabha is a moderating influence on such sudden changes, because changes happen gradually over 6 years, so it won't be run by any current passions ruling the country. So in that sense it has an important role which is different from that of Lok Sabha.

Rajya Sabha 成员的角色

Shruti: So now I want to come to the role and purpose of Rajya Sabha members. One way to think about it is it is counter-majoritarian in the populist sense — you have general elections and you can have one party or one sentiment sweep. Rajya Sabha, as you just pointed out, is continuous with a rotation of members, one-third gets replaced. So it can serve as that kind of a counter-majoritarian check.

A second kind of check one can think of is: it's supposed to be a council of states and its members are actually supposed to represent state interests. So it can be a check against over-centralization or these anti-federal tendencies. They always said in India that India is a federal nation with a unitary bias, and I used to say that that's like being a little bit pregnant — you are either pregnant or you're not, and you're either federal or you're not. So Rajya Sabha maybe can be a check against this unitary bias, whatever that is.

A third kind of check is just in terms of short-run versus long-run thinking. People who are standing in general elections, they're on a 5-year clock, right? They need to get re-elected, whereas Rajya Sabha members don't have to worry about immediate re-election of that kind. So they can be a check against very short-term measures, especially things that are introduced just pre-election or something like that. Do you think that's how the council thinks of itself?

Madhavan: No, I don't think the members think of it like that. But I would actually — not fully this part of a federal nature, but partly. I'm saying partly because even when we have seen — there have been occasions when we have had a state election and a Lok Sabha election at the same point of time. For example 2019, let's say a state like Odisha — BJP did extremely well in the Lok Sabha election. On the same day BJD won the state election. So the population of the state is choosing differently when they are electing the representative to the national parliament versus when they are electing the representative to the state assembly. They are taking a different view for whatever reason — they are possibly much more discerning than what people give them credit for.

So Rajya Sabha, who are they elected by? By the MLAs of the state. So it's quite possible that the composition of people coming to Rajya Sabha from that state party-wise could be very different from the composition of MPs coming to Lok Sabha from a general election from that state. So there is another way it actually represents what people want in different places.

And I agree with you in one thing, which is that Lok Sabha members have to win an election and therefore have to convince the people in their constituency that they are the best choice available, and they have to satisfy people in the constituency all sorts of grievances and solve their problems. Rajya Sabha members don't have such pressures. They have to convince, at least in theory, members who are themselves elected by others. So it's an indirect election. It is presumably a more discerning decision-making because they are representatives of the people who are electing them, who are not necessarily driven by the immediate passions of the day.

At least that's in theory. In practice, of course, since by and large it's on party lines, you are getting the proportion of what they have there at that point of time. That's what it represents — the party interest and the party strengths in parliament. And we have anyway moved away from the original thinking that they are representative of the state, by even removing the domicile requirement. The Kuldip Nayar case decided that. So you don't even need to be domiciled in the state.

So you actually see fairly often a party leader who has, for whatever reason, not been able to get elected to Lok Sabha, getting elected to Rajya Sabha from a very different state because that is where the party has the numbers to get them into parliament. We've also seen — even in the current cabinet there are several senior ministers who have been brought into Rajya Sabha from a state where they probably had no direct connection in their earlier life.

Constitution calls it the Council of States. Rajya word is there. But it might be more accurate to say the "other house" or the "second house" — or, as some of them call themselves, the "house of elders." The minimum age is 30, and Lok Sabha's minimum age is 25, so okay, they are possibly older. Because you can't even say second house — why should you call Lok Sabha the first?

作为人才通道的 Rajya Sabha

Shruti: So the way you've set up how Rajya Sabha actually acts, as opposed to whatever the constitutional plan was, it seems to do two things well. One is it represents the party interests reasonably well. But the second part you discussed is quite interesting because it's almost become a way of bringing in talent that is not easy to elect. Most famously, of course, Manmohan Singh, who was a two-term prime minister but was brought in initially through the Rajya Sabha.

Madhavan: He was a Rajya Sabha member from Assam. Manmohan Singh has never been a Lok Sabha member. He once contested a seat in Delhi, which he lost. He never stood for any bi-elections anywhere during his prime ministerial term.

We've seen that in states also which have a second house. Nitish Kumar is always a council member. He was never an MLA — he might have been earlier, but when he was chief minister, at least the last couple of terms he was an MLC.

Shruti: And there are only a very handful of states which are bicameral. So the second feature of Rajya Sabha has been bringing in talent that is usually considered not easily electable — we need technocratic expertise. Manmohan Singh was the prime example. I think Arun Jaitley at one point was brought in through the Rajya Sabha.

Madhavan: Not at one point — always a Rajya Sabha member. He contested and lost one Lok Sabha election in Punjab, in Amritsar.

Shruti: So is Rajya Sabha the counter-majoritarian force from a talent point of view?

Madhavan: Possibly. It's a good way to get in talent, definitely. But there is a larger role that Rajya Sabha has played from time to time simply because of this slow change that it has. For example, from the '90s to a few years ago, there was one party — or a coalition — which commanded majority in Lok Sabha and formed the government. So earlier NDA, then UPA, then of course the BJP from '14 to '24 had a majority on its own, then back to a coalition of NDA because BJP on its own doesn't have 272. But they have majority in Lok Sabha.

Much of this period they did not have a majority in Rajya Sabha. So in that period it really worked as a check. The government had to, on any important issue, get a consensus across with the major opposition parties who could otherwise stall and not allow them to pass a bill. So you need to actually build a consensus, which in a democracy is a good thing. If you want policies to be stable for a long time, you want at least the major parties on all sides to be committed to it, because who knows tomorrow they may come to power, and you want them to continue a policy. Continuation of policy is a good thing.

So let the compromises be made, adjustments be done before the policy is implemented, so that what we have implemented has a by-and-large broad agreement in it. That sort of a role Rajya Sabha was playing. Now of course the coalition which has a majority in Lok Sabha also has a majority in Rajya Sabha. So that particular check is no longer available at this point of time — or is weaker at this point of time. But it could change with time. Who knows?

二、Money Bills 与威斯敏斯特系统

Shruti: So coming to money bills — Rajya Sabha technically can vote on money bills, but Rajya Sabha can also be bypassed on money bills.

Madhavan: Rajya Sabha actually — the technical word used — Rajya Sabha doesn't pass a money bill. It returns the money bill.

Shruti: They're not voting on it?

Madhavan: Yes, they are — but then they are returning it. They're not passing it.

Shruti: The vote is not binding in some sense. They can be overruled.

Madhavan: Exactly. They can be overruled. And they can be bypassed altogether — not bypassed, it's overruled. In sense, every bill passed by Lok Sabha has to go to Rajya Sabha. Rajya Sabha, if it does not act in 14 days, it is deemed to be passed.

Shruti: Exactly. That's what I mean by bypassed. So Rajya Sabha need not vote on money bills. Now, one of the reasons — I mean I've always believed that that's a bad idea, right? Because we need fiscal checks and balances as much as you need any other kind of political check and balance. India is also very fiscally centralized. So the council of states not opining on money bills itself is a problem as a design feature.

Now, specifically when it comes to modern times, one thing we've seen is during elections there is this massive bloat in transfer payments and welfare payments. This started in the mid '90s as India started growing richer. The revenue started coming in, but post-Aadhaar and Jan Dhan accounts and electronic transfers of money and direct cash transfers, this problem has really snowballed. You talk to any politician or political economist — it's a kind of competitive populism almost. If one state gives so many rupees in subsidy or a welfare entitlement, or one state gives it to a particular category like women, then another state must match, and the same is true for political parties.

Given that Rajya Sabha doesn't have the same election pressures as Lok Sabha, I think in modern day and age it's even more detrimental that money bills don't have to pass through Rajya Sabha. Do you think this is a problem? Is it a general problem? Or money bills just don't matter?

为什么 Money Bills 必须只归下议院

Madhavan: I think money bills in a Westminster system have to be exclusively in the domain of the house which elects the prime minister. There's no choice. I'll tell you why. And this is where the parliamentary system differs from, let's say, the presidential system. I'm just taking US because that's the one which most of us understand.

So you keep seeing once in a while in the US, government shutdown because the Congress hasn't given money. You will never see that in a parliamentary system by design, because if a money bill doesn't pass — which is a government shutdown — the government of the day goes. It's a no-confidence motion. So you cannot have that. And that's actually a good design feature because you don't want government shutdowns. US has got stuck into that system because it was designed in the 18th century for whatever reason.

Given that money bills have the power to throw out a government, and we say only Lok Sabha has the power to decide who will form the government, you cannot have by definition Rajya Sabha having a say on it, because otherwise you're giving Rajya Sabha the power to do a no-confidence motion against the prime minister. If they vote on a money bill, the prime minister goes. How can you give them the no-confidence power? So Rajya Sabha cannot — that's an intrinsic design feature. There's no way out of it.

Shruti: I want to pause here for a minute. I know what you mean by "it is an intrinsic design feature" of how we do things now.

Madhavan: No — by any form of Westminster system. Bicameral, where one house has exclusive power to decide the head of the government, the other house cannot have the power to remove the head of the government.

Shruti: Which just means that you need to whip up enough votes and form consensus, same as any other system.

Madhavan: Not necessarily. Let's just take an example. Let's say BJP has the majority in Lok Sabha, Congress has a majority in Rajya Sabha. Lok Sabha elections happen, BJP appoints the prime minister. First bill comes — Congress says "we'll unseat him," defeats the money bill. The prime minister goes. What do you do? You can't have it. And they will do it. It's not a question of whipping up a consensus — it's a chance for the opposition to dethrone the current government. No opposition of any political party is going to let go of that. So that's why you will find this in every Westminster system with two houses.

Shruti: It's an easy enough tweak, though, that you say that money bills can pass in Rajya Sabha and a defeat of a money bill does not mean a no-confidence motion. The reason that a defeat of a money bill has to lead to a no-confidence motion — this comes back actually from the UK, where this evolved sometime in the 19th century, because if the government has been elected on a particular campaign and the government cannot have the funding to implement the measures on which it has been elected, a government cannot function. That is the theory behind it.

Madhavan: So the government should have the ability to allocate funds for what it sees as important because it has been elected on those lines. So no one else should have the say. So therefore the house which elects the government —

Shruti: I don't agree with the last part of "no one has a say." There are lots of things on which we give other people, outside of elected representatives, a say. You have parliamentary standing committees. You have all sorts of measures within parliament.

Madhavan: They are all recommendatory. I'm just saying no one else should have the power to dethrone. If you don't have the financial power or ability to implement your policies, you might as well not be there.

Shruti: There's a difference between not having the financial ability versus this is the exact plan that must pass. A money bill is typically a particular configuration of a particular set of budget appropriations. It's not saying that the government can't have the money — you come up with a better set of appropriations and you can pass it.

Madhavan: But that's not the government's plan. If the Lok Sabha members don't agree, they should be voting against that. So the whole idea is — and I'm coming back to my favorite topic — let's assume there's no anti-defection law and there's no whip. Then the prime minister has to convince a majority of Lok Sabha members, including from his or her own party, that this is a good measure. That is the correct check you give. So we are now thinking, and we got socialized to think, that there'll be a way people have to vote on party lines, and then you're trying to think of solutions.

Shruti: Not at all, actually. I completely agree with you that anti-defection has to go, and we'll get into it properly. But we are also, unlike other Westminster systems, we have things like GST where we explicitly have revenues coming from the state. There is a GST council; certain percentage of a very large pool of revenue comes from the states. And the council of states is not allowed to vote on how that is appropriated. So I do think that blindly following 19th century UK law may not quite work for modern-day appropriations, irrespective of anti-defection which needs to go. So I agree with you that you need to separate perhaps whether the government falls and the prime minister is voted out if a money bill gets rejected — but that's an easy enough add.

Madhavan: It's not — but that is, I think, fundamental. It's fundamental to the working of a government. For example, the US actually I think is a serious design problem, but the Congress controls the purse and the executive is expected to perform, and the only check the Congress has on the executive — that's the problem there. In India, parliament has a much better check on the prime minister. Parliament can throw out the prime minister tomorrow. In the US, unless the president is caught doing whatever "high crimes of treason" and whatever they call it, and impeached on that, you cannot kick out the president because you don't think the president deserves to be president any longer. So you're stuck with that.

Shruti: Well, the president can be impeached also by Congress.

Madhavan: Impeached only for crime of some type — not for incompetence, not because you don't like his policies. In India — I'm not sure if we're doing that in India either.

Madhavan: We have done it in the past. We've done it enough times in the past. And look at the UK — the previous conservative party I think changed the prime minister more often than whatever you can think of. And already the Labour Party — somebody led them to power with the largest ever number and has been kicked out in two years.

Shruti: But I don't think the UK is a good model, because the UK is not federal in the same way that India is federal, and its fiscal pool of resources doesn't have the same problem.

GST 委员会的设计难题

Shruti: So let me go to the GST which you pointed out. Now there are two sides of the money bill, right? One is the revenue side and the other is the expenditure side. And both are money bills. And in both, Lok Sabha has exclusive power here. I think GST is particularly problematic on the revenue side. Just consider the following situation: GST Council votes and decides to do a particular tax rate or make some changes. Those have to be implemented by passing a law in Lok Sabha and in every state legislature. Let's say three state legislatures decide to not pass it. They have a sovereign power. Or Lok Sabha decides not to pass it — but Lok Sabha is unlikely to do that, simply because the central government has a veto power in the GST Council. So if they did not want this, they would have blocked it there. But some state government which is out-voted — let's say they decide — what happens to your GST?

GST actually, I think, the GST Council was a compromise to get states to give up their sovereignty on certain taxes, and I don't think it's a good design.

Madhavan: I think it's the opposite. I think it's a good design. Wait till it fails. It's too early.

Shruti: Fair enough. But I think what ends up happening is no matter who has how much veto power — if only one state is a hold-out, the GST Council eventually only passes things that everyone is likely to accept and implement.

Madhavan: Not necessarily. Otherwise it will fail. And we don't know how it plays out. It's all of 5 years old. Think of, let's say, a state like Tamil Nadu — a large number of manufacturing, some item is flowing through, because it's a big manufacturing hub. So if they don't pass something, what happens to all your input credit etc. on that?

Shruti: No, it won't pass then, because it has to be the same rate all across for that particular thing.

Madhavan: So the GST Council has passed something, agreed on something. GST Council has to have three-fourths power. Tamil Nadu is part of the one-fourth and has voted against, has been out-voted fairly. GST Council has adopted according to the constitution. But the guy in Tamil Nadu says "I don't want this. I'm not going to pass this in my assembly." What do you do? There is no — then it's a complete failure of GST. You have to go to Supreme Court to see how to interpret the law.

Shruti: Incidentally, the first version of the GST bill had a recourse of Supreme Court, and it was pointed out that that would go against the constitutional design or something.

Madhavan: It need not be about the exact rate. In some way, one state doesn't agree to that change. Supreme Court cannot force a state assembly to pass a law. So in that case, then GST Council's recommendation is useless, because GST has to be one rate. It has failed.

Shruti: No, but I don't think it'll fail, Madhavan. That's my point.

Madhavan: I mean, lots of things that were designed in 1950 constitution — they designed it fairly well assuming people are not — but tax rates have a huge advantage which is very quickly you reveal the differences.

Shruti: That's fine, but lots of things assumed a certain type of behavior by people. I would say — if I may use the word — that everyone will behave honorably, and without pointing out particular instances, we know that that hasn't played out properly. Just to name one set of offices — governors. If I may say. The constitution writers thought of the governor's office as something — and so my point is, GST is only what, 2017, so it's exactly 9 years old.

Madhavan: No, but here the failure is very clear.

Shruti: So in the case of governor, no one ends up paying for it.

Madhavan: If the constitution says that nobody can force a state legislature to pass a law, then GST Council has to revise it. That's it. Otherwise it's not one country, one tax system.

Shruti: You're still not going to get a situation where you get a different rate in Tamil Nadu and a different rate in the rest of India. So the design —

Madhavan: But that's not a failure of GST design.

Shruti: It is. GST — a cleaner design would have been "forget the consensus, it's a union tax." So you have one country, one tax, and you will share it with the state — maybe 50% goes to the state, 50% goes into the divisible pool. You design that, but you say the sole authority — because if you want one country, one tax, then you need to have one decision-maker. You can't have one country, one tax, 29 people with some people having the ability to later throw a spanner in the works.

Madhavan: No, actually I think that's great for taxation. You need people to have an ability to throw spanner in the works.

Shruti: So one small state with equal power can —

Madhavan: Sure.

Shruti: Then you can have a complete logjam.

Madhavan: I think that's good. You need to build consensus on certain rates. I remember the very first GST bill which came, it used the word "consensus." Then people said "what does consensus mean? Does it mean everybody? Does it mean supermajority?" So right now they have defined it as a super majority — 75%, with 33% of the union and 66% of the states.

Shruti: But effectively it's 100% because they can't do it without the state.

Madhavan: No, but some — if two states say something else. Okay. And today it'll get passed. Everybody else passes their laws, two states don't. What happens? We haven't seen that situation.

Shruti: Partly because it's too early in the game.

Madhavan: Too early. And today we are also seeing that a large number of states has the government of the same party as the union. So it's too short a period to actually make any conclusive statements on how it's likely to play out. I'm just saying as a design — designs should account for things going wrong and not assume things are always going to go right.

Shruti: Yeah. But I don't think people disagreeing on tax rates is things going wrong.

Madhavan: I agree. But my point is, if somebody disagreeing on tax rate can block everybody else who's agreeing on that tax rate — that's things going wrong.

Shruti: So everyone has a veto power. That doesn't bother me, because the nature of the tax is it's a very regressive consumption-based tax which affects every single constituent —

Madhavan: Let's not get into that.

Shruti: I don't want to get into the concept of taxation. But GST touches everybody no matter how tiny a thing they buy. Unlike income tax, unlike property taxes.

Madhavan: I mean, nobody is saying it's not regressive. That's a description. But how does one design that is a different thing? I'm okay with what you call a "failure." I'm okay with them requiring a very high degree of consensus.

Shruti: Let's not have a GST. Let's not have a consumption tax if you have that point of view. My point is, if you're going to have a GST — my assumption is the fact that you have agreed on having a GST means you have agreed to have the regressive tax. I'm not going to one level before that saying "should we even have a GST." Because then the question is —

Madhavan: That's a different question. You might have agreement on a regressive tax and a disagreement on the exact rate depending on how rich or poor your constituents are.

Shruti: Agree. So it's not only the rates GST Council decides — lots of design issues.

Madhavan: Lots of design issues. I'm somehow okay with states being able to veto GST. My bigger problem is Rajya Sabha not having any say on it, given that this is fundamentally a center-state —

Madhavan: It doesn't matter. We have already agreed Rajya Sabha does not represent states in any case, we discussed that. So Rajya Sabha is just called Council of States — let's forget it, let's just call it the second house.

Shruti: Okay, so on both the revenue and expenditure side, you're like: this has nothing to do with the state, so it's fine anyway.

Madhavan: It has nothing to do with the Council. I mean, participants into the state, say it's irrelevant, right? Other than the fact that they're elected by MLAs.

三、反叛法（Anti-Defection Law）：印度议会的核心病灶

Shruti: So now coming to anti-defection, which is the other big part. In the previous conversation we were having about money bills, you were like — given that we have anti-defection, there are a number of issues in terms of members of parliament voting their conscience. So a prime minister or the head of government doesn't necessarily need to persuade every single Lok Sabha member on whatever tax or any bill that they wish to pass, right? And now it just gets sort of made official through the party whip. A few questions overall on anti-defection — and this is irrespective of money bill. Does it have any role at all in a constitutional democracy?

Madhavan: No, it should not have any.

Shruti: And in a way we are the only country of any significance which has this. I tried to find other countries and I found things like Zimbabwe and Sierra Leone and things like that. So maybe just for the listeners who are completely uninitiated and not as obsessed with getting rid of anti-defection as you and me — do you want to give them a very brief primer on what it is?

什么是反叛法

Madhavan: It's a very simple one. Two parts to it. A person is elected to Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha, or to any legislative assembly. So person becomes an MP or an MLA on a party ticket.

(A) That person cannot move from this party to another party. There's one exception, I'll just — which is playing out, so it's important, I'll tell that first. But that one is fine.

(B) If the party issues a whip — which means what? The party leadership sends a note to every member of that party in that house on any vote to say "you have to vote yes, or you have to vote no, or you have to abstain." It could be one of the three things. You have to follow that order. If you do not follow that order, that vote counts — but the party can then remove you. Not from the party, which is fine — I mean, you're a member of a club, you don't agree, you're kicked out from the club, that's fine — not from the party, but from the membership of the house itself.

So the price you pay is extremely high. So most people, unless it's on most things, would not take that call.

Shruti: And just to be clear, most Westminster and presidential systems almost everywhere in the world, there is some form of a whip, but it's not official and it's not constitutionally mandated the way we have in India, where literally if you don't vote the whip that your party has officially issued, you can get thrown out of your parliamentary seat — which the party didn't elect you to, the people elected you to — which is frankly shocking.

Madhavan: Exactly. In other places, obviously the party doesn't like you if you vote against the party. And what can they do? They can deny you party positions. They can deny you a ticket in the next election. They can throw you out of the party immediately. But you will still be a member of parliament. And if people in your constituency like you enough, you can again get elected from that next election, or you can decide not to continue — but that's your call. You do not pay the consequences of being kicked out of the house the very next day or next week.

"20 人房间"思想实验

So this effectively makes every member completely subservient to whatever the party leadership tells that person to do. How do we actually characterize what is representative democracy? What does it do? It says that people at large — which is all citizens — are choosing a set of people to represent them, to actually take decisions on their behalf on public issues for a limited period of time, which is the election cycle. So you're actually handing over your power to someone else to exercise on your behalf, saying that these are the right people to exercise power on my behalf. And they are expected to take considered decisions on your behalf. If they do not take those considered decisions — this power is given to them for let's say 5 years — at the end of five years you kick them out and give someone else that power. That's a core design of a representative democracy.

Here, I have elected somebody to take considered decisions on my behalf, but that person now has to take decisions as ordered by someone else. So it goes to the core of the design.

Shruti: They're no longer members who are representing the people who elected them. They are members representing the party interest.

Madhavan: So you don't need them. So what you need is — let's say you have an election like we had, everyone is elected, I'm rounding off numbers. Let's say BJP has 240 people, Congress has 100, Samajwadi has 37, and so on. All you need is one person from BJP comes, presses a button and you say 240. One person from Congress presses a button, you say 100. Why do we need so many? Why do we need a parliament which is such a — now even a larger Lok Sabha building?

Shruti: But then how will they get all their nice bungalows and life and perks?

Madhavan: You give them that. You give them that. And hold the actual parliament in a boardroom which can seat 20 people. 20 parties, one member each. Take up a room in some place — 20 people can sit and you have 20 buttons and done. I mean, I'm obviously trying to make a point that —

Shruti: No, but you're absolutely right. I don't think this is an exaggeration. This is what's actually happening. And then they'll come and make some speeches — for the record. But when the voting comes, they have absolutely no choice.

反叛法的后果：无法罢免总理

Madhavan: So that's what the anti-defection law has done. And what it has actually done over a period of time is much worse. Once you have this design, what are the consequences? Somebody is elected as a party boss, as a chief minister, as a prime minister — whatever — that person cannot be removed if that party has a majority in that house. Cannot be removed for the next 5 years.

And just look at the UK. I think what — the sixth PM going out and the seventh is going to come in in last 10 years. No one completed a term from either party. And it's not because there were general elections. It's because within the period of that House of Commons, the members of the party lost faith in that person. Of course, there was Rishi Sunak, who led them into the elections and their party lost badly. Starmer got a majority — more than 2/3 majority — and he has been removed in two years by the same party.
