This is inconceivable in any system which has an anti-defection law, because you can't remove. So you're effectively electing a person to hold power for the next 5 years. So a no-confidence motion can only work in India in a coalition government. It cannot work if a government has won a majority. So you have taken away the core design of the Westminster system — which is the government is accountable to parliament — and this final point of accountability is a no-confidence motion. And now there's no no-confidence motion.

为什么议员自己不推动改革？

Shruti: So here's my question: Why are parliamentarians going with this? It makes no sense to me why they would give up their power in such a way.

Madhavan: If you talk to them, many of them don't like it. But obviously all party leaderships like it, because they have the power.

Shruti: No, you can imagine a number of parliamentarians banding together and going to the Supreme Court to get this invalidated and so on. They just don't seem to do any of it.

Madhavan: People have gone to the Supreme Court. Supreme Court has said this is fine.

Shruti: But which people have gone to the Supreme Court for the changes? Well, I think it does change the optics. If there were 50 or 100 Lok Sabha members —

Madhavan: They won't go. They won't go because they'll have to pay the consequences.

Shruti: Not really. They can't lose their seat in parliament just because they approached the Supreme Court.

Madhavan: But that is like saying "who will bell the cat." It's the same story, right? Contrary to their individual interest, they have to bell the cat. So they will all murmur — in individual they'll be cribbing about it. They will even speak about it in public. We had them speak about it in public at our conference, which is an open conference. Enough of them have spoken. People have even written about it. In fact, from both the leading parties: Manish Tewari had introduced a bill — his bill said restrict to no-confidence and money bills. Jayant Sinha, when he was an MP, had introduced a private member bill which said "omit the 10th schedule" — straightforward one-line bill.

So in that sense there are people from — and several others have actually done it. And this is a public statement in a way, by introducing a private member bill in parliament. So they have actually taken that stance.

Now, I don't know whether Supreme Court will do anything. Frankly — let's not, honestly, I don't — it is so blatantly unconstitutional. But I do have a collection of one-rupee coins. So I will not talk about the Supreme Court.

Shruti: I think we have to play that clip as our promotional material for this podcast. So is there any way we're ever going to get rid of the anti-defection? Or this is just now part of our design feature and we live with this forever?

福利政治与选民的成熟

Madhavan: No, I think we'll get rid of it sometime. I tell you when that'll happen — don't expect it to come from — and, in many other things, like you talked about the freebie subsidies etc., I think it's actually a good thing, which is why I'm saying that I don't — if I'm looking at the public finance and economics, I don't like it. But I'm looking at democracy — well, people are voting for it. Politicians are saying "if you vote us, we'll give you this." They are saying "yes, therefore we'll vote for you." That is democracy. So it's actually very democratic system playing out.

Shruti: I'm not interested in only living in a democratic majoritarian system. I'm interested in living in a system which has checks and balances precisely to that too.

Madhavan: Agreed. But this is not democratic majoritarian system.

Shruti: It is — you're taking someone else's money and giving to someone else. It must have a check.

Madhavan: It's not someone else's money.

Shruti: It is someone else's money. They tax someone for it and they can put them in jail for not paying it.

Madhavan: That's true of every taxation system. So either you say —

Shruti: Yes, I agree, which is why we need — so frankly I have — I mean, there are people who are very ideological, saying that taxation is violence. I don't agree with that.

Madhavan: I agree. There are checks on it. It has to pass. They promise, then they bring in a budget. They put it in the budget. The budget has to pass the house.

Shruti: All this has to happen with anti-defection where the —

Madhavan: But there are checks. It's not that — what I'm saying is, fortunately in India, by and large — I don't know why — most parties have been actually fiscally reasonably conservative over a period of time. So you will see election promises are large; after getting elected on all those schemes, they will find the ability to put in conditions, put in this, put in that, and not actually fulfill.

Shruti: I agree with you partially. I think that is more true of the union government than state governments for sure. Look at Maharashtra's Ladki Bahin — what was said and what is implemented is fair enough — very very different. But they typically tend to make more of these welfare entitlements and promises in general, and now increasingly — this is another design feature we've only seen in the last 7-8 years, right? This is very post everyone having Jan Dhan accounts and Aadhaar cards and having this kind of deep penetration where you can press a button and things happen. I think you're going to get more of this.

Madhavan: Possibly. It's actually a signal of the failure of the state to provide public goods. So if you're not giving me good public health, if you're not giving me good public schools — okay, let me at least take the cash. I don't blame people for demanding.

Shruti: But I do want a design system which has some checks and balances. And this is perverse, right? Because now I'm giving you cash — I don't feel any obligation as a state to give you the public goods because I'm just buying you off with cash.

Madhavan: So it's actually — I agree with you — it's a perverse system which is playing out. But I think, like many of these things, these always have an expiry. For example, just look at the promises made in the recent TN elections by the DMK government. They didn't win. In fact the promises made by TVK was much less than what was made by — in fact that was part of the campaign, right? So it's not that — I don't think voters are stupid. I don't think these things win you all elections, and political parties are smart. Politicians are smart — once they see that this is no longer incremental advantage on winning elections, they will drop it and move on to something else. I am nowhere near as smart as them, so I don't know what they will move on to. But they are incredibly smart. They know the pulse, they know what's going on.

四、议员的角色：宪法期待 vs. 选区期待

Shruti: So going back to what is the role of a parliamentarian or a state legislative assembly member — one thing I found which is remarkable about Indian representatives at whichever level, they are deeply in touch with their constituents. I've grown up in Delhi — anytime you go to one of these Lutyens bungalows, there will be like a long line of people either from the constituency or representing them. Every small and large problem: "cattle are dying in our village," some road needs to be built, whatever it is — no problem is too small or too big. Sometimes it's personal problems and family problems, because they consider the legislators the head of their community or something like that.

So it's quite remarkable how much they are in touch with their constituents. Now, the way we think about parliament, we want this representation to be very legible, right? Folks like you and me, anyone who studied political science or the Westminster system — we want our representatives to actually attend parliament. We want the attendance to be made public. We want their vote on every topic to be made public. These are things India is not good at and doesn't do. We want, of course, people to vote their conscience, which they can't with anti-defection. We don't want debates to just be where things get staged — when things are actually discussed in the party office but actual debates.

But their constituents don't seem to care too much about it, and they seem to know exactly what their constituents care about. So is this just one of those instances where theoretically what we think parliamentarians and legislators should function as is not happening, and it's not legible to people like you and me who are students of democracy and political science? Or is this just — there is a problem in the way we run this?

Madhavan: Oh, big question, right? I've interacted with a large number of MPs. Most of them don't like what's going on. They did not get in here to solve these sort of — I remember once, it's a hilarious story if you think of it. I met an MP for breakfast and he said he had a bad sleep the previous night because he was woken up at 3:00 a.m. with a call from the constituency. He says "I'm staying in Delhi, somebody's calling — my buffalo is now" — is that an MP's job to fight somebody's buffalo in North Bihar?

Shruti: Okay, I've got one better for you — and I got this story from a LAMP fellow, and I won't name the LAMP fellow or the member of parliament or the state.

Madhavan: I heard this also.

Shruti: Right. It is — the wife has left with the dowry she brought, she left with all the gold, and it belongs to me and the wife has left. So these are really the problems that they get.

Madhavan: They're not particularly happy solving them. They're also caught in this whole bind, because if they don't address — in fact there is a very nice framing that was in one of our conferences. This was Prithviraj Chavan, he recited a story, and the framing he used was: "there is a constitutional expectation of my role, and there's a constituency expectation of my role. I want to do the constitutional expectation. But if I don't do the constituency expectation, I am not back here next term to do the constitutional expectation." So to balance the two things.

真正的病灶：地方政府的失败

Now, part of the problem — and I think a large part of the problem — is the failure of our representative governance in the lower ranks. So you don't have proper city governments, proper panchayats, proper etc.

Shruti: Sometimes proper state governments also, if there is president's rule and other things.

Madhavan: I'm not even talking about president's rule. I'm talking about basic issues like MPs have to address all the time — issues like street light not working, garbage not being taken. They don't even have power to do anything. They can just make a call from New Delhi and it'll count for something.

The role is of the local government. Local government is failing, and people don't make that much of a distinction. Lots of people come to the representative — "MP is the representative, MP should be doing it." In any case, we have — what's the number, 1.4 billion? Today by 550 is a very large number. It's like 25-30 lakh people per constituency. Double the parliament — 15 lakh — big deal.

If you want the member of parliament to have a reasonable level of connect with a large part of the constituency over 5 years, what's the maximum number? Maybe 100,000 people. That means your parliament will be 15,000 people. If you have one lakh — the UK parliament, the constituency size is one lakh, mine is one lakh.

Shruti: But in India, the original constitutional design was 750, because we are a large country. But if we do 7 lakh, 50,000, it's going to be more than 1,800.

Madhavan: No, but 750 doesn't solve the problem. 7 lakh 50 and 30 lakh is the same, because your problem is that the MP cannot know most people. I don't know how to swim — give me 20 ft or 60 ft, it's the same. You have to give me 3 ft of water. Once you're putting me over six, it's all the same.

So that problem doesn't get solved anyway. So we might as well have 550 people.

Shruti: So we can't solve this unless we have multiple levels of —

Madhavan: Exactly. And that is in the design. But we haven't implemented — so the state governments are in a way — our states are the size of a European nation. So state governments in their domain should be like that, and then we should get down to the next round, which is where a lot of local problems need to be solved.

Now the problem is many of these things are failing, and part of it — they're failing is because the higher rung doesn't allow them to work. Not only they don't devolve — one is: when we say devolve there are multiple ways of devolution. One is devolution of money.

Shruti: Forget money before that — devolution of power. They don't devolve power.

Madhavan: Even before that: when was the last time Bangalore had — Bangalore is I think an important city. Bangalore Corporation elections — when were they held? I think it is 2015. Since 2020 Bangalore has not had a corporation. You're talking about possibly one of the bigger, more important economic engines of the country. So you're not having elections. Mumbai — what happened? All of Maharashtra. So you're talking about devolving? Forget devolving — existence of a body is not there. It has been run by an appointed administrator who is an IAS officer. So there is no accountability to the people at that level. The IAS officer is not the representative of the people, so they don't call them.

So then people reach out to the next level, because that's the first level of representative they can reach to. So the MP is caught in a bind which they don't like also. They have not come to parliament to settle these problems. They have come to parliament to think about larger issues. In their own mind, they want to do that.

Shruti: They're trapped in the same system.

Madhavan: They are trapped in the same system. So the solution out of this is to fix the lower end. And then these things will automatically slowly get shifted, and some of an obsession — I would say — with having parliament meet more often etc. will happen. Again, like everything else — I know you believe in markets — there's also a market. If people want MPs to be in parliament and think of these larger things rather than be in the constituency and solve their problems, parliament will start meeting more often. Today the people in the constituency want the MP right there and not in Delhi to be solving their problem sitting there. Do I want to come from my constituency and line up in his house in Lutyens Delhi, or do I just want to take my local bus and go to his house which is 20 km away or 10 km away? Which would I prefer? I would prefer the latter because it's easier.

Shruti: No, but you've opened up a huge can of worms now, because this fundamentally means that Indian voters are thinking of all these representatives as sort of political fixers as opposed to actually building out the operating system that governs us.

Madhavan: Yes.

Shruti: No, but that is a bigger failure. It's not a failure of money bills or anti-defection or Westminster. That's a huge failure.

Madhavan: Why are they thinking of it? Because the alternate system — as the words you said, "political fixer" — which is required at the panchayat level, has failed. If you fix — if I may use the word — the "political fixer" problem at that level, then this automatically gets resolved.

Shruti: But that can't get fixed until they legislate properly.

Madhavan: So the problem again is at the state government level, not at the parliament level. State assemblies have to start devolving power there. And that will happen if there is enough pressure coming from the people to do that. And some states have done it well. And also — we've had local governments only for 25-30 years in the best of states where elections were regularly held. So that also, I think, is something we are learning — we are learning how to have democratic participation at the local level, more so in grams than in cities. So there is that distinction, but hopefully over a period of time that also matures.

五、州立法机构的荒诞：一年只开 24 天

Shruti: But how do you view state legislatures? Because we've had state legislatures since the beginning of the republic. They've had as much time to mature as parliament, as any other post-Indian post-freedom constitutional function. Was this an original design problem? Did we ruin it with the role of the governor? Did we ruin it with over-centralization and Planning Commission kind of holding the purse strings? What is the reason that our state legislatures just don't function like a legislature?

Madhavan: Well, let me ask you a question first. Can you guess — last calendar year, 2025 — average across all states, number of days that they sat?

Shruti: I would say it's under 50.

Madhavan: I'll give the numbers later. What is the highest?

Shruti: Highest must be 85 or thereabouts.

Madhavan: What's the lowest?

Shruti: 10-ish, you know, because some states are really really badly functioning.

Madhavan: Okay, I'll give you the answers. Average is 24. And it was the highest in the last 4 years, slowly going up from '22, '23, '24. So I keep saying that by Viksit Bharat we'll be 47, and maybe we'll hit 100 or something like that at this rate. Highest last year was Odisha at 43. Kerala has done sometimes slightly better. Kerala, Odisha do 50-ish some years. Nobody has ever done 80 in my living memory. The lowest last year was Nagaland — seven.

Shruti: Seven. Okay, I'm close on the lowest. There are several below 15 because if the average is 24 — the sum above, obviously this is a distribution, several below 15. So they are not even meeting. I didn't expect them to be so tightly clustered. I thought there'd be some states that do really well and some states that do really badly.

Madhavan: Really well is 45. Very bad news.

Shruti: That was my average in my head. Wow. That's extraordinary.

Madhavan: So if you think of it — normal 2 months is 60 days. Take off 8 days for weekends, 52, and maybe a couple of other days off. So if a normal person goes to office for 2 months, that person is working more days in office than MLAs are working in the assembly. They're of course working in the constituency, that's a different story, but how much are they working in the assembly which is at least constitutionally their primary role?

一天通过 40 部法案

Shruti: And now there is a by-product to this, which is sometimes they pass 40 bills in a single day. There is no debate, there is no discussion — table and you pass. And most of our assemblies are unicameral.

Madhavan: We have the data. There are several states where every bill has been introduced, passed the same day or the very next day. Last year, Karnataka had the maximum number of bills passed — they had 85.

Shruti: In a single day?

Madhavan: No, they passed totally 80-odd bills in that year, out of which one day they passed 20-something, another day they passed some 17 or 18. So they pass them all off in one day. Done. And this is I'm talking about the lower house because Karnataka does have two houses.

So most states pass most bills without discussion. Some exceptions: Kerala actually sends most bills to a committee, but the committee design is very interesting. Unlike parliament, where by design committees do not have a member of the government — it's an entirely legislative committee, no executive — Kerala, the committee is chaired by the respective minister the committee it goes to. And last year, in a majority of the bills referred to the committee, the committee report came within 2 to 3 days of being referred to. Of course there are some bills where they have taken much longer, they've actually done detailed —

Couple of years back Kerala passed a public health bill. They did detailed consultations. They actually had public consultations in four cities across the state, which was open — anybody could actually walk in. So we were invited to one, so I had actually gone off to Trivandrum for that. It was actually in a hall. All you had to do in the assembly was to show some ID document to get in, sign a register and you can come into the hall. So there's a proper public consultation, and they were listening to people and they were taking notes. So Kerala has done some stage.

Maharashtra is not too bad. Karnataka is actually one of the better ones. Rajasthan has improved. Haryana was a state which used to pass every bill on the same day. Then the last set of five years — not now, just before the election — they had a different speaker. That speaker decided that bills will go to committees. So I'm actually always optimistic, in the sense the changes just require some person to take a call, and there are such people who come in.

Shruti: So we actually see that one way to put pressure on the system to improve is actually to just put all the information out so the people know and there's pressure. So that's — PRS is extraordinary. I know this thing about most states passing bills on the same day because of PRS reports. I know anything about state legislatures only because of PRS reports.

Madhavan: And actually the state data is way more difficult to compile than parliament. Parliament has an excellent website. Some states have good websites, but not all.

议会程序的小改革

Shruti: But you know, even in parliament — like now if I get into nitty procedure — there is no automatic division call unless someone requires the division to be called, except for constitutional bills, right? Our quorum requirements are very low, they're just 10%. There is no mandatory quorum call — a member has to ask for it. This is all not law, this is all nerdy geeky parliamentary procedure that you live by. Why are we not able to fix these sorts of little things? Because it will make such a big change — like just changing the parliamentary procedure on requiring a quorum for this can all come from the speaker, the secretariat.

Madhavan: See, quorum actually is not a problem, simply because there is rarely — the Lok Sabha doesn't have 50 people.

Shruti: Fair enough. But I feel as a practice we should do it.

Madhavan: What I'm saying is it doesn't matter, because even if there is — one, somebody has to notice — there are less than — somebody has to complain. So quorum I'm not bothered about, because it's not a burning problem.

The voice vote problem — I actually think of it as an even more fundamental problem. Which is that I have elected — or not even elected, this — I may or may not have elected that person, but I live in a particular part of India, and he's my representative or she is my representative. That person, the way the parliamentary system is supposed to work, is that person is accountable to me, and that accountability is enforced in the next election, when I can vote that person out. And on what basis do I do that? I am seeing that person's performance in parliament. I want to know whether that person voted for the bill, against the bill. Now, of course, one could say — back to the favorite topic — the person didn't have a choice. Okay. But was the person even present in the house? We don't even know that. So that I think is an information which should be the right of the voter to know whether the representative was there and acting on their behalf. The voice votes take away that.

So we have been arguing for a long time that all votes — at least, let's say if you're voting on a bill, clause by clause voting, don't bother, because it's a time thing — but the final vote should be —

第三次读程序的形式化

Another way which people actually don't talk about, where we have short-changed the constitutional provisions: every constitution actually explicitly states that every bill shall have three readings. We have the first reading, which is introduction of the bill — hardly ever any discussion is voted, fine. We do have on many bills a proper second reading. I have, in the 20 years of PRS that I have been watching parliament closely, never seen a third reading — other than, no, there has always been a third reading, because it's mandatory. So I'll tell you what happens.

First reading is introduction. Then it may or may not go to committee, come back, there's a discussion, and then there's a clause-by-clause vote on every clause. That's the second reading. And then what is the third reading? "It is that the bill as amended may be passed." In the UK, this is usually known on a different day and there's still a discussion on it. In India there's a 2-second thing: clause by clause done, the bill as amended is voted upon — "ayes." It's always ayes because it's agreed upon by now. And that's a 2-second thing. So we don't even do —

I don't consider this a third reading.

Shruti: What I'm saying is constitutionally it's a third reading.

Madhavan: It's like — I remember one of my nieces, very smart kid, went and asked her grandmother "can I go to play?" Grandmom said no. Went to dad and said "can I go to play? I've already asked Grandmom." Dad said "okay, go." So this is like that, right? I mean, constitution says do a third reading. I have done a third reading. Is the bill passed? Yes, because I have done the third reading.

Shruti: So what is the barrier in changing these sorts of rules? Because I imagine parliamentarians —

Madhavan: So this should not be difficult. And we have tried to discuss this with some of them, and many of them say the vote should be there. So let me put the different types of things. Anti-defection is a big one, because you need a constitutional amendment.

Shruti: Yeah.

Madhavan: What does this require? The rule says that there will be a voice vote. If any member asks for a division, division should be carried. Of course, it gives speaker the power to not do it, unfortunately. But it ordinarily says all you need is — you don't need 100 MPs, as you said, to go to Supreme Court. You need one member to ask for a division every time. Or three or four of them who say "this is a cause we have taken up." And ideally three-four across parties, so it doesn't become very partisan. "There's a call we have taken up. We are a group of, let's say, young MPs who want to reform parliament, because we are going to be here for a long time. And this is an important reform. We'll take this — it's not a partisan issue, it's just cleaning up parliament issue. And on every bill one of us will ask." Because all of us may not be present in the house — there's something burning in the constituency, political thing, we may not be there — but one of us will ask. That's all it requires. It's a relatively easier thing.

Shruti: Then you've been doing this a long time. You know a lot of parliamentarians — what do they say when you recommend it?

Madhavan: No, we don't tell them "you do it." We tell them there's an issue. Because it's their choice. My job is to point out issues. My job is not to tell them what to do — because they know what to do.

六、州长的角色：一个不该被选民选出的关键角色

Shruti: I want to talk a little bit about the role of the governor. This is another one of those anti-defection problems where there is a two-fold issue, right? Like if I look at it as a geeky thing — we have a very fundamental design flaw where we've made the governor, who has some very important parts including who's actually invited to form a government in a particular state (which is one of the most important functions), and then the letter recommending president's rule.

Madhavan: Yes, these are two functions which are — I mean they are, I won't even say the spirit of democracy — they are core democratic functions. It is crazy that we've done this with an unelected person.

Shruti: Now, other than the fact that we've done this with an unelected person, this person was appointed by the union cabinet — which is the other problem, right? Serving at the pleasure of the president. So it's not even one of those situations where they were appointed by the previous legislature and they continue, or something which makes a little bit more design sense.

We've had a vast improvement in president's rule, which me and my co-authors have found empirically — but that's one judgment. Exactly. But that also shows that simple things like requiring a floor test and having some very simple procedural safeguards can make a big difference, even with a system as flawed as we have with the governor.

Why don't the states protest, come together, have any kind of — I mean this is something that is impacting every single state legislature across the country and union territories also. Why is this simply not an issue in Indian democratic politics, and only people like you and me are frothing at the mouth about the governor?

设计难题

Madhavan: So this is not an easy solution. It's not an easy design to make. Because you got to the nub of it: somebody has to do one role, which is, after an election, decide whom to invite. Someone has to do it. Now, who that someone will come to — but you need to have somebody doing it. Second, and this is a slightly wider question: someone may have to recommend partition. I'm not even saying someone has to — it's a slightly open question.

Shruti: I think we should have no president, but that's a separate —

Madhavan: Because there could be occasions — let's say some state decides to secede. Extreme circumstances. So you might need to have to hold the union together. But that is the design of constitution. It is like you started off by saying — unitary, federal, whatever, right?

Shruti: That's why I think India is not federal.

Madhavan: It's not — it's a union of states. I mean, that's very clear. Because parliament can decide that one state can be split into two.

Shruti: By a voice vote.

Madhavan: By whatever. So the core is — once that is the — you have to abide by that design. Let's not get into at that level, right? At some level we have to start — okay, this is a design, and not start from zero.

Shruti: Okay, so I'm with you. Let's start with someone has to do these two functions.

Madhavan: Now, how do you find that somebody? You cannot have an elected person, simply because — and this was discussed in the constituent assembly, and I agree with them — then there'll be two people who have legitimacy of having been elected: the chief minister and the governor. You cannot do that.

So one possible thing is — I'm thinking out loud — where do we have the problems here? The governor does multiple things. The governor does these two, and the governor does many other things also. Governor is often the chancellor of universities. Governor has to give assent to a bill, okay, or refer to president. And a bunch of other things.

Now let us say "whom to call." We have broadly solved it after the Bommai judgment — like whoever you call has to prove majority on the floor. You call the wrong person, that person will not last. So it's a very temporary problem which will get resolved.

President's rule again has to be ratified by parliament. So there is a second round of check by each house — both Rajya Sabha, Lok Sabha have to vote on it. So you're having some level of check. Governor cannot act unilaterally.

Shruti: They can't go completely rogue, but they are totally agents of the union cabinet, which is the problem to start with.

Madhavan: But even president's rule — it's a limited thing, it's 6 months.

Shruti: Well, except in the case of Punjab and this and that —

Madhavan: But those are exceptional cases. Kashmir and Punjab are the only cases where they hardcoded, and those are exceptional times. We've had presidents rule more than 128 times. I agree. But all of them — the max limit is 6 months, twice one year. And in the recent past, last 20 years or so, we've not had president rule often at all.

Shruti: No, that's just — but that's a consequence of Bommai, and only Bommai.

Madhavan: I agree. But now we are living in the post-Bommai region. So we actually have not had the governor problem, if I may say so, with respect to these two issues.

Shruti: We've had governor issues with the first one.

Madhavan: The first one — but to invite, we've seen this in Maharashtra, some serious chaos, agreed. That gets resolved if you say — and the Supreme Court has intervened enough times that there should be a floor test within 2 weeks. So if the person calls the wrong person who doesn't have a majority, in 2 weeks it falls. Of course, you have first mover advantage and all that, I'm not getting into that.

Shruti: But we should get into that given anti-defection law.

Madhavan: In fact, anti-defection law prevents wrong person.

Shruti: Not really, because now there's an incentive to buy groups wholesale.

Madhavan: That's true. But what I'm saying is, let's stick to — let's keep expanding the problem, you'll never find a solution. I mean, you're an economist — ceteris paribus.

解决方案：剥离州长的所有权力

Let's say these two are problems. They are not "no problems," but they are things that we have at least figured out now how to manage. So let's assume we have the same system of appointing government — and you amend the constitution to say the governor has exactly two roles. These bills will be passed by the assembly, and that's it. They don't go to anybody. Assembly passes bill, law. The governor is not the chancellor or default of universities. Somebody else you make. But governor has no role. Give him the big bungalow — we spend so much money on everything — let the guy have his day. Maybe the guy can come on Republic Day and Independence Day.

Shruti: But you're basically saying the way out of this mess is to start stripping away powers.

Madhavan: All powers. So that this becomes a very like almost like a paper — so you need a person for these two roles. You don't need a person for the other roles. So take out the roles.

Shruti: I do think it's important to give assent.

Madhavan: No, irrelevant.

Shruti: The way we are passing bills in our legislatures — 5 minutes ago, we were talking about how crazy that is.

Madhavan: That doesn't matter. That is the will of the people. Why should an unelected guy decide to give or not give —

Shruti: Because the design is that we have checks and balances against majoritarian checks.

Madhavan: No, these are not checks and balances. So if the legislators pass a bill which is unconstitutional, then it goes to the court. If it's constitutional, they have the right to pass. They have been voted to do that. An unelected guy doesn't have —

Shruti: Here the really big problem is that he's not elected.

Madhavan: And an elected guy will have a dual power center. So you cannot have an elected guy. So the only way out is to not give that power. And frankly even if you look at the union parliament — only one case in the last 70 years where the president has returned their assent. There have been a couple of instances, actually one instance where the president sat on it, which I think was wrong.

Shruti: Should have returned it if you don't like it.

Madhavan: But that's about it. So it is just a process which they sign on the dotted line, right? So why have that process — and that's what you want in the states. So you take the governor off all the other roles.

Shruti: I don't mind the other — all the other roles other than the two which are — where you can ideally take the governor off everything, but you need somebody to do these two, that we agreed on.

Madhavan: I don't even agree on the second one. I don't think president's rule should exist at all.

Shruti: Fine. I don't have strong views on that. I have not thought about it too much. But I'm saying, as long as you have it, you need to have somebody to recommend. And you have to have somebody to decide whom to call. If you did not have these two, I would say get rid of the governor's office itself. But if these are the only two things — let the person do only these — do only those two things.

Shruti: Actually I really like this idea. You start stripping things off.

Madhavan: Give the person whatever the nice-looking hat and power to unfurl the flag. Let the person do all those ceremonial —

Shruti: Like the British monarch.

Madhavan: Do all those things. Provide entertainment, go on a buggy or whatever.

Shruti: I don't mind. We spend a lot of money — we can spend a few more on this. We have enough cash. And I think there's some room for ceremony — calling the opening of a house, all this Republic Day stuff. I agree with you. I don't mind the ceremony of it.

Madhavan: Have you seen — you're saying ceremony — did you see the photo of the West Bengal Chief Justice when the new judge was being sworn?

Shruti: No.

Madhavan: You should look it up. It's a photograph. There should be video also, I'm sure. The person is dressed up like a 19th century Englishman. Because that is their ceremonial dress on that day.

Shruti: Oh, wow. Okay, I'll go look this up.

七、PRS 的意义：为议员提供研究支持

Shruti: So I want to ask you about PRS as we close. Now PRS is an extraordinary institution, and your core belief — at least the way PRS was both created and has functioned — is that if legislators had better information, had better research aid and access given to them, had groups of people with whom they could consult (because there's no legislator who can know about everything), and you've conducted all these workshops, you do budget sessions, you provide legislative aides, you provide so much information about the functioning of the institution — the idea is if we do that, then legislative processes become better, parliament will function better, and so on so forth.

Has that thesis played out according to you, given your experience with PRS in the last 20 years? And this is not me asking if PRS has done a good job — PRS has done an extraordinary job — but has it translated into all the things you thought it would translate to?

Madhavan: I come back to the basic premise. We are a representative democracy. So if a representative democracy has to function, the core representative institutions have to function — which is parliament and state legislatures. Let's stick to parliament, because what I'm saying about parliament is equally true there. Just as a shorthand — because if parliament doesn't function, then a democracy fails. So it's a necessary condition.

Now the next necessary condition is for MPs to be able to function. They should have sufficient amount of information, research analysis, support system etc. Now, is that a sufficient condition? No. Is that a necessary condition? Yes.

So what we are trying to do is to provide some of the necessary condition and hope that that is used. Now what has played out is on most bills we do get calls from MPs — and across all parties — trying to understand what are the issues and what are the nuances of the bill. So they actually want to know. This was possibly not the case maybe 20 years ago, simply because they did not have somebody who would be providing them this information in an easily understandable manner.

What we don't do — and they also know that — is to try to say "this is the right thing to do or this is the wrong thing to do." Because it's up to them, and they have to add their ideology on top of that. And they're actually happy about it, because we're not trying to push them in any particular direction. And also we'll be bipartisan or across all parties.

We just try to explain things in a way. The model institution for us is the Congressional Research Service in the US. They do a fantastic, extraordinary job. Of course they are extraordinarily well resourced too. Unfortunately the Indian system doesn't provide a public institution with that sort of mandate or resources or independence.

一个乐观的例子

We have seen them using [PRS] more and more. On a very simple metric: we conduct some of these sessions when parliament is on, where we call an expert to have an interaction with them on a topical issue. For example, in the last one year, given the things that are happening, we had called somebody to come and talk about critical minerals. Someone came about nuclear power — they're very technical. We had one of the former foreign secretaries coming and talk about the situation related to Iran, what's going on, US, Iran, etc.

These are held at 9:00 a.m. Nobody is going to get a re-election benefit because of attending these sessions. Nobody's party is going to give them an extra leg up because they're attending. Why are they coming here? They are coming here because they want to understand. They want to learn. They're not getting any other direct benefit. And they're also not getting any other direct support to learn these things on their own. And they are actually paying an opportunity cost, because one, they are here — they're not meeting constituents lining up outside their house at that 1 hour. So they're actually possibly losing some votes. Who knows?

So that means that they are keen to understand. I still remain quite optimistic about the way things happen. There is enough of a critical mass to stay optimistic.

Shruti: And there is some genuine learning happening, despite all the design flaws and anti-defection and official whip. Whether they're allowed to vote their conscience or not, they have opinions on things.

一个关于反叛法的动人故事

Madhavan: I have to tell you this story. This story is 20 years old, about — there was a bill on amending the Contempt of Court Act. What the original bill said was, it is not contempt of court if what you say is the truth. Then it went to committee etc., it came back and changed to say that it should also not be malafide, it should be in public interest etc.

So there was this MP from CPM called Varkala Radhakrishnan — very senior person, he had been Speaker of Kerala. His party had issued a whip to support the bill. And he was himself a senior lawyer, and he understood the issue, and he felt that if it's true, it's true. Like defamation — why should I have any other parameter?

So he stood up. And it was a beautiful speech. He says, "Sir, I stand to support the bill." Next 8 minutes he demolished the bill. He argued saying everything about it is wrong. And then he says, "Therefore Sir, I support the bill."

So he has abided by the whip and explained what he actually believed. So people have done that.

Shruti: And eventually hopefully we'll get rid of anti-defection.

Madhavan: I think slowly there is — I mean, yesterday somebody wrote in The Hindu about that, or Express, I don't remember, one of the papers. So 15 years ago when you talked about it, people used to say "oh we should strengthen the anti-defection, defections are happening." I think slowly people are realizing the harm it has caused.

Shruti: And because obviously the first instinct is defections are bad, and therefore you should actually have a stronger anti-defection law, right? I mean, that's the natural instinct.

Madhavan: Because most people didn't know that whip was attached to it also.

Shruti: Not only that — unless you dig deep into it, understand the second-order consequences, then you realize the harm it does. On the face of it, "I voted for somebody who's belonging to party — if that person is moving, that person is behaving dishonorably, so it should be kicked out." That's an obvious conclusion.

Thank you so much for doing this. And I'm hoping that in the future you and I don't have to talk about anti-defection anymore because the problem has been resolved, and we'll get into other crazier problems. We didn't talk about delimitation, so hopefully you'll come back and we'll talk more about that.

Madhavan: Thanks. It was fun talking to you, as always.

结尾

Ideas of India is produced by the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. If you enjoy this podcast, please subscribe on Apple Podcast, Spotify or your favorite podcast app. Help us grow by giving us a rating and leaving a review. Follow us on Twitter @srajagopalan and @ideasofindia. Also check out our initiative commemorating 30 years of India's market reforms at the1991project.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、反叛法的悖论：印度议会已变成一个"20人房间"
Madhavan 在对话中提出了一个惊人的思想实验：既然反叛法（Anti-Defection Law）+ 党鞭制度让每个议员都必须按党派指令投票，那么印度实际上不需要 543 人的下议院——只需要一个 20 平米的会议室，每个政党派一个人按按钮就够了。这不是夸张，而是对制度实质的精准还原。
**让人意外的观察**：印度是当今世界上极少数将"反叛法"写进宪法的重要民主国家（其他基本是津巴布韦、塞拉利昂这类国家）。这意味着，从制度形态上看，印度议会的"投票自由度"比英美德法日甚至韩国、以色列都低得多。我们习惯把印度视为"世界最大民主国家"，但**在议会自治的核心维度上，印度其实是全球异类**。
更深的悖论在于：一个原本设计用来"防止腐败叛党"的法律，最终把整个议会变成了"党魁的橡皮图章"——把小恶（个别议员叛变）解决掉的代价，是失去了大善（议会对政府的问责能力）。这与生物学上的"过度免疫反应"（如自身免疫疾病）在结构上惊人地相似：防御系统压制了它本应保护的宿主本身。
---
## 二、跨领域连接：GST 委员会 vs. 加

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1453期】重磅：AI端侧落地！IBM暴雷的真正原因！CPI,PPI分析！✨20260715#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、暴雷背后的"存储挤压效应"：一场被误读的产业链地震

IBM这次暴跌26%，市场普遍解读为"AI冲击软件"，但真正的信号被绝大多数人忽略了——这是一次由存储涨价引发的企业IT预算重新分配事件。企业客户的IT预算是一个"零和游戏池"，当存储芯片涨价把水位推高，主机、软件这些"传统必需品"的份额就被强行挤压。

这里有一个反直觉的洞察：IBM的"暴雷"实际上是存储行业景气度的反向指标。当你看到IBM的主机订单被挤掉，你应该反过来去看美光、SanDisk、闪迪的订单簿——那里才是钱真正流去的地方。这就像水管里的水流，一头堵了，另一头必然涌出。散户在恐慌抛售IBM时，可能正在错过存储链的进场信号。

而更深层的问题投资TALK君点到了但没展开：这100万订单是"延期"还是"永久性打折"？ 这个问题的答案决定了IBM是估值杀还是基本面杀，但管理层刻意回避——回避本身就是答案。

二、CPI与消费的"薛定谔悖论"：一个宏观数据无法解释的裂缝

这里出现了一个让我极度警觉的信号：美国银行公布的消费同比增速从Q1的4.5%再加速到Q2的6%，但同期CPI核心环比却是0%。这两个数据在数学上几乎不可能同时为真。

一个跨领域的类比：这就像相对论和量子力学的矛盾——两个都被验证过，但它们不能同时描述同一个宇宙。要么消费数据里藏着大量"以价换量"（实际单价在跌），要么CPI的商品篮子权重严重滞后于真实消费结构（比如AI订阅、服务型消费没有被充分捕捉）。

我的大胆预测：如果这个裂缝在Q3继续扩大，市场将在某个财报季突然"觉醒"——不是通胀回来了，而是通胀的测量方式过时了。这才是美联储未来两年真正的定价难题——不是加不加息，而是他们用来判断加息的那把尺子，本身是否还准确。

三、端侧AI落地的"苹果PC轮回"：硬件更新周期的隐形按钮已经被按下

Prime Intellect把千问模型压缩到能在iPhone上运行——这条被淹没在噪音中的新闻，是过去两年AI叙事最重要的转折点。

但真正的洞察在于一个跨领域连接：这次的端侧AI落地，本质上是在重演2010年iPhone对PC产业的"反向替代"逻辑，只是这次反过来了。当年是"云端算力+瘦客户端"击败了本地PC，现在则是"厚客户端+本地算力"要重新分走云端的一杯羹。

而这里有一个几乎所有人都忽略的连锁效应：端侧AI越强，硬件规格越卷，硬件规格越卷，Apple/高通/联发科的ASP（平均售价）越高，进而带动整个手机BOM表向存储和先进制程倾斜——这又回到了第一段说的IBM存储挤压效应。

闭环出现了：IBM的暴雷（存储挤压）、消费数据的反常（AI服务未被计入）、端侧AI落地（硬件更新周期）——这三条看似不相关的新闻，其实是同一个"AI物理层重构"的三个截面。市场把它们分开报道，恰恰是最大的认知盲区。谁能把这三条线连起来看，谁就抓住了未来18个月的主叙事。

📝 转录文本

开场与市场回顾

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小涛。

这两天的市场上窜下跳，从指数去看其实波动并不大，如果剥开指数去看背后的一些走势，芯片板块、硬件这一块回调的幅度比较大。在过去一个多月的时间里边，我在各式各样的场合、在视频里面、在直播里边一直和大家分享——你大仓位这些硬件板块一定要做好移动止盈，一个非常简单的、被动型的防守。

那么如果指数从最高点回调了10%、15%，就止赢一部分的仓位，剩下的我们认为基本面没有改变的可以继续持有，但是没有必要满仓，甚至于是加杠杆持有这些标的。那这一波回调会非常非常的难受。所以最近我也看到在社交媒体上，有一些人就是"盈亏同源"——你怎么赚来的钱，现在就怎么亏回去。所以很多时候希望我们即使在做价值投资的时候，围绕估值这个角度去出发，进行仓位上的一些控制，就通过仓位去控制一下风险，好吗？

今日议题预告

那今天我聊一下周二和周三公布的宏观数据：周二的CPI数据、周三的PPI数据。然后我聊一下和财报相关的，我们在财报季度了嘛，那聊一下周二盘前发布财报——银行财报主要是通过美国银行的财报去看一下消费者的整体的一个健康状况。然后聊一下周二IBM他们提前发布了财报，那在这份财报里面你可以认为是暴雷吧，那为什么暴雷、他们聊了哪些内容、哪一些是我们应该从公告里边去获取的一些信息，而并不是跟着市场的这个节奏去做，教大家如何去理解一下IBM的这一份公告。然后聊一下周三盘前发布财报的阿斯麦。

最后有一条新闻和大家分享，这条新闻是我认为在最近这段时间——就最近这段时间噪音开始多了，有没有觉得无论是宏观也好、芯片这边硬件也好等等——在那么多的一些噪音里面，我认为有一条新闻是和基本面相关的，这条新闻是我们应该去抓住的，因为这预示着未来在端侧这一块的AI非常有可能将会落地，好吧。

那我就废话不多说，马上开始今天内容。在开始之前，大家记得留言、点赞、加转发，让更多的人可以看到我们这期内容。

一、资产价格回顾与利率预期

请快速回顾一下周三资产价格的走势。美元指数和美债收入都出现了下跌，周二也出现了下跌，那主要回馈的是周二的CPI比市场预期要好，然后周三的PPI数据也比市场预期要好。那随着利率的一个下跌，7月份和9月份降息这个概率也下跌了。

周日的一些视频里面和大家分享，我认为市场定价了7月份50%概率这个降息过于悲观了，就7月份要去降息的话过早了，那最早的可能性是在——最早第一次降息的可能性是在9月份。但其实退后一步去看，7月份降息还是9月份降息其实没有那么的重要。为什么？是因为两年期的美债收入，债券市场早就已经定价了降息了。所以最终美联储降还是不降，等到美联储会议那一天，市场其实就已经紧缩了一个多月了，对吗？

从上一次凯文·沃什的美联储会议之后，市场就已经开始定价降息了。所以从那一个时间点开始，市场就已经紧缩了。降息的目的是通过紧缩的货币政策去把通胀压下来。那所以即使美联储还没有降息，也不知道什么样的时间点会进行降息，但是市场因为凯文·沃什的讲话已经紧缩了，这才是凯文·沃什所关心的，这才是最重要的一点，好吧。

那随着这两天的数据出来之后，起码7月份降息的这个概率现在已经跌到了10%几，那基本上7月份是不大可能降息了，9月份降息的概率还是在50%左右。

二、三大指数与板块分化

然后我们看一下三大指数，在周三出现了反弹：标普0.4%、纳指0.6%、道指0.3%。那在盘中的波动还相对会比较大一些。随着美元、美债收入的一个下跌，黄金出现了小幅反弹。我认为黄金这个反弹可能会持续，因为这两天确实宏观的这个风险、降息这个风险要小了很多了，但是黄金这块的反馈并不是那么的多。原油反弹了0.3%，VIX出现了5%的下跌，比特币和罗素2000都出现了反弹。

总体来说，周三的市场都出现了小幅反弹，但我们看一下板块这边分歧非常的大。有下跌的板块：能源板块、公共事业、科技板块下跌的幅度都是将近1%。

保险板块的周期切换

保险板块下跌了3.1%，为什么？因为在周三盘前PGR（Progressive）他们发布了第二季度这个财报，看到了什么？看到了6月份他们总体的这个赔付率比市场预期要高了一些，来到了90%。

那是不是意味着接下去保险行业、财险行业会进入另外一个周期？什么是另外一个周期？就是以前是涨价，涨价之后你会发现他们的赔付率会变得非常的好；当他们涨价的这个幅度开始放缓之后，赔付率就跟上了；赔付率跟上了，你的财报自然就不好看。所以保险板块进入了一个赔付率上升的一个周期，然后直到上升到保险公司开始亏钱，然后再次进入下一个涨价周期。

因为我知道很多散户手里拥有像Lemonade这类的公司，我说过像Lemonade这类的公司，你可以看好它的一个基本面，可以看好AI对于它的基本面上的一些影响或者利好，但是这一类公司没有经历过一个非常像样的整个保险行业的一个下行周期。所以这个周期是一个非常好的去考验Lemonade到底有没有这个赔付率上的一个优势。那么如果其他的保险公司赔付率都在上升，反而它没有上升的话，那厉害；那么那如果和其他的保险公司一起上升的话，那就说明它还是一家和传统保险公司一样走周期的一家公司，好吧。

芯片与大科技的资金轮动

芯片板块在周三也出现了2.2%的下跌，很多硬件板块、很多硬件下跌的会比较多一些。那主要周三非常强势——大科技上涨了2%、3%，非常的强势，等权指数下跌了0.2%。

那大科技在过去一周的这个反弹4.7%，那么等权指数只有0.4%，也再次拉近了大科技和等权指数之间的这个回报差。这个回报差其实我经常在视频里面聊——at one point这个回报差高达12%、13%。我记得YTD大科技是负的，那自从我那天聊完之后，我说大科技你还是可以去布局，从那天开始你看大科技现在反弹到——现在YTD来到5.1%，也拉近了和等权指数之间的这个回报差，现在回到了6%左右。

也就是说在过去这一段时间——你看过去一个月嘛，对吧——这两者之间的一个回报差被拉近了6%。那而相反在过去一个月里面，你可以看到芯片板块是下跌了6.9%。所以在过去一个月比较明显出现了这个轮动，资金从这些芯片板块返回到了大科技这边。

三、CPI数据解读

然后我们来看一下CPI数据。那CPI市场预计——我只看核心环比，市场预期是0.2%，然后最后的数据是0%。这一组数据非常非常的好。

我在昨天的直播里面（在App里面直播里面）我有聊到，我说这个数据本身是一个非常小概率的事件，可能就1%这样的可能性，但我们还是看到了这样的一个数据。然后我去细致的看了一下那份CPI报告，我里边几乎挑不出任何的毛病，就这是一份非常完美的CPI数据。如果这样的一个数据持续下去的话，那基本上美联储压制通胀的这一个目标就已经达成了。

那么你环比2%——你当然，这个我不觉得它可以持续啊。因为你环比如果0%的话，也就代表你是没有通胀、零通胀，对吗？所以这个趋势——环比增速0%——一定是不可持续的，那这是第一点。但无论如何，这是一个非常好的一份CPI报告。

银行财报揭示的消费真相

但我并不认为我们应该去线性外推。为什么？是因为周二的盘前几家银行发布财报，其中包括了JPMorgan、包括了美国银行，两家公司都表达了几点：

第一点，坏账这一块都比他们原本的预期要好，也就是坏账率比原本银行的预期要好。

第二点，消费这一块，我们看美国银行第一季度的同比增速是4.5%。在发布财报之前，我就和大家说一个magic number、神奇数据就是5%——如果消费的同比增速可以保持在5%，这就代表消费者完全没有任何放缓。在第二季度同比增速6%，也就是说在第二季度消费这一块的增速反而出现了一个再加速。

这个再加速并不是没办法去理解。那么因为毕竟4月份川普pivot之后，那整个战争出现了反转之后，4月份、5月份股市又涨得那么的好，所以大家消费者都出来消费了。第一季度没有因为战争出现非常明显的放缓，但是第二季度因为战争风险的这个消退，反而消费者出现了一个再增速。所以第二季度6%的这个同比增速非常非常的高，是我在过去几年里面都没有看到过。

所以我就没有办法把消费增速那么的强劲和通胀——6月份的通胀环比增速0%去reconcile（协调），你互相之间没有办法去验证。当然你又问我，我更加相信什么？我当然更加相信美国银行的财报。那么美国银行财报消费者的这个数据非常的准确，它一是代表了整个经济到底有没有问题——美国的经济GDP 6%实际上来自于消费，那所以消费没有问题，GDP就不会有问题。那第二点就是这一个消费里面，它是名义消费的增速6%，那包括了通胀，它里面也包括了通胀。那你消费增速那么的强，我没有办法去想象通胀环比增速现在是0%。

所以我认为这组数据本身绝对是值得去乐观的，但是没有必要过于乐观，更不应该把这组数据本身去做一个线性外推。

四、PPI数据与降息路径

然后周三的PPI数据呢，PPI数据也非常的好，甚至于比数据里边所显示出来的更好。什么意思？PPI数据我们一般可以看这个Final Demand，Final Demand是把一些乱七八糟的东西全部去掉之后是一个比较真实的一个数据，这个Final Demand对于整个GDP的这个影响也是最直接的。

市场预期环比增速是0%，我们现在看到数据是负的0.3%，就是比市场预期低了0.3%。同时上一个月的这个数据环比增速还进行了下修——通常来说当上一个数据进行了下修、而且这个下修的幅度很大（环比1.1%下修到了0.6%），通常当上一个数据环比增速进行下修之后，这一个月的数据容易超出上个月的数据——这是一个数学上的问题，这并不是什么逻辑上的一些问题。但非但在上个月下修的这个前提下、反弹没有出现前提上的反弹没有出现上升，它反而更加出现了下降，还是一个负增长，就说明这一个月的PPI数据也非常的好。

所以PPI数据有了、CPI数据也有了，这一个月的核心PCE的数据非常有可能会非常的低。至于是什么原因，我并不知道，我也没有看到任何消费者的这个放缓。所以我还是认为数据本身非常的好，这也是几乎出乎所有市场意外的一组数据。

债券市场对于降息这个语气自然就下来了，非常好的这个结果，债券市场也非常欣喜看到这样的结果，所有人都非常欣喜看到这样的一个结果。但是因为银行的财报公布了消费者的增速仍然非常的强劲，我并不会过度乐观，就是你知道OK数据是这样的，但是我不会去做一个线性外推。

降息的这个概率像我前面说的——7月份降息这个概率已经跌到了10%，就几乎10%，然后再在29日会议之前的话，不出意外的话应该会跌到10%以下，就其实也没有太大的一个往下跌的这个空间了，5%左右。那么你永远不可能是0%，因为市场永远有一部分人会去对冲7月份有可能会出现降息，但绝对是一个非常非常小概率的事件。9月份市场现在还是预期有55%的可能性会进行降息，然后在2026年今年年底之前还会有一次降息，这个可能性就是——总共加一起啊，今年会降息一次，就是现在是这个预期。

在周日的时候，最高点是在降息1.6至1.7次左右。所以在过去几天的时间里面，因为这两组数据的公布——PPI数据和CPI数据，降息的概率、整个26年降息概率从1.74降到了一次，降了0.6次至0.7次左右。

五、IBM"暴雷"的真相

OK，我们再换一个主题——IBM。那IBM在周二盘前是发布了一个公告，那是提前公布了他们的这个财报。一般来说，公司提前发财报都不是一件好事。除了一家公司如果股价真的跌得非常的厉害，然后财报本身非常的好，有些时候为了提前释放这个利好的消息，公司会提前公布财报——通常提前公布财报都是糟糕的。

那IBM把他们的第二季度数据公布出来之后直接跌了26%，然后在周三继续出现了下跌。那这是我昨天在X上写的一篇文章，我看完之后在第二天在周二的早上我就写了这篇文章。

数据与官方口径

我们先看一些数据，先看一些客观、或者他们在公告里面所想要表达的：市场原本的预期营收是17.9个billion，最后的数据17.2，直接营收这边miss的5%不到。所以营收是一个非常大的一个miss。

那公司说到你营收的miss的话，公司就给了一个理由，他说我们内部销售分配出了问题——什么问题？就是我们看到企业把他们的IT这一块的花费优先安排到了服务器、存储。为什么？因为存储——他们背后的甲方在给IT budget、给IT花费的时候，他们看到存储这块涨价涨得非常厉害，所以优先先去买存储、先去囤，囤完之后再去考虑要不要把额外、要不要把以后的这些IT花费再次买回到IBM、就再次花到IBM身上——还不知道。就这个是不确定的。现在他们确定的是他们要去囤存储，因为存储芯片涨价涨得非常的厉害啊，所以优先把IT花费给了服务器、给了存储，而不是他们Z大型的一个主机。

那因为他们Z大型的这个主机这个硬件本身，原本他们预计在一季度这个订单是可以签下来，但最终这个订单没有签下来。而因为这个订单没有签下来，硬件的订单没有签下来，连带着和主机配套的这些软件销售也不如预期。

新闻叙事的误导

那市场的一个解读就非常的奇怪，这是我们必须要自己看完公告、然后你才能够理解公告到底表达什么。但是我们如果单纯的去看新闻，新闻——我很多时候我和大家强调过很多次，新闻很多时候是从结果出发去解释、去给出一个合理的一个解释，但往往很多时候价格不代表是一个合理的解释。

在周二的时候，这条新闻出来之后，IGV板块出现了下跌。那然后路透社马上一篇新闻出来说IBM说AI对于软件这一块可能影响到了他们的一个营收。但实际上公告里面想要表达的是因为存储挤掉了他们在硬件这一块的一个销售，包括他们的内部的一个销售其实没有意识到这个问题，但没有意识到这个问题已经晚了。所以因为销售没有意识到这个问题，因为硬件受到了影响，所以连带他们的软件也受到了影响。而他们的软件其实并不是传统的一个SaaS，他们的软件本身并不会被AI所破坏。

那所以看完公告再去看一些新闻，你会觉得非常的无厘头——这个新闻你不知道他是怎么做的这个理解。那你唯一可以去解释是因为价格出现了下跌，所以新闻这么去写。那然后散户之间就开始传起来说：哎，软件板块又受到了下跌，因为IBM给了一个比较悲观的这个预期。但实际上你去看的话，IBM的这个公告本身和软件其实几乎没有太大的一些关系。

的确，他们自己的配套软件受到了影响，但是他们自己配套软件这个影响主要是他们硬件受到了影响。然后他们自己的能源和存储服务37%——是他们最好的一个季度。所以存储和能源这一块销售还是非常的好。然后他们也提到了客户被快速变化的网络风险、网络安全这块给分心了，所以在周二网络安全股票也出现了上涨。所以周二很多市场里面的走势就是和IBM的这份公告是相关的。

股东视角：延期还是打折？

这边是公告里面所表达的，但是更加重要的是我们在看这类公告，比如说如果我们是股东的话，我们需要看什么？

我们需要看：因为通常你说一家公司，你的CEO、你的管理者告诉你——哎，这一个季度营收不如预期，是因为我下面的一些客户他们把自己的IT花费优先给了其他。那我自然的一个问题就是OK，你优先给了其他，给了服务器、给了存储，可以，那这个订单你是不是丢掉了？还是说这个订单的甲方，他们未来比如说原本这个订单是100万，那现在是不是这个订单还是100万，是100万延后了？还是说原本100万的这个订单最终我们只会达到50万或者60万，剩下的40万和50万被我们甲方分配到其他地方去，而这一个变化是永久性的？

这才是决定了能不能够影响到基本面。但是管理层在这一次公告里面没有主动去聊这个问题。那我对于这个问题的一个解读就是可能是一半一半、就介于两者之间。什么叫介于两者之间？就是并不一定这100万的订单就完全没有了，最终可能就是40万和50万；而不是这个订单往后延——如果只是这个订单往后延的话，对于基本面是没有任何的影响。

比如说像在阿斯麦身上，经常看到很多订单是往后延，往后延的这个订单无论是因为他们的交付也好、怎么样也好，这个订单本身往后延，因为你这个营收无非就是营收晚了一个季度呗，或者晚了两个季度拿到。但是这个是100万，你这个营收不是晚了一个季度、两个季度，而是你这个营收打折扣了，你原本的这个营收没有那么多了。

那所以这个问题才是在财报会议里边——我相信财报会议里边一定会有分析师去问这个问题——那作为如果作为IBM的这个股东的话，这个问题的结果将会决定这家公司基本面是不是出了问题。明显呢在周二出现了非常大幅度下跌的情况下、周三继续下跌，起码市场的这个担心我认为是合理的。

六、阿斯麦（ASML）财报与估值

OK然后我们再换一个话题，换一家公司，我们聊一下阿斯麦。阿斯麦是在周三盘前发布财报，财报会议我听了，我们没有太多想总结。

需求信号：跳过27年直接排28年产能

他们在27年扩产30%、28年继续扩产30%。财报会议里面聊到——几乎在以前很多年以前，我们从来没有看到过——是我们现在还在26年，我们已经在思考2028年的这个产能了，27年都已经跳过了，就从来没有看到过背后的需求那么的大。

包括有传出来阿斯麦要进行涨价。在我录视频之前，我看到有消息传出来说台积电可能不是非常的满意。台积电最终一定会妥协的，就是这两者之间肯定是——你要涨价，你一定是和你客户去说，就是你先给一个5%的一个涨价幅度，然后你客户说太高太高了，然后你就"哎行吧，我最后给你3%"，一定是这样的。而不是直接丢给客户说"哎，我涨你3%"，对吧？中间一定是有一个谈判的一个过程。

因为毕竟台积电到现在——我应该是台积电——仍然是阿斯麦最大的这个买家，现在英特尔也是阿斯麦这个买家。那作为最大的这个买家，你一定是有一个讲价权在那边的。阿斯麦作为一个开价的时候——我也要涨价啊，一定是先给一个比较高的价格，台积电谈一谈，然后稍微把这个价格往下降一些。但是涨价几乎是逃不了，因为阿斯麦是有绝对的这个定价权在那边，他说涨价就得涨价啊。

估值区间的历史规律

所以来看一下估值。其实我很久没有看阿斯麦的这个估值了。那2026年EPS这个增速市场预期29%，27年和28年预期在43%和25%。那我算了一下，根据现在56倍的这个PE，其实阿斯麦的PEG ratio把26年全部算进来，阿斯麦这个PEG ratio在1.7倍，其实并不高。

这张图我们如果很想去比较的话——其实这张图我放出来之后，大家现在看多了之后（因为我经常和大家分享个股的一些估值、包括指数的一些估值），横向做比较非常的有必要，因为你会发现你能够非常简单、非常直接的就能够找出这个规律。

这是阿斯麦过去5年的这个估值。我上面画的这条线——46倍的这个forward PE，和下面这条线——22倍的这个forward PE。不难找出这个规律吧对吗？上面46倍是一个估值的一个相对比较高的这个区间，高于46倍甚至到了46倍、甚至在45倍以上，就应该要小心，可以做一些减仓的行为，如果仓位太大的话。

听清楚我的假设——一定是如果仓位太大，你去做一些减仓；如果仓位不大或者你还想加仓的话，那没有必要在45倍的时候你就去减仓，因为你知道短期之内估值过高了，但是你仓位也不够，那仓位不够的情况下没有必要去减仓，反而你是在等它如果跌下来估值再回来的时候，你右侧可以选择继续加仓。那所以很多时候估值再高啊，到底是减仓还是继续持有，某种程度是决定于我们现在的仓位是多少、我们还想不想上加仓。

苹果仓位的思考插曲

好比我自己苹果的仓位，我现在都不用看我知道苹果的估值现在很高，可能是在最近这段时间的一个相对比较高的位置。因为苹果破新高了嘛，那么而且又不用发财报，现在就单纯这个价格破新高，那苹果的估值在一个高位。但是我不认为我自己苹果的仓位是够了，我一直和大家说我想加苹果。

上次我和大家分享是估值到了一个合理的区间，没有苹果的可以去加仓、可以去建仓，但是我可能还在等一个技术面上如果暂停国下，那天下跌了6%，我说可以再等一下，那天我说整个K线非常的丑陋，那么我等如果再往下下跌，我马上就加。最后他根本就没有给我任何的机会，所以我完全没有加到，但是它这段时间的一个反弹非常的大。

那所以我试图去找到一个基本面估值和技术面之间的这个共振，结果我技术面这边没有满足。那在我那天视频里面我有聊过，如果没有满足它反弹的话，我也可以接受，但它如果跌下去、如果跌下去的话，我就加仓。但是我说从没有仓位、要去建仓的这个角度去看的话，已经完全进入了一个净建仓的一个阶段了，好吧。

回到阿斯麦估值

所以回到阿斯麦的这个估值，上面46倍非常明显的一个上区间，下面22倍也是一个非常明显的一个上下区间。上一次跌到22倍是在2022年的4月份、3月份、4月份——当时最低点是什么？当然是配合了关税，另外一点是配合了中国那边说他们也能够造这些光刻机新闻。全部加在一起，阿斯麦22倍forward PE的这个估值其实已经非常的低了。

那么当然这些东西都是看后视镜的，你只有在看后视镜你会发现——哦，原来25年的估值那么的低。真的在25年的时候，多数人是不敢买的，为什么不敢买？22倍的这个估值价格也会相对的比较低，当时一定有新闻告诉你阿斯麦的基本面可能出现了问题，然后我们看了这些新闻之后一定会做一个线性外推，那么认为它的估值不可能回归，但实际上25年、26年我看到了AI的这个落地之后，阿斯麦的价格包括估值也出现了大幅度的反弹。

所以这段时间估值从46倍回调到了39.5倍，我认为合理。很多人不乐意，对吧，阿斯麦那么好的财报凭什么又是高开低走、觉得有些问题？就估值上的一个回调很正常。那上区间46倍、下区间22倍、中位数36倍，那么如果跌到了、阿斯麦的基本面没有问题、一直没有问题、管理层又非常的好，所以他如果跌到了36倍，你想开仓阿斯麦的话就完全可以开仓了。那么而在现在39.5倍这样的一个位置呢，我个人认为就是继续等下去。

七、Bloom Energy（BE）建仓分享

讲到估值，稍微带一嘴，Bloom Energy（BE）今天我开通了这个标的。这个标题是——我在年终的时候买过，后来在3月份的时候被卖掉了，被我在整理仓位的时候被卖掉了。这个标的其实是卖飞了、妥妥卖飞，当时是130、140吧。现在继续买回来，仓位不到2%的这个仓位。

那从估值、从PEG ratio这个角度去看，我认为现在已经进入了一个击球区间了。从最高点回到了将近40%不到一些，1.9的这个PEG ratio，未来三年的EPS这个增速非常的高——171、107、64，对吧？所以即使他只做到现在市场给了这个预期的话，匹配性的这个估值现在看起来估值非常的贵——210倍的这个PE，我认为是合理的，就起码进入了一个合理的一个区间了。因为它未来的这个增速非常的高，那么如果能够超出、如果财报能够超出现在市场这个预期的话，那这一个PE就会继续出现下降。

八、端侧AI落地：被淹没的关键新闻

最后呢我们聊一下——嗯是我认为在最近这段时间噪音里面非常值得去关注的一条消息。Prime Intellect他们宣布了把千问这个模型他们压缩完之后，压缩完之后的这个模型已经可以在iPhone上运营了。

这意味着什么？这意味着端侧这一块的AI终于可以落地。而端侧这一块的AI落地完之后，才会有了C端对于AI的这个真正的一个落地，或者C端真正每一个消费者真正可以感受到AI。

因为C端这一块、端侧这一块的AI落地：一，你不需要去调用任何的API，就不会有任何的成本。对于大多数的人来说，他们能够接触到AI、愿意去接受AI，一定是以免费的这个方式去接受的，或者说看管控的这种版本。

AI Agent的必然路径

包括未来的AI Agent、AI智能体，我在前段时间有分享过我的观点，我说AI Agent要落地的话，token的价格一定要下来，而闭源模型你几乎是没有办法拿它去做AI Agent，因为AI Agent根本就调用不起闭源模型——闭源模型的API价格太高了。有了开源模型之后已经看到了一个比较好的一个趋势，那现在更往前推一步，你把开源模型压缩完之后放到端侧这一块，你不需要调用任何的API，你没有任何调用API的成本，就你本地的这个AI模型已经非常的厉害。

那么所以我们可以想象到，未来如果这一个压缩版本之后的一个AI模型厉害到的一个程度可以作为Agent，那每个人的手机里面都会有一个AI智能体，而这个AI智能体本身就已经会非常的强大。

硬件更新周期的重启

那么那时候我们还是将会面临一个——我认为所有的手机端、硬件端还是会有一笔非常大的一个更新。就像在两三年前，当时AMD当时苏妈在聊AI PC的时候，很多人都认为你为啥要AI PC？AI PC有什么意义吗？那么因为当时没有任何的一个AI落地，你AI PC上你硬件堆上去，你没有AI，你对硬件没啥用。

但是现在反过来了，那么现在如果这个AI模型已经强大到了——你压缩版本之后还是压缩完之后还是非常的强大，且你可以在端侧这一块运行的话——那对于硬件这一块的需求又直接上去了。因为你的硬件拉的越高，硬件的GPU、CPU拉的越高之后，你自然可以运行的这个模型、本地模型就会越厉害；而你本地模型越厉害，你能够帮助每一个消费者或者每一个个体能够做到事情就越多。那么所以有了本地的这个AI模型之后，AI PC变得非常的合理，包括未来的AI手机也会变得非常的合理。

所以我认为这条消息绝对是值得关注的，因为这条消息本身也验证了AI在端侧这一块的一个落地。且现在这家公司本身和苹果是有一个开拓型的一个合作之间的关系，没有非常的深度，现在大家都在探索。为什么苹果要和他们探索？如果他们真的能够帮助AI在端侧这一块落地的话，已经可以压缩到一个非常好的一个模型的话，对于苹果来说是makes perfect sense。

那同样的在端侧这一块模型的一个落地，也不代表未来AI在云这一块的需求就没有了——就完全是两个不同的一个市场，或者说不同的一个需求、满足不同需求的一个市场。那就好比在云上运行的开源模型和闭源模型，不是说你有了开源模型之后闭源模型就完全没有它那个意义了，每一个模型一定都有它的一个意义、有它的一个定价、有它可以去解决的一个应用场景。

结尾：赞助与告别

最后是活动这边，今天为大家带来的是Investing.com。那这个平台本身免费的数据里面就有很多有用的东西，宏观的数据啊、财报数据啊、财报会议啊都有。那今天要和大家聊的是他们的一个AI选股器，这边有三个以前非常成功的例子，比如SMCI、Apple、Eleven那。

这一次7月份的活动优惠的幅度、折扣是非常的大，高达60%。总共有两档：一档是Pro，一档Pro Plus。加币是9.49（Pro），Pro Plus的话是24.49，那换成美金的话差不多7.5美金左右，好吧。

大家如果对于Investing.com他们这一次订阅感兴趣的话，可以在置顶的留言里边找到订阅的连接。

那今天这期内容就到这边，我们下期见，嗯。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、暴雷背后的"存储挤压效应"：一场被误读的产业链地震
IBM这次暴跌26%，市场普遍解读为"AI冲击软件"，但真正的信号被绝大多数人忽略了——**这是一次由存储涨价引发的企业IT预算重新分配事件**。企业客户的IT预算是一个"零和游戏池"，当存储芯片涨价把水位推高，主机、软件这些"传统必需品"的份额就被强行挤压。
这里有一个反直觉的洞察：**IBM的"暴雷"实际上是存储行业景气度的反向指标**。当你看到IBM的主机订单被挤掉，你应该反过来去看美光、SanDisk、闪迪的订单簿——那里才是钱真正流去的地方。这就像水管里的水流，一头堵了，另一头必然涌出。散户在恐慌抛售IBM时，可能正在错过存储链的进场信号。
而更深层的问题投资TALK君点到了但没展开：**这100万订单是"延期"还是"永久性打折"？** 这个问题的答案决定了IBM是估值杀还是基本面杀，但管理层刻意回避——**回避本身就是答案**。
## 二、CPI与消费的"薛定谔悖论"：一个宏观数据无法解释的裂缝
这里出现了一个让我极度警觉的信号：**美国银行公布的消费同比增速从Q1的4.5%再加速到Q2的6%，但同期C

item.title: [Zhang Xiaojun Podcast] 146. 对Physical Intelligence柯丽一鸣4小时访谈：Pi的开源模型研究，机器人的江湖、族谱与主角
item.dc:creator: Zhang Xiaojun Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、令人意外的观察：写小说和做机器人是同一件事的两面

柯丽一鸣同时是研究员和网文作者，这看似是"理性与感性"的分裂人格，但深挖下去会发现一个反直觉的真相——她做的是同一件事：都是在探索"生产力剧变后，人与自我的关系如何重构"。

她说自己是"懒惰主义者"，把强化学习当作让机器人自己练习、把自己从劳动中解放的工具；同时她写科幻小说，探索的核心命题也是"生产力爆炸后人做什么"。这不是巧合——她其实在用两种介质做同一个田野调查：白天在硅谷的实验室里，她用代码"实验"未来；晚上在网文里，她用叙事"预演"未来。研究是她的forward pass（前向计算），小说是她的backward pass（反向传播）——两者共同构成一个闭环：现实中做出的东西反哺想象，想象中推演的社会形态又倒逼她思考自己在做什么。

这解释了一个更深的悖论：为什么她说自己是"悲观主义者"，却每天热情地推进robot brain？因为她根本不需要"意义"来驱动自己——她的驱动来自贪婪算法和动态规划的辩证：每一步做局部最优不等于全局最优，所以九分就够，剩下一分留给意外。这种"结构性懒惰"，恰恰是这个时代最稀缺的清醒。

二、跨领域连接：机器人史 = 自然语言处理史 + 12年

访谈里有个被淹没的关键洞察值得放大——Pi的技术路径几乎在复刻NLP从2016到2023的完整演进史，只是滞后了大约十年。

看这条平行线： - NLP的"传统派" 靠语言学专家分词、句法分析 → 机器人的"传统派" 靠工程师手工调参、规划路径 - NLP的转折点：数据驱动 + Transformer干掉了语言学 → 机器人的转折点：模仿学习 + VLA大模型正在干掉运动规划 - NLP的可验证任务（代码、数学）解决了reward hacking → 机器人现在正卡在"如何定义'叠好衣服'"的reward function上 - NLP的scaling law先在语言涌现 → 机器人的scaling law现在在"多少个AirBnB才够泛化"上被反复验证

这个类比可怕的地方在于：如果历史真的重演，那么机器人领域的"GPT-3时刻"（能力涌现、烧钱军备赛开始）、"ChatGPT时刻"（普通人开始使用）、以及"Cursor时刻"（应用层大爆发）的时间点其实是可以粗略推算的。柯丽自己也说了"Pi 0是能力，Pi 0.5是泛化，Pi 0.6是性能"——这个三段式，恰好对应GPT-1、GPT-2、GPT-3的发展逻辑。

更妙的隐喻是：Jitendra那句"Robotics is far too important to be left for roboticists"（机器人太重要了，不能只交给机器人专家）——这句话在NLP领域已经被验证了一遍（大模型的突破者恰恰不是传统语言学家），现在正在机器人领域重演。Pi的年轻研究员们不是"机器人科班"，正是他们的优势而非劣势。

三、被忽视的系统性真相：机器人的终极形态不是人形，是"忒修斯之船"

大家都在争论"人形 vs 非人形"这个二元对立，但柯丽在访谈末尾丢出了一个几乎所有人都没注意到的第三条路——未来的机器人是"动态装配的忒修斯之船"。

她提到Pi内部有个现象：一台机器人在不断维修中，所有零件都被换过，编号还是原来的编号，但已经不是原来那台机器人了。她说"要不要给它换个新名字？"——这个看似技术管理层面的小问题，实际上戳破了整个人形机器人叙事的假设。

深层的启示：人形机器人的信仰是"世界是为人设计的，所以模仿人最通用"。但柯丽指出一个更颠覆的可能——汽车不是自然界存在的形态，但整个世界的公路都是为汽车而改造的。如果机器人足够有用，人类会为它改造环境，而不是反过来。

这就把机器人的终极形态问题从形态选择（form selection） 变成了形态演化（form evolution）：一台机器人的手坏了，换成螺丝刀；要煮咖啡，换成温度计手指；要爬楼梯，加两条辅助腿。这不是科幻，而是硬件模块化 + 通用brain的自然推论。

跨领域连接：这个思路和生物学里的"内共生理论"（Endosymbiotic Theory）惊人相似——线粒体最初是独立的细菌，被真核细胞"吞入"后变成器官。未来的机器人可能不是被"设计"出来的，而是被"组装+进化"出来的——每个功能模块都有独立起源，通过通用brain这个"细胞质"结合。柯丽说"我最想做的是宠物机器人"，其实这不是玩笑：宠物是最不需要模仿人形的物种，恰恰可能成为通用brain落地的最佳载体。

一个令人不安的推论：如果这个方向是对的，那么Tesla Optimus这种All-in人形的路线，可能不是走向未来，而是走向一个"局部最优但全局次优"的死胡同——就像柯丽说的贪婪算法。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Hello everyone, I'm Xiaojun. Today, our guest is a researcher of Pai, a member of the Physical Intelligence, a company that focuses on the research of robot brains. It was established only two years ago, and its estimated value is more than $5 billion. It is one of the most popular unicorns on this track. Some industry people expect it to become an open AI in the field of robots.

Klee is mainly responsible for the study of strengthening learning in PIE, and is also one of the authors of PIE's core paper. In addition to research, he also pursues to find balance between work and human expression, so he also writes novels in his spare time, which makes today's chat more interesting.

In this program, we will talk about the characters of the popular Chinese characters in the robot world. The global robot team is very concerned about the work of Py in the robot brain. Kri also explained in detail their research ideas and technical details from Py0 to Py0.5 to Py0.6. But because we recorded this episode relatively early, we haven't been able to get to their latest model, Pi 0.7. I will add the guest's technical explanation about this work in the show notes.

Next, I will interview Koli Yiming. I look forward to our joint progress in 2026 with AI.

精彩片段预告

"我们不做人型。因为他们说我不会来如果我做人型。你知道？"

"What I've always wanted to do is that robots can make themselves. I think this is a development of robots. It may be a milestone. Because it's like a race. It's a continuation. It can be a kind of reproduction through itself. I think robots can assemble and make themselves. It's a reflection of reproduction."

"The first issue is Piling. Its keyword should be ability. The second one is Pi 0.5. Its keyword is 'fantasy'. The latest one is Pi 0.6. Its keyword is 'performance'."

"Do other people in your company pay attention to the development of Chinese robots? I think they all pay attention. This is an indispensable part."

一、研究员与小说家的双重身份

Our guest today is K a researcher from the physical intelligence team of the GuiGu robot company. K is a well-known company in the exploration of the GuiGu robot brain. But two days ago, I talked to K and found that he is not only a researcher, but also an online writer.

K, why don't you introduce yourself first? 你觉得做机器人和写小说有什么共通之处啊?

I think there are some creative needs. For example, this robot has completed many tasks and is not good enough. You need to think of some new ways. And in novels, maybe the story you really want to write hasn't been written yet. You also need to create it.

很多时候需要一些比较天马行空这样的想法。另外也是有一种执行力吧。像我在做研究过程中,其实经常是有了一个想法以后,慢慢地把这个想法落地,中间需要一些工程这样的,写代码这样的事情。而且小说有的时候是有了一个命题,就为了让读者感受到这个命题,开始一个字一个字地在那里码。

I'm curious, what novels do you usually write?

I usually write more sci-fi novels. This is also one of my favorite novel categories. I think sci-fi novels must have something to do with the era of GuiGu's current operation. 因为在科幻小说中,比较痴迷的一些类型都会涉及到两个命题吧。第一个是生产力变化以后人们的生活是怎么改变的。第二个命题是在和我们的社会风暴很不一样的这种未来科幻的社会里面人与人之间的关系是怎么样的。

因为我觉得在第一个命题上面可能和我的工作会更相关一些吧。工作时候就经常会考虑到我们做这些人工智能的研究,做这个机器人是一种自动化。如果你能够把很多现在人做的事情给自动化的提升一下效率以后,人去做什么样的事情,是一个其实每天都会想的一个命题。

第二种呢,I think the relationship between people is a constant theme. When you write novels, you may read other people's novels, and you may get allergic to the thoughts of people who lived in different worlds thousands of years ago. I think there should be some human nature in the future of the technology society that is changing in terms of productivity.

二、AI时代的人际关系变革

在AI时代,当机器人即将进入我们的生活,你觉得人会发生什么变化?

I think this is... 我觉得这个过程中还是没有具体的结论。Because when it comes to the relationship between people, I am a relatively introverted person. You can feel a little bit from each person's different research style. I think my research style is often to bury myself in a cave and have a hard time for a while. But in order not to become a closed car, you must take your head out of the soil.

在过去,when I was studying for my PhD, I was a very unique player. You want to achieve something, so you just do it. If you encounter any problems, you can explore on the Internet, learn, and use your skills to organize a small team to solve the problem.

After graduating from the university, I joined a company that makes robots with a giant smart brain. A deeper feeling is that——为了完成一个任务,要大家齐心协力,这中间的很多人与人之间的关系联系会比我之前的那种可能要紧密一些。

同时呢,我在想,如果生产力变化以后,因为最近硅谷不是有很多这种cloud code可以自动帮你编程的这种小工具。And AI's communication, I took away the part of communication I needed to have with people. It reduced my dependence on this. Even our current work processes, we are also controlling 3-4 agents, and then let the agents do a lot of work. This has changed the relationship between the people in the team.

所以你在跟人沟通之前会先去找Cloud Code?

没错。

然后Cloud Code成了人和人中间的那个环节?

没错。我觉得这个关系就是,我在构思一些小说的时候是会受到这个关系的启发。

One is that we are in an era of information explosion. After the information explosion, many of your messages used to be just me browsing online. But is it possible that I have a bot that can see all the information on the internet faster than me, see more than me, and understand my preferences, and feed me everything? At the same time, if everyone becomes the default mode in the future, which is having your bot——

Foreign technology can develop rapidly, you may need to ask a lot of people to do it. For example, a water repair shop. This is a problem that is always a problem in American society. You need to rely on people to repair water pipes. But in the future, it may also be the robot to solve it. Even including the many projects you are doing now, as the intelligent body develops, you may need to pull a human team before. In the future, it may be a team of artificial intelligence or robots.

三、人与人关系的松弛化

I think it can make the relationship between people more loose. Maybe in the primitive society, it's more difficult for a person to hunt and survive alone in the vast nature. And now, if you find a place to bury yourself, it seems that there is no such big problem. But in the future, maybe you can explore in the universe. Maybe you can find a small planet and you can settle on this planet directly. You can get a lot of robots to help you in this.

And then in a very noisy... There are always new messages coming in. In an environment where you can find a space to be quiet. It's actually a little harder than my previous life. So maybe I'm looking for a cave. I've been here for a while and I've had some wishes. But I also think that people are social animals. Staying in caves for a long time. People like me may also want to come out and take a look.

说到这个还挺有意思,我记得有一个老师,他说他对于他下一代的小孩不和人谈恋爱了,只跟机器人谈恋爱,一点惊讶都没有,现在你能接受这个事情吗?

I think I'm quite acceptable because I'm a second-generation person. I have a lot of friends like that in my life. Their way of life is very different from the old generation. But because I'm familiar with their friends for a long time, I can even understand what they do. I don't want to be in a relationship with humans. Or some of their social preferences. And also the love for the second-dimensional creation of people who are just lying. Including myself, I also participated in some of these second-dimensional co-operative activities.

四、AI毁灭世界的文学反思

哪些文学作品、电影作品,或者各种文学作品,帮助和影响了你对机器人的思考?

It's hard to point out which specific work it is in a short time. 但我觉得有一些,或者我看过一小类的文学作品之前,which describe the destruction of the world of artificial intelligence. This theme makes me feel that from the perspective of my limited perspective, it's not possible. Will time really go this far? So there will be some reflection. Why is this logic not established?

I think if we take a quick look at the current situation in 2026, we will think, "What is the worst thing that the smart body has done?" I remember a few days ago, someone gave a lot of power to a smart body and asked it to clear its mailbox. As a result, the smart body deleted all its mailboxes after receiving this power.

And then, the responsibility it takes——for example, I actually had a stroke before. During the process of the injury, I was asking for medical advice from everywhere, but the medical care in the American society is a bit difficult. At this time, I will send my medical information to this intelligent body to let it answer me. But in fact, during this process of answering, I will not really believe that it is impossible to say what this AI tells me. I must still bring it to a human expert to confirm it with him. Because I don't think that if I really do something based on his recommendation, he can take responsibility.

And in this kind of human society, who can take responsibility will build trust. I think this kind of trust is still not yet in the process of evolution. We completely believe in an intelligent body that gives him so many rights.

And finally, I think he can do things now that are relatively limited. For example, our robot. We sometimes have a joke in the industry. This joke may have been outdated. This is about three or four years ago, I was chatting with a student who was about to graduate. We were arguing about what we were doing every day. We were asking the robot to put a red square on a blue square. Isn't this what three-year-olds do? We spent five years here every day, studying a doctorate, and teaching robots. Maybe it's possible, or maybe not as good as a three-year-old.

五、成长经历:从安徽芜湖走出的信息学少年

因为写小说和写网文其实都要从新人世界有很多的取材,很多是取材于生活。我很好奇你会把你在派的工作经历的哪一个部分作为素材提取出来,放到虚构的作品里去。

I'm actually a person who is very open to work and life. I think the work experience at Pyme definitely affected my thinking. 比如说你在技术发展中走到了哪一步?比如说技术发展中的一些核心关键,一些更特别的事情,会有更多的思考,更具体的思考。And my online articles are often empty. Oh, I want to write something after protecting my identity online.

所以你能告诉我们你的笔名是什么吗?

No, it's "Help, Horse-Rack". Horse-Rack is a good name for protection.

让我们聊聊你的成长。我发现桂谷很多人从小就在参加竞赛。你能给我们讲讲你的成长经历吗?你是那种通过竞赛改变命运的人吗?

Can you count this category? I think so. When I have some feelings in the process of growing up, but not as deep as I do now, I think I have some key points in my life that have been influenced by my interest in the information science competition.

I was born in Anhui Province when I was a child. I went to school in Wuhu City, Anhui Province. This is a city that is known for its internet connection. It's a city, but it's actually a city. Objectively speaking, its educational resources may be different from big cities like Shanghai. But what's very coincidental is that there is an excellent computer science teacher in our city.

她当时是在我们城市那边做学生的时候,因为一些事故受了伤,然后失去了双手。So this experience made him, in the end, when he was on leave, it might have affected him, and made him choose to go back to his hometown to become a computer teacher. But because he is a very smart and excellent person, so during his education as a computer student, he treated him as a teacher. I even never really talked to him face-to-face. But I think I have learned a lot from him.

I was only 8 years old when I started learning this. At that time, we kids didn't think too much. We just thought that we could play computer games. We were so happy. As long as I didn't have to learn some complicated things, I could play some things that were not so complicated, I was very happy. So during this process, I accidentally laid down some of the basics of programming, and step by step, I went on the path of competition.

六、大学专业的三级跳:心理学→经济学→计算机

During the process of growing up, I also explored other career developments. For example, I actually studied psychology in college. 最后又转成了经济学。

你大学本科是进心理学?

我是心理学开始的。对,我的大一,因为美国大学它是可以换专业的嘛。我的大一的选择的专业其实是心理学。在学过了一些心理学以后,感觉到了第一,学的东西非常有趣。第二,我实在是不太适合做心理学。

为什么?

我觉得有时候人是有一些打破砂锅问到底的这种性格在我身上。If you study psychology, you will find that this thing makes sense. But if you want to break the sand, you may be able to do it. But after many years, or if you do something else, it's not direct enough. Maybe we are obsessed with solving problems, solving very specific problems.

So I went to study economics at that time. I wanted to understand the world of my life. But I finally returned to the computer. That's the reason. I think this can help me understand the world, but not help me understand what I want to do in this world. So in the end, I still turned back to a relatively more science-based computer path.

七、竞赛培养的思维方式与个性影响

竞赛对你的个性有很大影响吗?

It's not that big of a deal, because I was still more into the game when I was in the competition. Because when I was a kid, it was just a hobby for me. We just thought it was fun. Because when I first started learning, I was learning Logo. It's not like how it is now, where you type in words. It's a picture-like interface.

当时还有一本教材是中学生学C语言,其实这可能是我这辈子唯一认真读完的编程教材,后来的话都是网上现学现卖。因为在读这个教材过程中,作为一个小孩子,他接触编程,我觉得真的是有一种思维会不太一样吧,他会用讲故事的方法教你很多编程的原理。比如说递归,就是跟灯神去许愿,然后许了一个以后让他再给你三个愿望。就说这些东西都非常的自然而然的就能够融入到我的生活之中,把它有一些内化了。

所以在这个层面上就搞竞赛或者说很早的去学习编程这件事情对我的思维有一些影响,但可能是比较润物细无声的。我并没有感觉到他对我的这种性格造成了一些啊就是非常明显非常突然的那种变化。

八、板寸头、校园暴力与正义感

我就记得我when I was in elementary school, my parents picked up a board head for me the first day.

当你还在读小学的时候,你就去理了个板寸,然后大家都会笑话你。

我记得开学第一天,我去上学的时候哭了。因为大家都笑话我,我承受不了。我就是不知道怎么处理,我就哭了。可能是我小学时候哭得最惨的一次。然后当我小学毕业的时候,我成了学校里的大姐大。

你对此有什么想法?我们可以坐下来聊聊。

我觉得学校中使用暴力不是一件好事。I think there are some factors that are not balanced in personality. There will be some pursuit of fairness and justice. 然后我也会想办法在小学时候完成这类事情。比如说,当我上小学时,我会看到有人欺负别人。我就会说:"你在做什么?如果你打不过,我们就打。如果你打不过,你就去警察局。"达到一种心中那种正义的状态。

在你心中,有什么是别人觉得可以接受,但你觉得是触发了你对这个世界不公平不正义的感知,然后你要反抗的?

我觉得生活中确实没有那么多事情是绝对的公平,甚至公平的定义都很模糊吧,可能不同的人有不同的想法。I just try to maintain a kind of fairness within my capacity.

九、离开安徽:对教育资源不均的反思

I think that on the one hand, I think it's a bit heart-breaking. When I left the country, I had some thoughts. During my education in China, I wanted to go to a school like Tsinghua Peking University. I wanted to take the exam. But because I was in Anhui Province, there were three or four people in every 10,000 people who could go to Tsinghua Peking University.

I think I was in the competitive class in high school. I learned step by step from the traditional path of life and education. I'm not an excellent student. I'm just an excellent student who really loves to study and spends a lot of time studying. I'm just a middle school student in a competitive class.

So I know that unless I transfer to another school and work hard, I can't compete with the other 10,000 students. But when I came out, I found that other provinces have some advantages in terms of educational resources. 比如说在大城市,这一万个人里面有八十个人都能上清华北大,这种数字在安徽是想都不敢想的,会觉得是有一些资源分配上的事情。

但是可能在现在这个时间点上,更多的能够想的是如何通过创造更新的生产力,来在某个层面上抹平这些公平吧。

十、叛逆经历:高二剪板寸独自背包东南亚

你觉得你不是这种激烈教育的优胜者吗?

I can't give my classmates, especially those in junior high and high school, a high level of respect. I can't be as strict as them. Because we are in the competition class. Everyone in the competition class is very hard working. They know what they want. Unlike me, I've been a little bit... Although I look very obedient, I don't do anything particularly bad. But I'm not that obedient. I just want to learn.

你小时候做的最叛逆的事情是什么?

The most rebellious thing? I think because I did too many rebellious things, it's hard to comment on the most rebellious thing. 前三名?哦,其实我到高中的时候又把我的头发剪成了板寸。

因为当时就非常想去东南亚去旅行看一看,然后觉得自己一个人在东南亚可能不太安全,就把头发剪成了板寸,然后整个人打扮得就是一个非常男孩子的样子。这样走在大街上可能大家也会,就是该怎么说呢……

这个可以这么总结吧,就是可能是在东南亚背包的这段经历,相对而言是比较叛逆的,因为它是一个没有明显的目的,也没有明显的目标,也不知道它对我的人生有什么影响,但是就是非常的想做。

但其实还比较幸运的是,就家里人也没有那么的强烈的反对,他们只是觉得好奇怪啊,怎么做这个事情。保护好自己的安全,然后每天就是不停地骚扰我说,就赶紧劝回来吧。

你是高几去的东南亚?

大概就是高二底,就当时其实...I was thinking about going abroad. I was in a high school that was studying biology. So I didn't know I was going abroad. It was a quick change in my mind and goal. I went for a little bit of implementation for half a year. Then I found myself having a little free time. I thought about what I wanted to do. I went to Southeast Asia. Because I really liked the Indian mythological novels. I started to be fascinated by the Indian religious architecture. I wanted to go there and have a look.

去了多久?

去了大概两个星期。

一个人去的吗?

对,我一开始,但是我后面我爸爸妈妈也来找我了,他们可能还是有点不太放心。

你是为了自己在东南亚的安全才剪了短发?

I think so. But actually it's very convenient. Because during my junior high and high school years, my friends all knew that I was a very unruly person. I had to take care of my hair, comb my hair, and tie my clothes.

十一、留学美国:偶然中的决定

你是怎么突然决定出国留学的?

At that time, I couldn't get any grades. My teachers, classmates, and parents would advise me to work hard and complete the short version. I could still rush. I thought, "Why should I work hard? Why should I complete the short version? Why should I rush? I can't do it. I don't agree with this."

So, with this thought in my mind, it was very difficult for me to go to a good school like Tsinghua Peking University through my career path. There was another opportunity.

I was attracted to it, but I didn't think I could get into it. So I decided to study at the University of China. The University of China is close to my parents' home, and it's a good university. But it's not good for me. So I can't leave home. So I thought I wanted to go somewhere far away. And then I happened to meet the founder of YuXie. He went to Yale to study abroad. I thought I could go to study abroad too. Then I talked to my family. Then it just happened to be in the middle of the second grade. It turned into "Why don't we go to study abroad?"

十二、古龙武侠、游戏与创作启蒙

Because I'm a fan of Goulong. So it's a kind of social reflection in my imagination. And because this story is very pure, it has a lot of people's love and hatred, all set in the most intense moment. So when I play this story scene, I will get a very big psychological satisfaction.

这个游戏的什么让你着迷?

我觉得它柔和了几个我非常感兴趣的元素,第一个是它的美术风格。I think this is a very important point of my obsession with human culture, that is, you are here to experience a lot of different things. It can be set up through this scene, and the tone and these very gloomy, completely different worlds from your current life, quickly pull you to a completely different kind of...

你也可以在游戏中体验很多不同的生活,所以我认为这是一种对成为战士感受的捕捉。

然后还有一句台词:"谁不知道?"我觉得这句台词很酷。但是我很着迷于这种非常纯粹的情感邂逅。有一种"你身负重伤"的感觉。你说,在我们的现代社会,你怎么能承担一个……大多数人,你怎么能承担一个深深的伤口?

你可以在游戏中体验这个。因为我喜欢这个游戏,我没有觉得接近它。我可能事先想过,如果有一天我能创造自己的内容,我会非常高兴。然后看看别人创造了什么,我非常喜欢的内容,之前的路怎么走的?我们也可以更接近它。

十三、内向到外向:留学带来的社会化转变

那你为什么没去做游戏?

I think writing novels is a way of self-reflection that I created. And doing research and development in the field of AI is probably a way of showing that I am pursuing efficiency. 我在大学换的这些专业,我觉得都对写小说非常有帮助。But in the end, I found that the sense of accomplishment of the work I really want to do is largely due to the fact that I have done something that others can't do.

有两点。The first is that the environment in which I used to live is relatively stable. So when the relationship between people and people around you is too stable, you actually don't feel its existence.

这不符合企业家的商业精神。在我大学里,有很多活动和课程鼓励这种情况发生。As a very introverted person, I was like, "I can't help it. I can only say more." Otherwise, I really don't know what I'm doing.

走动开始真的变成比较能跟别人说话的类型。如果你和高中的我说话,可能会发现我这个人就不爱说话。但是你和现在的我交流,我觉得我是在交流这方面有了很大的提升。

十四、中英美三国文化的对比观察

你觉得两个社会的文化的基础有什么不一样吗?你刚才还觉得在之前的成长环境里面,你能感受到很多的不公平,那你到了美国之后呢?

不公平依然存在,只不过我觉得——China has been through a very rapid development. I studied in the UK, so if you put China, the United States and the UK on a scale, you will get a very interesting feeling. People in China, our parents' generation, may have a lot of turmoil, opportunities and some changes. Sometimes people will joke about you counting three times, who is not a person who is in the field?

相对更加固化以后造成的人们之间的关系和你所处的这个社会阶层和你的这个喜好品味的一个非常大的影响。我觉得中国在这个轴上可能是相对而言在当时是最百花齐放的。

那美国和英国呢?

我觉得英国可能在这条轴上相对而言是最保守的,而美国是有一些保守,但是都到硅谷来了,大家好像又抛弃了之前的那些旧的东西,在这里又开始开创新的东西。

你已经成功地逃离了家。你已经成功地离开了一千英里之外的家。你得到你想要的了吗?

I think it's in the inner world. When I left, I didn't have a big purpose. I didn't say what I needed to get. I slowly found out what I was going to do on the way. And I still like this industry and this job that I'm in now. I have faith in technology. You can say that.
