I think there's going to be a layer on top of that. We have some inklings of it, we started to build towards, but the last layer of abstraction on top of this is probably the coordination layer. So you have knowledge, and you have execution, you have coordination.

And at the coordination layer, we've started to expose some of these in ways that aren't very obvious, but we're beginning to think of these things called like strategies, where basically it's almost like a meta-harness, right? The true low-level harness is designed for execution. But the next one is about: okay, if tokens aren't really fungible and you need to give them different jobs — like maybe this token is advising versus this token is executing, this token is dreaming versus this token's executing, so on and so forth — you want to start composing these kind of orchestrated strategies that go together. And they should sit on top of all these things, because at the end of the day you still need to execute, and the execution still needs to know what to do. So everything, in theory, should kind of ladder together.

And so I think you know if you were to look at our roadmap and maybe project forward a little bit where you kind of expect us to go, we'll move more and more from the knowledge layer to the execution layer, and from the execution layer to the coordination layer, in terms of the abstractions that you can see us put out.

生态视角：不是围墙花园

Sequoia: That's a really cool. How do you think this all comes together into a broader ecosystem beyond just the things that you guys are building? How do you help support people building products on top of it, and how do you help them get the most out of all these pieces?

Angela: Yeah, I think this is like super top of mind for us. We really want to find a way to support as many people in doing this as we can. I think we're still learning — a lot of the industry has evolved. We've seen a lot of different pieces get spun up and spun down.

And I think the operative part for Caitlyn and I has been in the category of making sure at least at the base layer that we provide as many primitives across the board as possible. So this knowledge/execution/coordination layer — we want to give all of that out to everyone, so that people can start to compose and create on top of that. And that's just from a pure builder point of view.

Then there's a point of view around how do you kind of plug in with us, right? We're also building first-party products of our own. We've also created some ways to embed natively with us — like for example connectors, which are built on top of the MCP spec. We try to be more open about those types of things. And we're starting to figure out what are the right bits and pieces.

But what we're really trying to do is get to a place where, you know, in a company, if they're able to get created and built on, they can build whatever products that they want. They can build agents if they need to. And then those agents and those products could be things that could plug into other agents. Some of those agents could be Claude agents, some of those agents could be other people's agents. But we want to be able to enable that kind of transactability across the board.

标准与安全

And then I think in order for all of that to ultimately be true, there is a bit around standard setting. And I think there's the traditional standard setting, which is around how do systems interoperate — and that's things that you've kind of seen us do with skills and MCP. But they're at, again, like the builder layer.

I think at a higher-order layer there's also a bit around interoperability and standard setting around how do we all treat safety together. We've talked to a lot of these companies, and this is less from philosophies aside, just more like no one really wants to have technology that's, for example, doing negative things on their service, right? So cyber I think is a great example of this — you want to protect your own systems from negative actors or bad actors. And so these kinds of standard settings of how we find ways to partner with more and more people to be like, "yeah, we all kind of want to make sure our critical infrastructure is good, we all want to prevent fraud or any of those things from happening, and how can we work better with each of these members."

I think on the last layer, we're still evolving, and I think we're still very much trying to find ways that we can be better and work with the rest of the industry to bring people along. But those are kind of the higher-order primitives or pieces that we wish to be in place, so they can work with folks to ultimately solve this.

电力隐喻

I think if I were to take a step back at the end of the day on all of these things — you know, this technology is so transformative. And if it's a little bit like electricity, in the sense like: before electricity, you had to have a candle, and it was like you can only do so many things. But with electricity, the reason why it's such a transforming technology for all of us — and so greatly of a utility — is because you can actually wire it into everything. Everyone is able to actually access it. We also have standards and ways to plug in and do all the pieces that we need. And that's not something that anybody can do by themselves. They always have to work with the ecosystem and work with partners to figure out a path forward.

开放 vs 围墙花园

Sequoia: How do you think about the philosophy of building an open ecosystem versus a walled garden? And how do you think about what products are really important for you to own first-party versus where you're perfectly happy to plug into other components of the ecosystem?

Caitlyn: Yeah. So maybe using Angela's kind of layered cake that we talked about a little bit earlier: you'll see that on some pieces of this, execution for example, what we've done within something like Claude Managed Agents — and I think over time you'll see us try to make this a little bit more modular — we actually aren't precious about "you should run these things on our infrastructure, it should be sandboxes that we control, or it should be a storage layer that we control."

For example, we launched self-hosted sandboxes, and we partnered with Modal and Vercel and Cloudflare and a bunch of other folks — even like Amazon's new microVMs — to have a first-class offering where you can go plug any of those things in. We launched MCP tunnels, so that you can call out to your MCP servers that are behind your firewall, right? And be able to punch through there. And so for some of these things, whether it runs on our infrastructure versus somebody else's infrastructure is actually not important to us. Because the thing that's important to us is more that the architecture of how you put together these agents in a way that will be powerful, in a way that will be reliable and scalable — we have strong opinions on that, and you can kind of just conform to the interfaces that we put out there and plug those things in. We think that that generally is a thing that works really well.

Form factor 是动态的：Claude Design 的意图

Angela: Yeah. I think on the kind of verticals where we might build products — you know, I think we kind of have two frames here.

The first one is: we are always trying to figure out a form factor — like an evolving form factor. We, by the way, don't think form factors are static. It's a dynamic thing. What might be awesome for one year's worth of AI development will probably not be awesome for the next year's worth. And we just kind of try to have that mentality.

We tell the team overall around Anthropic — everyone's always trying to be like "is this AGI-pilled enough?" And then we always have this mentality of like, you know, we built something, it works, it was cool for a year, and maybe it's not the right next thing, and so throw it away, try again. And we tell platform users the same thing. I just think that's probably attached to the technology. So yeah, one principle is trying to always constantly find this new form factor.

Sometimes we'll launch products in certain areas to try to showcase a new type of form factor. It's not necessarily because we think it's the biggest ham or the most important thing to go after. But sometimes you're like, okay, this has always been a really difficult thing, and people have always communicated this way or tried some things this way, and can we show that maybe there's a slightly different way. And because the model capabilities are so advanced now, can we try to express it a bit differently?

Sequoia: What's an example of that?

Angela: Yeah, you know, like Claude Design is a little bit of that way. I think, depending on how you squint, you might see it as a way that we're kind of going into design as one of the verticals. But more often than not, if you take a look at what we're trying to do with that product, there's a couple of decisions that were made in there.

The first one is that you can actually try to offload more and more and more to Claude. And so it tries to be kind of opinionated on: you know, just just talk to it and let it really try to figure out. And yes, you can still edit it and then do these kinds of things, but kind of discourage a little of that and more just like let just talk to Claude to go figure it out.

The second thing was: it was really trying to express that actually code is a way to solve for things that you wouldn't normally think would be the way. So a lot of people who have built kind of generative slide decks or designs or whatever will pick the way of "they have some kind of design system, you integrate against design system." It's almost the traditional classic WYSIWYG style of designing something. And with Claude Design, it was like, okay, can we try to just use code purely, have Claude generate that code, and would it do a good job? And we found through some experiments early on it looks like it can kind of do that. And how can we showcase that to the world.

So that's an example. We have a lot of other internal projects, and this kind of falls in the category of expressing form factor. We'll all try it out internally, it'll be super cool for like two weeks, and then we move on to the next thing. We never even ship the thing, frankly. But yeah, we actually do a lot of product experimentation in that area, and that's like our Labs team.

第二个框架：TAM 与 Token-hungry 行业

And then there's the second category, which is that we actually do look at TAM. Like, we're a business. We do look at TAM. We do look at areas that we think there'd be reasonable agentic operations that would happen in those areas.

We do tend to have an orientation towards things that are more token-heavy. And by token-heavy or token-hungry — maybe is the way I would say that — what we mean is, you know, once you spend a call, like one turn, you look at the end of that turn and you say "am I done, or am I actually so glad that I did that thing I want to do more of that thing?" We like industries where the answer to that question is "I want to do more of that thing."

So coding is obviously the one that we all know. And the great thing about coding is that what it's actually doing is: once you finished a turn, you look at that and you're like, "that was incredible, I'm like unlocked, I'm going to do more, I'm going to build more, I can do more."

And there's other services where it's like, actually when you finish that turn, you completed the job and you just move on. You know what I mean? And so we tend to go into the ones that are a bit more like there's this iterative flow — you're going to build more, generate more together.

And then the last angle that we kind of take a look at is: you know, there's going to be certain business functions that we're like, "they are the buyer that we like to go to." We want to help them optimize their workflows, help them create better products there. And I think we've been pretty transparent with some of the verticalization — we've done finance, we've done legal, and we've tried to narrow in on specific areas where we feel like by having the right context and the right tools and putting it together in a good form factor is probably useful for us to be able to do.

Caitlyn: And in each of those areas, we're trying to do a bit of like showing the art of the possible across all the different ways that you would accomplish those outcomes.

So for finance for example is a good one. You could be a company that solves problems in finance, and you could build directly on the messages API and you can just get some tokens and you can build everything else on top; or you could be someone who builds on Claude Managed Agents, you can get a lot more out of the box; or you could say "I'm going to build a plug-in that…" or a connector, right, that's going to sit within one of our products, and within those form factors. When we did — we recently launched Claude for Financial Services — it's like, okay cool, we've got packages of skills and things like this that you could choose to use within our product, within other people's products. We even launch cookbooks on "here's how you would use Claude Managed Agents to go and do these things."

And so I think for us, it's all kind of an experimentation around like, you know, we provide people all these different pieces and see where they run with it. And then sometimes we put together products that are just packaging of all of these things — like Claude tag I think is a really good example. Like we had been seeing people in the industry go and say — like Shopify did this with River, Square Block recently did this with BuilderBot. There's a few of these examples where people said, "I'm going to build like an agentic platform internal to my company, and I'm going to try to give it all the right context, and I'm going to make it accessible from Slack or from various other platforms that you'd want it to be accessible at." And I think Claude tag was very much a packaging of all those same things — that anybody could choose to build something similar, but this is how we're doing it internally, and if you would like to just kind of plug in and go, here's what that looks like.

Claude tag：被误解的"组织层 harness"

Sequoia: What do you think people misunderstood about Claude tag? Because there was all this ruckus about "oh my gosh, it's just a Slack bot." Tell us what the magic of tag is.

Angela: No, I think it's a great question. And I do think it actually showcases a little bit of where maybe the future could be going.

If you look at products in the past, people are like, "Oh, you really attach to the form or the UI almost, right? Like it looks like this, so it's super cool." And I think when you look at tag — yeah, like the way you interact with it is that you literally tag it in Slack. And so yeah, that is the interface, but that's not really the important part.

The important part is all the context engineering and architecture that we put underneath the hood, so that tag just works. It really should just feel like a coworker. If you go to a company and you onboard and a coworker comes into your channel, then you can chat with it. It's proactive, it figured out what's useful for you, and it just gets stuff done for you.

And so if you think about, especially like non-technical audiences, this is a huge unlock. You literally create a channel, and then you @claude — or sometimes you don't even @claude — and you're like "hey I want to be able to do this and do that and I can't figure out this, and how do I actually submit an expense report again?" Traditionally, you think about how to solve that workflow, you are going all over the place and you're talking to your manager, you're talking to your spin buddy, and it's really really complicated. And today, now you just go talk to Claude tag.

And we do a lot of the hard work on doing the context engineering, the proactivity, a lot of the harness pieces. I think Andrej Karpathy said it really well — it's like an org-level harness. There's a lot of complexity baked into that. Like Caitlyn mentioned, you can use our APIs to go and construct that, you have to do a lot of the experimentation yourself obviously, but this is an opinionated take from Anthropic on how you can have this really awesome always-on kind of agent for your entire company.

冰川比喻

And the bit that's futuristic, I guess, is that a lot of that complexity is actually — it's like an iceberg. All the stuff underneath it is actually becoming the harder and harder and more useful part that we're trying to push through. And I think we'll see more and more like that kind of tip bit that's outside in the water — the interface can actually constantly swap.

Today, right, like Slack is a place where a lot of people collaborate, a lot of businesses collaborate. But also a lot of people collaborate in teams — some people collaborate by a WhatsApp group, or they text each other, or some people still email each other. Those could be the form factors that actually completely — you can imagine agents just going there and taking up the same form factors as humans have taken up. It was almost like a very boring take, but it's actually the most forward one, because you want the agent and you want AI to basically be like another person. It's helping you, but it's very intelligent, can figure out all the context, and you can always have it to be a really helpful assistant.

Harness 与 Context 的最佳实践

Sequoia: Totally. You talked about context and then harnesses quite a bit, and so your team has such an opinionated point of view on what it takes to build an exceptional agent. I imagine a lot of that comes down to the context engineering and the harnesses. Maybe like what best practices or advice would you share with people about what you need to get right on the harness and what you need to get right on the context?

Caitlyn: Yeah. So it's interesting because we've kind of talked about — you know, we launched Claude Managed Agents as this very generic but high-performing harness because we've done all the nitty-gritty work that's actually really boring and not super interesting around how do you deal with prompt caching, how do you deal with context management, how do you clear old stuff out of the window, sometimes you call tools programmatically so you don't pull everything into the context window and you can keep it clean. There's a lot of those sort of details on the lower-level harness layer.

And I think, honestly, best practices are just stuff like: prompt caching — do it. You're going to save a lot of money and token costs. Obviously try to keep your context window clear. And then putting those things together in a harness that will be performant is sometimes specific to the task that you're trying to accomplish, right? And then of course evals. I'm surprised we got this far into this thing before one of us said the word "evals," but you need evals to make sure that what you're trying to accomplish is performant.

策略层的兴起

But I think where we're starting to go — and Angela mentioned this a little bit earlier — is more of a concept of strategies or meta-harnesses. Because I do think that yes, you can again make this lower-level harness going to be performant, and maybe that's interesting for you to do yourself, or maybe not and you offload it to us. But this concept that you can take any given token and spend that token on just executing, or you could take that same token and choose to actually reflect on your past agentic sessions and write learnings to memory so that the next agent does a good job, or you could take that token and advise with a bigger model so that a smaller model can execute and do a better job. Or you can say "execute, execute," and then like a grader comes in like "did you do a good job? No, you didn't, try again." Right?

And so I think the interesting innovation is going to come more at that higher level — on the meta level. And I think optimizing within those strategies is something that our team is really excited about, and we're starting to do a lot of work there. And I think a lot of other people are starting to feel really excited about this concept of strategies and the jobs you give to tokens. Because again, yes there's best practices on stuff like prompt caching and exactly how you clear stuff out of your context window and how you write your evals, but I don't know that there's necessarily so much juice to squeeze in a lot of cases out of that layer as compared to a layer higher than that.

删除 harness 的哲学

Angela: Yeah. And one of the reasons for that, I think, has to do with the generations of the models. If you look like two years ago, a lot of the harness was like a scaffold to kind of tell the model to go from point A to point B. And you had to really build in a lot. You practically build one wall here and one wall here, so like the thing would go in a straight line.

And now the models are actually very very steerable. And so a lot of that steering you could just put in the prompt. Like "go from point A to point B" and the model will go from point A to point B. So a lot of, if you have harnesses that are designed to do that kind of steering, you can delete that part. Like that part we actually frequently encourage — you can delete part of those harnesses. I think various people have said things along those lines. And that's what people oftentimes mean when they're like "the model will kind of consume some of the scaffolding." In that sense, for sure, if your scaffolding is telling it to go in direction that it can just intelligently figure out, that I think will increasingly continue to be so.

But as a result of this, what the harness needs to start doing is more allow it to run longer. And so that's where that execution bit tends to be. I think it sounds like a maybe somewhat silly point, but I do think it results in a lot of differences. Because because you can go in the direction that you tell it to go, you obviously don't want it to stop at B — you're going to be like, "okay now go from B to C and then go to F and then go to Z and then come back to me on A." You know, something funky like that.

In order to be able to do a lot of those things, the kinds of harnesses that you do are less the steering harness, and it's more like these strategy harnesses that Caitlyn's mentioning — which allow you to operate at a slightly higher level of thinking, which matches a lot of the intelligence gains that we're trying to see with the model.

Task-specific Harness：验证逻辑是关键

Sequoia: Do you think task-specific harnesses make sense — or vertical-specific or task-specific harnesses?

Angela: I think people have different opinions on this. Our opinion is yes.

I don't think there's a general harness. I think there are some capabilities that are obviously very general and they tend to be very useful. Like coding is a capability that is very useful because you can use it across so many things, and software has just eaten so much of what is capable. So our ability to write software is therefore useful.

I think when you think about very very specific types of domains, they're going to require a couple of pieces of the harness to be sort of customized. One of that, I do think, is how you choose to handle sort of errors — between when you do something and you hand something off to the model. So in domains where you require an extreme level of verification, that logic of how you handle that — like, again, I think it sounds small, but I totally understand why some people feel like they really want to own the harness. Because tweaking that last bit will give you a ton of juice, and especially domains like legal and finance where there's a lot of consequences to you not getting it perfectly correct — is really going to matter, and that's going to be the difference between your product and someone else's product being the thing that the user ultimately uses.

And then there are other domains for which I would say it's not going to matter as much, because you're able to compress it into like a general model capability.

So the tweaks that, I guess, where we feel like the domain specificity is really going to matter is the specific verification logic between the model and your execution. And then, I think it's going to be about some of these higher-order strategies on how well you're able to actually allocate your token budget.

The context bit is actually a little overdone. Like yes, you're going to throw in context, and that's — but any harness can actually handle a lot of context. And so that's just more like you have the data, and if you have the data then obviously you're uniquely qualified to do something useful.

Harness 的多重定义

Caitlyn: Yeah. And I think when people say harnesses, they often mean a lot of different things, and I think this is why, in part, there's so many different opinions on this.

You can think of a harness as literally just like a loop — that's like "okay cool, user, model, user, model, tool," you know, like that sort of thing. Then you could think of the harness as also all of the tools that are packaged up with the harness. And there's just a lot of different definitions of these things.

And I think the stuff that can be pretty generic and less interesting to own and deal with is what I was kind of saying earlier — is like getting your prompt caching right, right? Like maybe that is not the world's most interesting thing. Choosing to clear out old tool calls from the context window and things like that are maybe a little bit less interesting. And you go a layer higher into some of the stuff Angela's talking about, and then you get into like, "okay yeah, these are things that I might want to own and control."

And so it's interesting: with Claude Managed Agents, the thing that we built today, we call it higher-order, but it's not really that high-order — in the sense that you can choose to define all of the tools that you want to bring in as custom tools with the harness, right? We give you a lot of knobs to control, you can define skills, you can do your system prompts, you can do a whole bunch of different things, MCP servers and things like this.

And I think where we want to get to is a point where you can literally just tell an agent "here's the outcome I want and here's the budget that I want to spend, ready set go," and you may be like "don't think about any of those things underneath." And so I think there's just a few different layers of this, right, that for certain things, you might want to sit at a different layer of what you actually go and control. And you can probably get better outcomes within some of those layers by doing a little bit more optimization work.

从最先进用户身上看到的东西

Sequoia: Very cool. One of the things I'm curious about — and one that I love about infrastructure and platform teams — is that you get to see what the most advanced users in the world are using and learn from them. I'm curious, what are some things that you're seeing and learning from from the people building on your platform?

Angela: There's some people that have been doing some really funky ways of handling context. We ourselves explore this a lot. That's actually one of the reasons why tag is such a great product — there's a lot of really awesome context engineering that's happening.

We've seen some teams be really clever about how they do that, and they are able to think through: okay, if I have all these contexts in a bunch of different places, how can I proactively go reach out to them? How can I try to generate enough permissions across each of them, and then feed that all into an agent? And it's interesting that this is kind of the level of innovation that we're actually very excited by. It doesn't express itself as a completely different product form factor, but what it actually does express itself as is maximally useful to users.

And we've been seeing this more and more with internal use cases instead of external ones. So companies who are becoming more AI-native, basically — they're the ones we're seeing increasingly more and more innovation out of. And so we've had customers try to do this for their — they've built their own custom SDLC kind of setup in very very innovative ways. We've had ones who do that for their entire back office, and just the nuances of how they stream in context I think has been actually really interesting in terms of how they've been putting together the pieces. So that's been one category that's been really really fascinating.

老系统与 computer use

Another category that's been really interesting has actually been with companies that are dealing with really old-school software. So there's a lot of healthcare companies that we engage with, and, you know, like they're like "the systems I'm working with, they don't even have APIs." Like that's a dream.

And so how can they use computer use and things like this to be able to start to automate and create more connectivity with our systems? That area of innovation I think has been really exciting. It's been really interesting to see people try all sorts of crazy stuff — from taking a laptop and trying to run a bunch of things on it, to auto-generate a bunch of things that then their agents can go and use. This has actually been probably an area of a lot of innovation coming from a lot of our customers that we want to find ways to support better and see, like, okay maybe how can we make this easier for you, how can we help you with some standardization, how can we get it so that you can just have a spec and then Claude can then respect it, and so it's much easier for you to organically connect a lot of these things.

But yeah, maybe the general theme I would just give you is: interestingly, a lot of the innovation that's most exciting out there right now has been this kind of context and connectivity layer, which has been really fascinating.

Agents as MCP servers

Caitlyn: Yeah. Like a good one in that — we were working with a customer who they've built some agents on Claude Managed Agents. They also have some agents they built on other models and other platforms, and they've optimized each of these agents to be good at the things that they want. They want these agents to all be able to work well together.

And they kind of were like, "Wow, galaxy brain — what if I expose an MCP server on top of this agent so that it can then go and have this other agent call a tool on that agent, right? And have these things just be more modular and be able to work together." And we were like, "yeah totally." And we sat down with them and worked through it, and it worked perfectly, and it was pretty cool.

And so we're seeing a lot of, again, that connectivity layer that I think is one of the cooler areas where people are innovating.

制造业的兴起

But outside of that, one thing that has been cool is just seeing the shift in — I guess like industry trends — of where we're seeing a lot of our usage come from. Talked a lot about coding: coding as a category, of course, absolutely exploded. There's so much going on there. And we're starting to see some of these emerging trends more recently. We're starting to see manufacturing really pick up as just a category where people are building with AI. And one of our PMs like getting on a flight to Detroit to go figure out what these customers need and what's going on.

And so I think we're going to start to see a lot more just kind of outside of the box of what people think about today sort of use cases, which we're really excited about.

从 Token Maxing 到 Token Rationalization

Sequoia: It seems like — we went through a token-maxing moment of history, and now there's like the token-rationalization moment of history. What are your thoughts on that, and what should companies be doing, and how does the platform team think about enabling that?

Angela: Yeah, I mean it makes sense. It makes sense from the — you start to rationalize. I really like that framing.

I think there's a couple things that are top of mind for us on this front. Again, it makes sense — and as these models get more and more capable, you're going to hit levels of intelligence-maxing that are there, that then you want to do the next dimension. And the next dimension after intelligence will either be cost or it will be speed. And you just kind of go through that across all possible task complexities in the distribution.

And as we see that happen, something that's really top of mind for us that we try to spend some time with users on is: what you don't want to do is stop AI usage, right? Like that's kind of the wrong move. And we do actually see some of our customers do that.

So oftentimes the way that AI spend has erupted inside their company has been through some kind of shadow IT, you know — their employees just want to use it, they find a way, they end up procuring it themselves, and before you know it, half your org has found some way to have installed Claude Code. And in that world it is kind of hard to manage because these things are, again, very token-hungry ultimately.

And so what we try to encourage our customers is like: okay, you don't want to stop the innovation. If you are getting returns on top of this, you are shipping faster than ever before, you can run more operationally efficient — then those are gains. And so the area that we actually try to encourage people is: if there is a way for you to construct, again, a strategy that allows you to design an architecture that says "given a task, assesses level of complexity" — I mean I'm effectively describing a router — but there are ways to do this that are, I think, a bit better now. And so like this task comes in, has a certain level of complexity; for that level of complexity you can define some rules, but for the most part, right, if it's a hard task you should probably route that to a big super-smart model, and if it's not a hard task you can route that to cheaper models.

Designing that, I think, has a little bit of — there's a lot of technical complexity in that, but it's very very doable, and we actually encourage people to try those kinds of things.

Sequoia: Offer, rather.

Angela: I think within the Claude space it will make sense. It's actually one of the strategies we imagine designing. Because the way that we're kind of thinking about a lot of these things is: it almost feels like every month there was a new era of something. And if we take a step back, this seems to be really fast. And so what are the different ways that are recomposable, so we can redesign very quickly for any new whatever the cool thing is that month. And so this is in that category of things where we feel like we can actually just recompose a lot of our primitives and then design it.

I think the bit that we do feel really strongly about on the model-routing front is: we are designing our platform for Claude, and we want to make sure that Claude is great at solving all these things. So we'll restrict to that space rather than, you know, I don't think we're that interested in saying "okay, and then you should route to a different model" or whatever.

Sequoia: Makes sense.

Caitlyn: Yeah, and — well, some of that too is just: I think we have a strong belief that harnesses and just the agentic layer should be tuned to the model family that you use it with. And so I think there was a period where people were kind of like, "yeah cool, I can build a harness and build an agent and then just plug in a different model underneath," and they were excited about routers from that perspective. And I think we started to see — like Vercel just did this with Harness Agent, for example — like some of these players in the space come up a layer of abstraction and say, "actually, plug in the whole harness and the whole agent that's tied to a model family," which makes a lot of sense. And so what we could provide is a little bit better, smarter — how do you mix and match the right models within the model family underneath that thing? If that makes sense.

But yeah, on the general question of token-maxing, costs, and these sorts of things, I think we're just kind of going through what feels like a normal natural cycle for companies and figuring out how to make the best use of this technology and run their businesses really well and really effectively.

Stripe 的类比

And, um — it's interesting, like, before working at Anthropic I was at Stripe, and we were kind of in the very reasonable era of like: we paid a lot of attention to our AWS bill. And so if someone were to have built some background job and they didn't quite configure it correctly and this thing's burning through CPU or whatever it is at any given moment and causing a big increase in spend that's not actually worth it — right, like we have put in place the guardrails to find that and then go ask that engineer very nicely to please turn off their background job that's not within the bounds of what they should be spending for the thing they're trying to accomplish.

I think those are the things with AI that people are going to start to go and figure out. And I think, to Angela's point, a thing that gets dangerous is when you're just like, "here's a cap and you're stuck within your cap, like, ready set go." But I do think that encouraging innovation, encouraging people to create really excellent outcomes with this stuff, and then coming in from the side and looking and saying "okay, well, there are few different ways that we probably could have accomplished that outcome, right? One is you take Opus and you run it all night and you do something crazy. And another is maybe to get a little bit smarter with the strategies that you put together in order to create that same outcome within a lower cost." I think that's the next layer of thinking that everyone's going to start to do.

未来展望：Strategies、Bug Hunting、Best-of-N

Sequoia: Very cool. Is there anything that you guys are excited about building over the next few months that you can share, a hint at what might come next?

Angela: Yeah. I mean, I know we said this word like 20 million times, I apologize, but we really are trying to build ways for you to compose strategies. And so that is an area that we're trying to move into — that kind of coordination layer of the abstraction.

And we want to start at this front because the types of problems that we see people building — they are at a layer where in order to get the most return on this, you have to be a little clever about what is the nature of the problem that you're solving.

So to give you something concrete: when you try to solve for, let's say you want to build an agent that's trying to do bug hunting. And you could just send one off to go and do that, and it's going to give you a certain type of return, a level of return of possibility. And then people kind of get stuck at that and they're like, "okay, my next options are I can just swap the model for a different, probably bigger model, or I could let it run longer." And that's pretty much the only two levers that you have to try to make this bug-hunting agent.

From a lot of experimentation, when we do these kinds of things, there's actually a thing like — those two things are still true, but you actually have a third lever, and it tends to actually do a lot more than you think it does. Which is that actually, if you were to like best-of-N the thing, it would give you a lot more returns. But like just to be — just saying those words are fine, and there's plenty of papers and people have published it, to actually build that thing and put it into production so you can actually test it on users and see the results for yourself, that's really really freaking hard. And you end up building all these custom harnesses, so on so forth, like, you know, all that stuff.

But we're seeing this is where the alpha is. And it's hard. And so in the same very simple philosophy that we talked about at the beginning, like, if it gives you the return that you want and it's hard, we're going to try to make it easy for you, so then you can use it to then run the experiments you actually need to run.

Sequoia: Reminds me of when people are talking about agent swarms a year ago. It's some version of that.

Caitlyn: Has it been a whole year?

Sequoia: Yeah.

Caitlyn: Oh my god.

Sequoia: I know. We're finally there.

Caitlyn: Yes.

Angela: Yeah. No, I think that's a type of strategy — exactly. In the same way that you have, you know, one big one that separates a bunch, as another type of strategy. And I think people have thought about this maybe in the way of human organization.

I guess it could be similar, but if you take it to kind of its ends, it's actually more just like the token has a job. And I think it's this job piece that we're really indexed on. And we see a lot of returns too, and that's the thing that we want to spend time with users and the rest of the ecosystem on — like, how can we just make that easier for folks to then experiment? We can give you five jobs off the top of our head, and we'll probably — like, that's what we have internally. And if we give this out to the rest of the ecosystem, there's probably going to be like 100,000, 200,000 who knows what other combinations that people could put together.

企业级：模块化与开发者体验

Caitlyn: Yeah, we want to be able to keep doing this hill-climbing on how do you get the most value, the most intelligence per dollar, and just put that power in people's hands.

But around the edges of that, we have these personas that have kind of just like things they have to work through in order to be able to really deploy AI either within their companies or within their products. And that's like the sort of enterprise-ready security and compliance controls and things like this. But really even just making the platform more modular in the right ways — like being able to plug in different pieces of the solutions that we're building. Like "I want to use memory for this thing over here," right, or whatever else it is, and having a truly excellent developer experience around that.

Because we spent a lot of time with enterprises who are like, "okay, I have this walled garden and I need to figure out exactly how I can plug these solutions in." And so we've got a part of our team that's innovating on things like strategies and jobs and trying to help you maximize intelligence, and they're like, "that's really cool but I can't actually use any of that for XYZ reasons." So I think solving those problems is really really important to us.

But then the other persona is, you know, the like weekend developer who's like "I want to go and build something useful for myself," right? And they're often doing that on top of our platform and on top of many other just pieces of developer platforms in the community. And I think for some of those folks, there's more that we can do to provide solutions that are maybe more open or more hackable, or whatever it might be, for those folks to just go wild with what we can offer them and have this really excellent developer experience.

And so I think there's a lot of stuff that maybe I would put in the category of table stakes that I'm really excited about, because I think those are the things that then unlock getting people to say, "okay, yes, this thing works for me and now I can plug in on some of the stuff that you guys are doing that's really innovative and hill-climby to get more intelligence and save costs and things like that."

结束

Sequoia: Wonderful. Caitlyn, Angela — I feel — I mean, you are building one of the most important developer platforms in the world. And talking to the two of you over time, I just feel really optimistic that that platform is in very thoughtful hands, that care about the ecosystem. So, thank you for taking the time today to share what you're up to, and we look forward to what's ahead.

Caitlyn & Angela: Thanks for having us.

Sequoia: Thank you guys.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的哲学翻转：从"电力"隐喻到"代币不可互换"
Angela 用电力做类比，但真正锋利的洞察藏在她随口说的另一句话里——**"tokens aren't fungible"（代币不是可互换的）**。这句话是对她自己电力隐喻的隐秘反叛：电力最伟大的特质恰恰是**可互换性**（一度电就是一度电，无论用来烧咖啡还是启动手术台）。但 Anthropic 的下一层抽象——"策略层"——建立在完全相反的假设上：这个 token 用来"建议"，那个 token 用来"执行"，另一个 token 用来"做梦"。
这里出现了一个跨领域的惊人对应：这不像电网，倒像**亚当·斯密的别针工厂**——同一种劳动力，一旦被分工赋予不同角色，产出会指数级放大。更精妙的映射来自计算机科学的 **Actor Model**（Carl Hewitt, 1973）：分布式系统性能提升的关键从来不在"更多算力"，而在"更好的消息传递协议"。Anthropic 正在把这个五十年前就存在的洞察，重新走一遍，只是这次演员是 token。
## 二、冰川反转定律：AI 时代的 UI 会变得越来越"无聊"
Cla

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1452期】宏观课堂：中性利率，判断加息还是加息周期？✨20260713#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被悄悄改写的"中性利率"：一场没有公告的货币政策革命

这期视频最反直觉的观察，其实藏在数字的位移里——美联储自己眼中的中性利率，已经从过去十几年默认的 0.5% 悄然抬升到了 1.2% 左右，翻了一倍还多。这不是一个技术性调整，而是一次"没有新闻发布会的范式转移"。

有意思的是：市场几乎没有为此定价情绪波动。为什么？因为它是通过 SEP 点阵图这种"极其无聊"的方式渗透出来的，没有一个鲍威尔的发布会瞬间。制度性变化往往不是靠戏剧性事件推进的，而是靠"无聊的年报"完成的——这一点和企业治理、宪政演化其实同构：真正的权力位移几乎从不发生在头条里。

由此推出的结论也很反直觉："再加息两次"在这个新框架下其实是"停在中性利率+缓冲区"，是收敛动作而非加息周期的开启。市场把加息读作"鹰派信号"，但按这套算法反而应该松一口气。

二、AI 资本开支的 FOMO，与 2018 年鲍威尔的最后一次加息，是同一种"路径依赖病"

分析师把大科技的 AI 资本开支节奏（500 亿→1000 亿→2000 亿）和存储板块的杠杆 FOMO 并列，其实指向一个更深的模式：FOMO 不是散户病，而是所有决策系统在"看不到止损点"时的通用故障。

跨领域连接一下：这和 2018 年鲍威尔那次"明知市场信号相反、也要再加一次"的决定，是同构的——都是路径承诺（path commitment）压过了实时数据反馈。鲍威尔当时的执念是"我不能显得软弱"，大科技现在的执念是"我不能在 AI 竞赛中掉队"。心理机制完全一样：决策者恐惧的不是错误，而是被解读为犹豫。

真正冷峻的洞察是：当所有玩家都在同时 FOMO，个体理性的"必须投"叠加成集体的"必然过投"——这套逻辑在军备竞赛、房地产周期、生物学中的孔雀尾巴演化里都完全相同。分析师无意识地讲了一个进化生物学的故事。

三、Kevin Warsh 的"反 Forward Guidance"是一次制度撕裂——它比加息本身重要得多

大部分观众会把注意力放在"加不加息"上，但视频里真正埋着的地雷是：Kevin Warsh 想复刻 Greenspan 的"不给指引"路线，等于要拆解过去 20 年 Fed 与市场之间的一份隐性契约。

这里有一个几乎没人点破的类比——Forward Guidance 本质上是一种"央行版本的可预测性溢价"，功能等同于宪法中的程序正义。市场愿意接受各种坏消息，前提是"你告诉我流程"。一旦流程本身变得任意，所有资产的隐含波动率都会重定价，与加息几次无关。

更微妙的是：2008、2020、2023 三次危机之所以没崩盘，都靠"市场对 Fed 反应函数的信任"提前吸收了恐慌。如果这份信任被 Warsh 亲手拆掉，下一次危机的"信任缓冲垫"就没了——这不是货币政策问题，是系统韧性问题。

所以真正值得警惕的，不是"7 月加不加息"，而是未来两年可能出现一种全新的、需要重新学习的美联储沟通语法。市场会经历一段"制度适应期"的波动，而这种波动无法用宏观模型定价，只能靠时间。

📝 转录文本

开场

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析小桃。

以前大家给过我一个建议，希望我多上来为大家做视频，即使每天没有一些新闻可以聊的时候，也上来和大家唠嗑唠嗑。那行，今天大家的建议我听进去了，所以今天我就上来花比较短的时间和大家唠嗑一个话题——中性利率。

正好因为周一的市场，Chris Waller 美联储成员的讲话，使得加息的概率再次上升，应该是上升到了最近这段时间最高的一个位置。所以借这样的一个新闻本身，和大家聊一下一个概念：在我们聊加息的时候，必须要知道"中性利率"这个概念。知道了之后，我们就知道加息的幅度到底会停在哪里，是不是再次会打开一个加息周期。

好吗？那我就废话不多说了，直接开始。在开始之前，大家记得留言、点赞、加转发。

一、快速过一下周一市场

美元、美债市场这块出现了上涨。美债收益率这一块 7 个低基点，十年期 6 个基点，TLT 继续出现下跌。

三大指数也出现了下跌，但道指的下跌幅度相对会小一些。黄金下跌，原油上涨 9.4%，战争的这个风险再次上升。VIX 上升了 14.2%。

我觉得无论是伊朗也好，美国也好，没有这一个能力和实力，包括有这个耐心本身再次开启大规模的战争，我认为这个环境已经不存在。短期之内一些风险对于市场这边消耗的一些估值，我认为都是合理的。

包括整个芯片板块、存储板块出现了一些下跌，包括和芯片板块相关的一些估值。我看有些标的都是从最高为什么 20 多块钱，现在已经到了几块钱了，就下跌的幅度非常的大。前段时间市场聊得非常多的一些市场里面的一些"股神"等等，你看一下最近的一些标的，起码是 30% 以上的回调幅度。

那么如果你能够满仓扛住，那非常的厉害；如果你相信他未来这个基本面，那继续拿着也没有关系；如果因为最近这段时间的下跌，就感受到好像自己拿不住了，那要思考一下自己在前段时间到底是投资、到底是投机、到底在做什么。

我们不能因为它上涨的时候，用各式各样的理由来说服自己，自己买的就是基本面，这支个股未来会越来越好；而当它下跌的时候，就完全影响到我们自己对于这支个股的信仰或者认知了。特别是短期之内，两个月之内，一个月下跌、一个月上涨、一个月下跌，难道公司的基本面就改变了吗？绝对不会因为在两个月之内就出现非常大的改变，对吗？

所以，下跌是非常好的去考验自己对于持股的信心、对于个股的理解，包括对于个股上仓位的把握。因为你仓位如果把握不是非常好的话，那最近这段时间的下跌，整个账户回调的幅度就会比较大一些了。

板块表现

板块这边其实今天上升的板块还是不少的：必需品、消费、能源板块、公共事业……基本上防守属性比较强的一些板块都出现了上涨。

芯片板块继续回调 5%。罗素价值 1000 应该看呢——罗素 1000 价值板块上涨了 0.3%。大科技下跌了 1%，然后等权指数几乎是在横盘。

所以整个周一出现了一个比较明显的 B 线，而下跌的一些板块主要是集中在过去这段时间做得比较好的（当然大科技除外，大科技相对会弱一些）。

二、市场对加息的最新定价

前面我聊到了市场对于加息这个定价继续在上升，来到了最近这段时间概率最高的一个时间段：

  • 7 月 29 日（也就是两周之后）最近的一次美联储会议，市场已经在预计有 45% 的可能性会加息一次。
  • 9 月份那一次美联储会议，市场现在预计基本上会加息一次。
  • 在年底之前，市场现在预计会加息 1.8 次，接近于两次了。
  • 如果我们继续再往外推看一下 2027 年，那基本上从现在开始去算，2027 年最高的这个点位也就是 2.2 次。

也就是市场预计从现在开始，在未来一年半左右的时间里边，美联储的隔夜利率只会上升 2 次到 2.5 次左右。

三、历史回顾：2018 年鲍威尔的"最后一次加息"

在和大家讲中性利率之前，先讲一件历史上的事件——2018 年年底 12 月份。

当时整个 2018 年是处在一个加息的周期里边，然后因为当时其实也没有通胀上的一些压力，通胀的预期基本上是保持在 2% 左右。

在 2018 年底的时候，其实市场早在 11 月份，两年期的美债收益率就开始下跌。以前我在视频里面和大家分享过：两年期的美债收益率是美联储隔夜利率的一个指引。市场通过两年期美债收益率的下跌或者上涨来告诉美联储：你们接下去应该是降息还是加息。

回过头去看都是一样的：两年期美债收益率的方向领先于美联储的隔夜利率。我们可以理解为市场永远是带着美联储去走。

那在 2018 年的时候，两年期的美债收益率先是从 10 月、11 月就已经开始下跌，也就是市场早就开始定价——美联储你要进行降息了。但是当时的鲍威尔毅然决然在 12 月份决定要加息最后一次，把美联储的隔夜利率最高点从 2.25% 加到了 2.5%。

鲍威尔背后的思考：什么是中性利率？

他背后的思考是：如果长期通胀的预期保持在 2%，那美联储的隔夜利率保持在 2.5%，我们把 2.5% 减去 2% 的通胀预期得到 0.5%，这个 0.5% 我们就叫它为中性利率。

什么是中性利率？ 就是如果美联储把隔夜利率保持在 2.5%，或者说 0.5% 的中性利率，对于经济也好、对于劳动力市场也好，它不会起到一个加速或者放缓的作用——正正好，这就是一个非常好的例子。

美联储背后的思考、他们理解的中性利率到底是多少：在过去起码十几年以来，在美联储的视角里边，中性利率差不多一直就是保持在 0.5% 左右。

那么在通胀的预期上加上中性利率，就等于美联储的隔夜利率、或者美联储隔夜利率这个平衡点到底会出现在哪里。当然这个中性利率很多时候你是没有办法用公式去算出来的，它更多的是一个市场自己能够感知的一个结果。

2019 年鲍威尔的转向

什么意思？当时 2018 年美联储决定最后加息 25 个基点，把隔夜利率的上区间带到了 2.5% 之后，2019 年鲍威尔开启了一个降息周期。为什么？因为它加完那一次息之后，2018 年年底的时候股市开始下跌，然后 2019 年有了川普去逼着美联储去降息，然后有了比较经典的"鲍威尔向川普妥协"或者"鲍威尔 Pivot"，这是一个非常经典的例子。

2019 年美联储再次打开了一个降息周期。也就是说你回过头去看，在 2018 年 11 月份，两年期美债收益率往下跌、市场往下跌，是一个非常正确的决定。因为市场在定价什么？在定价整个经济并没办法去承担你 2.5% 的隔夜利率——也就是说，当时的中性利率并不在 0.5%，只是鲍威尔认为在 0.5%。

四、当下的中性利率：美联储自己怎么看？

带着这样的概念，我们来看一下：在上一次的 SEP 里边，所有美联储成员对于接下去经济的预期给了自己的猜测，或者说给出了自己的看法或观点。

所有美联储成员对于 2027 年和 2028 年（也就是一年半之后和两年半之后），都给出了核心 PCE 到底停在哪里： - 2027 年在 2.5% - 2028 年在 2.1%

然后在这个基础上，他们也给出了美联储的隔夜利率应该在哪儿。把美联储的隔夜利率减去他们看待 PCE 到底能够停在哪里，这个差值就是他们眼中的中性利率。

所以在美联储的视角里面： - 2027 年中性利率在 1.1% - 2028 年中性利率在 1.3%

那我取个中间值就是 1.2%。也就是在当下、在美联储的视角里面，中性利率已经远远不是 0.5% 了，中性利率已经超过 1% 了。这就是美联储他们其实默认的看法。

倒推：加息两次到 4.25% 意味着什么？

所以我们取个中间值，假设中性利率在 1.2%。做一个倒推：假设接下去美联储加息两次（前面我们看到市场预期也是加息两次），现在的隔夜利率上区间 3.75%，加息完两次之后隔夜利率就会到达上区间 4.25%。

那 4.25% 它包含了什么？ - 1.2% 的中性利率（美联储在 SEP 里面所做出的预期） - 2.5%（2027 年通胀的预期核心 PCE）

记住我这边一直聊：我们通常聊通胀的话都是聊核心 PCE，并不是聊广义的 CPI。

包括有人问我：现在市场预期 6 月份的通胀数据、CPI 的数据在负的 0.1%，那为什么要加息呢？首先负的 0.1% 是 CPI 环比的 CPI；其次，负的 0.1% 是环比广义的 CPI，广义的 CPI 里面包括了油价、食品，通胀波动会非常大。所以看广义 CPI 的环比增速是最没有意义的一个衡量通胀的指标。要去看通胀的话，最好的一个指标是环比核心 PCE 的通胀增速。

好了，我们继续讲下：2.5% 是 2027 年通胀的预期，然后你把 1.2 加上 2.5 加上 0.5，基本上就得到了 4.25%。

剩下的 0.5 是什么？ 就是当如果说隔夜利率在 4.25% 的时候，整个货币政策就处在一个紧缩的阶段。所以要把通胀带回到 2% 的话，是需要一个比中性利率更加高的紧缩利率，才能够把通胀给压下来，能够理解吗？所以 0.5% 我们可以把它理解为是一个用来去收缩货币政策的利率。

所以这样看下来，美联储加息两次把隔夜利率加到 4.25%，我认为是非常的合理，而且马上就可以停在 4.25%，然后继续做观察。

五、加息 ≠ 加息周期：为什么这次不一样

那为什么这一次的加息和"打开加息周期"是两个概念呢？我并不认为会打开加息周期。当然这个前提是我不认为通胀会出现反弹。

需要观察的通胀风险

我们现在确实看到了一些通胀的风险，这个风险主要是来自于比如说存储这一块——现在大科技疯狂 AI、疯狂硬件，使得现在的 3C 产品价格出现上涨。

3C 产品价格的上涨会不会扩大？会不会仅仅局限在 3C 产品？如果仅仅局限在 3C 产品的话，OK。但是如果 3C 产品的涨幅开始扩大到其他一些板块，比如说汽车——汽车也需要芯片，汽车芯片价格如果上涨导致汽车的价格上涨，那你再次会影响到商品通胀里面非常大的一部分大头：新车价格和二手车价格。

所以这一块是需要观察，但是暂时来说我并看不到通胀会出现反弹，主要还是服务通胀现在还是保持在一个相对比较低的增速。

一段内心独白：给大科技的一句劝

最后这边是我内心的独白。

我说其实如果大科技可以稍微放缓一些他们资本开支的增速——我说放缓资本开支增速，并不是放缓资本开支。上年 1000 亿，今年预计 1000 亿，我不说明年要变成 900 亿（900 亿比上 1000 亿变成同比负增长了），我并不是要它负增长。而是：你上一年是 500 亿，今年到了 1000 亿，下一年你不需要再翻一倍变成 2000 亿，你只需要 1500 亿、1400 亿，增速从 100% 放缓到 40%、50%，甚至 30%——你稍微放缓一些。

对于很多东西都省事：无论对于当下市场去怀疑 AI 泡沫论，还是 ROI 有没有投资回报，甚至对于通胀风险这一块的影响，包括对于最近证券股价——最近市场杀股价就杀那些"写支票"的人。对吧，你如果写支票，你稍微少写一些，那市场也会对你的股价进行一定的正面反馈。

那么当然这只是我自己的先得性独白，我并没有办法影响到大科技他们这些资本开支。只是我上次有提到过、我在 X 上有提到过：我说苹果很多产品涨价 15% 到 20%，包括那一次宝马也在 X 上说，从来在历史上没有看到过那么大幅度存储这一块的涨价。

我认为对于这些大科技敲响了一个警钟。因为我们之所以看到像现在这样的结果，就是这些大科技他们所有人都在 FOMO 出来的结果。而 FOMO 出来的结果，对于所有在市场里面的人都不是一件好事。

我们最近这段时间，从短期这个角度去看，存储这一块的 FOMO 我们现在已经看到结果了：你加了那么多的杠杆下去，我们就看到最近这段时间杀杠杆的回调。那大科技去 FOMO AI 这一块的资本开支和投资，完全是一样的道理。

六、Chris Waller 的讲话：Forward Guidance 之争

那为什么今天关于 7 月份和 9 月份加息这个概率出现了上升？一部分的原因是因为 Chris Waller——以前我称他为"小鹰王"。在 2022 年的时候，他属于小鹰王，大鹰王是最鹰的那一位叫做 James Bullard，他后来从美联储这边退下来了。

他今天说到——小鹰王 Waller 说到："市场应该尽可能拥有详尽的信息，让人猝不及防并不能改善人们的状况，制造惊喜或突发状况并不是一个好主意。"

与凯文·沃什 vs 格林斯潘路线的对照

他的看法其实和凯文·沃什是有一点相悖的。凯文·沃什的看法——凯文·沃什最敬重的人是谁？格林斯潘。

格林斯潘是非常出名地不给市场指引，"我有可能会做出一个和现在市场预期完全相反或者不一样的决定"。我并不是说这种方法本身是好还是坏，因为起码回过历史去看的话，格林斯潘是非常成功的美联储主席之一。所以凯文·沃什是非常敬重格林斯潘，他要向格林斯潘去学习。

但是如果他这么去做的话，我并不认为市场可以非常好地消化。在过去 20 多年的时间里边，市场已经习惯了美联储会给出答案，但如果现在美联储这个"锚"对于市场来说没有的话，市场就可以去猜。

就像我前面说的：市场已经定价了 9 月份要加息一次，但是最终如果没有一些数据出来去调整市场的预期（我指的是比如说一些比较好的通胀数据，周二要发布通胀数据了），如果通胀数据是 OK 的、中性的，然后中性的数据也没有让市场去放缓对于加息的猜测的话，那到最终在 9 月份之前，市场已经带着一次加息的期待进入美联储会议，结果最后凯文·沃什说"我们这一次不加息"——这绝对会把市场打乱。

虽然说他不加息这个决定，对于市场的预期来说是相对鸽派的，能够理解，因为市场预期加息一次结果他们说我不加息，是相对鸽派的决定；但是这一个行为本身已经打乱了过去 20 年整个的运营机制，而这种运营机制是建立在市场和美联储之间的默契和共识上面，这是非常非常重要的——就是 Forward Guidance。

他现在把这个 Forward Guidance 给去掉，中间的透明度也没有、也打乱掉的话，那市场需要时间去适应。所以我不是说格林斯潘或者凯文·沃什这个想法是正确或者对的，只是它的这种选择和现在市场的运行机制是背离的、是不一样的，所以市场需要去适应这段时间。适应的过程必然带来的是一个波动。

危机时刻的信任储备

包括我们可以想一下：2008 年的金融危机、2020 年的疫情、包括 2023 年的 SVB，如果市场对于美联储没有一个非常好的把握，当有一些危机出来的时候，市场仍然要去猜美联储会怎么去做，或者没有办法完全去信任美联储能够进来救市的话，这中间的信任市场如果没有的话，那这个波动会比以前要大得多。

所以现在很多机构——我看了很多专业的机构，包括 Piper Sandler 这个团队——都认为凯文·沃什会借由这一次 5 个工作小组去拖延接下去美联储在利率政策上的决定。我非常理解他们为什么这样去看，我也认为合理，这是一个比较合理的推测。但是暂时我没有办法说服我自己。

当然了，这一周会有凯文·沃什的听证会，听证会里面也许也有一些额外的信息。但是 at the moment（在现在的话），我没有办法去想象凯文·沃什最终可以和市场反着去做。

当然他如果要坚持走格林斯潘那一套的话，就应该和市场反着去做：市场预计要加息一次，你最后说我不加，因为我认为还没有到那个时间点，我告诉你们我有 5 个工作小组，5 个工作小组结果出来了之后，我才告诉你们我是加息还是保持不变。他可以这么去做，但是如果这么去做的话，等于是打破了以前的默契。

也许他真的可以开辟自己的一条道路——如果敢这么去做的话——对我来说就是一个新的美联储主席、新的一种形式的方式。但是如果真的是这么做的话，那市场需要时间去 adjust 去调整、去适应。

除了前面那句话，Chris Waller 还说：有可能美联储需要打开加息的选择，然后去控制核心通胀。

七、总结与展望

今天希望借由这期视频：

第一点，和大家讲清楚什么是中性利率。因为中性利率本身对于接下去整个加息节奏，起码在现在通胀预期的基础上，接下去加息节奏和幅度会有一个比较好的把握。

第二点，我认为美联储最多加息两次。在加息两次这种情况下，我并不认为对于股市会造成非常大的伤害，短期之内会造成波动、会造成影响。

我在对下半年做展望的时候就说过：美联储这一块是会对于市场造成一定的影响，那最近这两天我们就看到了实际上的影响。所以下半年我认为会有些噪音——宏观也好、IPO 也好、流动性也好，就是以前我们所关注的这些风险可能要回来，对于市场会造成一些影响。

但是美股的核心，我认为还是基本面。那基本面就是反映在 EPS 上面，只要 EPS 的增速没有问题、EPS 的量没有问题，我并不认为美股这一波的牛市就结束了。

八、Investing.com 广告合作

最后是 Investing.com，他们 7 月份这一次的活动优惠幅度高达 60%。这边是他们 AI 的选股策略，有三个以前成功的例子：英伟达、SMCR、Eleven。

其实 Investing.com 这个网站本身、包括他们的 APP 本身，我认为已经是一个非常好的在投资上面的数据来源，而且是一个免费的工具。其实大家即使不订阅的话，我也强烈建议大家可以去下载、可以去用、可以去看。无论财报季度、美联储的一些会议、一些数据本身，上面应有尽有。

在这个基础上，如果想订阅的话，现在这个活动其实非常的好。加币这一块他们有两档： - 一档是 Pro：折扣下来每一个月加币才 9.49 - 一档是 Pro Plus：24.49（如果换成美金的话，可能在 7 点几，可能 7 美金左右吧）

好吧，所以这次 7 月份的折扣幅度还是非常的大。大家感兴趣的话，我会把链接放在置顶的留言里边。

那今天这期视频内容就到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被悄悄改写的"中性利率"：一场没有公告的货币政策革命
这期视频最反直觉的观察，其实藏在数字的位移里——**美联储自己眼中的中性利率，已经从过去十几年默认的 0.5% 悄然抬升到了 1.2% 左右**，翻了一倍还多。这不是一个技术性调整，而是一次"没有新闻发布会的范式转移"。
有意思的是：市场几乎没有为此定价情绪波动。为什么？因为它是通过 SEP 点阵图这种"极其无聊"的方式渗透出来的，没有一个鲍威尔的发布会瞬间。**制度性变化往往不是靠戏剧性事件推进的，而是靠"无聊的年报"完成的**——这一点和企业治理、宪政演化其实同构：真正的权力位移几乎从不发生在头条里。
由此推出的结论也很反直觉：**"再加息两次"在这个新框架下其实是"停在中性利率+缓冲区"，是收敛动作而非加息周期的开启**。市场把加息读作"鹰派信号"，但按这套算法反而应该松一口气。
## 二、AI 资本开支的 FOMO，与 2018 年鲍威尔的最后一次加息，是同一种"路径依赖病"
分析师把大科技的 AI 资本开支节奏（500 亿→1000 亿→2000 亿）和存储板块的杠杆 FOMO 并列，其实指向一个更深的

item.title: [All-In Podcast] The Trillion-Dollar Industries AI Is Disrupting: Voice, Law & the End of the Billable Hour
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的系统性真相：这不是"AI 颠覆行业"，而是"计费单位的相变"

大多数评论把 11 Labs 和 Legora 归为"AI 应用层公司"，但如果你把镜头拉远，会发现一个更冷峻的模式：这两家公司都在攻击同一个东西——按"时间"计价的商业模式。

  • 律师的计费单位是小时（billable hour）
  • 配音演员、客服、销售的计费单位是小时/通话
  • 咨询、催收、冥想教练、甚至 Darth Vader 的授权……都是"时间租赁"

AI 不是在"提升效率"，它在摧毁时间作为价值计量单位的合法性。当 Kirkland 的合伙人自己承认"我们通过高估 associate 的时薪来补贴合伙人的真实价值"，这个模式已经在结构上不成立了——它一直靠信息不对称维系。Legora 的 legal engineer 之所以要"陪伴律所转型"，本质上是在帮客户完成一次从劳动力经济到知识产权经济的心理断奶。

一个让人意外的观察：11 Labs 支付给声音 talent 的 2200 万美元分成，其实是"时间经济"在死亡前的最后一次转生仪式——你花一小时录音，可以被复制到无限次交互中。这不是"给配音演员发工资"，这是把人类劳动力证券化。

二、跨领域连接：11 Labs 的"踏板"和 Legora 的"forward-deployed lawyer"是同一个东西

Jason 反复推销的脚踏板 + Whisper Flow，和 Legora 学 Palantir 搞的forward-deployed lawyer，表面毫无关联，但它们指向同一个反直觉的洞见：

AI 时代的瓶颈不是模型能力，而是"人类如何把上下文塞进去"的带宽。

  • 脚踏板解决的是：键盘输入太慢，导致人类给 LLM 的 prompt 被"疲劳截断"
  • Forward-deployed lawyer 解决的是：Kirkland 合伙人的隐性知识无法通过 API 传给模型

Mati 说了一句被大家忽略但极其重要的话："人们对 AI 说话时比对人类更诚实"（金融催收案例——用户愿意告诉 AI 真实的财务困境，因为没有羞耻感）。这意味着 AI 不只是在"替代人类交互"，它在创造一个人类历史上从未存在过的、无羞耻感的信息通道。

跨界连接：这与心理咨询中的"匿名书信疗法"、天主教告解室的隔栏、日本"树洞邮筒"是同构的。人类需要一个"去人格化的倾听者"来说真话，而 AI 首次以工业化规模提供了这个基础设施。金融、医疗、教育的数据质量会因此发生阶跃——不是因为模型更强，而是因为人类第一次愿意说真话。

三、隐藏的战略反讽：Legora 说"不做 fine-tuning"，恰恰暴露了下一次范式转移

Max 明确反对做通用法律智能模型——"总浪费时间"。但他做tabular review 的窄模型（100 文档 × 100 prompts = 10,000 次 API 调用）。这是一个极其精明的判断，但它同时揭示了一个隐藏的临界点：

当推理成本降到某个阈值以下，"垂直窄模型"和"通用大模型 + 好 prompt"的经济性会在 12-18 个月内交叉。 Max 现在做的窄模型，未来会变成他自己的技术债。他知道这一点——所以他真正的护城河根本不是模型，而是那句被顺口带过的话：

"我们做全球所有司法辖区的立法、判例、监管更新的抓取——这活儿很痛苦，但一旦规模化，就是真正的数据护城河。"

意外的连接：Legora 在做的事，本质上和彭博在 1980 年代做的"金融数据管道"是一模一样的——不是靠算法，靠"愿意去做没人愿意做的脏活"。Bloomberg terminal 至今卖 2 万美元/年不是因为它软件多牛，而是因为它把全世界金融碎片信息统一了 schema。

Legora 不是 AI 公司，是"法律世界的 Bloomberg"。而 11 Labs 也不是语音 AI 公司，是"人类声纹的 ASCAP + 迪士尼授权体系"。他们都在偷偷建立新的元数据基础设施，而竞争对手（Anthropic、OpenAI、Lexis Nexis）都还在旧战场打上一代战争。

这才是"啊哈时刻"：当所有人都在追逐模型能力时，赢家在悄悄地重建世界的元数据层。

📝 转录文本

开场：Airwallex 广告

You're on a bit of a heater, huh?

It's the best time to be building and revenue has surged, but you face really intense competition. Let's go right at that to start.

If you were building a global financial system from First Principles today, you wouldn't build it on 50-year-old legacy rails. You'd build Airwallex, one AI native platform for global accounts, cards, and payments. It is designed to make the entire world feel like a local market. Others are bolting AI onto broken infrastructure, but Airwallex was built for the intelligent era from day one. Stop paying the legacy tax and start building the future at airwallex.com/allin. Airwallex — built for the future.

一、11 Labs 的收入曲线：从 0 到 6 亿美元

350 million in what, 2 or 3 years, and I'm hearing numbers five or 600 million now. Tell us about the revenue ramp of the company from the moment you released the software to today. The product's been in market for 40 months, 50 months, you tell me.

Spot on. We started the company in 2022. First year was all about building the research and the product to really kickstart the work. We built the first text-to-speech model that finally could sound human. Released it in 2023, beginning of 2023. Then it took us roughly 20 months to get to the first 100 million in ARR. Roughly 10 months to get to 200, 5 months to get to 300. And that's how we closed end of last year — and now we are at 600.

You're at $600 million in revenue. This is just extraordinary. How many employees now? Because the company's obviously hit incredible valuations, but you have to fill in that valuation and you're competing at a very high level for talent. So tell us about how many employees you have now and how you maintain the culture of the company when revenue is ripping, investors are throwing money at you, showing up at your doorstep — I mean, quite literally. But you've got to run the company. You've got to build a culture. So how many employees now and how are you dealing with these competing priorities?

二、组织架构：600 人如何维持文化

Yeah, that's the key element. For us, the element of how we can maintain the culture despite the quick growth is critical — how we optimize both the interview cycle, how we are bringing people on board, how we onboard them. With 600 people today, also very quick growth on the people's side.

As a company we combine research and product. We are building a communication platform for AI. On the research side this includes everything across audio — generating speech, transcribing speech, orchestrating speech for interactions. On the product side, this is how we can complete the entirety of the customer journey: from marketing and creating assets and localizing them internationally, through customer support with voice agents, to proactive enablement of how voice agents can help in operations, training and sales.

This requires a lot of different talent, and a part of that revenue growth is actually a reflection of the functions we've grown over time. So from the original team — very research, very engineering-heavy — from the first 10 people we had zero attrition. Everybody is still at the company from those core research and engineering talent building together with us.

So far we've been able to out-compete, and I think — credit to my co-founder who is an incredible researcher himself — we've been able to assemble a team that is truly excited about solving audio, solving interaction, and building that research. If they are looking for an opportunity out there and looking for a company to join and solve that, we are one of the leading, if not the leading, place to do that.

三、软件开发的范式转变：嵌入式工程师

And you started before AI was so impactful at making software.

Right.

So when you were starting four years ago, five years ago and working on this, building software was limited to a low percentage of the population of planet Earth — the number of people who could write code. And now here we are, we went from vibe, we had a no-code moment, then vibe coding, and now we actually have people building production code who are not developers. You have developers going 10x and token-maxing. How has building software changed internally? And how do you deal with making sure that the code is really high quality — because people are paying you this money and are going to demand really high quality product since they're spending so much money with you.

Yeah, it's also true that 2022 was still the year where topics of the day were crypto and metaverse. So it was the best time to start because we could actually take a bit of time to focus on what we thought is the future.

The way we are structured is a lot of small teams — especially across product and engineering, but also in how we think about go-to-market — optimized for specific industries: telco, financial services, healthcare. So every unit is very tightly knit together. It's usually five to 10 people teams that run ahead.

Inside of each of those teams, the decision we took — which is slightly different than how it's usually structured — we embedded engineers in every place, even in the places which aren't engineering. So our talent team will have an engineer, our legal team will have an engineer, our revenue engineering or go-to-market engineering have engineers embedded all across. Those people will have two roles.

One is of course creating automations and bringing the software inside of that team. But second is actually helping everybody else — making sure that people are adopting AI, but also there's a security check for everything they deploy. Because ultimately, if you're not using a lot of the coding software, a lot of the co-working software, then you're probably in the wrong spot. If you're using too much of it, that is also a flag because you maybe are not doing that in the right way.

As you start bringing that into the sites of the organization that never were exposed, they frequently can create but not necessarily review whether that's actually doing behind the scenes all the secure ways. So that's an essential role in the company.

Yeah, it's fantastic that everyone can build software until you put it into production and you have a leak. Or that person leaves the company and people forget they built that software and it's just deprecating on its own.

四、产品经理消失了：AI 时代的岗位重塑

The other thing that seems to have changed is management. When you had 10 developers in your pod or six, you had a UX designer, you might have a pure graphic designer, you'd have a product manager, they rolled up. And then suddenly, you know, we watched over the past 3 years: "Oh, hey, this is pretty good at summarizing what happened on the call. Oh, it is actually creating action items and it's telling us what to do next. Oh, and it's doing all the different stories in our Kanban board." And now, how do you think about product managers and management as the CEO and as the co-founder?

Yeah, we —

You fired them all, right? We don't have any PMs, right?

Did you ever, or have you never did?

Never did.

Okay.

A little bit of what you mentioned was also before the true AI impact started. The ideal person in that role can code, can understand the customer, can understand design. Of course, that's very hard to find — there's no truly that many people that are expert in all of those fields at the same time. So we optimize for profiles that are experts in at least one of those fields but understand at least one other field really well.

To your point, what we are seeing now: if you can do a little bit of all with AI, you can maybe step-change from being an amateur to being an advanced level — maybe not an expert level. So suddenly you are not bottlenecked on all the other functions to do your work. In growth — phenomenal part, growth engineering — a person can design an experiment, ship an experiment, it's working, and bring it back.

We also have the privilege where we are using a lot of our product ourselves. So to be able to do that, ultimately to help everybody else create voice agents, we ourselves need to create voice agents too. We are seeing that also in the non-traditional functions, even in go-to-market. Like, you need to be able to create a version of that — if we are offering that to the customers, too, we do it. We created our inbound AI SDR agent that, in addition to the form that you fill on the website, you have an agent that you can call.

And people can give all the information in a much easier and quicker way, but the second thing that happens is people also leave a lot more information. So you can get connected to the right problem and right person a lot quicker. So we are seeing that kind of phenomenon all the time — where actually using a lot of tooling makes you yourself better in your job overall, and in ElevenLabs' specific tooling that we are solving for customers.

五、语音交互的临界点：从"我要按 0"到"我要 AI 客服"

Yeah, it seems like the use case of calling on the phone and talking to a computer — previously going through voice jail — was incredibly arduous and painful and annoying. It made you just say "operator" and hit the zero button as fast as possible. But now it seems to have turned a corner where talking to a human, I almost feel bad. I'm like, "I am so sorry I'm wasting your time with this," and the AI is just so much more precise and the fidelity is so great that when you tell them what you're looking to do and you cut them off, you don't feel bad, you don't have to make small talk.

Is that what you're seeing in your customer base — in terms of the ability in real time to interrupt the agent, to interrupt the conversation and just move faster — has made consumers and companies basically embrace the technology?

Yeah, it's slowly becoming that you will be asking for: "Give me an AI agent, effective call, AI operator." We are seeing a transition. That's the biggest fuel of the recent growth for us — enterprises, sales team just doing incredible work, but then finally the product combines the reliability that's core with the orchestration for a lot of the AI models, but also the knowledge and the integrations to provide you the right experience.

It was a step change in the last 12 months, and especially in the last six, of how good that experience became. It's like this golden era for consumers and customers is coming, where you're going to actually open a website, call an agent, and have the agent have information from your past interactions and deliver that help.

I think we'll see this kind of interesting phenomenon — combining your previous question and this — where now of course you are reaching frequently when you have a problem and you're asking for help, but ultimately the whole interface will change and morph depending on how you are operating. With voice being — helping you in the background find that information — will shift from reactive to proactive to help you get that help before you potentially ask for it. And we are seeing those examples too.

六、Whisper Flow、脚踏板与"意识流 prompt"

Seemed to me that speech-to-text had a major blocker again in fidelity. 10 years ago, lawyers would put on Dragon Dictate if you remember that terrible software — they'd get a headset. It seemed like the big blocker was you felt like an idiot talking to a computer in an office. And so people who did it quietly in their office kind of got away with it.

But now we see something very different. The whisper in the office — people very quietly talking to their computer, giving it a prompt, talking to their agents. And now there's a ring — you can press it. I use a really cool product called Whisper Flow. I don't know if they use ElevenLabs on the back end.

They use — they use us, and a few others as well. They are doing phenomenal work too.

Whisper Flow is just a tremendous product. And then I got a pedal. Does anybody here use a pedal on their computer? Raise your hand. There's one dork, two dorks. Any others? Raise it high. She's half a dork. Okay, so there's about three and a half dorks here. Next year, this is going to be — do you have a pedal?

I don't. I have —

Have you considered a pedal?

I should consider a pedal. I love the devices that you can wear, and the —

I have the Plaud. It's incredible.

Plaud Pocket. Phenomenal. Like, so good. And especially in events like this, I feel — if you pre-preempted that you're recording, of course. But how incredible would it be that all the signal on the conversations that otherwise disappear — you maybe tap few notes here and there to try to get signal afterwards — if you can just have that automatically fill your specific notes and make sure you do your follow-ups. Phenomenal.

All right, so let me make the case for the pedal. I have three pedals under the desk and I'm trying to figure out what the company is. But with Whisper Flow, you press down, it turns on, and you talk, and then you let it go. And one of the annoying parts of working with an LLM is typing. And you're kind of exhausted when you're giving it the prompt. So you stop prompting.

But if you're a professional artist like me and a talker, this is incredible. Because when I press the pedal down, I just give a stream of consciousness now. And it turns out what these LLMs actually do really well with is taking a massive stream of consciousness where you just keep talking and talking. So I'll give it a one- to two-minute prompt, then I let go, and it has changed everything. Everything.

The whole experience is changing so much. A similar version of what we see happen: you know how you have a thought, and then you're like, "Okay, I actually want to change and say something else." Now you have those two contexts combined and the experience you get as an answer is so much better. So we already see that as an experience.

But even the previous example — people are adjusting how they speak to AI versus how they speak to a human. People are asking —

How so? Yeah, how should you speak to the LLM? We saw Sergey Brin say "threaten it with bodily harm." It's a very effective technique if you haven't tried it. But what are the things that are different when you're talking to the LLM?

七、羞耻感的消失：AI 作为"无人格倾听者"

A specific emotional example. We work with a lot of financial services companies — Revolut, Klarna, PicPay. And some of the frequent cases, not in all of them, is of course how you remind people about payment, or you collect the debt from the people that aren't answering. Frequently, people would naturally feel ashamed of telling the real situation. With AI, people are much more open to share what actually happened, give the information. Suddenly this emotional block of like "in front of another human I don't want to be able to say all of that" is very different.

Usually people are more snappy with AI voice agents. It's like quick responses —

Yeah, you don't mind cutting it off.

Exactly. So you can go through to the point you want much quicker. Which — you needed to change a little bit of the interaction model too, which is working. But we'll work on the pedal and whether we should do that.

八、声音就是身份：模仿、保护与商机

A little bit about celebrities on the platform. You have some celebrities on there. You also have an issue with impersonation. I know this because somebody was like, "Oh my god, I love your bulldog videos." Many people know I'm a big fan of bulldogs. I currently have three. And I said, "I'm sorry, I don't know what you're talking about." And they sent me a channel where somebody had created a bunch of dogs telling jokes and they made one, and I guess they were looking for a podcaster. So they used the This Week in Startups archive and ElevenLabs to create my voice and do this huge channel.

I contacted them and I said, "Oh my god, it's very flattering. How did you do this?" This is like a year or two ago. And they said, "Oh, I used ElevenLabs." So I emailed you about it. I'm like, "How do you protect against this in advertising, in the law in the United States?" I'm not sure about here in France — I'm sure they have 17 laws for this. We have one.

You guys are great at regulations, unless — no offense. And the French guy over here is like, "Oh, mon Dieu!" — that's my French angry developer guy. "I cannot smoke in the Louvre!" This is crazy.

And so it's super interesting with this right to privacy. And I think you got a quick education on this, because you've had a couple people write you a legal letter. What it basically means is you can't take somebody's voice and use it to do commerce in the world. You can use it for parody — there is fair use. I can do a Donald Trump impersonation up here if I like. We're going to take about 5% of ElevenLabs stock — is it okay? Put them in Trump accounts. Sounds good? Okay. And for that you get to come to the White [House]. Okay, thank you.

Nasty guy. Wouldn't give 5%. Loves socialism, but not America. It's the problem with the Nordics. Nasty, nasty socialism.

Then I noticed when my guys wanted to clone my voice so that they could fix the ads where I mispronounce something or I do the wrong promo code — "use the code JCAL20" — they were like, "It's 25, dummy." And I'm like, "Okay, I have dyslexia." And then they redid it, and it was like, "I'm sorry, you cannot clone Jason's voice." And then it's like I have to go in there and do it, and you put a bunch of protections in there.

So explain what's happening in that regard, in terms of people's concerns around this, and then the other side — which is the opportunity, because I think you got Jamie Foxx and some other folks actually that you paid for their voices.

九、三重防护 + 语音 marketplace

Yeah. Voice is identity and IP. When you speak a certain way, people recognize it, can feel that emotion. To some extent, it could be a problem, could be an opportunity. As you did impersonation of President Trump, it's similarly something that is possible even of a human — not specifically AI.

But for us on the safeguard side, over the last years we took the role: as we are leading on our development, we also need to lead on a lot of the safeguards. That's a critical element. We do three things.

One, trace everything that's generated so we can take action when needed.

Two, now we moderate both on the voice and text level. So if you were to input something that would be commercial in nature or would try to scam someone, that gets flagged, we can block it.

Three, because over the last years we've seen the development of those models more broadly — how can we create systems for the wider world so people can upload a sample and get information whether it's AI or not immediately? We do it for ElevenLabs, but we also do it for other open-source models.

The interesting part: given that it's such a good IP and part of your element, it opens up new opportunities. So we partnered with Matthew McConaughey on creating a world —

All right, all right.

And across languages, and it's the first —

I haven't got paid a lot of money for these independent films, but oh, ElevenLabs stock is juicy. Yum, yum.

Could you do it? Could you do it in Spanish?

It's a fugazi, fugazi.

Yeah. But the crazy thing with the AI technology open is that now the voice can be carried not only in English but also in Spanish and Italian and Portuguese, and you can still have exactly that element of emotions coming through. So that's a good example. But we've seen that with MasterClass.

What do you pay these guys? What does it cost to get Matthew McConaughey? Is this like an eight-figure deal, seven-figure deal? You give him a little equity?

Always depends. So MasterClass, for example, is a good example where they worked with talent directly. And here you have — previously — static content that you would learn from. Now you have interactive content. So you have Gordon Ramsay teaching you how to cook in the kitchen. He can scream at you if you're not doing —

Raw scallops raw!

So that is definitely a —

So they're doing characters now, or AI instances using ElevenLabs, so you can interact with them as part of your subscription?

Exactly. But as a company, what we now do — and this from the beginning — we created a marketplace where people can create their voice, we authenticate it, you can share it, and you earn money. Today, we paid back over $22 million back to the community of talent.

Really? Those voiceover actors who got paid as hourly workers — sometimes they get a little back-end if they were doing a commercial or something — now they can spend an hour reading, create an ElevenLabs voice, and then license it out?

100%.

Do they get to pick their price, or you pick the price?

Depends on the model. We do both. You can either give it a default that lets us distribute that slightly more optimally, or you can pick yours. The use case is going to be different. It opens up a set of incredible opportunities in the dynamic context, in other languages.

十、最动人的用例：给失去声音的人重新说话

But maybe a last one on that: voice is such a big part of identity. And probably our most important work was actually working with people that lost their voice due to ALS, due to throat cancer, and working on bringing that voice back.

We worked with congresswoman in the US, Jennifer Wexton, who lost it and wanted to continue to inspire others that you can do incredible work despite that. It was the first speech delivered in Congress.

More recently, I think this was my most heartwarming story: there was a woman who wanted to get married, lost her voice before she could get married.

Oh, wow.

And then they decided to redo the marriage together, do the vows again. And you could see the whole family just — for the first time hearing the vows. You could feel the emotions that you can see in any other any other way, because the voice is such a connecting thing.

Yeah. And you've done it for some iconic voices. My understanding is the estate of James Earl Jones — I'm not sure if he passed. Is James Earl Jones alive? Can somebody —

He passed, right?

Yes. But before he passed, I think he did a deal with Disney and he said, "Listen, for my family, I would like to license the Darth Vader voice for all time to Disney." They gave him some incredible deal. And then they were left with, "Well, how do we actually do this? Do we get a voice impersonator?" But instead, they went to you. Talk a little bit about that deal and how it went down. And is that what they used recently in some of the new films with Darth Vader? There's a new Darth Maul series where they have Darth Vader — did you power that?

I don't know what I can say about the new things. But definitely the big use case — that big completely new experience — was in the gaming space. Fortnite (Epic Games) launched Darth Vader, which people and players could interact with live, in partnership with the estate, in partnership with Disney. So every player after reaching a certain stage could have Darth Vader interact and help you solve the missions.

We are seeing that kind of mode coming up more and more often — how you can effectively extend your likeness, your publicity, into interactive use cases, bring it across the world together. Now we are working on — one of the public ones is Headspace. So Headspace has a great meditation. This is the second greatest meditation app right behind Calm —

Which you are an investor of.

I am. I didn't realize.

Right. I did, and it was a $4 million company. Their team is incredible.

But anyway, you were working with the second place.

Not exactly the second place, but exactly to the working part. They localize a lot of the content, and Calm, I think, is trying some of the interactive elements. Could you have a meditation lesson that's personalized to you? Which we would hopefully love to —

And imagine just, you know, so many voices. "David Sacks is defending Trump. Take a deep breath in. Breathe out. Breathe in. Breathe out."

Maybe you should license the voice to Calm.

I mean, that would be interesting.

十一、前沿模型的合作与竞争悖论
