Let's talk a little bit about being up against some of the greatest entrepreneurs ever who want to take your business from you. Specifically, Dario and Anthropic, Sam from OpenAI. They want your business. They've been pretty clear about it.

I think you have used the frontier models in your product, but you must be thinking, "My Lord, am I enabling my own demise by partnering with them?" And there's all these open-source models. So how do you think about your partnerships with those type of frontier models and the fact that they want to kill your company?

On the first part: given we create a platform, we try to provide all LLMs out there. So our customers can pick Anthropic, OpenAI, open source, Google models. Being agnostic to the specific model is actually helpful, because customers can make sure that they build a harness, build the agent orchestration, create the voice element of how that agent interacts with the world, how the marketing interacts with the world — but they are not dependent on any model. So for us, that part is actually good, because we can provide that to the customers.

On the second big part: of course, the space is overlapping increasingly — models, our platform, applications, everything is becoming a little bit more fuzzy. For us, the still defining piece was focusing on that one layer of: how does interaction look like? How does communication look like? We've been able to out-compete them on voice models, both on text-to-speech, speech-to-text, on the turn-taking, on music. Here, our research team is a set of magicians that are able to continuously do it time and time again.

Part of the reason: on the research side, it's the architecture that matters, not the scale. You really need to change how the model operates. Two, you need very specific data. There's of course a wide set of data out there, but it's unlabeled data. That's where we spend a lot of time. So we built an internal team of over a thousand contractors that label all those audio assets to make them good.

Then as you think about the rest of the product stack, we want to create a fully verticalized solution for that communication angle. The product understanding — the right workflow in financial services is very different to healthcare, very different to telcos. We spend all of our product team to figure out how that works, and those companies don't.

And then ultimately the last piece is the ecosystem. Can you build the wider set of integrations, voices that you use, templates for the agent, authentication that you can benefit from instead of starting from scratch? So far we've been able to create a new mode for that.

Certainly, though, you must be concerned about — hey, the reinforcement learning, the data leakage. They say they're not using your data, but they're kind of using your data. And so do you have an open-source project internally, in case of glass we got to break this? And when do you think you'll be able to discontinue working with them if you had to?

We know that some companies are continuously trying to figure out how to distill and use the data. So that is an existing problem, and we have a few mechanisms to stop it or slow it down — not stop it.

On the open-source question, or creating our own versions: we are looking a little bit closer on how we could use our expertise. We won't focus on knowledge work, we won't focus on coding, but any interaction — and how you can combine all those pieces together and make sure this is great. Yeah, we want to own it. So we are spending more time there.

But it's also just great to be in the arena and compete with those guys, and every so often show that we can do it and do it better.

Yeah, it's pretty clear in my estimation that's where you will wind up. And the ability to make your own language model today — especially with all these great models out there that are now open-sourced — it's going to be pretty easy for a company with your level of resources. So why wouldn't you at least offer it as an option? And then I guess there's cost. I mean, you must be shipping tens of millions of dollars to the frontier models every year.

We ship a good amount. We are good partners with them. But it's ultimately showing up in the value we can create too. So a lot of what we spoke at the beginning of — how we can elevate ourselves as an organization too — is definitely helpful.

I think they've done tremendous work. It's almost crazy that each of us has like a Turing — if you were to chat with an agent now, it feels like the Turing test will be complete. It's as smart as another human. And we hope this year we'll do that same thing for voice — where any conversation feels like you are speaking with another human.

Yeah, I think you're there. It just depends on the application and what question you ask. But it definitely passes it. If we were to look at the tests that were created to define artificial general intelligence, or just to define artificial intelligence, we passed all of those. These were tests that were created 30 or 40 years ago. We need a new set of tests right now. I think the new test is like: can this be more intelligent than every single person on the planet times 10? And if we get anything less than that, we're kind of like, "Oh yeah, it's not smart."

I mean, these things — we're kind of there on AGI, don't you think? That we've kind of achieved it, we just haven't deployed it?

There are definitely places where we did achieve it.

Yeah, for sure. All right. Continued success. Let's give it up for Mati from ElevenLabs. Well done. Thanks for coming out. I'm going all in.

I'm going all in.

十二、Legora：法律科技的爆发式增长

You're growing also at a very significant clip.

Exponentially.

Is it exponential? No, it's not exponential.

Oh, it's a sustained 50% quarter over quarter for the last seven quarters.

50% quarter over quarter for the last seven quarters. Yeah, that's pretty darn fast.

So I think we actually just became — as of the close last week on Tuesday — one of the fastest enterprise companies with a direct sales motion to go from one to 100–150, beating Sierra with one quarter.

Amazing. So people — there's a couple of things in life that people really hate, and paying lawyers is like way up on the top of the list. With your tools, obviously you got your contemporary and Harvey and people — it's just a small company in the States. And then you also have Claude and other folks who also want to be in your business. So this is a big prize to take. I don't know, 80% of what we pay lawyers for and compress it by 90%?

What is the realistic power law here, in terms of making — for startups in the audience — your legal bills dramatically drop in cost?

十三、法律市场的结构：1 万亿服务 vs 400 亿软件

Yeah. And I'm seeing it already in the startup space. I had one startup that hit a million in revenue. They had closed multiple rounds of funding. Obviously, a decent couple dozen employees. They didn't have a corporate lawyer.

No.

And I said, "Whoa, whoa, whoa. You have a million dollars in revenue. Somebody should review the contracts." And they're like, "ChatGPT, bro." And I'm like, "What about the cap table?" They're like, "ChatGPT, bro." And I was like, "Okay, and HR?" And they're like, "Same thing, bro." And I'm like, "Okay, it's going to be a fun diligence target one day."

Well, that's what I said. I said, "Hey, when you do the Series A, they're going to ask that some of this stuff be reviewed." Like, "Do you guys have IP assignments?" They're like, "Yeah." I'm like, "How did you know to do IP assignments?" First-time founders are like, "We asked ChatGPT." And I'm like, "Okay, wow. I just turned into uncle, like, I guess."

So take us through what you think is happening out there. This is not uncommon, right?

But a seed-stage startup operates very differently from one of the biggest banks in the US. And so the way to think about the market — or at least the way that we like to — is you have this enormous bucket of legal services which today is being done manually. It's a trillion dollars every year into legal services, which is very fragmented.

But the software spend into legal technology is about 40 billion. So it means there's 4% software, 96% service — which is bananas. The software piece should be much bigger than that. And so the software piece naturally will grow into the service revenue.

But also, legal is a very supply-constrained market. The demand for legal services is much larger than what there are lawyers or legal services available. And so many of the legal service providers are now using technology to serve new use cases, new market segments, and to actually package new products.

What's an example of that?

An example of that is Cooley — actually, they started serving startup founders directly with a sort of software platform. You just log onto the platform, they pumped it full with their material and their precedent, and then you have the startup material there, and they've embedded workflows that reviews the contracts.

十四、Billable hour 的死亡：律所定价模型的崩塌

What I think is interesting is that it starts to break this model where you charge out associates for very high hourly rates, and you have a billable hour model. Actually, if you look in law firms, the way that business model works is you overcharge for the associates and you actually undercharge for the partners.

I don't know if they're undercharged. I mean, I got a bill recently and it was $1,800 an hour. But for a senior person — I think the associates were $800.

Well, at Kirkland, it can go up to $4,000 an hour. But the thing is: 30 minutes of a Kirkland partner's time when it really matters can be worth a lot more than that.

A lot more than that. If it's bet-the-company litigation, or you avoid a pitfall that would have cost the company tens of millions of dollars, well worth it.

Exactly. But the only way they know how to price that is to overcharge for the associates.

But as you're saying, the enterprises are looking at this and they're going, "Huh, we're spending a lot of dollars on legal services. Let's take this in-house."

Oh, really?

Absolutely. We're doing this partly at Legora. We acquired four businesses so far this year. We did the diligence in-house with our own tool, and the fastest transaction we did was 12 days from LOI to closing.

Because your motivation as the founder is to get the deal done. The motivation of the lawyer is to not have you sue them if they screw up the deal.

Right.

And to make as much money as possible, which means to drag it out. Which means their incentive is — even if they don't say it explicitly — to drag it out. Your incentive is to close it as quick as possible.

Yeah. And I think a lot of law firms are also experimenting with different pricing models where you do a fixed fee for a transaction or for a fundraise. In litigation, you can take a part of the success fee when you win the case.

And so I think it's very interesting how one of the biggest industries in the world is now being completely transformed and reshaped as a consequence of the tech.

十五、律所的心理断奶：焦虑与生存机会

And are those law firms feeling like they're being disrupted, or is this a huge opportunity? And did that switch at a certain point in time, or has it switched for them?

There's a lot of anxiety and a lot of fear. These law firms are enormously profitable and big businesses. Kirkland & Ellis turns around $10 billion a year.

How many lawyers did it have?

Four or five thousand.

Wow.

Per partner, they make between $5 and $10 million every year in profits. So when something like AI comes along, that poses existential threats and existential opportunity. And that's actually a big part of my job — to help articulate with the leadership teams that we work with. Because we will only be as successful as our customers are.

And so we actually have a very unique role at Legora as well, which is called the legal engineer. In the same way that Palantir has forward-deployed engineers, we have forward-deployed lawyers. Their job is to sit down with the Kirkland partners and help them transform their business from a pre-AI to a post-AI world.

And it's sort of like document management and PCs were 20 or 30 years ago, when they were printing out and keeping drafts in a library in a storage facility, and they had to sort of walk them through and hand-hold that.

Absolutely. But I think the difference —

The difference is those were mild productivity gains.

Yeah, this can do a lot of the work. And so it's really reshaping what it also means to be a junior lawyer going into this occupation.

What does it mean? Are those jobs going to still exist, or a lot of the lawyers who are coming out of school going, "Oh my god, was this a good idea or a bad idea?"

The job will exist. The tasks will be different.

In order to have a partner-driven model, you need to bring people up the ranks. In the same way as you do with software engineers — you need to have junior engineers so that one day you can have senior engineers who know what they're doing. But the way to get there is very different. The way of getting there today will not be "lock yourself in the physical data room, read through every single document, mark the errors, and go fax it." It's also no longer just "look in the virtual data room and Control-F." It's orchestrating the agent that will be doing that work.

十六、全球化法律：从州级证书到跨境合规

When you look at that work, you have a global backdrop — attorneys obviously very famously localized, right? Is this going to create attorneys who can operate across borders in a way that didn't exist? And are you starting to see that? Is that something that's built into the product?

Even in the United States, it's state-level certification obviously. Doing a non-compete in the Northeast is very different than doing it in California — they're not very enforceable or enforceable at all in California, as people know, but they're quite enforceable if you're in Boston. So talk about that. Because that seems to be a place where there could be massive gains from AI.

100%. And it's really two things. The data that Legora sits on top of is, on one hand side, the firms' and enterprises' own data — their precedent, their organizational data. And secondly, we do the hard work of gathering all the cases, all the legislation, all the regulatory updates for every jurisdiction in the world. And that is very painful. But once you start to do that at scale, it builds a real data moat.

So in the system, if you are the GC of a company in California and you just landed your first customer in South Africa, Legora can be adapted to the local legislation in South Africa. We actually had a case of this where, instead of having to call a lawyer who then knows a lawyer in that region who will respond to the query, they can get an 80% accurate response immediately that they can start working off of.

The better that gets, the more interesting things I believe you can do. Because this data has really never been structured before, and there are so many people who are working with setting policy and building regulation — and this is an enormous inefficiency in society.

十七、正面硬刚 Lexis Nexis 与 Westlaw

And Lexis Nexis has been a juggernaut and the legacy player — in all the case law and regulations. They have a massive data moat, but they only make a couple of billion dollars a year. And if you put your revenue and Harvey's revenue together, you guys are probably already — just the two of you — making hundreds of millions of dollars.

So they must be looking in the rearview mirror at you like the Tyrannosaurus Rex in Jurassic Park and going, "Holy shit. Like, are they coming for our business?"

And then here you are on stage saying, "Hey, we're doing all the manual hard work of getting that information into what I assume is a proprietary language model." We'll get to that in a second.

Are you going to just try and buy Lexis Nexis? I know it's part of a larger enterprise. Or are you just going to kill it?

Well, I think that some of the existing providers and the sort of legacy players have a really hard time pivoting into becoming AI-native businesses.

Sure.

And they have a really hard time meeting and catching up to the tempo that we run at. They can't get the talent. They don't work our hours. And they're so political in their organizations that it's just hard to move.

At the outset of AI, many believed and made a bet that those organizations who had all the data was going to be the winners. As we're starting to see in the market, that's no longer the case.

I think there's a real opportunity for us to partner with content providers. And we're already doing this in many of the smaller jurisdictions like in Germany, in France, in Spain. The US is peculiar because it's such a duopoly on legal research, as Westlaw is the other one.

Westlaw and Lexis Nexis.

Exactly. But yeah, if you look at how their stock is doing —

Or are they getting priced in with the AI uncertainty?

Yeah.

Yeah, that's one way of putting it.

Yeah, they're getting crushed.

十八、法律数据的"脏活"垄断

And I would assume there's some power law here. They might have an incredible breadth of old case law that they scanned in and went to the courthouses and did all that work on, sent to India to be double-blind typed in. Like, they literally —

You're right. That's what you have to do.

Yeah, they literally had two different people type in the cases or OCR them, then check them, look for the differences. I mean, because you can't get it wrong.

Nope.

But today with the AI tools, the AI tools are really good at doing what they did manually.

Yes.

You still have to ship the books because you have to physically scan. This is very strange in the US, but Westlaw basically has a monopoly with the American government to report on the cases. So they're not owned by the public in a way. They're owned by —

That's crazy.

The company. You guys are very good at capitalism.

Sometimes too good. Sometimes too good. But I mean, Harvard has a project. There's the CourtListener.

They're trying. They're trying.

Yeah, it doesn't work. Or rather, put it this way: you cannot build a legal research solution that doesn't have all of the data.

Because if you go to WilmerHale, and a litigator at WilmerHale — the best law firm in the world — says, "I'm going to use this to go after Elon, or do a billion-dollar case," you better make sure you have all the cases.

So it's the opposite of the power law. You don't just need the top 80%. You actually need all of it.

All of it. Which means you have to go to courthouses and ask them for a copy, to print it out and pay them 10 cents a page.

Well, there's other ways of getting it, but in practice, yes. You have to physically get the books all the way to India. You need to open them. You need to scan them, because you need to get what's called page citations. I never thought in college I would get this nerdy about legal data, but here we are.

十九、Agent 时代的法律工作重塑

What's interesting is that these previous generation of databases were very much: search in the database, find the case, and then the lawyer does their work, right? What's really interesting about especially the agents following the release of Opus 4.5 and 4.6 is they can now start to do really intelligent case strategy. They can actually start to combine the witness statements, the cases, and they can really do end-to-end work.

Which I think is moving us from a world where AI is just augmenting to AI is actually really doing things. And your job becomes to orchestrate and to manage those agents, as we're seeing in coding.

And so you have partnerships with — I'm assuming — Anthropic and OpenAI.

Yes.

And you spend millions or tens of millions of dollars on tokens.

Absolutely.

And they are also competing with you on the margins.

They are not competing in our product category at all. From —

For now.

Well, you know, from the outside, Claude has a legal offering. Yeah, which is basically a bundling of markdown skills files and a couple of integrations. And so I think what's really helpful about that is that it illustrates to everyone how applicable AI is in law.

What it also does is it drives a lot of initial usage there, and then you hit the ceiling or you understand how shallow it is, and then you call us, right? So it's actually a big pipeline generator for us.

二十、模型策略：不做通用 fine-tuning，只做窄模型

Got it. So they start experimenting. Boom. And we were just talking with the CEO of ElevenLabs about — hey, building your own models is pretty doable these days, and every 6 months it gets easier and easier. So are you working on your own models using open source to then fork it and make your own models? Is that the future for your firm?

I don't believe in fine-tuning or building any general intelligence models. I think that's a total waste of time and money.

I do believe in very narrow models for narrow use cases that you also drive a lot of scaling. So you can drive both cost and latency down. An example of this for us is we have a big feature called tabular review, which is basically the number of documents times the number of prompts. So 100 documents, 100 prompts, 10,000 API calls. If you make a fine-tuned model at extracting contract data, it's very applicable there. But it doesn't make sense to build a general legal intelligence model like some of our competitors are attempting.

二十一、合规就是货币：数据泄漏与信任

Yeah. And how do you mitigate against the data leakage issue with your customers? These are highly regulated industries with a lot at stake. So putting in this recent case you're working on in litigation — if any of that were to seep into a language model and then come out the other end, this is disastrous. You have a higher level of responsibility.

And compliance is our currency.

So it's actually one of the reasons why it's really hard to sell into law. There's a lot of legal AI companies and very few are making it through. Not because it's hard to build stuff — it's actually quite easy to understand where you can build value — but getting it to the customer is very hard. But that's something we cracked pretty early on. And once you're in, it's much easier to expand. So that's also one of the driving forces behind our M&A strategy.

But yeah, we're hosting national secrets, weapons manufacturers with their contracts on Legora, and we work with governments.

Does that mean you have to put it on-prem as well?

We don't do on-prem. Deploying in a VPC is very time consuming, and it creates a lot of dependencies which slow down your roadmap and the execution forward.

All right. Continued success. Max, thanks for taking some time for us. I'm going all in.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽略的系统性真相：这不是"AI 颠覆行业"，而是"计费单位的相变"
大多数评论把 11 Labs 和 Legora 归为"AI 应用层公司"，但如果你把镜头拉远，会发现一个更冷峻的模式：**这两家公司都在攻击同一个东西——按"时间"计价的商业模式**。
- 律师的计费单位是**小时**（billable hour） - 配音演员、客服、销售的计费单位是**小时/通话** - 咨询、催收、冥想教练、甚至 Darth Vader 的授权……都是"时间租赁"
AI 不是在"提升效率"，它在**摧毁时间作为价值计量单位的合法性**。当 Kirkland 的合伙人自己承认"我们通过高估 associate 的时薪来补贴合伙人的真实价值"，这个模式已经在结构上不成立了——它一直靠信息不对称维系。Legora 的 legal engineer 之所以要"陪伴律所转型"，本质上是在帮客户完成一次**从劳动力经济到知识产权经济的心理断奶**。
一个让人意外的观察：**11 Labs 支付给声音 talent 的 2200 万美元分成，其实是"时间经济"在死亡前的最后一次转生仪式**—

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Who's Really Funding the War on AI Data Centers?
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

意外的错位：标题与内容的"最小二乘"

首先必须指出一个耐人寻味的错位——视频标题指向"谁在真正资助反 AI 数据中心的战争"这样一个高度政治化、涉及宏观资本流向的议题，而实际转录内容却是一段极简的线性回归入门讲解。这种"标题—内容不匹配"本身就是一个系统性信号：要么是抓取环节出错，要么是内容平台的推荐叙事与实际信息之间存在裂缝。有趣的是，这恰好是线性回归试图消除的东西——残差（residual）：预测与现实之间的距离。你以为点开的是一段关于权力的调查，得到的却是一段关于"如何用直线解释世界"的数学入门。这本身就是一个隐喻。

跨领域连接：广告支出、AI 基建与"斜率的政治学"

讲者用"广告投入 X → 销售 Y"作为线性回归的引入例子。把这个例子平移到 AI 数据中心的语境，会产生一个出人意料的映射：

  • 把 X 换成"数据中心资本开支"，Y 换成"AI 能力增长"，整个硅谷当前的资本叙事就是一条被强行拉直的回归线。
  • 而反对派（社区、环保组织、地方政府）的核心论点，其实是在说：你的模型漏掉了太多变量——电力、水、土地、社会成本、噪音、地缘政治。

换句话说，所谓"反 AI 数据中心的战争"在数学层面其实是一场关于模型规格（model specification）的战争：谁有权定义 X，谁有权忽略哪些遗漏变量偏差（omitted variable bias），谁又能决定哪些成本被划入"随机噪声"。这也解释了为什么这类争论极难有结论：双方根本没在拟合同一条回归线。

隐藏的系统真相：为什么"最简单的模型"最危险

讲者反复强调这是 "the simplest possible case"——但在系统性视角下，最简单的模型往往最具意识形态性。线性回归有三个隐含承诺：

  1. 关系是线性的（现实中很少）
  2. 误差是对称的（现实中常常是重尾分布）
  3. X 是外生的（现实中 X 与 Y 经常互为因果）

放到 AI 数据中心讨论里，这三条假设恰好对应三种被系统性忽视的真相：边际收益递减、黑天鹅式基础设施崩溃，以及"建了就自然有需求"的自我实现预言。

啊哈时刻：你以为在学统计入门，其实在学一整个时代如何用"直线"讲故事。而真正的资金流动、真正的权力博弈，都藏在那些被最小二乘法平方掉的残差里——一旦被平方，方向（符号）就消失了，只留下大小。这也是为什么"谁在资助反对派"这个问题如此难以回答：答案本身就是一个被系统平方掉的负号——它的能量还在，只是被剥离了方向感，看起来像是随机噪声。

📝 转录文本

Introduction: The Simplest Case

I want to talk about linear regression, making the simplest possible case for it.

Setting Up the Data

Imagine you've got some data, X and Y. X might be how much money you spend on advertising and Y might be your sales.

Now, if we plot these points, we might see a relationship where as X goes up, Y goes up.

The Goal: Finding the Best-Fitting Line

The goal of linear regression is to find the best fitting line through these points. This line allows us to predict Y for any given value of X.

Defining the Line

We define this line with two parameters: the slope and the intercept.

The Method: Least Squares

By minimizing the sum of the squared differences between the line and the actual data points... ...we find the line that best represents our data.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Latent Space] The AI Memory Problem: Why Long Context Isn’t Enough — Dan Biderman, Engram Co-founder & CEO
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Engram 不是在"改进记忆"，它是在把 LLM 从"失忆症患者"变成"有肌肉记忆的厨师"

Dan 反复用厨师做比喻，但他真正戳破的东西比比喻本身残酷得多——当今所有 LLM 都是电影《Memento》里那个每天醒来重看笔记的 Leonard。这不是"上下文窗口太短"的问题，而是一个几乎被 AI 圈集体忽视的认知科学真相：人类记忆至少有两套系统——陈述性记忆（notes/RAG/context） 和 程序性记忆（weights/intuition）。过去两年整个行业疯狂扩大"陈述记忆"的容量（100 万、1000 万 token context），却几乎没人在动"程序记忆"这一层。

这里有个惊人的历史回响：认知神经科学早在 1995 年就有过一次几乎一模一样的辩论——McClelland 的"Complementary Learning Systems"论文论证海马体（快速学习、易冲突）必须与新皮层（缓慢学习、稳定泛化）互补，否则会发生"灾难性遗忘"。Engram 正在给 LLM 补上"新皮层"，而整个行业过去两年只在扩建海马体。 Dan 的 PhD 是计算神经科学出身——这不是巧合，而是他看到了别人看不到的层。

二、被埋在闲聊里的一个准 Prophecy：18 个月后每家公司都会拥有"互联网量级"的私有数据

Dan 在切洋葱时随口说出的这句话，是全场最容易被低估的判断。它的含义是：

  • 今天的 "enterprise RAG" 假设是私有数据 << 公共互联网，所以只做检索、不做训练。
  • 但当 agent 开始为员工生成代码、文档、演示、会议纪要——每家公司自己就变成了一个 mini-internet，而且是高信噪比、高专有性的那种。
  • 这意味着 "pre-training on the internet" 不再是频率最高的训练模式；"post-training on the company" 才是。

跨领域连接：这正是 18 世纪印刷术革命的翻版。 印刷术出现后，最贵的不再是"复制文本"（对应今天的 inference），而是"整理档案与建立索引"（对应今天的 continual learning）。18 世纪谁掌握了目录学与档案分类，谁就掌握了权力——梵蒂冈、大英图书馆、法国国家档案馆的崛起就是这段历史。Engram 押的正是"AI 时代的档案学"——不是让模型读完所有档案，而是让模型内化档案。

三、"效率 = 智能"——一个被商界严重误解的物理学命题

Dan 最有分量的一句话被夹在最后一个煎锅片段里："efficiency and intelligence cannot really be decoupled." 这不是营销话术，是热力学。

  • 生物大脑的智能上限被"每天 20 瓦"的能量预算严格约束。
  • 进化没有选择让人类拥有 100 万 token 的"工作记忆"——而是选择了极致压缩 + 灵活提取。
  • 一个人一天摄入约 10^11 bit 的感官信号，能长期记住的不超过 10^6 bit。压缩比一百万倍——这正好是 Engram 声称 cartridge 相对于 raw context 的压缩比。

最出人意料的连接：Dan 举的例子——Llama 70B 读一篇 Taylor Swift 维基词条要占 80GB HBM，而 Llama 70B 整个模型权重才 140GB——这个数字如果推广到全社会，等于说人类每次"回忆一件小事"都要重新加载整个大脑皮层。这不仅是工程效率问题，它意味着当前 LLM 违反了神经经济学的基本原则：大脑绝不会为一次查询付出全皮层激活的代价，它有 sparse attention、有 memory consolidation during sleep（！）、有 predictive coding。

所以真正的洞见是：Engram 的"cartridge"其实是在给 LLM 补"睡眠"——把白天读的东西固化成权重，第二天不用再重读。而"context rot"其实就是 LLM 版本的睡眠剥夺症候群。这个视角一旦切换，你会发现整个 AI infra 领域正在悄悄从"更大的皮层"转向"更好的睡眠架构"——Test-time training、continual learning、cartridges——它们全都是 AI 睡眠周期的组成部分。而 Dan 的公司名"Engram"本身就是心理学术语：记忆在神经系统中的物理痕迹。名字里就藏着答案。

一个额外的"啊哈"——关于以色列 Founder 的隐藏模式

Dan 不经意间点破了 Israeli Special Forces → Founder pipeline 的真正机制——不是纪律，不是勇气，而是"18 岁就要为预算跟成年人谈判"。这本质上和 YC 训练创始人的方法一样：过早暴露在 stakeholder management 环境里。这解释了为什么 Wiz、Engram、Palo Alto Networks 的创始人不像 20 出头的斯坦福辍学生那样"极客感"，而是像 30 岁的成熟经理——他们在别人还在写作业时就已经在做 B2G sales pitch 了。这是一个被硅谷 pattern-match 严重低估的"人力资本套利"。

📝 转录文本

开场：让创始人下厨

Host: I'm too absorbed in the cooking, man. I'm sorry. I'm fried. Um, the entire show I pretended to be the cooking expert here. It has to be pure crazy. Just kidding.

Hey guys, welcome to the Latent Space Cooking Show where we invite founders and researchers and let them cook. Today we have a very special guest, co-founder and CEO Dan Biderman of Engram.

Dan: Nice to be here.

Host: Yes, thank you for coming. Um, I heard — or we got connected through Jack — who was saying that you're a very good cook. And again, congrats on the $98 million seed round. Do you cook often? How frequent is it? I assume you're constantly researching and building.

Dan: Yeah. So, I would say since I started the company, there's a little bit less cooking of this type. Uh, cooking other things, but cooking is something I've been doing since I was a kid. My parents cook, my grandparents used to cook, and it's a way for me to kind of relax and digest things and think things through.

Host: Great. Yeah. Well, I'm very excited. Today's a little different where we're actually going to have Dan lead us through one of the recipes. Do you want to reveal to us what we're making?

Dan: Yeah. So, I was thinking we can make beef meatballs with some vegetables and have them swimming in some kind of white sauce with white wine and chicken stock. It's a pretty nice Mediterranean type meatballs that I've kind of stumbled upon recently when visiting my parents in Tel Aviv and cooking stuff with what they had at home. So, I thought it would be nice to redo this with you and see how it feels.

Host: Great. Well, I'm very excited. Do you want to kick us off?

Dan: We're going to fry the onion. I'm going to chop it and going to chop some garlic and then we'll make them into balls and fry them up.

Host: Fry them. Great. Okay. Well, let's get started. We'll both do it in parallel. Awesome.

Dan 的背景：Tel Aviv、海军特种部队、计算神经科学

Host: But yeah, I guess to start, I'm kind of curious about your background. So, you wanted to become a professor, if I recall correctly, and you also have experience in the Israeli military or special forces. How did that turn you into becoming a founder of an AI research company?

Dan: Yeah. So, I guess it's easy to connect the dots looking backward, but in real time, it was not. I didn't grow up planning to be this founder NSF working on this hot topic in AI. Things came to it.

So I grew up in Tel Aviv and, as you said, was an officer working on special operations — specifically naval special operations — which was very entrepreneurial. It was basically finding and identifying crazy things we could do and proving to people that we should scale resources and people to actually do them. So it was a lot of researching, a lot of pitching, and then a lot of hard work to make things real.

And then after I finished this, I went to university and studied cognitive neuroscience in Israel in an interesting program where a lot of Israeli novelists and professors and scientists and even chefs — you know, the Israeli chef — went to this program many years before me. So I went there, got really curious about the brain, curious about human behavior, curious about statistics.

And then I went to New York to do a PhD in computational neuroscience, which is a community that's been one of those stubborn communities that cared about neural networks for many decades before it was a hot topic here in the Valley. So I was there and studied with some statisticians and physicists and biologists trying to understand how neural networks can be used to understand the brain, to understand animal behavior.

And then decided that I wanted to go deeper into the LLM world after seeing ChatGPT. So started working at Mosaic, where I worked on LoRA and met a bunch of great people. And then from there I went to work with Chris Ré at Stanford and Scott Linderman, who both are co-founders of my current company — and where I met my co-founder Sabri and my other co-founders Jack and Jesse, who work on very similar topics from their labs in Cornell and Berkeley.

以色列特种部队与 Founder 文化

Host: No, it seems like a very research focused and also serendipitous. Going back a little on your background — is there something about the Israeli special forces? Because I think it was a Wiz co-founder, right, who are also ex-special forces.

Dan: Yeah. Uh, we work pretty closely with Assaf Rappaport, the Wiz CEO, who's a mentor to me.

There's interesting things going on there. I would say the most important thing is actually — when you're 18, it's joining the intelligence or taking on responsibility. The interesting bit about it is that you're not just in a student mode, you're not just heads down doing exams and projects. Rather, you interact with grown-ups and you have resources and you argue for your budgets and things like that. And it forces you to kind of build a little bit of social skills, a little bit of maturity, which I benefited from.

You know, there's a reason many countries in the world choose not to have everyone go to the army. It's a long time and it's a package deal. And I was in the Israeli army in different years, not the ones that are the recent ones. So it's not like I recommend everyone goes to the army, but I would say that it forces you to think about other aspects of your personality, not just the intellectual ones.

And also generally, I would say Israel as a culture is a place where you get multiple shots at goal. If you're not the best in high school, you still might have a good position in the military. And if you're really good, more doors open up for you for university. And even if you didn't do anything super interesting in military and got to university and found out you have good and deep inclinations in science and engineering, you can be the most successful person. And we have some of those Israelis who are leaders in various academic and technological places.

So I would say — the cards are dealt multiple times, more emphasis on social aspects, more emphasis on being mature, on being a team player. But again, it comes at a price that you're older when you get to things. So I started college when I was 24, which is a mid-PhD in an American university. But yeah.

Host: So it makes sense. I think it's great how it gives you a lot of shots on goal and is a very beautiful culture.

为什么盯上"上下文"这个问题

Host: And now fast forwarding to today back to Engram. What kind of prompted you to focus on this issue with context? Were there specific breakthroughs? Was it, you know, the performance of open source models or even just the issue of context rot, long horizon agents? Or was it just that you're very passionate about this area as a whole?

Dan: Yeah, I'll answer that in a second. Can we heat up some?

Host: Yes, let's start getting going.

Dan: So yeah, my PhD was focusing on a field that's not super in vogue today, but I think will become super crucial again, which is semi-supervised learning — you have very little data, you don't have infinite trillion tokens of data. You have a very small set of examples and from them you want to extrapolate and learn efficiently and achieve things more generally. So how to be data efficient.

So my whole academic work — basically all of my papers had the same plot: on the x-axis I had some cost, some resource, and on the y-axis I had accuracy. A sort of Pareto curve. And that's been my upbringing: efficiency, how to do more with less.

Um, salt. Yeah. Yeah, we can put some salt.

And I went to Chris Ré's lab at Stanford and there worked on agents — on ideas called Minions — and asked the question of how can we have agents interface with very large corpora of knowledge in a way that's economical. And when we introduced some patterns that involve sub-agents a bit before it was popular.

Oh, sorry. We can also put this — I realized.

And from that perspective of efficiency and of cost, we started asking ourselves: is there a more efficient way to interface with data that's not just agentic orchestration and things like this? Can we actually use the magic of training to have models that are efficient, faster, and can operate with fewer tokens?

Some of these ideas were developed in parallel by some of us — Jesse, Sabri, myself. And the common theme was that if you have a very large corpus of knowledge and you allow the model — you give the model time to study it in advance to basically ask itself questions, give itself quizzes, try to solve problems, and then train it using gradient descent in the same way you would pre-train a model — then you can create very compact representations of your context that you can later load in.

We call those cartridges. These are like capsules of knowledge you can load in and out of the model that are like a brain state that describes the model's world and the corpus in a way that's maybe a thousand-x more compressed. And when you use them, you can use far fewer tokens, be less confused, more accurate, and things like this.

Host: And these are task-specific cartridges?

Dan: So they can be corpus-specific — like documents inside a company. They can be task-specific — like you want to learn a certain skill.

厨师的比喻：Notes vs. 直觉

Dan: But the idea is that in many cases it makes sense to go beyond textual representations. So when we're cooking now and we have all these great textbooks or cookbooks here — Samin Nosrat, you know, the book from French Laundry — these are amazing books that I respect. But to say that because we have the books and we can read them, we deeply know how to cook to the same level of Samin Nosrat or Thomas Keller — it's incorrect, right?

We can read those things and we can repeat all their steps in a very robotic way. And current LLMs are like coming into the kitchen first time every time, reading the textbook, cooking the dish, measuring everything — but they don't have the intuition of a chef that's pinching salt and kneading dough and things like this.

So the kind of thing we're after with this training and creating those cartridges is this kind of intuition in the models that goes beyond notes and recipes to the kind of intelligence that allows you to come up with the next recipe — thing that hasn't been explored before — to do the next move and the next extrapolation.

Host: Yeah. I guess double clicking on building this intuition, could you contextualize it more on how it would be different from extracting — let's say using a cooking example — if you get all the useful notes and useful section from a cookbook that will help you actually understand all the complexities of making a dish, just providing it, I guess that would be similar to RAG, getting just those chunks and then understanding that in context and then providing an output.

Dan: Yeah. So this is an excellent method. Um, we do not take the bet — I'm putting two eggs in here if that's okay. And is this enough grated carrot and zucchini?

Host: Yeah, excellent. You can put it in here.

Dan: So we're not taking the bet that no notes need to be taken, right?

Host: Yeah. Put all of it inside.

Dan: All the greatest chefs in the world — they have notes and they have books and they have diaries and they document what worked and what didn't work with their experiments. But they also have brains and they have hands and a tongue that can remind them how something tastes and what worked and what failed and what was easy and what was hard.

So in all of our work we never say that textual representations are useless. We basically use them all the time and we construct those wikis and knowledge bases and things like this. At the same time, what we say is that the layer above those — the layer of intuition and learning that is in the form of numbers of parameters — is the full experience of the human chef. The best chef in the world is all the notes combined with a nervous system that reads those notes and can implement them and innovate them, and maybe don't return to the same notes over and over again. There are some dishes where they don't need to reread them.

And the current problem — we want the best of both worlds. Every knowledge worker, if they can't write notes and cannot document the events of the day, they would be at a disadvantage. But if you wipe their brain every evening, they would also be at a severe disadvantage.

18 个月后：企业内部数据将达到互联网规模

Dan: So we want to have the best of both worlds. And the thing is that textual representations can take you a long way. But the thing we're thinking about is: look at the rate at which knowledge is being created — with now agents working on behalf of knowledge workers creating artifacts, code, documents, presentations. I think that people don't fully comprehend the size of the knowledge workspaces they will deal with in 18 months.

In 18 months many companies would have maybe trillions of tokens of internal company proprietary data. I'm talking about like maybe trillions. It sounds exaggerated, but I don't think it's an impossibility if they're really AI native.

And what are trillions of tokens? When I was at Mosaic 2–3 years ago, we called this internet-scale data, pre-training data. So imagine every company has data that is basically internet-scale data.

Um, I might need salt and pepper and the breadcrumbs for this. And we don't have a sink here, so hands will be dirty. I hope the audience here is forgiving.

Host: Got to get your hands dirty. Yeah. Awesome. I guess the principle here I once heard from a chef is that a ratio of one-to-one of meat with everything else is usually a healthy way to make meatballs.

Dan: Yeah, that's fair. We are salt and pepper.

Host: Nice meatballs. They're a bit warm with those fried…

Dan: We're back with the — we're back with the fixed meatballs.

Host: With the fixed meatballs. It's team building activity here.

Dan: Yes.

Host: Let's put those spices that you brought. I trust you on these ones. Cumin. Let's put a little bit. Not a ton.

Dan: Yeah. We won't dump it like last time.

Host: Cumin is my wife's favorite spice.

Dan: Oh, yeah. Cumin is great.

Host: I have mixed feelings about it, but okay.

Dan: Good. So, we have the meatballs. Now is the time to make the balls.

Context Rot、Compaction 与"重脑权重"

Host: So, we were talking about — what were we talking about? I think I'm too absorbed. I'm too absorbed in the cooking, man. I'm sorry.

Dan: No, it's good. You were pitching things here. You're talking about how companies will have a corpus of data that will be at the scale of the internet.

Yeah. And maybe today some don't have it and maybe today you can go relatively far with textual representations. They're also interpretable. They're very good. But I just think at a certain scale even those textual representations will be hard to make. If you have trillion tokens, how do you create exactly a wiki or an index of those that you keep updated all the time? How big is this knowledge base that you create? How expensive will it be to process it with frontier models that know nothing about your company?

Host: So is it just that using frontier models will be expensive because they start from scratch every time?

Dan: So there's the element of expensive because you reread more things, you consume more tokens. That's one. But two is: for the agentic tasks of 18 months from now, inside those major repositories of knowledge and asking the models more and more things in underspecified ways — I suspect that the accuracy of the models would go down. The phenomenon of context rot, right? The model has to read more, it will be less accurate. And we know this. And it will remain the same thing at even the 10 million context window scale.

Host: Okay, that makes sense. Okay, let's go wash our hands real quick. We'll be right back.

Dan: Great.

Host: And we're back. Hands are all clean. So, now what's the next thing? Just frying the meatballs.

Dan: Yeah, let's fry those meatballs.

Host: You want to turn this one? You just press it and then turn it. Very intuitive.

Dan: And we'll put some oil.

Host: Okay. We could put these in.

Engram 真正要解的问题：长链 Agent 与 In-Weight Learning

Host: And while we do that, I guess more on the question of long horizon agents — what's the main issue that Engram is trying to solve? So right now if I were to steel-man the opposing view: maybe company internal data isn't actually big enough where I'd need to put it into the weights. What's the issue with just having RAG, or having specific models or even cheaper models since open source models are also very performant to handle a lot of tasks?

Dan: Yeah. So I would say a thorny question in the research community is: can you come up with an example where only in-weight training would work, where in-context learning will fail?

And turns out it's very hard to devise such examples. And for every example you give, someone can ask, "well, what happens if the next payable has a 10 million context window?"

And the way I see it — and kind of my scientific upbringing — I see all of these questions of continual learning and memory as questions of long context in disguise. If the models could see whole company data and in principle would have this infinite context window, what then is the limitation?

So the limitation is twofold. One is that we know even at very small scales that the more context you feed to the model, the more confused it gets. It's called the context rot. So you can feed in a certain number of tokens into the model and not get an error, but it doesn't mean that the model can reason in a holistic way about them. That's one thing.

Host: And what's the problem with compaction? Is compaction not as useful, would you say?

Dan: So I think compaction is improving by the day. And compaction for those in the audience who don't know what it is — it's like models actually managing their own context, evicting certain tokens and keeping others. Compaction works. That too — when you go into a longer horizon, compaction by definition is lossy. You discard some and keep other things. And I think it's a correct way to go. But it's also very deterministic — either you're in or you're out — in the current versions of compaction. Show these kinds of issues when very deep into the session you can get confused and forgetful.

So we think compaction will be part of the story. We think another part of the story is some sort of neural memory trace which too is a lossy thing — it evicts some and keeps some — but not in the text representation, in the weights representation.

But I would say the main thing we're trying to solve — the main thing for which continual learning is needed — one is token efficiency and cost, which is a major issue that wasn't actually an issue when we started the company late last year and became more urgent now. And problem number two is: if you can do the same thing with fewer resources, when you scale up to very large resources, suddenly you can take on tasks that were previously not possible. Way more long horizons, way more adaptive. And we're not quite there yet. We're focusing right now on the first component, which is getting these models to reason on large context with fewer tokens and do it in a way that's less confused.

But we think that eventually part of the solution for very hard tasks in science and engineering and defense and all that stuff will involve some form of gradient-based updates during long horizon tasks. Some people call this test-time compute or test-time training — these are just different names for the same thing.

KV Cache 的荒诞：Taylor Swift 词条 = 80GB

Dan: I think we have a proof of existence from pre-training that you can pack a lot of information in very few numbers very efficiently. So the examples we like to give: if you take a Llama 70B model and you load one article from Wikipedia which is a few tens of kilobytes, and you have the model read this — the brain state of the model when reading these few tens of kilobytes is like 80 gigabytes, 80 gigabytes on the HBM of the GPU.

Host: Yeah.

Dan: It's an insane amount. And the entire set of parameters of this model would be like 140 or so gigabytes FP16. So those 100 or so gigabytes with some distortion represent the entire internet. And this one article about Taylor Swift is like same order of magnitude memory consumption on the GPU. So it's highly memory-inefficient.

That's a systems problem. That's the KV cache monstrosity that the smartest people in the world are trying to solve from the chip side of things and from the software and kernel side of things.

So I would say another way to look at what we're doing from that angle — from the systems angle — is basically: if we can get a bit more technical, instead of doing those prefills where the model is reading and reading and reading a corpus, we are kind of like — we're destroying prefill. We're scaling training compute in some other time so we can load the thing into the model and it can just immediately start decoding, or prefill a little bit. And this kind of goes hand-in-hand with trends in how data centers are built out — disaggregating prefill and decode and doing this on different specialized cards. And this is part of our initial interest.

具体用例：Harvey 与法律/M&A 场景

Host: So, it seems like we're mostly ready here. I think it's a good time for our white wine on both of them. You want to do that? And do you have any tangible examples on this when you're talking about your approach? So if you think about, let's think for example about an enterprise or a firm or investment banking, a law firm or investment banking — they can have many client matters, uh many like a lot of knowledge work.

Dan: A lot, yeah. So many client matters — they have various clients, clients do financing, mergers and acquisitions and things like this and take loans and do deals.

You want to put this on?

Host: Not yet. No, not yet. We want to let it reduce a little bit.

Dan: Okay. Yeah. And so, for example, these are the kinds of things we work with Harvey — very very large file systems. And there's many queries that agents might run into. Either humans ask them or the agents have to solve them, which are these kinds of ambient hard questions that are not easily searchable with RAG.

For example, if you want to ask: "Which M&A deals haven't we completed this year?" To actually solve this problem, you have to go client matter by client matter, read all the files. You can't read in any place that it was not completed. You have to take the gist. You have to understand: the thing hasn't — the loop hasn't been closed. Thing hasn't been completed.

And now you can solve these tasks with Frontier models and compaction. And when you ask them to do so, they will consume thousands of dollars for queries that we think are harmless — that every employee in the company would be able to answer.

So this is just one example. But these kinds of holistic things — you can get the gist if you read everything, but you can't find a thing in one thing. The kind of queries where the whole is greater than the sum of its parts. That's where this magic of training comes in. And that's the magic that, you know, Ilya and others have shown us with pre-training, right? You learn the entire web in these sets of weights and suddenly the model can infer things, can generalize, can interpolate and extrapolate to new things. And that's the kind of knowledge we want to do.

And it's not a coincidence that we're training on the entire web and we're not just doing RAG over the entire web or putting it in the catalog and reading it in time — because we do think that learning from a lot of knowledge somehow creates these associations in the model.

Host: Gotcha.

Dan: So some of it is concrete problems of now. Other parts of it are bets that in 18 months from now, the scale of the data will require the methods that we know from pre-training work.

每个人一套权重：Tamagotchi 模型

Host: Gotcha. And so are you doing parameter-efficient fine-tuning for specific companies, like LoRA-based off of the corpus?

Dan: Yeah. So our ambition in the long term — but also start. Yeah.

So our ambition in the long term is that every person has a model — or a part of the model, or a set of weights — that represents their knowledge, their expertise. Learns from them. That the more time they spend with the model, the better it gets for them. The more data they give the model, the better the model is for them. They control those sets of weights. It's theirs.

Um, and the rice. Uh, and they're incentivized to — let's let it boil a little bit now. So they're incentivized to — we can put the spices. So they're incentivized to make it better in the same way that you would work with a Tamagotchi. The more you nurture it, the happier it is. And that's the kind of thing we would like to create with models. So that's the ultimate continual learning. And we think on the extreme scale, on the single-human scale.

Turns out this is not just a research problem. That's a major infra problem. And in the long long term, I do think these things will actually run on people's devices. We're seeing right now the new hardware on personal computers is already, you know, soon approaching the ability to run inference on close-to-trillion parameter models — which will be very interesting for that kind of personalization.

But in the shorter term we do think that the kind of large corpus of knowledge, very dense with a lot of expertise, can be found in the enterprise. That's where people spend most of their time. That's where AI is actually being used the most. So we go there, and that's our bet. And there our bet is that parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods — like LoRA, like cartridges, like memory layers, different things we contributed to as a research team — can actually represent knowledge and can be combined with other methods that are like context management, traceable methods that people can use and understand and audit.

So it's a combination. Again, it's like the chef that has the cookbook and has the recipes, has the diary, but also has the nervous system that learns from every session.

权重里存什么，Text 里存什么？

Dan: So it's boiling here.

Host: It's all — yeah. Pretty efficient here.

Dan: Yeah, very powerful stove.

Host: Great. And then medium, low, then wanted.

Dan: Let's mix it up. Yeah. Salt. Did you put some salt?

Host: Here, I'll put some salt in. Um, we're going to have yellow rice courtesy of Alan.

Dan: Yes. Um, great.

Host: Well, we're nearly there. With what you guys are attacking, how do you determine what should live inside of the weights, what should still kind of be handled with RAG, um where things should be orchestrated?

Dan: Yeah. So this is a major open question I would say for us and for everyone else. And it's not just an open question in AI and in a startup. It's an open question in the study of human memory from its inception. Like, what kind of knowledge should be internalized and what kind of knowledge should be externalized?

Does it make sense for you to remember everything you've seen as a person? People who have that capability often are not enjoying that capability. It can be very distracting. It's sometimes very scary. So a certain amount of forgetting is healthy. Certain things you want to remember in the weights and certain things you want to put in text.

So I would say it's an open research problem. The way to work on it is to train models — both to train models to manage it themselves — and that's an active area for us. Have the model know, without any explicit supervision signal, to determine: this kind of stuff I can pull from my brain, and that kind of stuff I rather keep in notes.

And you can imagine these kinds of things relate to like the saliency of certain parts in the data, how often, the frequency at which they repeat, and the affordances of like: what can you do when you know this fact from your brain? You can imagine that remembering, I don't know, remembering your room number in a hotel for tonight is less important than remembering your partner's phone number or remembering your address.

Host: So it's higher signal data.

Dan: Higher signal data. And now the thing is: if you start manually, heuristically saying "this is in, this is out," then it becomes a whack-a-mole. Every person in every enterprise has different data and you can't very easily pick and choose what goes in, what goes out.

So the holy grail is: have the model learn for itself. Have it operate with a notebook where it can take notes, have it operate with a brain — associative parameter-efficient thing that it can read from — and have it decide when to go to each, and do this with training in an unconstrained way. And we're working on it and more breakthroughs are needed. But I think that is the dream: that the model learns what comes in and what comes out and we get out of the way.

用户反馈的意义：Thumbs Up 是不是空转？

Host: Gotcha. Okay. And so that's the end goal you want to achieve — where it's completely autonomous. So there's no human in the loop to kind of tell it what to fetch?

Dan: The human in the loop can be the user. And if the user chooses to say "keep this in" or "keep this out," we would like the model to listen to the user. But we would not like to depend on the user, right? So user feedback is something we can learn from — we can learn from implicitly — but we don't want a person supervising every step because that's not the way people enjoy using language models.

But I would say the kind of models that we're building — unlike other models where you do thumbs up and thumbs down and you're basically helping the provider maybe in the next version give you something that's more workable — here, if you give a thumbs up or thumbs down or you say something, you know that someone is going to scale compute on what you said, and someone's going to go and practice to get better at what you said.

And this is kind of the thing we want to get to — like building trust with the user that they're listened to and they're making a model that's better. And it's not generally better for everyone — it's better for them.

Host: Gotcha. So the benefit is it being a tighter loop compared to, like you said, a general model provider — the UI having a thumbs up thumbs down, you don't know if that's actually going to contribute to the feedback.

Dan: Yeah, there's tighter loop and we use different machinery. If we allow ourselves to use the machinery of training, we know we can hammer that in. There's no uncertainty about it.

And another important thing to say is: not everything that a user tells you is ground truth, right? Not all of us, including myself, are Einsteins, and we can say things to the model where we think we're right and the model is wrong. And increasingly the models will get better and increasingly they'll know more things than we do. So the model in some way has to learn and understand and kind of discern which feedback is valuable and which feedback should be ignored. But I think the holy grail is to get out of the way and have the model learn it if you define the right objectives for training.

Host: Gotcha. Okay. So get out of the model's way.

Token 效率就是智能

Host: Have there been any moments that have really kind of shown you what is still needed, or what's still possible? Because it seems very ambitious to be in an end state where a model autonomously can handle this all. Um, and I think like multi-agent setups and even right now too — just constrained to coding — still has problems. And so I guess when you get to knowledge work and to specific enterprises, it seems a lot higher stakes. You can't make as many mistakes. And so have there been moments or even research topics that you're kind of seeing great progress in right now that can get you there?

Dan: Yeah, I would say we will share more of our results in the coming weeks and months and we're kind of keeping keeping it. Yeah. But I would say the theme, and the kind of thing you're seeing — we're not the only ones seeing it, but we're looking very closely into it — is behaviors around token efficiency: the ability of the models to go where they need to go and solve things faster.

Basically, it's this kind of view of intelligence where getting smarter means exerting less energy to solve increasingly harder problems. And these are the kinds of things we're going for. A lot of it comes into life in the form of efficiency and speed, but we will share more of that soon.

模型路由：不是取代，而是像最好的助手

Host: Okay. I guess on the token efficiency point, do you also heavily consider like model routing? Because I assume there may be some cheaper models that may do the same job for much less, but there may be some tasks that it may take a much cheaper open source model like $100 worth of tokens when a much smarter model may be able to do it in one shot, very quickly, for much cheaper.

Dan: Yeah, I would say that routing is an interesting thing. And there's a reason so many enterprises and computer scientists are looking into it because we have models that are overkill for many things. You don't need Fable to tell you how much water to put — soft to put — and rice and stuff.

At the same time, you do need to know when to go to Fable when you are trying to crack something that's above your pay grade. So I think routing is a great direction. Routing too — it's not that easy. As someone who's worked on it, you can train —

Host: Or what are the main challenges, having the experience of seeing the difficulties of routing?

Dan: So I think routing will be part of the solution there for sure. And I think many people, not just myself, say the solution is multi-model. It's not Engram taking over as one model and you teach it things and, yeah, you can close Stargate. That's not our approach here.

The solution will involve some form of routing. And in that part of routing, our thing would be like: you know, your best friend or your employee. You cannot fire someone who's been looking over your shoulder the whole time and can tell you where to look, where to focus, and can even go out and ask Fable things in a more targeted way with more context — that Fable can then go and work on it for days and solve very high stakes tasks for you.

So I think routing is great. It's just — like any other research area — it's an unsolved thing. There's been progress on it, but a lot more work is required to actually route things to the right model at the right time, the right cost. And models are continually updated. Versions are coming out.

Host: Yeah. Okay, that makes sense. It seems like there's still a lot of open questions and even some of the work you're doing is confidential, which makes sense.

团队：一群 Researchers 组成的公司

Host: And as you'll release more — I guess now shifting towards a team. How is it like working with such a mixed group of folks? Like some who had professors while you were studying and some that you also met and have left their PhDs or finished their PhDs as well like Jack. And yeah, having such a diverse group?

Dan: So I would say there's many strengths to our group. Um, I'm not sure diversity is one of them. So if you're watching this, we have a lot of researchers. We could diversify a little bit.

I would say for this niche that we're trying to solve — which is memory and continual learning, taking knowledge, shoving it into weights — I think our team is the most specialized team in that kind of thing. And our team is fun.

You know, Jack is a fun guy. And Jack is teaching us a lot of things about how to think clearly, communicate ourselves clearly internally and to the external world. Jesse in the same way — very complimentary kind of approaches. She thought a lot about how humans and AI work together in this closed loop of a machine and human.

Sabri and I were a tiny bit more on the systems side, and I — Scott and I worked on statistics. So it's all researchers. It's not diverse in this kind of way, but it is, as you said, diverse in our inclinations. Some of us are more mathy, some of us are more systems-y, some of us are more like AI leaders.

And so it's been interesting. The way we try to do this is to take those PhDs with a lot of experience and pair them up with those kind of up-and-coming, rising, cracked types that have joined our company. For example, Chisa or Drew from Stanford and Berkeley respectively. They have research backgrounds and have written papers, but they're kind of entering this field with a lot of momentum. And we like to pair them up with someone who's been around the field for a few years, has some intuitions, can warn them from the rabbit holes. This powerful combination of different levels of expertise, different levels of freshness of thought is making our place an interesting one.

And I would say culturally all of us from day one — in the fall/winter of '25 which was a crazy time in terms of frontier lab recruiting and AGI anxiety — all of us entered into the startup world in a very sober way, knowing that it's not just a research club in here, it's not just a drone club in here. The thing that's missing is products. And products need to be distributed. And you have to earn the right to play by selling things that people love. So we're working very hard on that as well.

一段轻松的插曲：谁最会吃

Host: That makes sense. I guess more of a fun question. Out of founders, who do you think has the best taste in food? I assume you guys sometimes order food or even go out — like are there some that stand out to you?

Dan: I would say my co-founder Sabri — he's consistently — he loves life. He loves good food. He's very well known in the company as someone who's the surf and turf guy.

Host: Gotcha.

Dan: He'll have it for lunch and dinner. And, um, being on DoorDash with him has been an inspiration for me. And I would say Jack and Jesse have good taste as well. Great taste as well. Yeah, right. It's fun to be with them. And we're now at a small office like an apartment, so we have all the lunches and often dinners together. And it's a bit like a family, you know. At home you see your parents and siblings — sometimes it's too much, but you're never going to forget that period.

Host: No, it does sound very fun. Have you cooked for them yet?

Dan: Um, have I? Maybe not enough. Maybe I should. Maybe this weekend I'll cook some.

Host: Okay.

Dan: They're cooking other things. They're cooking stuff on the science.

Host: That's true. They're cooking on the research.

Dan: Yeah, on the research. Yeah.

Call to Action：招什么样的人

Host: The rice should be done.

Dan: Should be done.

Host: Do we want to give it a try? Let's see. First bite. It's good. It's nice.

Dan: Oh, yeah. It is. Yeah. The bottom is definitely more cooked. Um, you can also probably let that sit. Now these guys — yeah. Would you say this is ready? These guys are probably ready. We can perhaps open it up a little bit. Let it concentrate a bit. We can let those guys concentrate for a couple minutes here.

Host: Do you have any call to actions? Are you guys looking for specific folks, hiring for types of researchers?

Dan: Yeah. Uh, I would say what we're trying to build — those systems that continually learn — obviously there are many open problems on the research side. How do you do this without destroying the model? How do you do it in a cost-efficient way? What data do you learn from? And stuff like that.

But it's also an extremely ambitious infrastructure problem. If you truly believe in the possibility that there's going to be trillions of tokens of enterprise data — or even personal data — and if you truly believe that we can get to the level where we have those kinds of parameter-efficient adapters for every person and team, you suddenly think about deployments that involve millions of different endpoints stored in different places that need to be efficiently read from disk to HBM, and then used at inference time, and swapped and updated.

It's going to be — if things work out for us — this thing will have a massive compute footprint and many new questions on systems and balancing of AI workloads in new ways.

So the kind of people that I think could enjoy them and help us a lot are: one, LLM performance engineers, research engineers who know how to make things go brrr. We have some of them — Cade Daniel who was one of the inference leads at Databricks and one of the core contributors of vLLM. And we are all kind of like systems inclined.

But we think infrastructure engineers — people who know how to work and build those large APIs and databases — would have very fun time working on questions that they can't find in other places.

And generally, we always are open to smart and creative people who think out of the box and are committed to working on interesting problems.

效率 = 智能：核心信念

Dan: And I also wanted to say something. I guess I was just thinking about it while I was talking before that a lot of the use cases for this kind of training and continual learning involve —

Let's give it a teeny bit more — bit less fluids there. Okay.

So the point for me — the principle — is: any kind of efficiency and intelligence, they cannot really be decoupled. Sometimes people think if you're building something that's more efficient that can save you dollars, therefore you're not in the premium category, you're in the cheaper product. And that — and intelligence — this is just purely wrong, right?

The more you can do with less, the more ambitious tasks you can solve longer term. So I think the current paradigm of scaling with AI has been doing more with more. And it took us extremely far and it will keep being a valuable way to build intelligence. But I think the next paradigm — and many leaders of the labs are seeing it — involves certain element of doing more with less to take on longer horizon tasks and harder problems generally.

So I think going beyond enterprises and going beyond efficiency, that's where I hope to go, if we solve the challenges that we're facing right now.

Host: Okay, that makes sense. I think thinking of both couples does seem very important, especially as you want to, like you said, do more ambitious tasks.

收尾：装盘、开吃、感谢

Dan: Yeah. Should we mix in the spinach or just let it steam?

Host: Try mix it up. Let's try it.

Dan: It will steam it, soften a little bit. And I could cut this lemon.

Host: Yeah. And then just squeeze the lemon in. Yeah. Okay. The sauce, right?

Dan: Yeah.

Host: Okay. Don't want to squeeze this one. This one adds a little less, but amazing. And where can people find you?

Dan: Where can people find us? They can find us at engram.com. They can find us in SF. They can write to me at dan@engram.com to talk about things.

And yeah, I would say as we're scaling up different parts of the company that involve engineering, that involve product and business, there's a lot of things to talk about beyond the frontier AI type research. And there's a lot for us to learn from smart people.

Host: Great. Awesome. That's exciting. Should we try it?

Dan: Yeah. I challenge every leader of a neolab to come to my house in Noe Valley and cook things with me. And I'm sure we can learn a lot from each other.

Host: Cheers.

Dan: Good.

Host: I think it turned out well now.

Dan: Yeah, very good. Wow, that's very good. Maybe a little bit Persian I would say, with the yellow rice. So great. I'm a big fan.

Host: Okay. Sean rice is amazing. Rice and finish is amazing on this one. Actually I'm just going to finish the whole thing after — meatballs a bit softer than…

Dan: Yeah. Swig — the entire show I pretended to be the cooking expert here.

Host: Okay.

Dan: It has to be pure crazy. Just kidding.

Host: This is like an eight out of 10. 9 out of 10.

Dan: Great.

Host: But yeah. No, I think that's basically it. It turned out pretty well. I mean, how was it? Was it fun? Did you enjoy it?

Dan: It was the funnest podcast I ever had. Makes you feel at home.

Host: That's true.

Dan: Easier to talk about things.

Host: Thank you again for coming and hopefully it was a fun time.

Dan: It was super fun. My weights have been updated.

Host: That's good. Night's again.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Engram 不是在"改进记忆"，它是在把 LLM 从"失忆症患者"变成"有肌肉记忆的厨师"
Dan 反复用厨师做比喻，但他真正戳破的东西比比喻本身残酷得多——**当今所有 LLM 都是电影《Memento》里那个每天醒来重看笔记的 Leonard**。这不是"上下文窗口太短"的问题，而是一个几乎被 AI 圈集体忽视的认知科学真相：人类记忆至少有两套系统——**陈述性记忆（notes/RAG/context）** 和 **程序性记忆（weights/intuition）**。过去两年整个行业疯狂扩大"陈述记忆"的容量（100 万、1000 万 token context），却几乎没人在动"程序记忆"这一层。
这里有个惊人的历史回响：认知神经科学早在 1995 年就有过一次几乎一模一样的辩论——McClelland 的"Complementary Learning Systems"论文论证海马体（快速学习、易冲突）必须与新皮层（缓慢学习、稳定泛化）互补，否则会发生"灾难性遗忘"。**Engram 正在给 LLM 补上"新皮层"，而整个行业过去两年只在扩建海马

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Sanctions Are Economic Chemotherapy – Sarah Paine
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

隐形的胜利：为什么最重要的成就永远无法被看见

Sarah Paine 提出了一个令人不安的认识论悖论：海洋秩序的成功本质上是不可证伪的。你无法证明你阻止了什么——因为它从未发生。这不仅是战略问题，更是一个深刻的心理学与政治学困境：民主政治奖励可见的成就（"我建了这条路"），而海洋战略生产的是不可见的非事件（"世界贸易今年没有崩溃"）。这解释了为什么每一代人都会周期性地怀疑北约、联合国、航行自由巡逻的价值——成功制造了让人们质疑成功的条件。这与公共卫生领域惊人地同构：疫苗接种率高时，人们开始怀疑疫苗；桥梁不塌时，人们抱怨维护预算太高。海洋秩序是全球化的免疫系统，而免疫系统的最高荣誉就是被遗忘。

化疗隐喻背后的时间尺度革命

"制裁是经济化疗"是本段最颠覆性的洞见，但真正的爆点不在医学类比，而在时间尺度的重新校准。西方媒体和政客几乎全部用"季度"或"选举周期"评估制裁——三个月没推翻普京？失败。两年没让金正恩下台？无效。但 Paine 悄悄把时间刻度拉到了代际尺度（generations），并用复利数学把它变成了物理学：1-2% 的年度增长差距，经过 70 年就是南北韩之间那道天堑。这里有一个跨领域连接值得警觉：这与巴菲特的价值投资哲学在数学结构上完全一致——放弃对单次交易的执念，接受"看似平庸的年化差距"，让时间做重活。换句话说，海洋大国是在用复利思维经营地缘政治，而大陆强权用的是赌场思维（一次决定性会战）。这也意味着一个残酷推论：任何以"任期内见效"为逻辑的民主政治，天然不适合执行海洋战略——这才是当前西方对华、对俄政策摇摆的深层结构性原因，而不是简单的党派之争。

"接受问题的存在"作为一种成熟：反乌托邦式的智慧

最出人意料的是 Paine 悄悄埋下的哲学立场：成熟的战略是放弃"消灭问题"的欲望。"朝鲜人想在家过悲惨生活，随他们去"——这句话表面轻佻，实际上颠覆了整个启蒙运动以来的"进步主义"世界观：即认为所有问题都应被彻底解决，所有邪恶都应被根除。海洋思维承认了一个佛教式的真理——问题不是用来消灭的，是用来管理的。这让我联想到一个跨领域的镜像：现代精神病学早已放弃了"治愈"抑郁症、双相障碍的执念，转向"控制在可接受的成本下"（manageable at acceptable cost），这与海洋战略的语言几乎一字不差。再往深处走，你会发现这甚至暗合斯多葛派的核心——区分你能改变的与你必须容忍的。所以海洋战略的隐藏心理学基础，其实是一种帝国级别的斯多葛主义：拒绝为完美主义支付核战争的代价。这也解释了一个反直觉现象：为什么真正长寿的海洋帝国（威尼斯、荷兰、英国、美国）在其鼎盛期，恰恰是最"不革命"、最保守、最愿意与讨厌的对手长期共存的——它们理解一个大陆强权永远无法理解的秘密：赢得永恒的方式，是放弃赢得一切。

📝 转录文本

海洋世界的隐形逻辑

The maritime world is invisible, and it took me 15 years to teaching at the War College to notice the obvious. Sometimes the obvious becomes obvious by stating it, and let's see if it works for you.

The continental world is visible, cuz it's about positive objectives. It's all about making things happen. So you want to take territory — you can see it. Either you did or you didn't.

But the maritime world is about negative objectives. It's preventing bad things from happening. And you can never prove that you prevented anything. Maybe no one was trying anything to begin with, and it's invisible, right? The thing you prevented never happened. Surprise, surprise, you can't see it.

大陆与海洋：正向 vs 负向目标

But in the continental world, it's all about positive operational objectives. You're destroying neighbors to take their territory. But at the strategic level, you are destroying wealth at a massive clip. That is a problem.

Whereas the maritime world, it's invisible — all of the bad things that you're preventing: anyone messing with the international trading order, which you're making your money from in part. You got a lot of domestic activity as well.

海军的和平时期使命

And navies, their missions more often than not are also about negative objectives. Not all of them. What are they? And think about naval missions. Most of them happen in peacetime. Yeah, wartime happens too. But for navies, it's mostly in peacetime. And what are they up to?

  • Mission number one: prevent the destruction of the global trading system. That would be important. Your prosperity hinges on that one.
  • The second, really important mission: prevent limits on freedom of navigation, cuz if you cannot get goods from port to port, it's pretty useless.
  • And thirdly, it is to deter expansion — into taking over big countries, taking over little countries.

Think about it. The bedrock principle of international law is sovereignty. If we're all going to be invading each other, when is international law there?

应对"流氓行为"的工具箱

And the antidotes to rogue behavior in this maritime world are alliances, diplomacy, sanctions, embargoes, containment — numerous instruments of national power. And navies are about deterrence, blockade, and commerce raiding.

What you're doing is you're preventing the continental problem, the elephant, from foraging. You're just saying, "You're getting a timeout from the rules-based order, cuz you can't behave yourself."

制裁作为"经济化疗"：复利的力量

And you can go, "Well, the continental problem's still there." Well, what's interesting — so you all know about compounded growth. It's powerful, right? Over generations.

What people don't think about — this back to negative objectives — sanctions are like economic chemotherapy. What you're doing is preventing 1 or 2% growth per annum, even with leaky sanctions, right?

Well, and you go, "So what?" Well, no — so very what: the difference over several generations is the difference between North and South Korea. I get it, North Korea's still there, but they got a much smaller piggy bank than they would have otherwise.

遏制而非消灭：可接受成本的世界观

And yeah, you're not getting rid of the problem. But if the rogue state has nuclear weapons, and you go, "Oh gee, we just want to get rid of it" — really, you want to have a nuclear war to get rid of North Korea? Isn't it —

This world is not about the operational win of totally eliminating a problem. It's rather containing it at acceptable cost.

So the North Koreans want to live miserable lives at home? Have fun with it. Go live miserable lives at home. And it's being contained at acceptable cost. That's what's going on.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
